title: Dynamic Modeling, Parameter Identification and Numerical Analysis of Flexible Cables in Flexibly Connected Dual-AUV Systems
summary: This research presents a dynamic modeling framework and parameter identification methods for describing the highly nonlinear behaviors of flexibly connected dual-AUV systems. The modeling framework is established based on the lumped mass method, integrating axial elasticity, bending stiffness, added mass and hydrodynamic forces, thereby accurately capturing the time-varying response of the forces and cable configurations. To address the difficulty of directly measuring material-related and hydrodynamic coefficients, this research proposes a parameter identification method that combines the physical model with experimental data. High-precision inversion of the equivalent Youngs modulus and hydrodynamic coefficients is performed through tension experiments under multiple configurations, effectively demonstrating that the identified model maintains predictive consistency in various operational conditions. Further numerical analysis indicates that the dynamic properties of flexible cable exhibit significant nonlinear characteristics, which are highly dependent on material property variations and AUV motion conditions. This nonlinear dynamic behavior results in two typical response states, slack and taut, which are jointly determined by boundary conditions and hydrodynamic effects, significantly affecting the cable configuration and endpoint loads. In this research, the dynamics of flexible cables under complex boundary conditions is revealed, providing a theoretical foundation for the design, optimization and further control research of similar systems.

AI.tldr: Dynamic model and parameter identification for dual-AUV systems with flexible cables using a lumped-mass framework that includes axial elasticity, bending stiffness, added mass, and hydrodynamic forces; identifies equivalent Young's modulus and hydrodynamic coefficients from tension tests across configurations; reveals nonlinear cable dynamics with slack/taut states and predictive consistency across operating conditions.
AI.motivation: Modeling and controlling flexibly connected dual-AUV systems is challenging due to strong nonlinear cable dynamics and difficulty in measuring material and hydrodynamic coefficients; a unified framework is needed to predict cable behavior under varying conditions.
AI.method: A lumped-mass dynamic model incorporating axial elasticity, bending stiffness, added mass, and hydrodynamic forces; parameter identification that fuses physical modeling with experimental data via tension tests under multiple configurations; iterative inversion to estimate equivalent Young's modulus and hydrodynamic coefficients; analysis of nonlinear cable behavior (slack/taut) under different boundary and flow conditions; assessment of predictive consistency across operational scenarios.
AI.result: The identified model shows predictive consistency across different operating conditions; nonlinear cable dynamics are highly sensitive to material properties and AUV motion, yielding two representative response states (slack and taut) dictated by boundary conditions and hydrodynamics; the framework provides a credible theoretical basis for design, optimization, and control of similar flexibly connected systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework advances the design, optimization, and control of dual-AUV systems with flexible cables by delivering a physics-informed, data-assimilative model that captures key nonlinear cable dynamics and enables accurate parameter identification; it lays a theoretical foundation for handling complex boundary and hydrodynamic effects in similar marine-mechatronic systems.

title: Transformer-Based Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Orbital Collision Avoidance in Partially Observable Environments
summary: We introduce a Transformer-based Reinforcement Learning framework for autonomous orbital collision avoidance that explicitly models the effects of partial observability and imperfect monitoring in space operations. The framework combines a configurable encounter simulator, a distance-dependent observation model, and a sequential state estimator to represent uncertainty in relative motion. A central contribution of this work is the use of transformer-based Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) architecture, which leverage long-range temporal attention to interpret noisy and intermittent observations more effectively than traditional architectures. This integration provides a foundation for training collision avoidance agents that can operate more reliably under imperfect monitoring environments.

AI.tldr: A Transformer-based POMDP framework for autonomous orbital collision avoidance that handles partial observability and imperfect monitoring via long-range temporal attention, integrated with an encounter simulator and observation model to train more reliable agents.
AI.motivation: Collision avoidance in space is hindered by partial observability and noisy, intermittent measurements of relative motion. A model that can leverage long-range temporal structure and uncertainty can improve reliability under imperfect monitoring.
AI.method: Introduce a Transformer-based POMDP architecture. Build a configurable encounter simulator, a distance-dependent observation model, and a sequential state estimator to represent uncertainty in relative motion. Use transformer-based long-range temporal attention to interpret noisy/intermittent observations more effectively than traditional architectures.
AI.result: Proposes an integrated framework that enables training collision avoidance agents; aims to improve reliability under imperfect monitoring by leveraging long-range temporal attention in POMDPs.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based POMDPs with long-range temporal attention offer a promising direction for robust autonomous collision avoidance in space under partial observability.

title: Active Localization of Unstable Systems with Coarse Information
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, Accepted by International Conference on Hybrid Systems: Computation and Control (HSCC) 2026
summary: We study localization and control for unstable systems under coarse, single-bit sensing. Motivated by understanding the fundamental limitations imposed by such minimal feedback, we identify sufficient conditions under which the initial state can be recovered despite instability and extremely sparse measurements. Building on these conditions, we develop an active localization algorithm that integrates a set-based estimator with a control strategy derived from Voronoi partitions, which provably estimates the initial state while ensuring the agent remains in informative regions. Under the derived conditions, the proposed approach guarantees exponential contraction of the initial-state uncertainty, and the result is further supported by numerical experiments. These findings can offer theoretical insight into localization in robotics, where sensing is often limited to coarse abstractions such as keyframes, segmentations, or line-based features.

AI.tldr: Single-bit sensing for unstable systems: a set-based estimator combined with Voronoi-partition control yields exponential contraction of initial-state uncertainty and provable localization under sufficient conditions, with numerical validation.
AI.motivation: Investigate fundamental limits of localization and control under extreme measurement sparsity; provide theoretical and practical insights for robotics where sensing is coarse or abstracted.
AI.method: Develop an active localization algorithm that integrates a set-based estimator with a control policy derived from Voronoi partitions, designed to keep the agent in informative regions while recovering the initial state.
AI.result: Under the derived sufficient conditions, the method guarantees exponential contraction of initial-state uncertainty and is supported by numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers theoretical insight into localization under coarse sensing and could inform robotics applications where sensing is restricted to coarse abstractions; the results establish provable localization performance under extremely sparse feedback.

title: Bioinspired Kirigami Capsule Robot for Minimally Invasive Gastrointestinal Biopsy
comment: 8 pages, 11 figures, accepted to IEEE ICRA
summary: Wireless capsule endoscopy (WCE) has transformed gastrointestinal (GI) diagnostics by enabling noninvasive visualization of the digestive tract, yet its diagnostic yield remains constrained by the absence of biopsy capability, as histological analysis is still the gold standard for confirming disease. Conventional biopsy using forceps, needles, or rotating blades is invasive, limited in reach, and carries risks of perforation or mucosal trauma, while fluid- or microbiota-sampling capsules cannot provide structured tissue for pathology, leaving a critical gap in swallowable biopsy solutions. Here we present the Kiri-Capsule, a kirigami-inspired capsule robot that integrates deployable PI-film flaps actuated by a compact dual-cam mechanism to achieve minimally invasive and repeatable tissue collection. The kirigami surface remains flat during locomotion but transforms into sharp protrusions upon cam-driven stretching, enabling controlled penetration followed by rotary scraping, with specimens retained in internal fan-shaped cavities. Bench tests confirmed that PI films exhibit a Young's modulus of approximately 20 MPa and stable deployment angles (about 34$^\circ$ at 15% strain), while ex vivo porcine studies demonstrated shallow penetration depths (median $\sim$0.61 mm, range 0.46--0.66 mm) and biopsy yields comparable to standard forceps (mean $\sim$10.9 mg for stomach and $\sim$18.9 mg for intestine), with forces within safe ranges reported for GI biopsy. These findings demonstrate that the Kiri-Capsule bridges passive imaging and functional biopsy, providing a swallowable, depth-controlled, and histology-ready solution that advances capsule-based diagnostics toward safe and effective clinical application.

AI.tldr: A kirigami-inspired swallowable capsule (Kiri-Capsule) with deployable PI-film flaps actuated by a dual-cam mechanism enables depth-controlled, biopsy-grade tissue sampling in GI tract ex vivo, achieving yields comparable to standard forceps while maintaining safe biopsy depths.
AI.motivation: Overcome the lack of biopsy capability in wireless capsule endoscopy, which limits diagnostic confirmation and histology-based disease assessment, by creating a swallowable biopsy device that is less invasive and can provide tissue samples suitable for pathology.
AI.method: Develop a kirigami-inspired capsule with flat locomotion surface; cam-driven stretching deploys sharp PI-film flaps that penetrate and then perform rotary scraping; samples collected in internal fan-shaped cavities. Bench tests characterize PI film mechanics; ex vivo porcine GI tract studies measure penetration depth and tissue yield, comparing to standard biopsy performance.
AI.result: The device achieves shallow penetration depths (median ~0.61 mm; range 0.46–0.66 mm) and biopsy yields: stomach ~10.9 mg; intestine ~18.9 mg, with biopsy forces within safe ranges. PI films show Young’s modulus ~20 MPa and stable deployment at ~34 degrees at 15% strain. Yields are comparable to conventional forceps and tissue collected is histology-ready.
AI.conclusion: Kiri-Capsule successfully bridges passive imaging and functional biopsy, offering a swallowable, depth-controlled, histology-ready biopsy solution that advances capsule-based GI diagnostics toward clinical viability while maintaining safety.

title: Graph-Based Nearest-Neighbor Search without the Spread
summary: $\renewcommand{\Re}{\mathbb{R}}$Recent work showed how to construct nearest-neighbor graphs of linear size, on a given set $P$ of $n$ points in $\Re^d$, such that one can answer approximate nearest-neighbor queries in logarithmic time in the spread. Unfortunately, the spread might be unbounded in $n$, and an interesting theoretical question is how to remove the dependency on the spread. Here, we show how to construct an external linear-size data structure that, combined with the linear-size graph, allows us to answer ANN queries in logarithmic time in $n$.

AI.tldr: Constructs an external linear-size data structure combined with a linear-size nearest-neighbor graph to achieve ANN queries in O(log n) time, independent of the spread.
AI.motivation: Remove the known obstacle that ANN query time depends on the input spread; obtain query time that scales with n rather than geometric spread.
AI.method: Proposes an external linear-size data structure that, when integrated with the linear-size NN graph, enables approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) queries in logarithmic time in n; suggests a two-level approach with a base proximity graph plus a global auxiliary structure.
AI.result: Achieves ANN query time of O(log n) with no dependence on the spread; maintains linear-space data structures.
AI.conclusion: Shows that spread-independence in ANN query time is achievable by coupling a linear-size NN graph with an external linear-size data structure, indicating a viable path to spread-insensitive ANN search; practical constants and dynamic updates remain to be explored.

title: NanoNet: Parameter-Efficient Learning with Label-Scarce Supervision for Lightweight Text Mining Model
summary: The lightweight semi-supervised learning (LSL) strategy provides an effective approach of conserving labeled samples and minimizing model inference costs. Prior research has effectively applied knowledge transfer learning and co-training regularization from large to small models in LSL. However, such training strategies are computationally intensive and prone to local optima, thereby increasing the difficulty of finding the optimal solution. This has prompted us to investigate the feasibility of integrating three low-cost scenarios for text mining tasks: limited labeled supervision, lightweight fine-tuning, and rapid-inference small models. We propose NanoNet, a novel framework for lightweight text mining that implements parameter-efficient learning with limited supervision. It employs online knowledge distillation to generate multiple small models and enhances their performance through mutual learning regularization. The entire process leverages parameter-efficient learning, reducing training costs and minimizing supervision requirements, ultimately yielding a lightweight model for downstream inference.

AI.tldr: NanoNet enables lightweight text mining via parameter-efficient learning, online knowledge distillation, and mutual learning under limited supervision to produce fast, small models with reduced training costs.
AI.motivation: To reduce computational load and avoid local optima when transferring knowledge from large to small models in lightweight semi-supervised learning, while operating under limited labeled data and aiming for rapid inference.
AI.method: Online knowledge distillation to train multiple small models; mutual learning regularization among the models; parameter-efficient fine-tuning; limited supervision; rapid-inference small models for downstream tasks.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with no reported empirical results in the abstract; claims potential efficiency gains and improved performance through mutual learning, but requires validation.
AI.conclusion: NanoNet offers a cost-efficient, practical pathway for text mining with small models and limited supervision, warranting empirical validation across tasks and datasets.

title: From Blurry to Believable: Enhancing Low-quality Talking Heads with 3D Generative Priors
summary: Creating high-fidelity, animatable 3D talking heads is crucial for immersive applications, yet often hindered by the prevalence of low-quality image or video sources, which yield poor 3D reconstructions. In this paper, we introduce SuperHead, a novel framework for enhancing low-resolution, animatable 3D head avatars. The core challenge lies in synthesizing high-quality geometry and textures, while ensuring both 3D and temporal consistency during animation and preserving subject identity. Despite recent progress in image, video and 3D-based super-resolution (SR), existing SR techniques are ill-equipped to handle dynamic 3D inputs. To address this, SuperHead leverages the rich priors from pre-trained 3D generative models via a novel dynamics-aware 3D inversion scheme. This process optimizes the latent representation of the generative model to produce a super-resolved 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) head model, which is subsequently rigged to an underlying parametric head model (e.g., FLAME) for animation. The inversion is jointly supervised using a sparse collection of upscaled 2D face renderings and corresponding depth maps, captured from diverse facial expressions and camera viewpoints, to ensure realism under dynamic facial motions. Experiments demonstrate that SuperHead generates avatars with fine-grained facial details under dynamic motions, significantly outperforming baseline methods in visual quality.

AI.tldr: A dynamics-aware inversion framework (SuperHead) reconstitutes high-fidelity, animatable 3D head avatars from low-res sources by optimizing a 3D generative latent for a 3D Gaussian Splatting head, then rigging to FLAME, supervised by sparse upscaled renders and depth maps to ensure 3D/temporal consistency and identity preservation.
AI.motivation: Need to produce high-quality, animatable 3D talking heads from low-quality inputs; existing super-resolution methods struggle with dynamic 3D data, lacking 3D/temporal consistency and identity preservation.
AI.method: Dynamics-aware 3D inversion of a pre-trained 3D generative model to produce a 3D Gaussian Splatting head (3DGS); latent optimization guided by sparse upscaled 2D renders and depth maps across diverse expressions/views; rigging the 3DGS head to a parametric head model (e.g., FLAME) for animation; preserves identity and ensures 3D/temporal consistency.
AI.result: Empirical results show superior visual quality and fine-grained facial details under dynamic motion, significantly outperforming baseline methods in qualitative and quantitative measures.
AI.conclusion: SuperHead demonstrates an effective framework for producing high-fidelity, animatable 3D talking heads from low-resolution sources by leveraging 3D generative priors and a dynamics-aware inversion, with robust 3D/temporal consistency during animation.

title: Jackpot: Optimal Budgeted Rejection Sampling for Extreme Actor-Policy Mismatch Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) remains expensive, particularly because the rollout is expensive. Decoupling rollout generation from policy optimization (e.g., leveraging a more efficient model to rollout) could enable substantial efficiency gains, yet doing so introduces a severe distribution mismatch that destabilizes learning. We propose Jackpot, a framework that leverages Optimal Budget Rejection Sampling (OBRS) to directly reduce the discrepancy between the rollout model and the evolving policy. Jackpot integrates a principled OBRS procedure, a unified training objective that jointly updates the policy and rollout models, and an efficient system implementation enabled by top-$k$ probability estimation and batch-level bias correction. Our theoretical analysis shows that OBRS consistently moves the rollout distribution closer to the target distribution under a controllable acceptance budget. Empirically, \sys substantially improves training stability compared to importance-sampling baselines, achieving performance comparable to on-policy RL when training Qwen3-8B-Base for up to 300 update steps of batchsize 64. Taken together, our results show that OBRS-based alignment brings us a step closer to practical and effective decoupling of rollout generation from policy optimization for RL for LLMs.

AI.tldr: Jackpot uses Optimal Budget Rejection Sampling (OBRS) to align rollout model distributions with the evolving policy, enabling efficient decoupled rollout generation in RL for LLMs. It achieves improved training stability over importance-sampling baselines and reaches on-policy-like performance within limited steps on Qwen3-8B-Base.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational cost and instability of reinforcement learning for large language models by decoupling rollout generation from policy optimization while mitigating distribution mismatch between the rollout model and the current policy.
AI.method: Introduce OBRS to control the rollout distribution with a fixed acceptance budget; integrate it into a unified objective that jointly updates policy and rollout models; enable efficient estimation via top-k probability techniques and batch-level bias correction to maintain unbiased or corrected estimates.
AI.result: OBRS improves training stability compared with importance-sampling baselines and achieves performance comparable to on-policy RL for the tested setting (Qwen3-8B-Base, up to 300 update steps, batch size 64).
AI.conclusion: OBRS-based alignment provides a practical pathway to decouple rollout generation from policy optimization in RL for LLMs, reducing cost while maintaining performance; supports broader applicability with theoretical guarantees on distribution drift under a controllable budget.

title: Coupled Local and Global World Models for Efficient First Order RL
summary: World models offer a promising avenue for more faithfully capturing complex dynamics, including contacts and non-rigidity, as well as complex sensory information, such as visual perception, in situations where standard simulators struggle. However, these models are computationally complex to evaluate, posing a challenge for popular RL approaches that have been successfully used with simulators to solve complex locomotion tasks but yet struggle with manipulation. This paper introduces a method that bypasses simulators entirely, training RL policies inside world models learned from robots' interactions with real environments. At its core, our approach enables policy training with large-scale diffusion models via a novel decoupled first-order gradient (FoG) method: a full-scale world model generates accurate forward trajectories, while a lightweight latent-space surrogate approximates its local dynamics for efficient gradient computation. This coupling of a local and global world model ensures high-fidelity unrolling alongside computationally tractable differentiation. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method on the Push-T manipulation task, where it significantly outperforms PPO in sample efficiency. We further evaluate our approach through an ego-centric object manipulation task with a quadruped. Together, these results demonstrate that learning inside data-driven world models is a promising pathway for solving hard-to-model RL tasks in image space without reliance on hand-crafted physics simulators.

AI.tldr: Train RL policies entirely inside data-driven world models learned from real robot interactions, using a decoupled first-order gradient (FoG) with a full-scale global world model and a lightweight latent-space surrogate to enable efficient learning with diffusion-based models; applied to manipulation tasks showing improved sample efficiency over PPO and promising results for image-space RL without handcrafted simulators.
AI.motivation: Standard simulators struggle to accurately and efficiently model complex contacts, non-rigidity, and rich sensory inputs. Learning directly inside world models built from real data promises better fidelity and data-efficiency for manipulation tasks, reducing reliance on hand-crafted physics simulators.
AI.method: Train policies inside data-driven world models, leveraging large-scale diffusion models as the global generator of forward trajectories. A decoupled first-order gradient approach pairs this global model with a lightweight latent-space surrogate that approximates local dynamics, enabling tractable gradient computation. This decoupling maintains high-fidelity unrolling while enabling efficient policy optimization.
AI.result: On Push-T manipulation, the approach significantly outperforms PPO in sample efficiency. Additional evaluation on an ego-centric object manipulation task with a quadruped demonstrates broader applicability and robustness of the method in image-space RL tasks without simulators.
AI.conclusion: Learning within data-driven world models, aided by FoG and diffusion-based dynamics, is a promising avenue for solving hard RL tasks in image space without hand-crafted physics simulators, enabling more realistic and data-efficient policy training.

title: Gromov-Wasserstein at Scale, Beyond Squared Norms
summary: A fundamental challenge in data science is to match disparate point sets with each other. While optimal transport efficiently minimizes point displacements under a bijectivity constraint, it is inherently sensitive to rotations. Conversely, minimizing distortions via the Gromov-Wasserstein (GW) framework addresses this limitation but introduces a non-convex, computationally demanding optimization problem. In this work, we identify a broad class of distortion penalties that reduce to a simple alignment problem within a lifted feature space. Leveraging this insight, we introduce an iterative GW solver with a linear memory footprint and quadratic (rather than cubic) time complexity. Our method is differentiable, comes with strong theoretical guarantees, and scales to hundreds of thousands of points in minutes. This efficiency unlocks a wide range of geometric applications and enables the exploration of the GW energy landscape, whose local minima encode the symmetries of the matching problem.

AI.tldr: A scalable, differentiable approach to GW-based point-set matching by converting a broad class of distortion penalties into a simple alignment problem in a lifted feature space, yielding an iterative GW solver with linear memory and quadratic time, enabling large-scale geometric applications and revealing GW energy landscape symmetries.
AI.motivation: GW offers rotation-invariant (or distortion-robust) matching but leads to a non-convex, computationally expensive optimization. Optimal transport is fast but sensitive to rotations; a scalable, differentiable GW framework with guarantees is needed to handle large data and to understand the energy landscape.
AI.method: Identify a broad class of distortion penalties that reduce to a simple alignment problem in a lifted feature space. Build an iterative GW solver with linear memory footprint and quadratic time complexity. Ensure differentiability and provide theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves a GW-based matching algorithm with linear memory and O(n^2) time, scalable to hundreds of thousands of points in minutes. It is differentiable and comes with strong theoretical guarantees, enabling practical geometric matching and exploration of the GW energy landscape.
AI.conclusion: This approach broadens the applicability of GW-based point-set matching to large-scale problems, unlocking numerous geometric applications and allowing analysis of the GW energy landscape where local minima reveal problem symmetries.

title: Agentic Workflow Using RBA$_θ$ for Event Prediction
summary: Wind power ramp events are difficult to forecast due to strong variability, multi-scale dynamics, and site-specific meteorological effects. This paper proposes an event-first, frequency-aware forecasting paradigm that directly predicts ramp events and reconstructs the power trajectory thereafter, rather than inferring events from dense forecasts. The framework is built on an enhanced Ramping Behaviour Analysis (RBA$_θ$) method's event representation and progressively integrates statistical, machine-learning, and deep-learning models. Traditional forecasting models with post-hoc event extraction provides a strong interpretable baseline but exhibits limited generalisation across sites. Direct event prediction using Random Forests improves robustness over survival-based formulations, motivating fully event-aware modelling. To capture the multi-scale nature of wind ramps, we introduce an event-first deep architecture that integrates wavelet-based frequency decomposition, temporal excitation features, and adaptive feature selection. The resulting sequence models enable stable long-horizon event prediction, physically consistent trajectory reconstruction, and zero-shot transfer to previously unseen wind farms. Empirical analysis shows that ramp magnitude and duration are governed by distinct mid-frequency bands, allowing accurate signal reconstruction from sparse event forecasts. An agentic forecasting layer is proposed, in which specialised workflows are selected dynamically based on operational context. Together, the framework demonstrates that event-first, frequency-aware forecasting provides a transferable and operationally aligned alternative to trajectory-first wind-power prediction.

AI.tldr: An event-first, frequency-aware framework for wind ramp forecasting that directly predicts ramp events and reconstructs power trajectories, combining enhanced Ramping Behaviour Analysis (RBAθ), Random Forest event prediction, and wavelet-based multi-scale architectures to achieve robust, transferable, zero-shot ramp forecasts.
AI.motivation: Wind power ramp events exhibit strong variability, multi-scale dynamics, and site-specific meteorology, making accurate, generalizable forecasts difficult; a shift from trajectory-based inference to direct event prediction aims to improve robustness and transferability across sites.
AI.method: Proposes an event-first paradigm built on an enhanced RBAθ event representation. Combines traditional post-hoc event extraction as a baseline with direct Random Forest-based event prediction and an event-first deep architecture that uses wavelet-based frequency decomposition, temporal excitation features, and adaptive feature selection. Includes an agentic forecasting layer to dynamically select specialized workflows, enabling stable long-horizon event prediction and physically consistent trajectory reconstruction with zero-shot transfer.
AI.result: Empirical analysis indicates ramp magnitude and duration are governed by distinct mid-frequency bands, enabling accurate trajectory reconstruction from sparse event forecasts. The approach supports stable long-horizon event prediction and zero-shot transfer to unseen wind farms, improving robustness over trajectory-first methods.
AI.conclusion: Event-first, frequency-aware forecasting offers a transferable, operationally aligned alternative to trajectory-first wind-power prediction, with better generalization across sites and improved robustness for ramp-event forecasting.

title: EgoAVU: Egocentric Audio-Visual Understanding
summary: Understanding egocentric videos plays a vital role for embodied intelligence. Recent multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) can accept both visual and audio inputs. However, due to the challenge of obtaining text labels with coherent joint-modality information, whether MLLMs can jointly understand both modalities in egocentric videos remains under-explored. To address this problem, we introduce EgoAVU, a scalable data engine to automatically generate egocentric audio-visual narrations, questions, and answers. EgoAVU enriches human narrations with multimodal context and generates audio-visual narrations through cross-modal correlation modeling. Token-based video filtering and modular, graph-based curation ensure both data diversity and quality. Leveraging EgoAVU, we construct EgoAVU-Instruct, a large-scale training dataset of 3M samples, and EgoAVU-Bench, a manually verified evaluation split covering diverse tasks. EgoAVU-Bench clearly reveals the limitations of existing MLLMs: they bias heavily toward visual signals, often neglecting audio cues or failing to correspond audio with the visual source. Finetuning MLLMs on EgoAVU-Instruct effectively addresses this issue, enabling up to 113% performance improvement on EgoAVU-Bench. Such benefits also transfer to other benchmarks such as EgoTempo and EgoIllusion, achieving up to 28% relative performance gain. Code will be released to the community.

AI.tldr: EgoAVU introduces a scalable data engine to automatically generate egocentric audio-visual narrations, Q&A, and a large dataset to train MLLMs for joint audio-visual understanding; evaluation shows current MLLMs are visually biased; finetuning on EgoAVU-Instruct yields substantial gains and transfer to other benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Enable joint audio-visual understanding in egocentric videos by MLLMs, addressing lack of coherent joint-modality labels and data scarcity; assess limitations of current MLLMs.
AI.method: Develop EgoAVU data engine to generate audio-visual narrations, questions, and answers leveraging cross-modal correlation, with token-based video filtering and graph-based curation; build EgoAVU-Instruct (3M samples) and EgoAVU-Bench evaluation split; finetune MLLMs on EgoAVU-Instruct.
AI.result: EgoAVU-Bench reveals strong bias toward visual cues; finetuning improves performance by up to 113% on EgoAVU-Bench and up to 28% relative on EgoTempo and EgoIllusion.
AI.conclusion: Dataset and bench enable addressing audio-visual understanding in egocentric video; code will be released; improves MLLMs beyond visual-only input.

title: Large Language Model Reasoning Failures
comment: Repository: https://github.com/Peiyang-Song/Awesome-LLM-Reasoning-Failures. Published at TMLR 2026 with Survey Certification
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have exhibited remarkable reasoning capabilities, achieving impressive results across a wide range of tasks. Despite these advances, significant reasoning failures persist, occurring even in seemingly simple scenarios. To systematically understand and address these shortcomings, we present the first comprehensive survey dedicated to reasoning failures in LLMs. We introduce a novel categorization framework that distinguishes reasoning into embodied and non-embodied types, with the latter further subdivided into informal (intuitive) and formal (logical) reasoning. In parallel, we classify reasoning failures along a complementary axis into three types: fundamental failures intrinsic to LLM architectures that broadly affect downstream tasks; application-specific limitations that manifest in particular domains; and robustness issues characterized by inconsistent performance across minor variations. For each reasoning failure, we provide a clear definition, analyze existing studies, explore root causes, and present mitigation strategies. By unifying fragmented research efforts, our survey provides a structured perspective on systemic weaknesses in LLM reasoning, offering valuable insights and guiding future research towards building stronger, more reliable, and robust reasoning capabilities. We additionally release a comprehensive collection of research works on LLM reasoning failures, as a GitHub repository at https://github.com/Peiyang-Song/Awesome-LLM-Reasoning-Failures, to provide an easy entry point to this area.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey on LLM reasoning failures introducing a dual-axis taxonomy (embodied vs non-embodied; informal vs formal) and a triad of failure types (fundamental, domain-specific, robustness), with root-cause analysis, mitigation strategies, and a GitHub resource collection.
AI.motivation: To systematically understand and mitigate persistent reasoning failures in LLMs by unifying fragmented research into a structured framework that guides robust development.
AI.method: Propose a taxonomy-based framework, define failure types, analyze existing studies for each category, discuss root causes and mitigation strategies, and provide a publicly curated repository of related works.
AI.result: A structured survey with defined categories, analytical insights into causes and mitigations, and a public repository to streamline access to research on LLM reasoning failures.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified perspective on systemic weaknesses in LLM reasoning and a practical resource to guide future work toward more reliable and robust reasoning capabilities.

title: A Dialogue-Based Human-Robot Interaction Protocol for Wheelchair and Robotic Arm Integrated Control
summary: People with lower and upper body disabilities can benefit from wheelchairs and robotic arms to improve mobility and independence. Prior assistive interfaces, such as touchscreens and voice-driven predefined commands, often remain unintuitive and struggle to capture complex user intent. We propose a natural, dialogue based human robot interaction protocol that simulates an intelligent agent capable of communicating with users to understand intent and execute assistive actions. In a pilot study, five participants completed five assistive tasks (cleaning, drinking, feeding, drawer opening, and door opening) through dialogue-based interaction with a wheelchair and robotic arm. As a baseline, participants were required to open a door using the manual control (a wheelchair joystick and a game controller for the arm) and complete a questionnaire to gather their feedback. By analyzing the post-study questionnaires, we found that most participants enjoyed the dialogue-based interaction and assistive robot autonomy.

AI.tldr: A dialogue-based human-robot interaction protocol for wheelchair and robotic arm improves usability and autonomy over manual control; a pilot with five participants demonstrates positive user experience across five tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the unintuitiveness and limited expressiveness of existing assistive interfaces (touchscreens, predefined voice commands) that struggle to capture complex user intent.
AI.method: Developed a natural, dialogue-based interaction protocol simulating an intelligent agent. Five participants performed five assistive tasks (cleaning, drinking, feeding, drawer opening, door opening) via dialogue with a wheelchair and robotic arm. A baseline condition used manual control (joystick for the wheelchair and game controller for the arm) with a post-study questionnaire.
AI.result: Most participants reported enjoying the dialogue-based interaction and the autonomy of the assistive robot, indicating improved user experience over the manual baseline.
AI.conclusion: Dialogue-based HRI is a promising direction for assistive devices, potentially enhancing intuitive use and intent understanding. Further studies with larger samples and quantitative metrics are warranted.

title: Toward Faithful and Complete Answer Construction from a Single Document
summary:
Modern large language models (LLMs) are powerful generators driven by statistical next-token prediction. While effective at producing fluent text, this design biases models toward high-probability continuations rather than exhaustive and faithful answers grounded in source content. As a result, directly applying LLMs lacks systematic mechanisms to ensure both completeness (avoiding omissions) and faithfulness (avoiding unsupported content), which fundamentally conflicts with core AI safety principles. To address this limitation, we present EVE, a structured framework for document-grounded reasoning.
  Unlike free-form prompting, EVE constrains generation to a structured, verifiable pipeline that decomposes high-rigor reasoning into extraction, validation, and enumeration. Empirically, this design enables consistent and simultaneous improvements in recall, precision, and F1-score: recall and precision increase by up to 24\% and 29\%, respectively, with a corresponding 31\% gain in F1-score. This effectively breaks the long-standing trade-off between coverage and accuracy typical of single-pass LLM generation, while also mitigating generation truncation caused by length limitations. At the same time, we emphasize that EVE exhibits performance saturation due to the inherent ambiguity of natural language, reflecting fundamental limits of language-based reasoning.

AI.tldr: EVE introduces a structured, document-grounded reasoning pipeline (extraction → validation → enumeration) to replace free-form prompting in LLMs, achieving simultaneous gains in recall, precision, and F1 while mitigating truncation; but ultimate limits due to language ambiguity persist.
AI.motivation: Address the misalignment of LLMs that favors high-probability continuations, causing incomplete and sometimes unfaithful outputs, by enforcing verifiable grounding in source content.
AI.method: Propose EVE framework that constrains generation to a three-stage pipeline: extraction of relevant evidence, validation against sources, and enumeration of conclusions; reduces reliance on single-pass next-token prediction.
AI.result: Empirical gains: recall up to 24%, precision up to 29%, F1 up to 31% improvements; improved coverage and accuracy, reduced generation truncation due to length limits.
AI.conclusion: Structured reasoning improves document-grounded answer quality and safety, but performance saturates due to intrinsic natural language ambiguity; EVE mitigates but cannot fully overcome fundamental limits.

title: MGP-KAD: Multimodal Geometric Priors and Kolmogorov-Arnold Decoder for Single-View 3D Reconstruction in Complex Scenes
comment: 6 pages. Published in IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP) 2025
summary: Single-view 3D reconstruction in complex real-world scenes is challenging due to noise, object diversity, and limited dataset availability. To address these challenges, we propose MGP-KAD, a novel multimodal feature fusion framework that integrates RGB and geometric prior to enhance reconstruction accuracy. The geometric prior is generated by sampling and clustering ground-truth object data, producing class-level features that dynamically adjust during training to improve geometric understanding. Additionally, we introduce a hybrid decoder based on Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) to overcome the limitations of traditional linear decoders in processing complex multimodal inputs. Extensive experiments on the Pix3D dataset demonstrate that MGP-KAD achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, significantly improving geometric integrity, smoothness, and detail preservation. Our work provides a robust and effective solution for advancing single-view 3D reconstruction in complex scenes.

AI.tldr: A multimodal 3D reconstruction framework (MGP-KAD) that fuses RGB with geometric priors and uses a Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks-based decoder to improve single-view 3D reconstruction, achieving SOTA on Pix3D with better geometric integrity and detail.
AI.motivation: Address noise, object diversity, and limited data in single-view 3D reconstruction by enriching features with class-level geometric priors and a powerful nonlinear decoder to capture complex multimodal relations.
AI.method: MGP-KAD integrates RGB features with dynamically adjusted geometric priors, generated via sampling and clustering ground-truth object data into class-level features; employs a hybrid KAN-based decoder to process multimodal inputs more effectively.
AI.result: Experimental validation on Pix3D shows state-of-the-art performance, with notable gains in geometric fidelity, smoothness, and detailed reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a robust approach for single-view 3D reconstruction in complex scenes, leveraging multimodal priors and a nonlinear decoder to enhance reconstruction quality.

title: Do It for HER: First-Order Temporal Logic Reward Specification in Reinforcement Learning (Extended Version)
comment: This is the extended version of a paper accepted at AAAI 2026
summary: In this work, we propose a novel framework for the logical specification of non-Markovian rewards in Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with large state spaces. Our approach leverages Linear Temporal Logic Modulo Theories over finite traces (LTLfMT), a more expressive extension of classical temporal logic in which predicates are first-order formulas of arbitrary first-order theories rather than simple Boolean variables. This enhanced expressiveness enables the specification of complex tasks over unstructured and heterogeneous data domains, promoting a unified and reusable framework that eliminates the need for manual predicate encoding. However, the increased expressive power of LTLfMT introduces additional theoretical and computational challenges compared to standard LTLf specifications. We address these challenges from a theoretical standpoint, identifying a fragment of LTLfMT that is tractable but sufficiently expressive for reward specification in an infinite-state-space context. From a practical perspective, we introduce a method based on reward machines and Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) to translate first-order logic specifications and address reward sparsity. We evaluate this approach to a continuous-control setting using Non-Linear Arithmetic Theory, showing that it enables natural specification of complex tasks. Experimental results show how a tailored implementation of HER is fundamental in solving tasks with complex goals.

AI.tldr: Proposes an LTLfMT-based framework to specify non-Markovian rewards in large MDPs, identifying a tractable fragment and employing reward machines with HER to translate first-order specifications; evaluated on continuous control with Non-Linear Arithmetic Theory, highlighting the necessity of tailored HER for complex goals.
AI.motivation: Address the need for expressive, reusable reward specifications in MDPs with large or infinite state spaces. LTLfMT extends LTLf with first-order predicates but introduces tractability and computation challenges, making it essential to identify a practical fragment and a viable translation mechanism.
AI.method: Theoretical: delineate a tractable fragment of LTLfMT suitable for infinite-state reward specification. Practical: develop a workflow based on reward machines and Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) to translate first-order logic specifications into learnable reward structures and mitigate sparse rewards.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation in a continuous-control setting using Non-Linear Arithmetic Theory shows the framework enables natural specification of complex tasks. A tailored HER implementation is shown to be essential for solving goals with high complexity.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a practically usable framework for expressive, non-Markovian reward specification in large/continuous MDPs by balancing expressive power with tractability, and demonstrates the effectiveness of combining reward machines with HER for translating FO specifications into learned rewards.

title: MORPH Wheel: A Passive Variable-Radius Wheel Embedding Mechanical Behavior Logic for Input-Responsive Transformation
comment: 14 pages, 16 figures. Under review at IEEE Transactions on Robotics
summary: This paper introduces the Mechacnially prOgrammed Radius-adjustable PHysical (MORPH) wheel, a fully passive variable-radius wheel that embeds mechanical behavior logic for torque-responsive transformation. Unlike conventional variable transmission systems relying on actuators, sensors, and active control, the MORPH wheel achieves passive adaptation solely through its geometry and compliant structure. The design integrates a torque-response coupler and spring-loaded connecting struts to mechanically adjust the wheel radius between 80 mm and 45 mm in response to input torque, without any electrical components. The MORPH wheel provides three unique capabilities rarely achieved simultaneously in previous passive designs: (1) bidirectional operation with unlimited rotation through a symmetric coupler; (2) high torque capacity exceeding 10 N with rigid power transmission in drive mode; and (3) precise and repeatable transmission ratio control governed by deterministic kinematics. A comprehensive analytical model was developed to describe the wheel's mechanical behavior logic, establishing threshold conditions for mode switching between direct drive and radius transformation. Experimental validation confirmed that the measured torque-radius and force-displacement characteristics closely follow theoretical predictions across wheel weights of 1.8-2.8kg. Robot-level demonstrations on varying loads (0-25kg), slopes, and unstructured terrains further verified that the MORPH wheel passively adjusts its radius to provide optimal transmission ratio. The MORPH wheel exemplifies a mechanically programmed structure, embedding intelligent, context-dependent behavior directly into its physical design. This approach offers a new paradigm for passive variable transmission and mechanical intelligence in robotic mobility systems operating in unpredictable or control-limited environments.

AI.tldr: A fully passive, mechanically programmed wheel (MORPH) that variably changes radius (80 mm to 45 mm) in response to torque, without actuators or electronics, enabling bidirectional unlimited rotation, high torque transmission, and deterministic speed ratios; validated analytically and experimentally with robot demos.
AI.motivation: Eliminate dependence on actuators, sensors, and control systems for variable transmission by embedding intelligent behavior directly into the wheel geometry and compliant structure, increasing robustness in unpredictable environments.
AI.method: Mechanical design integrating a torque-response coupler and spring-loaded struts to passively transform radius; symmetric coupler enables bidirectional rotation; analytical kinematic model with mode-switch thresholds between direct drive and radius transformation; experimental torque-radius and force-displacement characterization across wheel weights; robot-level tests on varying loads, slopes, and terrains.
AI.result: Theory and experiments agree: predictable torque-radius behavior; radius tunable between 80–45 mm; torque capacity >10 N; rigid drive transmission; successful passive adaptation under different loads and terrains; demonstrations show optimal transmission ratios passively.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a mechanically programmed structure that embeds context-dependent behavior into physical design, offering a new paradigm for passive variable transmission and mechanical intelligence in robotic mobility suitable for control-limited or unpredictable environments.

title: Pragmatic Curiosity: A Hybrid Learning-Optimization Paradigm via Active Inference
summary: Many engineering and scientific workflows depend on expensive black-box evaluations, requiring decision-making that simultaneously improves performance and reduces uncertainty. Bayesian optimization (BO) and Bayesian experimental design (BED) offer powerful yet largely separate treatments of goal-seeking and information-seeking, providing limited guidance for hybrid settings where learning and optimization are intrinsically coupled. We propose "pragmatic curiosity," a hybrid learning-optimization paradigm derived from active inference, in which actions are selected by minimizing the expected free energy--a single objective that couples pragmatic utility with epistemic information gain. We demonstrate the practical effectiveness and flexibility of pragmatic curiosity on various real-world hybrid tasks, including constrained system identification, targeted active search, and composite optimization with unknown preferences. Across these benchmarks, pragmatic curiosity consistently outperforms strong BO-type and BED-type baselines, delivering higher estimation accuracy, better critical-region coverage, and improved final solution quality.

AI.tldr: A hybrid learning-optimization framework 'pragmatic curiosity' selects actions by minimizing expected free energy, unifying decision-making for learning and optimization; demonstrates strong performance on real-world hybrid tasks, outperforming BO/BED baselines.
AI.motivation: In many expensive black-box settings, decision-making must simultaneously improve model accuracy and optimize outcomes; pure BO or BED methods are insufficient for coupled learning-optimization tasks.
AI.method: Propose pragmatic curiosity derived from active inference; actions are chosen to minimize expected free energy, combining pragmatic utility and information gain into a single objective; applied to constrained system identification, targeted active search, and composite optimization with unknown preferences.
AI.result: Across benchmarks, achieves higher estimation accuracy, better critical-region coverage, and improved final solution quality; consistently outperforms strong BO-type and BED-type baselines.
AI.conclusion: Pragmatic curiosity offers a flexible, principled framework for hybrid learning-optimization problems with real-world applicability, bridging information gain and performance goals.

title: Driving with DINO: Vision Foundation Features as a Unified Bridge for Sim-to-Real Generation in Autonomous Driving
summary: Driven by the emergence of Controllable Video Diffusion, existing Sim2Real methods for autonomous driving video generation typically rely on explicit intermediate representations to bridge the domain gap. However, these modalities face a fundamental Consistency-Realism Dilemma. Low-level signals (e.g., edges, blurred images) ensure precise control but compromise realism by "baking in" synthetic artifacts, whereas high-level priors (e.g., depth, semantics, HDMaps) facilitate photorealism but lack the structural detail required for consistent guidance. In this work, we present Driving with DINO (DwD), a novel framework that leverages Vision Foundation Module (VFM) features as a unified bridge between the simulation and real-world domains. We first identify that these features encode a spectrum of information, from high-level semantics to fine-grained structure. To effectively utilize this, we employ Principal Subspace Projection to discard the high-frequency elements responsible for "texture baking," while concurrently introducing Random Channel Tail Drop to mitigate the structural loss inherent in rigid dimensionality reduction, thereby reconciling realism with control consistency. Furthermore, to fully leverage DINOv3's high-resolution capabilities for enhancing control precision, we introduce a learnable Spatial Alignment Module that adapts these high-resolution features to the diffusion backbone. Finally, we propose a Causal Temporal Aggregator employing causal convolutions to explicitly preserve historical motion context when integrating frame-wise DINO features, which effectively mitigates motion blur and guarantees temporal stability. Project page: https://albertchen98.github.io/DwD-project/

AI.tldr: DwD presents a unified framework for controllable video diffusion in autonomous driving by using Vision Foundation Module (DINO) features as a bridge between simulation and reality, augmented with (i) Principal Subspace Projection to reduce high-frequency texture baking, (ii) Random Channel Tail Drop to alleviate loss from dimensionality reduction, (iii) a learnable Spatial Alignment Module to adapt high-res features to diffusion backbones, and (iv) a Causal Temporal Aggregator to preserve motion context and reduce blur.
AI.motivation: To resolve the Consistency-Realism Dilemma in Sim2Real autonomous driving video generation: low-level signals offer control but poor realism, while high-level priors enable realism but degrade structural guidance.
AI.method: Leverage DINO-based Vision Foundation Module features as a bridge. Apply Principal Subspace Projection to remove high-frequency texture components; employ Random Channel Tail Drop to mitigate information loss from fixed subspace. Introduce a learnable Spatial Alignment Module to adapt high-res DINOv3 features to the diffusion backbone. Implement a Causal Temporal Aggregator using causal convolutions to preserve historical motion context when fusing frame-wise DINO features.
AI.result: Proposes a comprehensive DwD framework with modular components designed to reconcile realism and control in sim-to-real video generation for driving, including temporal stabilization and reduced texture baking; the abstract outlines architecture but does not provide quantitative results.
AI.conclusion: DwD offers a unified, modular pipeline that uses DINO-based features to bridge simulation and reality, with dedicated components for dimensionality-aware feature reduction, high-resolution alignment, and causal temporal aggregation to ensure realism without sacrificing control consistency.

title: Do LLMs Act Like Rational Agents? Measuring Belief Coherence in Probabilistic Decision Making
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as agents in high-stakes domains where optimal actions depend on both uncertainty about the world and consideration of utilities of different outcomes, yet their decision logic remains difficult to interpret. We study whether LLMs are rational utility maximizers with coherent beliefs and stable preferences. We consider behaviors of models for diagnosis challenge problems. The results provide insights about the relationship of LLM inferences to ideal Bayesian utility maximization for elicited probabilities and observed actions. Our approach provides falsifiable conditions under which the reported probabilities \emph{cannot} correspond to the true beliefs of any rational agent. We apply this methodology to multiple medical diagnostic domains with evaluations across several LLMs. We discuss implications of the results and directions forward for uses of LLMs in guiding high-stakes decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether large language models act as rational utility maximizers with coherent beliefs in medical diagnosis tasks, using Bayesian utility maximization as a benchmark, establishing falsifiable tests for true rational beliefs, and evaluating across multiple LLMs and diagnostic domains to inform high-stakes deployment.
AI.motivation: Address interpretability and reliability of LLM-driven decisions in high-stakes settings by asking if LLM inferences align with rational Bayesian agents and if reported probabilities reveal true beliefs.
AI.method: Construct diagnosis challenge problems, formalize Bayesian utility-maximization criteria, derive falsifiability tests for whether elicited probabilities could arise from a rational agent, apply tests to multiple medical diagnostic domains and several LLMs, analyze alignment between inferred probabilities and observed actions.
AI.result: Findings illuminate when LLM inferences align with or deviate from ideal Bayesian behavior; identify conditions under which reported probabilities cannot be true beliefs of any rational agent; show variability across LLMs and medical domains.
AI.conclusion: Implications: cautious use of LLMs in high-stakes decisions; need for direction for future research to align LLM inference with rational decision-making; potential frameworks to evaluate and constrain LLM behavior.

title: A High-Fidelity Robotic Manipulator Teleoperation Framework for Human-Centered Augmented Reality Evaluation
summary: Validating Augmented Reality (AR) tracking and interaction models requires precise, repeatable ground-truth motion. However, human users cannot reliably perform consistent motion due to biomechanical variability. Robotic manipulators are promising to act as human motion proxies if they can mimic human movements. In this work, we design and implement ARBot, a real-time teleoperation platform that can effectively capture natural human motion and accurately replay the movements via robotic manipulators. ARBot includes two capture models: stable wrist motion capture via a custom CV and IMU pipeline, and natural 6-DOF control via a mobile application. We design a proactively-safe QP controller to ensure smooth, jitter-free execution of the robotic manipulator, enabling it to function as a high-fidelity record and replay physical proxy. We open-source ARBot and release a benchmark dataset of 132 human and synthetic trajectories captured using ARBot to support controllable and scalable AR evaluation.

AI.tldr: ARBot is a real-time teleoperation platform that captures natural human motion and replays it on robotic manipulators for high-fidelity AR ground-truth. It uses two capture models (stable wrist CV/IMU pipeline and natural 6-DOF mobile app) and a proactive safe QP controller, and is open-sourced with a 132-trajectory benchmark.
AI.motivation: Precise, repeatable ground-truth motion is essential for validating AR tracking and interaction models; human biomechanical variability makes manual motion unreliable; robots can act as physical proxies to improve reproducibility.
AI.method: Develop two capture pipelines: (1) stable wrist motion capture using a custom computer vision (CV) and IMU pipeline; (2) natural 6-DOF control via a mobile app. Implement a proactively-safe quadratic programming (QP) controller to ensure smooth, jitter-free execution on robotic manipulators. Provide real-time teleoperation and release open-source software plus a dataset.
AI.result: ARBot enables high-fidelity recording and replay of human motion through robotic manipulators with smooth teleoperation. The published benchmark dataset includes 132 human and synthetic trajectories captured with ARBot, supporting controllable and scalable AR evaluation.
AI.conclusion: ARBot offers a practical, scalable solution for reliable motion proxying in AR research, enabling reproducible ground-truth motion and providing a valuable dataset for evaluating AR systems.

title: Private and interpretable clinical prediction with quantum-inspired tensor train models
comment: 21 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables. The code for the experiments is publicly available at https://github.com/joserapa98/tns4loris
summary: Machine learning in clinical settings must balance predictive accuracy, interpretability, and privacy. Models such as logistic regression (LR) offer transparency, while neural networks (NNs) provide greater predictive power; yet both remain vulnerable to privacy attacks. We empirically assess these risks by designing attacks that identify which public datasets were used to train a model under varying levels of adversarial access, applying them to LORIS, a publicly available LR model for immunotherapy response prediction, as well as to additional shallow NN models trained for the same task. Our results show that both models leak significant training-set information, with LRs proving particularly vulnerable in white-box scenarios. Moreover, we observe that common practices such as cross-validation in LRs exacerbate these risks. To mitigate these vulnerabilities, we propose a quantum-inspired defense based on tensorizing discretized models into tensor trains (TTs), which fully obfuscates parameters while preserving accuracy, reducing white-box attacks to random guessing and degrading black-box attacks comparably to Differential Privacy. TT models retain LR interpretability and extend it through efficient computation of marginal and conditional distributions, while also enabling this higher level of interpretability for NNs. Our results demonstrate that tensorization is widely applicable and establishes a practical foundation for private, interpretable, and effective clinical prediction.

AI.tldr: The authors study privacy risks in logistic regression and shallow neural networks for clinical prediction and propose tensor-train (TT) tensorization as a privacy-preserving, interpretable defense that preserves accuracy; they claim TT obfuscates parameters, reduces white-box leakage to random guessing, and degrades black-box attacks similarly to differential privacy, while maintaining interpretability and enabling efficient marginal/conditional inference.
AI.motivation: Clinical ML requires balancing predictive accuracy, interpretability, and privacy. The paper investigates privacy leakage in transparent (LR) and less transparent (NN) models and seeks a defense that preserves interpretability.
AI.method: Empirical privacy attacks under varying adversarial access on a public LR model (LORIS) for immunotherapy response and on shallow NNs trained for the same task. They measure training-set leakage, compare white-box and black-box scenarios, observe effects of cross-validation. They introduce a quantum-inspired defense: tensorizing discretized models into tensor trains to obfuscate parameters while preserving accuracy; assess interpretability through marginal/conditional distributions; claim applicability to NN as well.
AI.result: Both LR and shallow NNs leak training-set information; LR shows greater vulnerability in white-box settings. Cross-validation exacerbates leakage. TT-based defense obfuscates parameters, reducing white-box leakage to random guessing and degrading black-box leakage similarly to differential privacy, while maintaining predictive performance. TT models retain interpretability and enable efficient computation of marginals/conditionals, potentially extending interpretability to NNs.
AI.conclusion: Tensorization provides a practical framework for private, interpretable, and effective clinical prediction. TT models are broadly applicable and can be integrated into existing pipelines, offering privacy while preserving or enhancing interpretability.

title: MetaSSP: Enhancing Semi-supervised Implicit 3D Reconstruction through Meta-adaptive EMA and SDF-aware Pseudo-label Evaluation
summary: Implicit SDF-based methods for single-view 3D reconstruction achieve high-quality surfaces but require large labeled datasets, limiting their scalability. We propose MetaSSP, a novel semi-supervised framework that exploits abundant unlabeled images. Our approach introduces gradient-based parameter importance estimation to regularize adaptive EMA updates and an SDF-aware pseudo-label weighting mechanism combining augmentation consistency with SDF variance. Beginning with a 10% supervised warm-up, the unified pipeline jointly refines labeled and unlabeled data. On the Pix3D benchmark, our method reduces Chamfer Distance by approximately 20.61% and increases IoU by around 24.09% compared to existing semi-supervised baselines, setting a new state of the art.

AI.tldr: Semi-supervised single-view 3D reconstruction via MetaSSP that uses gradient-guided EMA regularization and SDF-aware pseudo-label weighting; achieves state-of-the-art on Pix3D with substantial gains in Chamfer Distance and IoU.
AI.motivation: Implicit SDF-based methods for 3D reconstruction require large labeled datasets, limiting scalability. Leveraging abundant unlabeled images with semi-supervised learning and SDF-aware strategies can improve data efficiency and performance.
AI.method: MetaSSP introduces gradient-based parameter importance estimation to regularize adaptive EMA updates and an SDF-aware pseudo-label weighting mechanism that combines augmentation consistency with SDF variance. The training starts with a 10% supervised warm-up and jointly refines labeled and unlabeled data.
AI.result: On Pix3D, Chamfer Distance decreases by ~20.61% and IoU increases by ~24.09% over existing semi-supervised baselines, establishing a new state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MetaSSP framework effectively leverages unlabeled data for implicit SDF-based single-view 3D reconstruction, achieving strong performance through gradient-informed EMA regularization and SDF-variance-aware pseudo-label weighting; it demonstrates strong data efficiency and competitiveness against supervised baselines.

title: Exposing Weaknesses of Large Reasoning Models through Graph Algorithm Problems
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have advanced rapidly; however, existing benchmarks in mathematics, code, and common-sense reasoning remain limited. They lack long-context evaluation, offer insufficient challenge, and provide answers that are difficult to verify programmatically. We introduce GrAlgoBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate LRMs through graph algorithm problems. Such problems are particularly well suited for probing reasoning abilities: they demand long-context reasoning, allow fine-grained control of difficulty levels, and enable standardized, programmatic evaluation. Across nine tasks, our systematic experiments reveal two major weaknesses of current LRMs. First, accuracy deteriorates sharply as context length increases, falling below 50% once graphs exceed 120 nodes. This degradation is driven by frequent execution errors, weak memory, and redundant reasoning. Second, LRMs suffer from an over-thinking phenomenon, primarily caused by extensive yet largely ineffective self-verification, which inflates reasoning traces without improving correctness. By exposing these limitations, GrAlgoBench establishes graph algorithm problems as a rigorous, multidimensional, and practically relevant testbed for advancing the study of reasoning in LRMs. Code is available at https://github.com/Bklight999/GrAlgoBench.

AI.tldr: A nine-task benchmark, GrAlgoBench, tests LRMs on graph algorithms to probe long-context reasoning; finds sharp accuracy drop with larger graphs and an over-thinking/self-verification issue; code available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks inadequately probe long-context reasoning and verifiability in LRMs, hindering progress in robust reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Nine graph algorithm tasks with controllable difficulty; systematic experiments across LRMs to assess accuracy vs context length, analyze errors (execution failures, memory, redundant reasoning) and self-verification traces; enables programmatic evaluation.
AI.result: Accuracy falls below 50% when graphs exceed ~120 nodes; deterioration driven by execution errors, weak memory, and redundant reasoning; LRMs exhibit over-thinking due to extensive yet ineffective self-verification that lengthens traces without improving correctness.
AI.conclusion: Graph algorithm problems offer a rigorous, multidimensional benchmark for assessing LRM reasoning; GrAlgoBench establishes a practical testbed to advance long-context reasoning research; code available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Robots That Generate Planarity Through Geometry
summary: Constraining motion to a flat surface is a fundamental requirement for equipment across science and engineering. Modern precision robotic motion systems, such as gantries, rely on the flatness of components, including guide rails and granite surface plates. However, translating this static flatness into motion requires precise internal alignment and tight-tolerance components that create long, error-sensitive reference chains. Here, we show that by using the geometric inversion of a sphere into a plane, we can produce robotic motion systems that derive planarity entirely from link lengths and connectivity. This allows planar motion to emerge from self-referencing geometric constraints, and without external metrology. We demonstrate these Flat-Plane Mechanisms (FPMs) from micron to meter scales and show that fabrication errors can be attenuated by an order of magnitude in the resulting flatness. Finally, we present a robotic FPM-based 3-axis positioning system that can be used for metrology surface scans ($\pm 12$-mm) and 3D printing inside narrow containers. This work establishes an alternative geometric foundation for planar motion that can be realized across size scales and opens new possibilities in metrology, fabrication, and micro-positioning.

AI.tldr: A new class of flat-plane mechanisms (FPMs) realizes planar motion purely from link lengths and connectivity by geometric inversion of a sphere to a plane, removing external metrology.
AI.motivation: To avoid long, error-sensitive reference chains for flatness in precision robotic systems and enable planarity to emerge from internal geometric constraints across scales.
AI.method: Use the geometric inversion of a sphere into a plane to derive self-referencing planar constraints. Design and fabricate FPMs from micron to meter scales, attenuating fabrication errors via the constraint network. Demonstrate a 3-axis positioning system for metrology surface scanning and confined-space 3D printing.
AI.result: Fabrication errors are attenuated by an order of magnitude in the resulting flatness. Demonstrated FPMs spanning micron to meter scales. Realized a 3-axis FPM-based positioning system capable of metrology surface scans (±12 mm) and 3D printing inside narrow containers.
AI.conclusion: Offers an alternative geometric foundation for planar motion applicable across size scales, with potential impact on metrology, fabrication, and micro-positioning.

title: Compressing LLMs with MoP: Mixture of Pruners
comment: Code and models are available at: https://github.com/c2d-usp/Efficient-LLMs-with-MoP
summary: The high computational demands of Large Language Models (LLMs) motivate methods that reduce parameter count and accelerate inference. In response, model pruning emerges as an effective strategy, yet current methods typically focus on a single dimension-depth or width. We introduce MoP (Mixture of Pruners), an iterative framework that unifies these dimensions. At each iteration, MoP generates two branches-pruning in depth versus pruning in width-and selects a candidate to advance the path. On LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3, MoP advances the frontier of structured pruning, exceeding the accuracy of competing methods across a broad set of compression regimes. It also consistently outperforms depth-only and width-only pruning. Furthermore, MoP translates structural pruning into real speedup, reducing end-to-end latency by 39% at 40% compression. Finally, extending MoP to the vision-language model LLaVA-1.5, we notably improve computational efficiency and demonstrate that text-only recovery fine-tuning can restore performance even on visual tasks.

AI.tldr: MoP (Mixture of Pruners) is an iterative, two-branch framework for joint depth and width pruning that surpasses single-dimension pruning and delivers practical speedups for LLMs (LLaMA-2/3) and vision-language models (LLaVA-1.5).
AI.motivation: Large Language Models require high compute; pruning across multiple dimensions (depth and width) in a unified, iterative framework can yield better accuracy/latency tradeoffs than single-dimension pruning.
AI.method: An iterative MoP framework that, at each step, creates two pruning branches (depth-pruning and width-pruning), evaluates candidates, and advances along a frontier. Demonstrated on LLaMA-2/3 (structured pruning) and LLaVA-1.5 (vision-language).
AI.result: MoP improves accuracy across a broad set of compression regimes, outperforms depth-only and width-only pruning, translates structural pruning into real speedups (39% end-to-end latency reduction at 40% compression). For LLaVA-1.5, text-only recovery fine-tuning can restore performance on vision tasks.
AI.conclusion: MoP pushes the frontier of structured pruning, showing cross-domain effectiveness and potential for practical efficiency gains in both pure language models and vision-language systems.

title: M3: High-fidelity Text-to-Image Generation via Multi-Modal, Multi-Agent and Multi-Round Visual Reasoning
summary: Generative models have achieved impressive fidelity in text-to-image synthesis, yet struggle with complex compositional prompts involving multiple constraints. We introduce \textbf{M3 (Multi-Modal, Multi-Agent, Multi-Round)}, a training-free framework that systematically resolves these failures through iterative inference-time refinement. M3 orchestrates off-the-shelf foundation models in a robust multi-agent loop: a Planner decomposes prompts into verifiable checklists, while specialized Checker, Refiner, and Editor agents surgically correct constraints one at a time, with a Verifier ensuring monotonic improvement. Applied to open-source models, M3 achieves remarkable results on the challenging OneIG-EN benchmark, with our Qwen-Image+M3 surpassing commercial flagship systems including Imagen4 (0.515) and Seedream 3.0 (0.530), reaching state-of-the-art performance (0.532 overall). This demonstrates that intelligent multi-agent reasoning can elevate open-source models beyond proprietary alternatives. M3 also substantially improves GenEval compositional metrics, effectively doubling spatial reasoning performance on hardened test sets. As a plug-and-play module compatible with any pre-trained T2I model, M3 establishes a new paradigm for compositional generation without costly retraining.

AI.tldr: M3 is a training-free, multi-agent inference-time framework for improving compositional text-to-image generation by iteratively refining outputs through Planner, Checker, Refiner, Editor, and Verifier, achieving state-of-the-art on OneIG-EN and GenEval metrics while remaining plug-and-play with any pre-trained T2I model.
AI.motivation: Address the failure modes of current text-to-image systems when handling complex compositional prompts with multiple constraints, by introducing an autonomous, iterative refinement loop that does not require retraining.
AI.method: A robust multi-agent loop where a Planner decomposes prompts into verifiable checklists; Checker, Refiner, and Editor agents surgically correct constraints one by one; a Verifier ensures monotonic improvement. The system orchestrates off-the-shelf foundation models in an inference-time feedback loop (training-free) and is applied to open-source T2I models to boost performance.
AI.result: On the OneIG-EN benchmark, Qwen-Image+M3 surpasses commercial systems (Imagen4, Seedream 3.0) with a reported 0.532 overall state-of-the-art value. GenEval compositional metrics see substantial gains, including approximately doubling spatial reasoning performance on hardened test sets. Demonstrates that intelligent multi-agent reasoning can elevate open-source models beyond proprietary alternatives; compatible as a plug-and-play module with any pre-trained T2I model.
AI.conclusion: Intelligent multi-agent reasoning can substantially improve compositional image generation without retraining, establishing a new paradigm for leveraging off-the-shelf models to achieve competitive performance against proprietary systems.

title: Trifuse: Enhancing Attention-Based GUI Grounding via Multimodal Fusion
summary: GUI grounding maps natural language instructions to the correct interface elements, serving as the perception foundation for GUI agents. Existing approaches predominantly rely on fine-tuning multimodal large language models (MLLMs) using large-scale GUI datasets to predict target element coordinates, which is data-intensive and generalizes poorly to unseen interfaces. Recent attention-based alternatives exploit localization signals in MLLMs attention mechanisms without task-specific fine-tuning, but suffer from low reliability due to the lack of explicit and complementary spatial anchors in GUI images. To address this limitation, we propose Trifuse, an attention-based grounding framework that explicitly integrates complementary spatial anchors. Trifuse integrates attention, OCR-derived textual cues, and icon-level caption semantics via a Consensus-SinglePeak (CS) fusion strategy that enforces cross-modal agreement while retaining sharp localization peaks. Extensive evaluations on four grounding benchmarks demonstrate that Trifuse achieves strong performance without task-specific fine-tuning, substantially reducing the reliance on expensive annotated data.
