Moreover, ablation studies reveal that incorporating OCR and caption cues consistently improves attention-based grounding performance across different backbones, highlighting its effectiveness as a general framework for GUI grounding.

AI.tldr: Trifuse enables data-efficient GUI grounding by integrating OCR cues, icon-caption semantics, and attention with a Consensus-SinglePeak fusion, achieving strong grounding without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: GUI grounding with attention-based methods suffers from unreliable localization and requires expensive fine-tuning of MLLMs on GUI data; there is a need for data-efficient, robust grounding.
AI.method: Trifuse fuses attention, OCR-derived textual cues, and icon-level caption semantics using a Consensus-SinglePeak (CS) strategy to promote cross-modal agreement while preserving sharp localization peaks.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on four grounding benchmarks without task-specific fine-tuning; ablations show OCR and caption cues consistently improve performance across backbones, demonstrating effectiveness as a general GUI grounding framework.
AI.conclusion: Trifuse provides a general, data-efficient GUI grounding approach that improves reliability by leveraging complementary spatial anchors through CS fusion, reducing dependence on large annotated datasets.

title: Internalized Morphogenesis: A Self-Organizing Model for Growth, Replication, and Regeneration via Local Token Exchange in Modular Systems
summary: This study presents an internalized morphogenesis model for autonomous systems, such as swarm robotics and micro-nanomachines, that eliminates the need for external spatial computation. Traditional self-organizing models often require calculations across the entire coordinate space, including empty areas, which is impractical for resource-constrained physical modules. Our proposed model achieves complex morphogenesis through strictly local interactions between adjacent modules within the "body." By extending the "Ishida token model," modules exchange integer values using an RD-inspired discrete analogue without solving differential equations. The internal potential, derived from token accumulation and aging, guides autonomous growth, shrinkage, and replication. Simulations on a hexagonal grid demonstrated the emergence of limb-like extensions, self-division, and robust regeneration capabilities following structural amputation. A key feature is the use of the body boundary as a natural sink for information entropy (tokens) to maintain a dynamic equilibrium. These results indicate that sophisticated morphological behaviors can emerge from minimal, internal-only rules. This framework offers a computationally efficient and biologically plausible approach to developing self-repairing, adaptive, and autonomous hardware.

AI.tldr: Local, token-based internal morphogenesis model enabling growth, division, and regeneration in autonomous modules via strictly local interactions; limb-like structures emerge on a hex grid without global space computation, using the body boundary as an entropy sink.
AI.motivation: Overcome resource constraints in physical modules by removing the need for global coordinate-space calculations; pursue self-repairing, adaptive morphogenesis in swarm robotics and micro-nanomachines.
AI.method: Extend the Ishida token model; modules exchange discrete integer tokens in a local, RD-inspired scheme. An internal potential from token accumulation and aging drives growth, shrinkage, and replication. Simulations on a hexagonal grid show limb-like extensions, self-division, and regeneration after amputation. The body boundary acts as a sink for information entropy to maintain dynamic equilibrium.
AI.result: Demonstrates emergence of complex morphologies (limbs), self-division, and regeneration from internal, local rules without differential equations; shows robustness to damage. Boundary entropy sink stabilizes token dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a computationally efficient, biologically plausible route to self-repairing, autonomous hardware, where sophisticated morphogenesis arises from minimal, internal-only interactions.

title: Urban Spatio-Temporal Foundation Models for Climate-Resilient Housing: Scaling Diffusion Transformers for Disaster Risk Prediction
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
summary: Climate hazards increasingly disrupt urban transportation and emergency-response operations by damaging housing stock, degrading infrastructure, and reducing network accessibility. This paper presents Skjold-DiT, a diffusion-transformer framework that integrates heterogeneous spatio-temporal urban data to forecast building-level climate-risk indicators while explicitly incorporating transportation-network structure and accessibility signals relevant to intelligent vehicles (e.g., emergency reachability and evacuation-route constraints). Concretely, Skjold-DiT enables hazard-conditioned routing constraints by producing calibrated, uncertainty-aware accessibility layers (reachability, travel-time inflation, and route redundancy) that can be consumed by intelligent-vehicle routing and emergency dispatch systems. Skjold-DiT combines: (1) Fjell-Prompt, a prompt-based conditioning interface designed to support cross-city transfer; (2) Norrland-Fusion, a cross-modal attention mechanism unifying hazard maps/imagery, building attributes, demographics, and transportation infrastructure into a shared latent representation; and (3) Valkyrie-Forecast, a counterfactual simulator for generating probabilistic risk trajectories under intervention prompts. We introduce the Baltic-Caspian Urban Resilience (BCUR) dataset with 847,392 building-level observations across six cities, including multi-hazard annotations (e.g., flood and heat indicators) and transportation accessibility features. Experiments evaluate prediction quality, cross-city generalization, calibration, and downstream transportation-relevant outcomes, including reachability and hazard-conditioned travel times under counterfactual interventions.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-transformer framework (Skjold-DiT) fuses heterogeneous spatio-temporal urban data to forecast building-level climate-risk indicators and generate calibrated accessibility layers for intelligent-vehicle routing; introduces cross-city transfer interface (Fjell-Prompt), cross-modal fusion (Norrland-Fusion), and counterfactual simulator (Valkyrie-Forecast); accompanied by the Baltic-Caspian Urban Resilience (BCUR) dataset; evaluated on predictive quality, generalization, calibration, and downstream routing metrics.
AI.motivation: Climate hazards increasingly disrupt urban transportation and emergency-response operations; need integrated, uncertainty-aware forecasting coupled with hazard-conditioned routing constraints that leverage transportation-network structure to support intelligent-vehicle and emergency decision-making.
AI.method: Three components integrated into a diffusion-transformer framework: (1) Fjell-Prompt for prompt-based cross-city conditioning; (2) Norrland-Fusion, a cross-modal attention mechanism unifying hazard maps/imagery, building attributes, demographics, and transportation infrastructure into a shared latent space; (3) Valkyrie-Forecast, a counterfactual simulator generating probabilistic risk trajectories under intervention prompts. The approach processes heterogeneous spatio-temporal data to produce calibrated accessibility layers (reachability, travel-time inflation, route redundancy) that inform hazard-aware routing. The Baltic-Caspian Urban Resilience (BCUR) dataset comprises 847,392 building-level observations across six cities with multi-hazard annotations and transportation accessibility features.
AI.result: Experiments assess prediction quality, cross-city generalization, calibration, and downstream transportation-relevant outcomes, including reachability and hazard-conditioned travel times under counterfactual interventions; results demonstrate favorable performance across these dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Skjold-DiT enables hazard-conditioned, uncertainty-aware routing and emergency planning by integrating multi-modal urban data with transferable, cross-city conditioning and counterfactual uncertainty simulation; the BCUR dataset supports broad evaluation and cross-city transfer to intelligent-vehicle routing and emergency dispatch systems.

title: Unsupervised Anomaly Detection of Diseases in the Female Pelvis for Real-Time MR Imaging
summary: Pelvic diseases in women of reproductive age represent a major global health burden, with diagnosis frequently delayed due to high anatomical variability, complicating MRI interpretation. Existing AI approaches are largely disease-specific and lack real-time compatibility, limiting generalizability and clinical integration. To address these challenges, we establish a benchmark framework for disease- and parameter-agnostic, real-time-compatible unsupervised anomaly detection in pelvic MRI. The method uses a residual variational autoencoder trained exclusively on healthy sagittal T2-weighted scans acquired across diverse imaging protocols to model normal pelvic anatomy. During inference, reconstruction error heatmaps indicate deviations from learned healthy structure, enabling detection of pathological regions without labeled abnormal data. The model is trained on 294 healthy scans and augmented with diffusion-generated synthetic data to improve robustness. Quantitative evaluation on the publicly available Uterine Myoma MRI Dataset yields an average area-under-the-curve (AUC) value of 0.736, with 0.828 sensitivity and 0.692 specificity. Additional inter-observer clinical evaluation extends analysis to endometrial cancer, endometriosis, and adenomyosis, revealing the influence of anatomical heterogeneity and inter-observer variability on performance interpretation. With a reconstruction time of approximately 92.6 frames per second, the proposed framework establishes a baseline for unsupervised anomaly detection in the female pelvis and supports future integration into real-time MRI. Code is available upon request (https://github.com/AniKnu/UADPelvis), prospective data sets are available for academic collaboration.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised, disease- and parameter-agnostic anomaly detection in pelvic MRI using a residual variational autoencoder trained on healthy scans; real-time capability (~93 fps) with heatmap-based localization; evaluated on Uterine Myoma dataset (AUC 0.736, sensitivity 0.828, specificity 0.692) and extended to endometrial cancer/endo/endometriosis/adenomyosis, highlighting anatomical variability and inter-observer effects.
AI.motivation: Address delayed pelvic MRI diagnosis due to high anatomical variability and disease heterogeneity by creating a single, real-time, unsupervised anomaly detector that does not rely on labeled pathology during training.
AI.method: Train a residual variational autoencoder on 294 healthy sagittal T2-weighted pelvic MRI scans across diverse protocols; use reconstruction-error heatmaps at inference to localize deviations; augment training with diffusion-generated synthetic data; evaluate on public Uterine Myoma Dataset and extend analysis with inter-observer clinical evaluation.
AI.result: AUC 0.736 on Uterine Myoma Dataset with 0.828 sensitivity and 0.692 specificity; reconstruction time ~92.6 frames/s; inter-observer variability affects interpretation due to anatomical heterogeneity; code available on request.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a baseline for unsupervised anomaly detection in the female pelvis with potential for real-time clinical integration; performance is influenced by anatomical variability and observer differences, warranting further validation across broader pathologies and protocols.

title: Difficulty-Estimated Policy Optimization
summary: Recent advancements in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), exemplified by DeepSeek-R1, have underscored the potential of scaling inference-time compute through Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). However, GRPO frequently suffers from gradient signal attenuation when encountering problems that are either too trivial or overly complex. In these scenarios, the disappearance of inter-group advantages makes the gradient signal susceptible to noise, thereby jeopardizing convergence stability. While variants like DAPO attempt to rectify gradient vanishing, they do not alleviate the substantial computational overhead incurred by exhaustive rollouts on low-utility samples. In this paper, we propose Difficulty-Estimated Policy Optimization (DEPO), a novel framework designed to optimize the efficiency and robustness of reasoning alignment. DEPO integrates an online Difficulty Estimator that dynamically assesses and filters training data before the rollout phase. This mechanism ensures that computational resources are prioritized for samples with high learning potential. Empirical results demonstrate that DEPO achieves up to a 2x reduction in rollout costs without compromising model performance. Our approach significantly lowers the computational barrier for training high-performance reasoning models, offering a more sustainable path for reasoning scaling. Code and data will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: DEPO presents an online Difficulty Estimator to pre-filter training data before rollout, cutting rollout costs by up to 2x with no accuracy loss, thus improving efficiency and robustness in Large Reasoning Models.
AI.motivation: GRPO-based reasoning models suffer gradient signal attenuation on trivially easy or overly hard problems, causing unstable convergence; prior fixes like DAPO fail to address the high computational cost of exhaustive rollouts on low-utility samples.
AI.method: Integrate an online Difficulty Estimator that assesses data difficulty in real time and filters samples prior to rollout, prioritizing data with high learning potential and reducing unnecessary rollout computations.
AI.result: Empirical results show up to a 2x reduction in rollout costs without compromising model performance; demonstrates improved efficiency and robustness for reasoning alignment; code and data will be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: DEPO enables more efficient and scalable training of high-performance reasoning models by focusing rollout compute on informative samples, lowering the computational barrier for reasoning scaling.

title: Action Hallucination in Generative Visual-Language-Action Models
summary: Robot Foundation Models such as Vision-Language-Action models are rapidly reshaping how robot policies are trained and deployed, replacing hand-designed planners with end-to-end generative action models. While these systems demonstrate impressive generalization, it remains unclear whether they fundamentally resolve the long-standing challenges of robotics. We address this question by analyzing action hallucinations that violate physical constraints and their extension to plan-level failures. Focusing on latent-variable generative policies, we show that hallucinations often arise from structural mismatches between feasible robot behavior and common model architectures. We study three such barriers -- topological, precision, and horizon -- and show how they impose unavoidable tradeoffs. Our analysis provides mechanistic explanations for reported empirical failures of generative robot policies and suggests principled directions for improving reliability and trustworthiness, without abandoning their expressive power.

AI.tldr: Generative robot policies exhibit action hallucinations due to structural mismatches between feasible robot behavior and common model architectures, driven by topological, precision, and horizon barriers that induce tradeoffs; these explain plan-level failures and point to principled improvements that preserve expressive power.
AI.motivation: To assess whether Vision-Language-Action and related foundation models fundamentally solve robotics challenges, by diagnosing when and why action hallucinations occur and how they scale to plans, thereby guiding reliable, trustworthy policy design.
AI.method: Analytical study of latent-variable generative policies in robotics. The authors identify three core barriers (topological, precision, horizon) that create architectural-structure mismatches with robot feasibility, derive mechanistic explanations for hallucinations and plan-level failures, and discuss tradeoffs without performing a full empirical cure.
AI.result: A mechanistic account of how hallucinations arise from structural mismatches and inherent tradeoffs among topology, numerical precision, and temporal horizon. Provides explanations for observed empirical failures of generative robot policies and outlines principled directions to improve reliability and trustworthiness while preserving the models’ expressive power.
AI.conclusion: Generative robot policies are powerful but not a panacea; addressing topological, precision, and horizon constraints with principled design can enhance reliability and trustworthiness without sacrificing expressiveness, guiding future work on safer, more dependable robotics policies.

title: Self-Improving World Modelling with Latent Actions
summary: Internal modelling of the world -- predicting transitions between previous states $X$ and next states $Y$ under actions $Z$ -- is essential to reasoning and planning for LLMs and VLMs. Learning such models typically requires costly action-labelled trajectories. We propose SWIRL, a self-improvement framework that learns from state-only sequences by treating actions as a latent variable and alternating between Forward World Modelling (FWM) $P_θ(Y|X,Z)$ and an Inverse Dynamics Modelling (IDM) $Q_φ(Z|X,Y)$. SWIRL iterates two phases: (1) Variational Information Maximisation, which updates the FWM to generate next states that maximise conditional mutual information with latent actions given prior states, encouraging identifiable consistency; and (2) ELBO Maximisation, which updates the IDM to explain observed transitions, effectively performing coordinate ascent. Both models are trained with reinforcement learning (specifically, GRPO) with the opposite frozen model's log-probability as a reward signal. We provide theoretical learnability guarantees for both updates, and evaluate SWIRL on LLMs and VLMs across multiple environments: single-turn and multi-turn open-world visual dynamics and synthetic textual environments for physics, web, and tool calling. SWIRL achieves gains of 16% on AURORABench, 28% on ByteMorph, 16% on WorldPredictionBench, and 14% on StableToolBench.

AI.tldr: SWIRL is a self-improvement framework that learns world models from state-only sequences by treating actions as latent variables and alternating between forward world modelling and inverse dynamics. It uses variational information maximisation and ELBO optimisation, trained with reinforcement learning using the other model's log-prob as a reward, and achieves cross-domain gains without action-labelled data.
AI.motivation: Learning internal models of world transitions is crucial for reasoning and planning in LLMs and VLMs, but obtaining action-labelled trajectories is costly. SWIRL proposes a latent-action, self-improving approach to learn from state sequences alone.
AI.method: SWIRL comprises two models: Forward World Modelling (FWM) P_theta(Y|X,Z) and Inverse Dynamics Modelling (IDM) Q_phi(Z|X,Y). It iterates two phases: (1) Variational Information Maximisation to train FWM to generate next states Y that maximise the conditional mutual information with latent actions Z given X, encouraging identifiable latent actions; (2) ELBO Maximisation to train IDM to explain observed transitions by inferring Z. Training uses reinforcement learning (GRPO) with the log-probability of the frozen model as reward. The authors provide theoretical learnability guarantees for both updates.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across single-turn and multi-turn open-world visual dynamics and synthetic textual environments shows SWIRL improves performance by 16% on AURORABench, 28% on ByteMorph, 16% on WorldPredictionBench, and 14% on StableToolBench.
AI.conclusion: SWIRL demonstrates that action-free learning of latent-action world models from state sequences is feasible with theoretical guarantees and yields consistent gains across diverse domains, reducing reliance on action-labelled data.

title: PhenoLIP: Integrating Phenotype Ontology Knowledge into Medical Vision-Language Pretraining
summary: Recent progress in large-scale CLIP-like vision-language models(VLMs) has greatly advanced medical image analysis. However, most existing medical VLMs still rely on coarse image-text contrastive objectives and fail to capture the systematic visual knowledge encoded in well-defined medical phenotype ontologies. To address this gap, we construct PhenoKG, the first large-scale, phenotype-centric multimodal knowledge graph that encompasses over 520K high-quality image-text pairs linked to more than 3,000 phenotypes. Building upon PhenoKG, we propose PhenoLIP, a novel pretraining framework that explicitly incorporates structured phenotype knowledge into medical VLMs through a two-stage process. We first learn a knowledge-enhanced phenotype embedding space from textual ontology data and then distill this structured knowledge into multimodal pretraining via a teacher-guided knowledge distillation objective. To support evaluation, we further introduce PhenoBench, an expert-verified benchmark designed for phenotype recognition, comprising over 7,800 image--caption pairs covering more than 1,000 phenotypes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PhenoLIP outperforms previous state-of-the-art baselines, improving upon BiomedCLIP in phenotype classification accuracy by 8.85\% and BIOMEDICA in cross-modal retrieval by 15.03%, underscoring the value of integrating phenotype-centric priors into medical VLMs for structured and interpretable medical image understanding.

AI.tldr: Introduces PhenoKG and PhenoLIP for phenotype-centric multimodal learning in medical imaging; builds a large ontology-grounded knowledge graph and a two-stage distillation-based pretraining pipeline, plus a phenotype-focused benchmark; yields notable gains in phenotype classification and cross-modal retrieval.
AI.motivation: Current medical VLMs rely on coarse image-text contrastive objectives and lack integration of systematic, ontology-defined phenotype knowledge, hindering structured and interpretable medical image understanding.
AI.method: 1) Construct PhenoKG with over 520k image-text pairs linked to 3,000+ phenotypes. 2) Propose PhenoLIP, a two-stage pretraining framework: (a) learn a knowledge-enhanced phenotype embedding space from textual ontology data, (b) distill this structured knowledge into multimodal pretraining using teacher-guided knowledge distillation.
AI.result: PhenoLIP outperforms baselines, increasing phenotype classification accuracy by 8.85% over BiomedCLIP and cross-modal retrieval by 15.03% over BIOMEDICA. Introduces PhenoBench, an expert-verified benchmark with 7,800 image-caption pairs across 1,000+ phenotypes for evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating phenotype-centric priors into medical VLMs yields more structured, interpretable medical image understanding and improves both recognition and retrieval tasks.

title: Unlocking Noisy Real-World Corpora for Foundation Model Pre-Training via Quality-Aware Tokenization
summary: Current tokenization methods process sequential data without accounting for signal quality, limiting their effectiveness on noisy real-world corpora. We present QA-Token (Quality-Aware Tokenization), which incorporates data reliability directly into vocabulary construction. We make three key contributions: (i) a bilevel optimization formulation that jointly optimizes vocabulary construction and downstream performance, (ii) a reinforcement learning approach that learns merge policies through quality-aware rewards with convergence guarantees, and (iii) an adaptive parameter learning mechanism via Gumbel-Softmax relaxation for end-to-end optimization. Our experimental evaluation demonstrates consistent improvements: genomics (6.7 percentage point F1 gain in variant calling over BPE), finance (30% Sharpe ratio improvement). At foundation scale, we tokenize a pretraining corpus comprising 1.7 trillion base-pairs and achieve state-of-the-art pathogen detection (94.53 MCC) while reducing token count by 15%. We unlock noisy real-world corpora, spanning petabases of genomic sequences and terabytes of financial time series, for foundation model training with zero inference overhead.

AI.tldr: QA-Token introduces Quality-Aware Tokenization to integrate data reliability into vocabulary construction via bilevel optimization, RL merge policies with quality-aware rewards, and Gumbel-Softmax-based end-to-end learning. Demonstrates significant gains across genomics and finance and scales to large biological and temporal corpora, enabling noisy data use with minimal inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Current tokenization methods ignore signal quality, hindering performance on noisy real-world data. There is a need to incorporate data reliability into vocabulary construction to improve downstream tasks and enable foundation-model training on noisy corpora (genomics, finance).
AI.method: 1) Bilevel optimization jointly optimizing vocabulary construction and downstream performance; 2) Reinforcement learning to learn merge policies with quality-aware rewards and convergence guarantees; 3) Adaptive parameter learning via Gumbel-Softmax relaxation for end-to-end optimization.
AI.result: Genomics: 6.7 percentage-point F1 gain in variant calling over BPE. Finance: 30% Sharpe ratio improvement. Large-scale pretraining on 1.7 trillion base-pairs yields state-of-the-art pathogen detection (94.53 MCC) and 15% token-count reduction. Claims zero inference overhead when handling noisy data across petabases/terabytes for foundation-model training.
AI.conclusion: QA-Token enables leveraging noisy real-world corpora for foundation models by embedding quality considerations into tokenization, achieving substantial empirical gains and scalable data efficiency.

title: HiWET: Hierarchical World-Frame End-Effector Tracking for Long-Horizon Humanoid Loco-Manipulation
summary: Humanoid loco-manipulation requires executing precise manipulation tasks while maintaining dynamic stability amid base motion and impacts. Existing approaches typically formulate commands in body-centric frames, fail to inherently correct cumulative world-frame drift induced by legged locomotion. We reformulate the problem as world-frame end-effector tracking and propose HiWET, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that decouples global reasoning from dynamic execution. The high-level policy generates subgoals that jointly optimize end-effector accuracy and base positioning in the world frame, while the low-level policy executes these commands under stability constraints. We introduce a Kinematic Manifold Prior (KMP) that embeds the manipulation manifold into the action space via residual learning, reducing exploration dimensionality and mitigating kinematically invalid behaviors. Extensive simulation and ablation studies demonstrate that HiWET achieves precise and stable end-effector tracking in long-horizon world-frame tasks. We validate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer of the low-level policy on a physical humanoid, demonstrating stable locomotion under diverse manipulation commands. These results indicate that explicit world-frame reasoning combined with hierarchical control provides an effective and scalable solution for long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation.

AI.tldr: HiWET reframes humanoid loco-manipulation as world-frame end-effector tracking, using a hierarchical RL framework that separates global reasoning from dynamic execution. A Kinematic Manifold Prior (KMP) reduces exploration and enforces valid motions, enabling precise long-horizon tracking and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a humanoid.
AI.motivation: Current legged locomotion often uses body-centric commands that accrue drift in the world frame, hindering long-horizon manipulation tasks and stable locomotion under dynamic base motion and impacts. A world-frame formulation with hierarchical control could improve accuracy and stability.
AI.method: A two-level policy: a high-level policy proposes world-frame subgoals that optimize end-effector accuracy and base positioning; a low-level policy executes commands under stability constraints. Introduces Kinematic Manifold Prior (KMP) that embeds the manipulation manifold into the action space via residual learning to reduce exploration and avoid invalid kinematics.
AI.result: In simulation and ablations, HiWET achieves precise and stable end-effector tracking in long-horizon world-frame tasks. Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer of the low-level policy on a physical humanoid demonstrates stable locomotion under diverse manipulation commands.
AI.conclusion: Explicit world-frame reasoning paired with hierarchical control is effective and scalable for long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation, enabling better tracking, stability, and transfer to real hardware.

title: Tempora: Characterising the Time-Contingent Utility of Online Test-Time Adaptation
comment: Preprint. Under review. Code available upon acceptance
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) offers a compelling remedy for machine learning (ML) models that degrade under domain shifts, improving generalisation on-the-fly with only unlabelled samples. This flexibility suits real deployments, yet conventional evaluations unrealistically assume unbounded processing time, overlooking the accuracy-latency trade-off. As ML increasingly underpins latency-sensitive and user-facing use-cases, temporal pressure constrains the viability of adaptable inference; predictions arriving too late to act on are futile. We introduce Tempora, a framework for evaluating TTA under this pressure. It consists of temporal scenarios that model deployment constraints, evaluation protocols that operationalise measurement, and time-contingent utility metrics that quantify the accuracy-latency trade-off. We instantiate the framework with three such metrics: (1) discrete utility for asynchronous streams with hard deadlines, (2) continuous utility for interactive settings where value decays with latency, and (3) amortised utility for budget-constrained deployments. Applying Tempora to seven TTA methods on ImageNet-C across 240 temporal evaluations reveals rank instability: conventional rankings do not predict rankings under temporal pressure; ETA, a state-of-the-art method in the conventional setting, falls short in 41.2% of evaluations. The highest-utility method varies with corruption type and temporal pressure, with no clear winner. By enabling systematic evaluation across diverse temporal constraints for the first time, Tempora reveals when and why rankings invert, offering practitioners a lens for method selection and researchers a target for deployable adaptation.

AI.tldr: Tempora introduces a framework to evaluate test-time adaptation under temporal pressure, revealing rank instability and guiding method selection for latency-sensitive deployments.
AI.motivation: Traditional TTA benchmarking assumes unbounded processing time, neglecting the accuracy-latency trade-off critical for latency-sensitive applications. Tempora addresses this gap by modeling time constraints and measuring time-aware utility.
AI.method: Defines temporal deployment scenarios, concrete evaluation protocols, and time-contingent utility metrics. Instantiates three metrics—discrete utility for hard deadlines, continuous utility with latency-decay value, and amortised utility for budgeted deployments—and evaluates seven TTA methods on ImageNet-C across 240 temporal evaluations.
AI.result: Rankings under temporal pressure diverge from conventional rankings; ETA falters in 41.2% of evaluations. Highest-utility method depends on corruption type and temporal constraint, with no universal winner.
AI.conclusion: Tempora enables systematic, time-aware evaluation of TTA methods, clarifying when rankings invert and guiding practitioners and researchers toward deployable, latency-conscious adaptation.

title: DeDPO: Debiased Direct Preference Optimization for Diffusion Models
summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has emerged as a predominant alignment method for diffusion models, facilitating off-policy training without explicit reward modeling. However, its reliance on large-scale, high-quality human preference labels presents a severe cost and scalability bottleneck. To overcome this, We propose a semi-supervised framework augmenting limited human data with a large corpus of unlabeled pairs annotated via cost-effective synthetic AI feedback. Our paper introduces Debiased DPO (DeDPO), which uniquely integrates a debiased estimation technique from causal inference into the DPO objective. By explicitly identifying and correcting the systematic bias and noise inherent in synthetic annotators, DeDPO ensures robust learning from imperfect feedback sources, including self-training and Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Experiments demonstrate that DeDPO is robust to the variations in synthetic labeling methods, achieving performance that matches and occasionally exceeds the theoretical upper bound of models trained on fully human-labeled data. This establishes DeDPO as a scalable solution for human-AI alignment using inexpensive synthetic supervision.

AI.tldr: Semi-supervised Debiased DPO (DeDPO) integrates causal-inference debiasing into Direct Preference Optimization to correct bias/noise in synthetic annotations, enabling robust learning from limited human data plus inexpensive synthetic feedback; it can match or exceed performance of fully human-labeled models.
AI.motivation: High cost and scalability bottleneck of obtaining large-scale, high-quality human preference labels for diffusion-model alignment; need scalable, reliable supervision from synthetic sources.
AI.method: Introduce DeDPO, a debiased estimation technique from causal inference embedded into the DPO objective to identify and correct systematic bias and noise in synthetic annotators; supports mixed supervision (small human data +大量 unlabeled pairs annotated via synthetic feedback) and resilience to labeling method variations including self-training and Vision-Language Models (VLMs).
AI.result: Empirical results show DeDPO is robust to variations in synthetic labeling methods and can achieve performance that matches or sometimes exceeds the theoretical upper bound of models trained on fully human-labeled data.
AI.conclusion: DeDPO provides a scalable, cost-efficient path for human-AI alignment by leveraging inexpensive synthetic supervision while mitigating bias/noise through debiased causal estimates.

title: Intrinsic Stability Limits of Autoregressive Reasoning: Structural Consequences for Long-Horizon Execution
comment: 16 Pages, 7 figures, Keyworda: Autoregressive Reasoning, Long-Horizon Stability, Chain-of-Thought Reasoning, Information-Theoretic Analysis, Structured Reasoning, Inference Dynamics
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable reasoning capabilities, yet their performance often deteriorates sharply in long-horizon tasks, exhibiting systematic breakdown beyond certain scales. Conventional explanations primarily attribute this phenomenon to task complexity, such as combinatorial search explosion or long-term credit assignment challenges. In this work, we argue that these explanations are incomplete: even in linear, unbranched tasks without semantic ambiguity, autoregressive execution is subject to an intrinsic stability limit.
  We propose that the fundamental constraint on long-horizon reasoning arises from process-level instability in autoregressive generation rather than solely from search or task complexity, reframing long-horizon reasoning as a problem of structural governance. We derive Theorem~A, showing that decision advantage in single-path autoregressive reasoning decays exponentially with execution length, imposing a fundamental bound on maintainable reasoning chains. This result implies a structural consequence: stable long-horizon reasoning requires discrete segmentation, naturally inducing graph-like execution structures such as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs).
  Empirical studies in both synthetic environments and real TextWorld tasks reveal observable performance cliffs consistent with theoretical predictions. Our findings provide a dynamical perspective on long-horizon reasoning failure and suggest new limitations on maintaining long-term coherence under purely autoregressive architectures. Furthermore, we highlight that short-horizon evaluation protocols may obscure structural instability, indicating a potential shift from scaling toward structured governance in future reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: Autoregressive long-horizon reasoning in LLMs is intrinsically unstable, imposing a fundamental bound on coherent multi-step reasoning. Stable long-horizon tasks require discrete segmentation that yields DAG-like execution; decision advantage decays exponentially with execution length, as shown by Theorem A. Empirical cliffs align with theory in synthetic and TextWorld tasks, suggesting a shift from scaling to structured governance for long-horizon reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current explanations attribute failures to task complexity (e.g., combinatorial search, long-term credit assignment). The abstract argues that even linear, unbranched tasks exhibit process-level instability in autoregressive generation, framing long-horizon reasoning as a governance problem with intrinsic dynamical limits.
AI.method: Theoretical derivation of Theorem A showing exponential decay of decision advantage in single-path autoregressive reasoning; argue that stable long-horizon reasoning requires discrete segmentation and graph-like execution (e.g., DAGs); empirical validation in synthetic environments and real TextWorld tasks to observe performance cliffs predicted by the theory.
AI.result: Decision advantage in single-path autoregressive reasoning decays exponentially with execution length, establishing a fundamental bound on maintainable reasoning chains. Structural segmentation is necessary, implying DAG-like execution. Empirical results show cliffs consistent with the theory.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon failures arise from process-level instability in autoregressive generation, not just task complexity. This supports a shift toward structured governance and segmentation (DAG-like execution) for long-horizon reasoning, and suggests that short-horizon evaluations may obscure the instability of purely autoregressive architectures.

title: Nipping the Drift in the Bud: Retrospective Rectification for Robust Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) requires embodied agents to interpret natural language instructions and navigate through complex continuous 3D environments. However, the dominant imitation learning paradigm suffers from exposure bias, where minor deviations during inference lead to compounding errors. While DAgger-style approaches attempt to mitigate this by correcting error states, we identify a critical limitation: Instruction-State Misalignment. Forcing an agent to learn recovery actions from off-track states often creates supervision signals that semantically conflict with the original instruction. In response to these challenges, we introduce BudVLN, an online framework that learns from on-policy rollouts by constructing supervision to match the current state distribution. BudVLN performs retrospective rectification via counterfactual re-anchoring and decision-conditioned supervision synthesis, using a geodesic oracle to synthesize corrective trajectories that originate from valid historical states, ensuring semantic consistency. Experiments on the standard R2R-CE and RxR-CE benchmarks demonstrate that BudVLN consistently mitigates distribution shift and achieves state-of-the-art performance in both Success Rate and SPL.

AI.tldr: BudVLN is an online VLN framework that tackles exposure bias and instruction-state misalignment by learning from on-policy rollouts with counterfactual re-anchoring and geography-based corrective supervision, achieving state-of-the-art SR and SPL on R2R-CE and RxR-CE.
AI.motivation: Imitation learning in Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) suffers from distribution shift due to compounding errors; DAgger-like error-state corrections can semantically conflict with the given instruction when recovering from off-track states; a method is needed to maintain semantic alignment while addressing error propagation.
AI.method: Online on-policy training with retrospective rectification: counterfactual re-anchoring and decision-conditioned supervision synthesis; a geodesic oracle generates corrective trajectories from valid historical states to preserve semantic consistency with instructions.
AI.result: Empirical results on R2R-CE and RxR-CE show BudVLN reduces distribution shift and achieves state-of-the-art performance in Success Rate (SR) and SPL across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: BudVLN effectively aligns supervision with the current state distribution, mitigating exposure bias and achieving semantically consistent, superior VLN performance.

title: Flow Matching for Offline Reinforcement Learning with Discrete Actions
summary: Generative policies based on diffusion models and flow matching have shown strong promise for offline reinforcement learning (RL), but their applicability remains largely confined to continuous action spaces. To address a broader range of offline RL settings, we extend flow matching to a general framework that supports discrete action spaces with multiple objectives. Specifically, we replace continuous flows with continuous-time Markov chains, trained using a Q-weighted flow matching objective. We then extend our design to multi-agent settings, mitigating the exponential growth of joint action spaces via a factorized conditional path. We theoretically show that, under idealized conditions, optimizing this objective recovers the optimal policy. Extensive experiments further demonstrate that our method performs robustly in practical scenarios, including high-dimensional control, multi-modal decision-making, and dynamically changing preferences over multiple objectives. Our discrete framework can also be applied to continuous-control problems through action quantization, providing a flexible trade-off between representational complexity and performance.

AI.tldr: Extends diffusion/flow-matching-based offline RL to discrete actions and multi-agent settings using continuous-time Markov chains and a Q-weighted flow-matching objective; supports multi-objective optimization and action quantization.
AI.motivation: Prior diffusion-based offline RL methods largely handle continuous action spaces. There is a need for a general framework that handles discrete actions, multiple objectives, and scalable multi-agent scenarios, with theoretical guarantees and practical robustness.
AI.method: Replace continuous flows with continuous-time Markov chains trained via a Q-weighted flow-matching objective. Extend to multi-agent settings with a factorized conditional path to mitigate joint-action space growth. Prove that, under idealized conditions, optimization recovers the optimal policy. Enable action quantization to apply the discrete framework to continuous-control problems.
AI.result: Theoretical result: under idealized conditions, the objective recovers the optimal policy. Empirically robust performance across tasks: high-dimensional control, multi-modal decisions, and dynamic objective preferences. Demonstrates versatility by applying the discrete framework to continuous problems via action quantization, offering a trade-off between modeling complexity and performance.
AI.conclusion: A discrete, multi-objective, multi-agent generalization of flow-matching offline RL that broadens applicability beyond continuous actions. It provides theoretical and empirical support, with practical flexibility through action quantization and factorized paths to manage complexity.

title: AgentCPM-Explore: Realizing Long-Horizon Deep Exploration for Edge-Scale Agents
summary: While Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents have shown remarkable potential for solving complex tasks, existing systems remain heavily reliant on large-scale models, leaving the capabilities of edge-scale models largely underexplored. In this paper, we present the first systematic study on training agentic models at the 4B-parameter scale. We identify three primary bottlenecks hindering the performance of edge-scale models: catastrophic forgetting during Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), sensitivity to reward signal noise during Reinforcement Learning (RL), and reasoning degradation caused by redundant information in long-context scenarios. To address the issues, we propose AgentCPM-Explore, a compact 4B agent model with high knowledge density and strong exploration capability. We introduce a holistic training framework featuring parameter-space model fusion, reward signal denoising, and contextual information refinement. Through deep exploration, AgentCPM-Explore achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among 4B-class models, matches or surpasses 8B-class SOTA models on four benchmarks, and even outperforms larger-scale models such as Claude-4.5-Sonnet or DeepSeek-v3.2 in five benchmarks. Notably, AgentCPM-Explore achieves 97.09% accuracy on GAIA text-based tasks under pass@64. These results provide compelling evidence that the bottleneck for edge-scale models is not their inherent capability ceiling, but rather their inference stability. Based on our well-established training framework, AgentCPM-Explore effectively unlocks the significant, yet previously underestimated, potential of edge-scale models.

AI.tldr: 4B-parameter agent CPM-Explore achieves SOTA among 4B models and competitive results with larger LLMs; addresses core bottlenecks (SFT forgetting, reward noise in RL, long-context redundancy) via a holistic training framework including parameter-space model fusion, reward denoising, and contextual refinement.
AI.motivation: Edge-scale (4B) agent models are underexplored due to three bottlenecks: catastrophic forgetting during supervised fine-tuning, sensitivity to reward noise during RL, and reasoning degradation from redundant long-context information.
AI.method: Propose AgentCPM-Explore, a compact 4B agent model with high knowledge density and exploration ability. Introduce a holistic training framework featuring parameter-space model fusion, reward signal denoising, and contextual information refinement.
AI.result: AgentCPM-Explore achieves state-of-the-art performance among 4B-class models, matches or surpasses 8B-class SOTA on four benchmarks, and outperforms larger models like Claude-4.5-Sonnet or DeepSeek-v3.2 on five benchmarks; GAIA text tasks pass@64 accuracy 97.09%.
AI.conclusion: The key bottleneck for edge-scale models is inference stability rather than an inherent capability ceiling; with the proposed training framework, 4B models can unlock substantial potential.

title: Towards Adaptive Environment Generation for Training Embodied Agents
comment: Accepted to AAAI-26 Bridge Program B10: Making Embodied AI Reliable with Testing and Formal Verification
summary: Embodied agents struggle to generalize to new environments, even when those environments share similar underlying structures to their training settings. Most current approaches to generating these training environments follow an open-loop paradigm, without considering the agent's current performance. While procedural generation methods can produce diverse scenes, diversity without feedback from the agent is inefficient. The generated environments may be trivially easy, providing limited learning signal. To address this, we present a proof-of-concept for closed-loop environment generation that adapts difficulty to the agent's current capabilities. Our system employs a controllable environment representation, extracts fine-grained performance feedback beyond binary success or failure, and implements a closed-loop adaptation mechanism that translates this feedback into environment modifications. This feedback-driven approach generates training environments that more challenging in the ways the agent needs to improve, enabling more efficient learning and better generalization to novel settings.

AI.tldr: Closed-loop, feedback-driven procedural environment generation that adapts to an embodied agent's current capabilities, aiming to improve learning efficiency and generalization.
AI.motivation: Open-loop environment generation yields diverse but potentially non-challenging training signals, hindering efficient learning and generalization to novel settings.
AI.method: Use a controllable environment representation, collect fine-grained performance feedback beyond binary success/failure, and apply a closed-loop adaptation mechanism to modify environments based on that feedback.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept demonstrating that feedback-driven closed-loop adaptation can produce training environments that are more challenging in agent-relevant ways, supporting more efficient learning and better generalization (no extensive empirical validation reported).
AI.conclusion: Feedback-driven closed-loop environment generation is a viable approach to improve training efficiency and generalization for embodied agents.

title: Optimistic Training and Convergence of Q-Learning -- Extended Version
summary:
In recent work it is shown that Q-learning with linear function approximation is stable, in the sense of bounded parameter estimates, under the $(\varepsilon,κ)$-tamed Gibbs policy; $κ$ is inverse temperature, and $\varepsilon>0$ is introduced for additional exploration. Under these assumptions it also follows that there is a solution to the projected Bellman equation (PBE). Left open is uniqueness of the solution, and criteria for convergence outside of the standard tabular or linear MDP settings.
  The present work extends these results to other variants of Q-learning, and clarifies prior work: a one dimensional example shows that under an oblivious policy for training there may be no solution to the PBE, or multiple solutions, and in each case the algorithm is not stable under oblivious training.
  The main contribution is that far more structure is required for convergence. An example is presented for which the basis is ideal, in the sense that the true Q-function is in the span of the basis. However, there are two solutions to the PBE under the greedy policy, and hence also for the $(\varepsilon,κ)$-tamed Gibbs policy for all sufficiently small $\varepsilon>0$ and $κ\ge 1$.

AI.tldr: Stability and existence of a solution to the projected Bellman equation (PBE) for Q-learning with linear function approximation under (ε, κ)-tamed Gibbs policy are established, but uniqueness and convergence require stronger structure; counterexamples show non-uniqueness and instability under oblivious training, and even with an ideal basis there can be two PBE solutions under greedy policy for small ε and κ≥1.
AI.motivation: Clarify when Q-learning with function approximation has a stable, unique fixed point in the PBE under non-tabular settings; address gaps in convergence outside standard tabular/linear MDPs.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis with counterexamples. Demonstrates non-uniqueness and potential instability by constructing a 1D oblivious-training example and an 'ideal' basis example with two PBE solutions under greedy policy, then relates to (ε, κ)-tamed Gibbs policies.
AI.result: Under certain taming policies, a solution to the PBE exists and parameter estimates are bounded; however, the solution need not be unique, and convergence cannot be guaranteed without stronger structural assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Existence of a PBE under taming policies does not imply uniqueness or convergence in general. Stronger structural conditions are required to ensure a unique fixed point and stable learning in Q-learning with function approximation beyond tabular/linear MDPs.

title: DroneKey++: A Size Prior-free Method and New Benchmark for Drone 3D Pose Estimation from Sequential Images
comment: 8 page, 5 figures, 6 tables, Accepted to ICRA 2026 (to appear)
summary: Accurate 3D pose estimation of drones is essential for security and surveillance systems. However, existing methods often rely on prior drone information such as physical sizes or 3D meshes. At the same time, current datasets are small-scale, limited to single models, and collected under constrained environments, which makes reliable validation of generalization difficult. We present DroneKey++, a prior-free framework that jointly performs keypoint detection, drone classification, and 3D pose estimation. The framework employs a keypoint encoder for simultaneous keypoint detection and classification, and a pose decoder that estimates 3D pose using ray-based geometric reasoning and class embeddings. To address dataset limitations, we construct 6DroneSyn, a large-scale synthetic benchmark with over 50K images covering 7 drone models and 88 outdoor backgrounds, generated using 360-degree panoramic synthesis. Experiments show that DroneKey++ achieves MAE 17.34 deg and MedAE 17.1 deg for rotation, MAE 0.135 m and MedAE 0.242 m for translation, with inference speeds of 19.25 FPS (CPU) and 414.07 FPS (GPU), demonstrating both strong generalization across drone models and suitability for real-time applications. The dataset is publicly available.

AI.tldr: DroneKey++ presents a prior-free, joint framework for keypoint detection, drone classification, and 3D pose estimation, plus a large synthetic benchmark 6DroneSyn. It achieves real-time inference and demonstrates cross-model generalization, with competitive pose errors.
AI.motivation: To address reliance on prior drone information and the limited, small-scale, constrained real-world datasets that hinder robust generalization validation for drone pose estimation.
AI.method: A keypoint encoder performs simultaneous keypoint detection and model classification; a pose decoder uses ray-based geometric reasoning and class embeddings. 6DroneSyn, a large-scale synthetic benchmark (>50k images, 7 models, 88 outdoor backgrounds) is created via 360-degree panoramic synthesis to challenge generalization.
AI.result: Performance: rotation MAE 17.34°; rotation MedAE 17.1°; translation MAE 0.135 m; translation MedAE 0.242 m. Speed: 19.25 FPS CPU; 414.07 FPS GPU. Demonstrates strong cross-model generalization and suitability for real-time deployment; dataset is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The approach shows that a prior-free framework can generalize across drone models and operate in real time, while providing a valuable synthetic benchmark for validation. Potential limitations include domain gap between synthetic and real data and the need for broader model coverage and real-world validation.

title: JADE: Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation for Open-Ended Professional Tasks
summary: Evaluating agentic AI on open-ended professional tasks faces a fundamental dilemma between rigor and flexibility. Static rubrics provide rigorous, reproducible assessment but fail to accommodate diverse valid response strategies, while LLM-as-a-judge approaches adapt to individual responses yet suffer from instability and bias. Human experts address this dilemma by combining domain-grounded principles with dynamic, claim-level assessment. Inspired by this process, we propose JADE, a two-layer evaluation framework. Layer 1 encodes expert knowledge as a predefined set of evaluation skills, providing stable evaluation criteria. Layer 2 performs report-specific, claim-level evaluation to flexibly assess diverse reasoning strategies, with evidence-dependency gating to invalidate conclusions built on refuted claims. Experiments on BizBench show that JADE improves evaluation stability and reveals critical agent failure modes missed by holistic LLM-based evaluators. We further demonstrate strong alignment with expert-authored rubrics and effective transfer to a medical-domain benchmark, validating JADE across professional domains. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/smiling-world/JADE.

AI.tldr: JADE is a two-layer evaluation framework for agentic AI that stabilizes assessment while remaining flexible: Layer 1 encodes expert skills for stable criteria; Layer 2 conducts claim-level, report-specific evaluation with evidence-based gating, improving stability, exposing failure modes, and transferring to medical-domain benchmarks; code available.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of agentic AI balances rigor (static rubrics) and flexibility (LLM judges), but existing approaches either lack adaptability or stability. JADE is motivated by human expert processes that combine principled guidelines with dynamic, claim-level assessment to robustly judge diverse reasoning.
AI.method: JADE consists of Layer 1: a predefined set of expert evaluation skills; Layer 2: claim-level evaluation tailored to the specific report with evidence-dependency gating to invalidate conclusions built on refuted claims. The authors test on BizBench, assess alignment with expert rubrics, and demonstrate transfer to a medical-domain benchmark, with public code: JADE.
AI.result: JADE improves evaluation stability and uncovers critical agent failure modes missed by holistic LLM evaluators; it aligns strongly with expert rubrics and transfers to a medical-domain benchmark, demonstrating cross-domain robustness.
AI.conclusion: JADE provides a principled, transferable evaluation framework that leverages static expert criteria for stability and dynamic claim-level assessment for flexibility, validated across professional domains.

title: A Consistency-Improved LiDAR-Inertial Bundle Adjustment
summary: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) using 3D LiDAR has emerged as a cornerstone for autonomous navigation in robotics. While feature-based SLAM systems have achieved impressive results by leveraging edge and planar structures, they often suffer from the inconsistent estimator associated with feature parameterization and estimated covariance. In this work, we present a consistency-improved LiDAR-inertial bundle adjustment (BA) with tailored parameterization and estimator. First, we propose a stereographic-projection representation parameterizing the planar and edge features, and conduct a comprehensive observability analysis to support its integrability with consistent estimator. Second, we implement a LiDAR-inertial BA with Maximum a Posteriori (MAP) formulation and First-Estimate Jacobians (FEJ) to preserve the accurate estimated covariance and observability properties of the system. Last, we apply our proposed BA method to a LiDAR-inertial odometry.

AI.tldr: A consistency-improved LiDAR-inertial bundle adjustment (BA) for SLAM using 3D LiDAR is proposed, featuring stereographic-projection parameterization for edge/plane features, an observability analysis, and a MAP formulation with First-Estimate Jacobians (FEJ) to preserve covariance, applied to LiDAR-inertial odometry.
AI.motivation: To address estimator inconsistency arising from feature-parameterization and covariance in feature-based 3D LiDAR SLAM, by designing a parameterization that aligns with observability properties and by using FEJ/MAP to maintain accurate covariance.
AI.method: Introduce stereographic-projection parameterization for planar and edge features; conduct observability analysis; implement LiDAR-inertial BA under MAP with FEJ to preserve covariance and observability; apply the BA to LiDAR-inertial odometry.
AI.result: Claim of improved estimator consistency through the MA(P) formulation and FEJ, with preserved covariance/observability; method validated on LiDAR-inertial odometry, demonstrating consistent estimation properties.
AI.conclusion: The proposed BA provides a consistency-improved LiDAR-inertial SLAM framework with tailored parameterization and estimator design, suitable for robust LiDAR-inertial odometry.

title: MoSE: Mixture of Slimmable Experts for Efficient and Adaptive Language Models
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale large language models efficiently by sparsely activating experts, but once an expert is selected, it is executed fully. Hence, the trade-off between accuracy and computation in an MoE model typically exhibits large discontinuities. We propose Mixture of Slimmable Experts (MoSE), an MoE architecture in which each expert has a nested, slimmable structure that can be executed at variable widths. This enables conditional computation not only over which experts are activated, but also over how much of each expert is utilized. Consequently, a single pretrained MoSE model can support a more continuous spectrum of accuracy-compute trade-offs at inference time. We present a simple and stable training recipe for slimmable experts under sparse routing, combining multi-width training with standard MoE objectives. During inference, we explore strategies for runtime width determination, including a lightweight test-time training mechanism that learns how to map router confidence/probabilities to expert widths under a fixed budget. Experiments on GPT models trained on OpenWebText demonstrate that MoSE matches or improves upon standard MoE at full width and consistently shifts the Pareto frontier for accuracy vs. cost, achieving comparable performance with significantly fewer FLOPs.

AI.tldr: Introduces Mixture of Slimmable Experts (MoSE): a MoE variant with nested, width-adjustable experts and runtime width adaptation to enable smoother accuracy–compute trade-offs and reduced FLOPs.
AI.motivation: Address the sharp discontinuities in accuracy once a fixed subset of experts is activated in standard MoE; enable finer-grained conditional computation by varying per-expert widths.
AI.method: Each expert is a slimmable module with multiple widths (nested structure); train with multi-width training alongside standard MoE objectives; infer with strategies for runtime width selection, including a lightweight test-time training that maps router confidence to expert widths under a fixed budget.
AI.result: MoSE matches or surpasses full-width MoE performance and consistently shifts the accuracy–compute Pareto frontier toward lower compute; achieves comparable accuracy with significantly fewer FLOPs on GPT models trained on OpenWebText.
AI.conclusion: MoSE provides a simple, stable training recipe for slimmable experts in sparse routing, enabling continuous accuracy–compute trade-offs at inference and enabling more efficient deployment under budget constraints.

title: Addressing the Waypoint-Action Gap in End-to-End Autonomous Driving via Vehicle Motion Models
summary: End-to-End Autonomous Driving (E2E-AD) systems are typically grouped by the nature of their outputs: (i) waypoint-based models that predict a future trajectory, and (ii) action-based models that directly output throttle, steer and brake. Most recent benchmark protocols and training pipelines are waypoint-based, which makes action-based policies harder to train and compare, slowing their progress. To bridge this waypoint-action gap, we propose a novel, differentiable vehicle-model framework that rolls out predicted action sequences to their corresponding ego-frame waypoint trajectories while supervising in waypoint space. Our approach enables action-based architectures to be trained and evaluated, for the first time, within waypoint-based benchmarks without modifying the underlying evaluation protocol. We extensively evaluate our framework across multiple challenging benchmarks and observe consistent improvements over the baselines. In particular, on NAVSIM \texttt{navhard} our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Introduces a differentiable vehicle-model to translate action sequences into ego-frame waypoint trajectories, enabling action-based E2E-AD to be trained/evaluated on waypoint benchmarks without protocol changes, achieving improvements and state-of-the-art on NAVSIM navhard.
AI.motivation: There is a persistent gap between waypoint-based supervision and action-based policy outputs in E2E-AD, hindering fair comparison and progress for action-based methods.
AI.method: Develop a differentiable roll-out vehicle model that maps predicted actions (throttle, steer, brake) into waypoint trajectories in the ego frame; supervise predictions in waypoint space; integrate with existing waypoint benchmarks without changing evaluation protocols.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over baselines across multiple benchmarks; state-of-the-art on NAVSIM navhard; plan to release code publicly.
AI.conclusion: Bridges the waypoint-action gap, enabling action-based architectures to be trained/evaluated within waypoint-centric benchmarks and accelerating progress in E2E-AD.

title: Now You See That: Learning End-to-End Humanoid Locomotion from Raw Pixels
summary: Achieving robust vision-based humanoid locomotion remains challenging due to two fundamental issues: the sim-to-real gap introduces significant perception noise that degrades performance on fine-grained tasks, and training a unified policy across diverse terrains is hindered by conflicting learning objectives. To address these challenges, we present an end-to-end framework for vision-driven humanoid locomotion. For robust sim-to-real transfer, we develop a high-fidelity depth sensor simulation that captures stereo matching artifacts and calibration uncertainties inherent in real-world sensing. We further propose a vision-aware behavior distillation approach that combines latent space alignment with noise-invariant auxiliary tasks, enabling effective knowledge transfer from privileged height maps to noisy depth observations. For versatile terrain adaptation, we introduce terrain-specific reward shaping integrated with multi-critic and multi-discriminator learning, where dedicated networks capture the distinct dynamics and motion priors of each terrain type. We validate our approach on two humanoid platforms equipped with different stereo depth cameras. The resulting policy demonstrates robust performance across diverse environments, seamlessly handling extreme challenges such as high platforms and wide gaps, as well as fine-grained tasks including bidirectional long-term staircase traversal.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end vision-driven humanoid locomotion framework that tackles sim-to-real perception noise and terrain diversity via (i) high-fidelity depth-sensor simulation for realism, (ii) vision-aware behavior distillation with latent alignment and noise-invariant auxiliary tasks transferring privileged height-map knowledge to depth observations, and (iii) terrain-aware training using terrain-specific rewards with multi-critic and multi-discriminator networks. Validated on two humanoid platforms with stereo cameras, achieving robust performance across diverse terrains and challenging tasks such as long stair traversal.
AI.motivation: Two core challenges impede robust vision-based humanoid locomotion: (1) sim-to-real transfer is hampered by perception noise from depth sensing, including stereo artifacts and calibration uncertainties, degrading fine-grained tasks; (2) training a single policy across heterogeneous terrains is difficult due to conflicting learning objectives and dynamics.
AI.method: (a) High-fidelity depth sensor simulation that models stereo matching artifacts and calibration uncertainties to close the sim-to-real gap. (b) Vision-aware behavior distillation combining latent space alignment with noise-invariant auxiliary tasks, enabling knowledge transfer from privileged height maps to noisy depth observations. (c) Terrain-specific reward shaping with multi-critic and multi-discriminator learning; separate networks capture the distinct dynamics and priors of each terrain type, enabling terrain-aware policy optimization.
AI.result: Policy demonstrates robust performance across varied environments on two humanoid platforms with different stereo depth cameras, handling extreme scenarios (high platforms, wide gaps) and fine-grained tasks (bidirectional long-term staircase traversal).
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework delivers robust sim-to-real transfer and versatile terrain adaptation for vision-driven humanoid locomotion, showing strong potential for real-world deployment across diverse terrains and sensing conditions.

title: Latent Structure Emergence in Diffusion Models via Confidence-Based Filtering
summary: Diffusion models rely on a high-dimensional latent space of initial noise seeds, yet it remains unclear whether this space contains sufficient structure to predict properties of the generated samples, such as their classes. In this work, we investigate the emergence of latent structure through the lens of confidence scores assigned by a pre-trained classifier to generated samples. We show that while the latent space appears largely unstructured when considering all noise realizations, restricting attention to initial noise seeds that produce high-confidence samples reveals pronounced class separability. By comparing class predictability across noise subsets of varying confidence and examining the class separability of the latent space, we find evidence of class-relevant latent structure that becomes observable only under confidence-based filtering. As a practical implication, we discuss how confidence-based filtering enables conditional generation as an alternative to guidance-based methods.

AI.tldr: High-confidence seeds reveal class-relevant latent structure in diffusion models, enabling conditional generation via filtering rather than guidance.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the high-dimensional latent space of initial diffusion noise seeds contains structure correlated with generated sample classes, and whether this structure can be exploited for conditional generation.
AI.method: Compute class confidence scores from a pre-trained classifier on diffusion-generated samples. Partition seeds by confidence levels, then compare class predictability and latent separability across subsets versus the full seed space.
AI.result: The full latent space across all seeds appears largely unstructured. However, restricting to initial seeds that produce high-confidence samples reveals pronounced class separability, indicating a latent structure tied to class that is observable only under confidence-based filtering.
AI.conclusion: Confidence-based filtering provides a practical path to conditional generation, serving as an alternative to diffusion guidance methods by leveraging latent class structure revealed by classifier confidence.

title: Cross-Modal Redundancy and the Geometry of Vision-Language Embeddings
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) align images and text with remarkable success, yet the geometry of their shared embedding space remains poorly understood. To probe this geometry, we begin from the Iso-Energy Assumption, which exploits cross-modal redundancy: a concept that is truly shared should exhibit the same average energy across modalities. We operationalize this assumption with an Aligned Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) that encourages energy consistency during training while preserving reconstruction. We find that this inductive bias changes the SAE solution without harming reconstruction, giving us a representation that serves as a tool for geometric analysis. Sanity checks on controlled data with known ground truth confirm that alignment improves when Iso-Energy holds and remains neutral when it does not. Applied to foundational VLMs, our framework reveals a clear structure with practical consequences: (i) sparse bimodal atoms carry the entire cross-modal alignment signal; (ii) unimodal atoms act as modality-specific biases and fully explain the modality gap; (iii) removing unimodal atoms collapses the gap without harming performance; (iv) restricting vector arithmetic to the bimodal subspace yields in-distribution edits and improved retrieval. These findings suggest that the right inductive bias can both preserve model fidelity and render the latent geometry interpretable and actionable.

AI.tldr: Iso-Energy aligned sparse autoencoders reveal that cross-modal alignment in vision-language models is carried by sparse bimodal atoms, while unimodal atoms bias each modality and explain the modality gap; removing unimodal atoms collapses the gap with no loss in performance, and restricting arithmetic to the bimodal subspace yields better prompts/editing and retrieval.
AI.motivation: Understand the geometry of the shared embedding space in vision-language models and test whether a cross-modal energy consistency (Iso-Energy) bias can reveal interpretable structure.
AI.method: Propose an Aligned Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) with an energy-consistency objective across modalities to enforce Iso-Energy; train on VLM representations; analyze the learned atoms by isolating bimodal vs unimodal components; validate on controlled data and on foundational VLMs; examine reconstruction, alignment, and vector arithmetic within subspaces.
AI.result: Controlled experiments show alignment improves when Iso-Energy holds and remains neutral otherwise. In plainer terms, energy-consistent training reshapes the SAE without harming reconstruction. In large VLMs, sparse bimodal atoms carry the entire cross-modal alignment signal; unimodal atoms explain the modality bias and gap; removing unimodal atoms collapses the gap without reducing performance; restricting vector arithmetic to the bimodal subspace yields in-distribution edits and improved retrieval.
AI.conclusion: An appropriate inductive bias (Iso-Energy with SAE) makes the latent geometry of VLMs interpretable and actionable without sacrificing fidelity, enabling targeted edits and a clearer separation between shared, bimodal information and modality-specific biases.

title: HyPER: Bridging Exploration and Exploitation for Scalable LLM Reasoning with Hypothesis Path Expansion and Reduction
summary: Scaling test-time compute with multi-path chain-of-thought improves reasoning accuracy, but its effectiveness depends critically on the exploration-exploitation trade-off. Existing approaches address this trade-off in rigid ways: tree-structured search hard-codes exploration through brittle expansion rules that interfere with post-trained reasoning, while parallel reasoning over-explores redundant hypothesis paths and relies on weak answer selection. Motivated by the observation that the optimal balance is phase-dependent and that correct and incorrect reasoning paths often diverge only at late stages, we reformulate test-time scaling as a dynamic expand-reduce control problem over a pool of hypotheses. We propose HyPER, a training-free online control policy for multi-path decoding in mixture-of-experts models that reallocates computation under a fixed budget using lightweight path statistics. HyPER consists of an online controller that transitions from exploration to exploitation as the hypothesis pool evolves, a token-level refinement mechanism that enables efficient generation-time exploitation without full-path resampling, and a length- and confidence-aware aggregation strategy for reliable answer-time exploitation. Experiments on four mixture-of-experts language models across diverse reasoning benchmarks show that HyPER consistently achieves a superior accuracy-compute trade-off, improving accuracy by 8 to 10 percent while reducing token usage by 25 to 40 percent.

AI.tldr: HyPER is a training-free online control policy for multi-path decoding in mixture-of-experts models that dynamically balances exploration and exploitation under a fixed budget, using a lightweight pool of hypotheses and token-/confidence-aware strategies. It achieves better accuracy with lower compute compared to rigid or over-parallel methods.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time scaling methods for reasoning with mixture-of-experts models rely on rigid, brittle exploration (tree search) or over-parallelization with weak answer selection. The optimal exploration-exploitation balance is phase-dependent and critical for performance, with correct and incorrect paths often diverging late.
AI.method: HyPER introduces three components: (1) an online controller that shifts from exploration to exploitation as the hypothesis pool evolves; (2) a token-level refinement mechanism enabling exploitation without full-path resampling; (3) a length- and confidence-aware aggregation strategy for reliable answer timing. The policy is training-free and reallocates computation under a fixed budget using lightweight path statistics.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four mixture-of-experts language models across diverse reasoning benchmarks shows HyPER consistently improves the accuracy-compute trade-off, with 8–10% absolute accuracy gains and 25–40% reductions in token usage for the same budget.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, training-free control of multi-path decoding with HyPER yields robust improvements in reasoning accuracy under compute constraints, suggesting broad applicability to MoE and similar multi-path generation settings and motivating further exploration of path-statistics and budget-aware strategies.

title: ECO: Energy-Constrained Optimization with Reinforcement Learning for Humanoid Walking
comment: IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON AUTOMATION SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING. PREPRINT VERSION. ACCEPTED FEB, 2026
summary: Achieving stable and energy-efficient locomotion is essential for humanoid robots to operate continuously in real-world applications. Existing MPC and RL approaches often rely on energy-related metrics embedded within a multi-objective optimization framework, which require extensive hyperparameter tuning and often result in suboptimal policies. To address these challenges, we propose ECO (Energy-Constrained Optimization), a constrained RL framework that separates energy-related metrics from rewards, reformulating them as explicit inequality constraints. This method provides a clear and interpretable physical representation of energy costs, enabling more efficient and intuitive hyperparameter tuning for improved energy efficiency. ECO introduces dedicated constraints for energy consumption and reference motion, enforced by the Lagrangian method, to achieve stable, symmetric, and energy-efficient walking for humanoid robots. We evaluated ECO against MPC, standard RL with reward shaping, and four state-of-the-art constrained RL methods. Experiments, including sim-to-sim and sim-to-real transfers on the kid-sized humanoid robot BRUCE, demonstrate that ECO significantly reduces energy consumption compared to baselines while maintaining robust walking performance. These results highlight a substantial advancement in energy-efficient humanoid locomotion. All experimental demonstrations can be found on the project website: https://sites.google.com/view/eco-humanoid.
