AI.motivation: To understand how active learning strategies interact with hypothesis spaces and proposal distributions when learning concepts with executable programs from an LLM, and to explain when information-theoretic queries outperform or underperform compared to human-like safe querying.
AI.method: Compare a Rational Active Learner (maximizes approximate expected information gain, EIG) with a human-like Positive Test Strategy (PTS) within a neuro-symbolic Bayesian framework. Hypotheses are executable programs proposed by an LLM and reweighted by Bayesian updating. Tasks are the Number Game, spanning simple to compound rules. Analyze failure due to support mismatch between EIG policy and LLM proposal distribution, observed as boundary queries driving posterior to regions where generators yield invalid/overly specific programs. Evaluate particle approximation and proposal validity.
AI.result: EIG is effective when falsification of hypotheses is necessary (e.g., for complex, exception-laden rules) but underperforms on simple concepts. The failure is traced to a support mismatch: highly diagnostic boundary queries push the posterior into regions where the LLM-proposed programs are invalid or too specific, causing a trap in the particle-based approximation. PTS is less information-rich but tends to preserve valid proposals by selecting 'safe' queries, leading to faster convergence on simple rules. The findings imply that confirmation bias can be a rational adaptation to maintain tractable inference in sparse, open-ended hypothesis spaces.
AI.conclusion: In sparse, open-ended hypothesis spaces typical of human thought, maintaining proposal validity and tractable inference may be more crucial than pursuing maximal information gain. The apparent confirmation bias may reflect a rational strategy rather than a cognitive error, and active-learning designs should align query strategies with the proposal distribution to avoid support-mismatch traps when using generative models like LLMs.

title: Force Generative Imitation Learning: Bridging Position Trajectory and Force Commands through Control Technique
summary: In contact-rich tasks, while position trajectories are often easy to obtain, appropriate force commands are typically unknown. Although it is conceivable to generate force commands using a pretrained foundation model such as Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, force control is highly dependent on the specific hardware of the robot, which makes the application of such models challenging. To bridge this gap, we propose a force generative model that estimates force commands from given position trajectories. However, when dealing with unseen position trajectories, the model struggles to generate accurate force commands. To address this, we introduce a feedback control mechanism. Our experiments reveal that feedback control does not converge when the force generative model has memory. We therefore adopt a model without memory, enabling stable feedback control. This approach allows the system to generate force commands effectively, even for unseen position trajectories, improving generalization for real-world robot writing tasks.

AI.tldr: A force-generative model converts position trajectories into force commands for contact-rich tasks; to generalize to unseen trajectories, a memoryless feedback-control approach is used, overcoming convergence issues seen with memory-enabled models in real-world robot writing tasks.
AI.motivation: Force commands in contact-rich manipulation are hard to obtain and hardware-dependent; existing foundation models like VLA can help but struggle with real hardware constraints, motivating a model that can infer forces from trajectories with robust generalization.
AI.method: Develop a force generative model that predicts force commands from position trajectories; integrate a feedback control mechanism; compare architectures with memory vs memoryless designs; evaluate on real-world robot writing tasks to assess convergence and generalization.
AI.result: Memoryful force generative models cause feedback control to fail to converge; adopting a memoryless model enables stable feedback control and improved generalization to unseen trajectories, improving performance in real-world writing tasks.
AI.conclusion: A memoryless force generative model with feedback control provides stable, generalizable force command estimation for unseen position trajectories in contact-rich tasks, enabling better real-world performance.

title: Provably avoiding over-optimization in Direct Preference Optimization without knowing the data distribution
summary: We introduce PEPO (Pessimistic Ensemble based Preference Optimization), a single-step Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)-like algorithm to mitigate the well-known over-optimization issue in preference learning without requiring the knowledge of the data-generating distribution or learning an explicit reward model. PEPO achieves pessimism via an ensemble of preference-optimized policies trained on disjoint data subsets and then aggregates them through a worst case construction that favors the agreement across models. In the tabular setting, PEPO achieves sample complexity guarantees depending only on a single-policy concentrability coefficient, thus avoiding the all-policy concentrability which affects the guarantees of algorithms prone to over-optimization, such as DPO. The theoretical findings are corroborated by a convincing practical performance, while retaining the simplicity and the practicality of DPO-style training.

AI.tldr: PEPO introduces a pessimistic ensemble approach to Direct Preference Optimization to mitigate over-optimization without modeling the data-generating distribution.
AI.motivation: Address over-optimization in preference learning in which performance depends on data distribution or an explicit reward model; seek a simple, distribution-free, pessimistic alternative.
AI.method: Train an ensemble of preference-optimized policies on disjoint data subsets, then aggregate with a worst-case construction that relies on model agreement to create a pessimistic objective; this is a single-step DPO-like procedure.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees in the tabular setting show sample complexity depending on a single-policy concentrability coefficient (avoiding all-policy concentrability); empirically strong performance with simplicity similar to DPO.
AI.conclusion: PEPO provides a practical, theoretically grounded alternative to DPO by introducing pessimism through ensemble agreement without requiring data distribution knowledge or a learned reward model.

title: Taming SAM3 in the Wild: A Concept Bank for Open-Vocabulary Segmentation
summary: The recent introduction of \texttt{SAM3} has revolutionized Open-Vocabulary Segmentation (OVS) through \textit{promptable concept segmentation}, which grounds pixel predictions in flexible concept prompts. However, this reliance on pre-defined concepts makes the model vulnerable: when visual distributions shift (\textit{data drift}) or conditional label distributions evolve (\textit{concept drift}) in the target domain, the alignment between visual evidence and prompts breaks down. In this work, we present \textsc{ConceptBank}, a parameter-free calibration framework to restore this alignment on the fly. Instead of adhering to static prompts, we construct a dataset-specific concept bank from the target statistics. Our approach (\textit{i}) anchors target-domain evidence via class-wise visual prototypes, (\textit{ii}) mines representative supports to suppress outliers under data drift, and (\textit{iii}) fuses candidate concepts to rectify concept drift. We demonstrate that \textsc{ConceptBank} effectively adapts \texttt{SAM3} to distribution drifts, including challenging natural-scene and remote-sensing scenarios, establishing a new baseline for robustness and efficiency in OVS. Code and model are available at https://github.com/pgsmall/ConceptBank.

AI.tldr: ConceptBank is a parameter-free calibration framework that adapts SAM3 for open-vocabulary segmentation under distribution shift by building a dataset-specific concept bank from target statistics; it anchors evidence with class prototypes, mines representative supports to suppress outliers, and fuses concepts to rectify drift, improving robustness and efficiency (code available).
AI.motivation: Promptable concept segmentation with SAM3 relies on static prompts, making it vulnerable to data drift and concept drift in the target domain. There is a need for on-the-fly, parameter-free adaptation that aligns visual evidence with prompts using target-domain statistics.
AI.method: Construct a dataset-specific concept bank from target statistics by (i) anchoring evidence via class-wise visual prototypes, (ii) mining representative supports to suppress outliers under data drift, and (iii) fusing candidate concepts to rectify concept drift; all without introducing trainable parameters.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates that ConceptBank adapts SAM3 to distribution drifts across challenging natural-scene and remote-sensing scenarios, establishing a new baseline for robustness and efficiency in open-vocabulary segmentation.
AI.conclusion: ConceptBank provides effective, on-the-fly calibration for prompt-based OVS under drift without additional parameters, enabling robust segmentation across diverse target domains; code and model are available online.

title: ScaleEnv: Scaling Environment Synthesis from Scratch for Generalist Interactive Tool-Use Agent Training
summary: Training generalist agents capable of adapting to diverse scenarios requires interactive environments for self-exploration. However, interactive environments remain critically scarce, and existing synthesis methods suffer from significant limitations regarding environmental diversity and scalability. To address these challenges, we introduce ScaleEnv, a framework that constructs fully interactive environments and verifiable tasks entirely from scratch. Specifically, ScaleEnv ensures environment reliability through procedural testing, and guarantees task completeness and solvability via tool dependency graph expansion and executable action verification. By enabling agents to learn through exploration within ScaleEnv, we demonstrate significant performance improvements on unseen, multi-turn tool-use benchmarks such as $τ^2$-Bench and VitaBench, highlighting strong generalization capabilities. Furthermore, we investigate the relationship between increasing number of domains and model generalization performance, providing empirical evidence that scaling environmental diversity is critical for robust agent learning.

AI.tldr: ScaleEnv builds fully interactive, verifiable environments from scratch to improve generalist agents; emphasizes procedural testing, tool graph expansion, and executable action verification to boost generalization on unseen multi-turn tool-use benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Interactive, diverse, scalable environments are scarce; existing synthesis methods struggle with environmental diversity and scalability, hindering self-exploration for generalist agents.
AI.method: ScaleEnv framework constructs interactive environments and tasks from scratch; ensures reliability via procedural testing; guarantees task completeness and solvability through tool dependency graph expansion and executable action verification; evaluates on unseen multi-turn tool-use benchmarks (τ^2-Bench, VitaBench) and analyzes the impact of increasing domain diversity on generalization.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant performance improvements on unseen multi-turn tool-use benchmarks, indicating strong generalization; provides empirical evidence that scaling environmental diversity enhances robust agent learning; explores relation between number of domains and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Scaling environmental diversity is critical for robust generalist-agent learning; ScaleEnv offers a reliable, scalable path to generate diverse, verifiable environments conducive to self-exploration and generalization.

title: Humanoid Manipulation Interface: Humanoid Whole-Body Manipulation from Robot-Free Demonstrations
summary: Current approaches for humanoid whole-body manipulation, primarily relying on teleoperation or visual sim-to-real reinforcement learning, are hindered by hardware logistics and complex reward engineering. Consequently, demonstrated autonomous skills remain limited and are typically restricted to controlled environments. In this paper, we present the Humanoid Manipulation Interface (HuMI), a portable and efficient framework for learning diverse whole-body manipulation tasks across various environments. HuMI enables robot-free data collection by capturing rich whole-body motion using portable hardware. This data drives a hierarchical learning pipeline that translates human motions into dexterous and feasible humanoid skills. Extensive experiments across five whole-body tasks--including kneeling, squatting, tossing, walking, and bimanual manipulation--demonstrate that HuMI achieves a 3x increase in data collection efficiency compared to teleoperation and attains a 70% success rate in unseen environments.

AI.tldr: HuMI enables robot-free data collection for humanoid whole-body manipulation using portable motion capture, learning via a hierarchical pipeline to convert human motions into dexterous humanoid skills; achieves 3× data-collection efficiency and 70% success on unseen environments across five tasks.
AI.motivation: Current humanoid manipulation research relies on teleoperation or sim-to-real RL, which face hardware logistics and reward-engineering challenges, limiting autonomous skill learning to controlled settings.
AI.method: Use portable hardware to capture rich whole-body human motion; train a hierarchical learning pipeline to map human motions to feasible humanoid skills, enabling diverse tasks in various environments.
AI.result: Five tasks demonstrated (kneeling, squatting, tossing, walking, bimanual manipulation). HuMI yields ~3× improvement in data-collection efficiency over teleoperation and achieves 70% success in unseen environments.
AI.conclusion: HuMI offers a scalable, data-efficient pathway to autonomous humanoid manipulation across environments by decoupling data collection from the robot and leveraging hierarchical policy learning; further work could explore broader task generalization and real-world deployment challenges.

title: ATEX-CF: Attack-Informed Counterfactual Explanations for Graph Neural Networks
comment: 30 pages, accepted by ICLR 2026, github code:https://github.com/zhangyuo/ATEX_CF
summary: Counterfactual explanations offer an intuitive way to interpret graph neural networks (GNNs) by identifying minimal changes that alter a model's prediction, thereby answering "what must differ for a different outcome?". In this work, we propose a novel framework, ATEX-CF that unifies adversarial attack techniques with counterfactual explanation generation-a connection made feasible by their shared goal of flipping a node's prediction, yet differing in perturbation strategy: adversarial attacks often rely on edge additions, while counterfactual methods typically use deletions. Unlike traditional approaches that treat explanation and attack separately, our method efficiently integrates both edge additions and deletions, grounded in theory, leveraging adversarial insights to explore impactful counterfactuals. In addition, by jointly optimizing fidelity, sparsity, and plausibility under a constrained perturbation budget, our method produces instance-level explanations that are both informative and realistic. Experiments on synthetic and real-world node classification benchmarks demonstrate that ATEX-CF generates faithful, concise, and plausible explanations, highlighting the effectiveness of integrating adversarial insights into counterfactual reasoning for GNNs.

AI.tldr: A unified ATEX-CF framework merges adversarial attacks and counterfactual explanations for GNNs, enabling both edge additions and deletions within a budget to produce faithful, sparse, and plausible instance-level explanations; validated on synthetic and real node classification tasks.
AI.motivation: To improve interpretability of graph neural networks by leveraging adversarial perturbation insights to generate high-quality counterfactual explanations, bridging the gap between explanation and attack methods.
AI.method: Proposes a joint optimization that allows both edge additions and deletions under a perturbation budget, grounded in theory to link adversarial perturbations with counterfactuals. It optimizes fidelity (prediction alignment with the counterfactual), sparsity (few changes), and plausibility (realistic perturbations) to produce concise, plausible explanations at the instance level.
AI.result: ATEX-CF yields faithful, concise, and plausible explanations. The framework effectively integrates adversarial insights into counterfactual reasoning for GNNs, with experiments on synthetic and real-world node classification benchmarks showing improved explanatory quality.
AI.conclusion: Integrating adversarial perturbation insights into counterfactual reasoning is beneficial for GNN interpretability, enabling realistic and informative explanations; the approach suggests broader applicability and potential extensions to other graph-related tasks.

title: SPDA-SAM: A Self-prompted Depth-Aware Segment Anything Model for Instance Segmentation
summary: Recently, Segment Anything Model (SAM) has demonstrated strong generalizability in various instance segmentation tasks. However, its performance is severely dependent on the quality of manual prompts. In addition, the RGB images that instance segmentation methods normally use inherently lack depth information. As a result, the ability of these methods to perceive spatial structures and delineate object boundaries is hindered. To address these challenges, we propose a Self-prompted Depth-Aware SAM (SPDA-SAM) for instance segmentation. Specifically, we design a Semantic-Spatial Self-prompt Module (SSSPM) which extracts the semantic and spatial prompts from the image encoder and the mask decoder of SAM, respectively. Furthermore, we introduce a Coarse-to-Fine RGB-D Fusion Module (C2FFM), in which the features extracted from a monocular RGB image and the depth map estimated from it are fused. In particular, the structural information in the depth map is used to provide coarse-grained guidance to feature fusion, while local variations in depth are encoded in order to fuse fine-grained feature representations. To our knowledge, SAM has not been explored in such self-prompted and depth-aware manners. Experimental results demonstrate that our SPDA-SAM outperforms its state-of-the-art counterparts across twelve different data sets. These promising results should be due to the guidance of the self-prompts and the compensation for the spatial information loss by the coarse-to-fine RGB-D fusion operation.

AI.tldr: SPDA-SAM introduces self-prompted depth-aware prompts for SAM via Semantic-Spatial Self-prompt Module and Coarse-to-Fine RGB-D Fusion, achieving state-of-the-art performance on 12 datasets.
AI.motivation: SAM's performance hinges on manual prompts and RGB images lack depth information, limiting spatial understanding and boundary delineation.
AI.method: A Semantic-Spatial Self-prompt Module (SSSPM) extracts semantic prompts from the image encoder and spatial prompts from the mask decoder; a Coarse-to-Fine RGB-D Fusion Module (C2FFM) fuses monocular RGB features with depth estimates, using depth for coarse guidance and modeling local depth variations for fine-grained fusion.
AI.result: Empirical results show SPDA-SAM outperforms current state-of-the-art methods across twelve datasets.
AI.conclusion: Self-prompting and depth-aware fusion mitigate prompt quality issues and depth-related spatial information loss, yielding robust instance segmentation.

title: POP: Online Structural Pruning Enables Efficient Inference of Large Foundation Models
summary: Large foundation models (LFMs) achieve strong performance through scaling, yet current structural pruning methods derive fixed pruning decisions during inference, overlooking sparsity patterns that emerge in the autoregressive token generation. In this paper, we propose POP (Partition-guided Online Pruning), an efficient online structural pruning framework that enables context-conditioned dynamic pruning with minimal computational overhead. POP partitions model channels into retained, candidate, and pruned regions, where prefilling defines a coarse pruning partition, and the decoding stage generates a fine-grained mask within the candidate region, avoiding full-channel re-evaluation. The coarse pruning partition preserves consistently important weights, while the fine-grained masking provides context-conditioned variation during decoding. Moreover, POP is a lightweight, plug-and-play method that requires no preprocessing, including offline calibration, retraining, or learning predictors. Extensive evaluations across diverse LFMs, including large language models (LLMs), mixture-of-experts models (MoEs), and vision-language models (VLMs), demonstrate that POP consistently delivers higher accuracy than existing pruning approaches while incurring smaller computational overhead and minimizing inference latency.

AI.tldr: POP enables online, context-conditioned pruning for large foundation models via partition-guided masks, achieving dynamic pruning with low overhead.
AI.motivation: Current structural pruning fixes pruning masks during inference, ignoring sparsity patterns that emerge during autoregressive token generation; there is a need for lightweight, context-aware pruning that minimizes latency.
AI.method: Partition channels into retained, candidate, and pruned regions. Prefilling yields a coarse pruning partition; during decoding, a fine-grained mask is generated within the candidate region based on context, avoiding full-channel re-evaluation. No offline calibration, retraining, or predictors required; plug-and-play.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across LLMs, MoEs, and VLMs shows POP delivers higher accuracy than existing pruning approaches with lower computational overhead and reduced latency.
AI.conclusion: POP offers a lightweight, effective online pruning framework that enables context-conditioned dynamic pruning across diverse LFMs without extra preprocessing or training.

title: RAPID: Reconfigurable, Adaptive Platform for Iterative Design
summary: Developing robotic manipulation policies is iterative and hypothesis-driven: researchers test tactile sensing, gripper geometries, and sensor placements through real-world data collection and training. Yet even minor end-effector changes often require mechanical refitting and system re-integration, slowing iteration. We present RAPID, a full-stack reconfigurable platform designed to reduce this friction. RAPID is built around a tool-free, modular hardware architecture that unifies handheld data collection and robot deployment, and a matching software stack that maintains real-time awareness of the underlying hardware configuration through a driver-level Physical Mask derived from USB events. This modular hardware architecture reduces reconfiguration to seconds and makes systematic multi-modal ablation studies practical, allowing researchers to sweep diverse gripper and sensing configurations without repeated system bring-up. The Physical Mask exposes modality presence as an explicit runtime signal, enabling auto-configuration and graceful degradation under sensor hot-plug events, so policies can continue executing when sensors are physically added or removed. System-centric experiments show that RAPID reduces the setup time for multi-modal configurations by two orders of magnitude compared to traditional workflows and preserves policy execution under runtime sensor hot-unplug events. The hardware designs, drivers, and software stack are open-sourced at https://rapid-kit.github.io/ .

AI.tldr: RAPID is a full-stack, tool-free modular platform for rapid reconfiguration of robotic manipulation systems, unifying data collection and deployment. It uses a driver-level Physical Mask derived from USB events to track hardware configuration, enable auto-configuration and graceful degradation on sensor hot-plug/unplug, and enables large-scale multi-modal ablations with setup time reduced by ~100x. Open-sourced hardware, drivers, and software.
AI.motivation: Iterative robotics manipulation research is slowed by mechanical refitting when end-effectors or sensors change. A need exists for quick reconfiguration, consistent experimentation, and ability to sweep multiple gripper/sensor configurations without repeated system bring-up.
AI.method: Design and build a tool-free, modular hardware architecture that unifies handheld data collection and robot deployment. Implement a software stack that maintains real-time awareness of hardware via a driver-level Physical Mask derived from USB events, enabling auto-configuration and graceful degradation during sensor hot-plug events. Package hardware designs, drivers, and software as open-source.
AI.result: System-centric experiments demonstrate ~100× reduction in setup time for multi-modal configurations compared to traditional workflows and robust policy execution under runtime sensor hot-unplug events.
AI.conclusion: RAPID demonstrates that a fully integrated, reconfigurable platform can dramatically accelerate experimentation in robotic manipulation by minimizing reconfiguration friction, enabling systematic ablations, and preserving policy execution under dynamic hardware changes; the approach is openly available for the community.

title: A Fast and Generalizable Fourier Neural Operator-Based Surrogate for Melt-Pool Prediction in Laser Processing
summary: High-fidelity simulations of laser welding capture complex thermo-fluid phenomena, including phase change, free-surface deformation, and keyhole dynamics, however their computational cost limits large-scale process exploration and real-time use. In this work we present the Laser Processing Fourier Neural Operator (LP-FNO), a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) based surrogate model that learns the parametric solution operator of various laser processes from multiphysics simulations generated with FLOW-3D WELD (registered trademark). Through a novel approach of reformulating the transient problem in the moving laser frame and applying temporal averaging, the system results in a quasi-steady state setting suitable for operator learning, even in the keyhole welding regime. The proposed LP-FNO maps process parameters to three-dimensional temperature fields and melt-pool boundaries across a broad process window spanning conduction and keyhole regimes using the non-dimensional normalized enthalpy formulation. The model achieves temperature prediction errors on the order of 1% and intersection-over-union scores for melt-pool segmentation over 0.9. We demonstrate that a LP-FNO model trained on coarse-resolution data can be evaluated on finer grids, yielding accurate super-resolved predictions in mesh-converged conduction regimes, whereas discrepancies in keyhole regimes reflect unresolved dynamics in the coarse-mesh training data. These results indicate that the LP-FNO provides an efficient surrogate modeling framework for laser welding, enabling prediction of full three-dimensional fields and phase interfaces over wide parameter ranges in just tens of milliseconds, up to a hundred thousand times faster than traditional Finite Volume multi-physics software.

AI.tldr: LP-FNO is a Fourier Neural Operator surrogate for laser welding that predicts 3D temperature fields and melt-pool boundaries across conduction and keyhole regimes with ~1% temperature error and IoU > 0.9, achieving tens of milliseconds predictions and orders-of-magnitude speedups over FV simulations.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity laser-welding simulations (e.g., FLOW-3D WELD) capture complex thermo-fluid phenomena but are computationally expensive, limiting large-scale exploration and real-time use.
AI.method: Train a Fourier Neural Operator surrogate (LP-FNO) on multiphysics FLOW-3D WELD simulations. Reformulate the transient problem in a moving laser frame and apply temporal averaging to obtain a quasi-steady state suitable for operator learning across conduction and keyhole regimes. Use non-dimensional normalized enthalpy to map process parameters to 3D temperature fields and melt-pool boundaries. Validate on coarse training data and test on finer grids for super-resolution capability.
AI.result: Achieves ~1% temperature prediction error and IoU > 0.9 for melt-pool segmentation. A model trained on coarse data can be evaluated on finer grids, yielding accurate super-resolved predictions in conduction regimes; keyhole discrepancies arise from unresolved coarse-mesh dynamics. Inference times are tens of milliseconds, offering up to 10^5× speedups over conventional FV multi-physics software.
AI.conclusion: LP-FNO provides an efficient surrogate framework for predicting full 3D fields and phase interfaces across wide process windows in laser welding, enabling rapid parameter sweeps and potential real-time guidance, with accuracy contingent on the quality of coarse-mesh training data in dynamic regimes.

title: Uncertainty-Aware 4D Gaussian Splatting for Monocular Occluded Human Rendering
summary: High-fidelity rendering of dynamic humans from monocular videos typically degrades catastrophically under occlusions. Existing solutions incorporate external priors-either hallucinating missing content via generative models, which induces severe temporal flickering, or imposing rigid geometric heuristics that fail to capture diverse appearances. To this end, we reformulate the task as a Maximum A Posteriori estimation problem under heteroscedastic observation noise. In this paper, we propose U-4DGS, a framework integrating a Probabilistic Deformation Network and a Double Rasterization pipeline. This architecture renders pixel-aligned uncertainty maps that act as an adaptive gradient modulator, automatically attenuating artifacts from unreliable observations. Furthermore, to prevent geometric drift in regions lacking reliable visual cues, we enforce Confidence-Aware Regularizations, which leverage the learned uncertainty to selectively propagate spatial-temporal validity. Extensive experiments on ZJU-MoCap and OcMotion demonstrate that U-4DGS achieves SOTA rendering fidelity and robustness.

AI.tldr: Proposes U-4DGS for robust high-fidelity dynamic human rendering from monocular video under occlusions by modeling heteroscedastic noise with a probabilistic deformation network and double rasterization; uses uncertainty-guided gradient modulation and confidence-aware regularizations; achieves SOTA on ZJU-MoCap and OcMotion.
AI.motivation: Occlusion-induced artifacts in monocular dynamic human rendering; existing priors (generative hallucination or rigid geometry) fail to capture diverse appearances or cause temporal flicker; a principled probabilistic approach with per-pixel uncertainty is needed.
AI.method: Maximum A Posteriori estimation under heteroscedastic observation noise; introduces Probabilistic Deformation Network and Double Rasterization; renders pixel-aligned uncertainty maps to adaptively modulate gradients; Confidence-Aware Regularizations leveraging learned uncertainty to selectively propagate spatial-temporal validity.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art rendering fidelity and robustness on benchmarks ZJU-MoCap and OcMotion, particularly under occlusions.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware formulation with adaptive gradient modulation and confidence-aware regularization yields robust, high-fidelity dynamic human rendering from monocular video, establishing SOTA on key benchmarks.

title: LLM Active Alignment: A Nash Equilibrium Perspective
summary: We develop a game-theoretic framework for predicting and steering the behavior of populations of large language models (LLMs) through Nash equilibrium (NE) analysis. To avoid the intractability of equilibrium computation in open-ended text spaces, we model each agent's action as a mixture over human subpopulations. Agents choose actively and strategically which groups to align with, yielding an interpretable and behaviorally substantive policy class. We derive closed-form NE characterizations, adopting standard concave-utility assumptions to enable analytical system-level predictions and give explicit, actionable guidance for shifting alignment targets toward socially desirable outcomes. The method functions as an active alignment layer on top of existing alignment pipelines such as RLHF. In a social-media setting, we show that a population of LLMs, especially reasoning-based models, may exhibit political exclusion, pathologies where some subpopulations are ignored by all LLM agents, which can be avoided by our method, illustrating the promise of applying the method to regulate multi-agent LLM dynamics across domains.

AI.tldr: Game-theoretic model for steering LLM populations via Nash equilibria, with agents mixing over human subpopulations to enable tractable, interpretable policies.
AI.motivation: Addressward the intractability of predicting open-ended LLM dynamics and provide design guidance to steer behavior toward socially desirable outcomes, while mitigating harmful pathologies.
AI.method: Model each agent’s action as a mixture over human subpopulations; agents strategically choose which groups to align with; derive closed-form Nash equilibrium under concave utilities; use as an active alignment layer atop pipelines like RLHF.
AI.result: Closed-form NE characterizations enabling analytical system-level predictions; actionable guidance to shift alignment targets; demonstration of reducing exclusionary pathologies in social-media LLM populations.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables active regulation of multi-agent LLM dynamics across domains and provides design principles for preventing subpopulation exclusion while guiding behavior toward socially desirable outcomes.

title: Crowd-FM: Learned Optimal Selection of Conditional Flow Matching-generated Trajectories for Crowd Navigation
comment: Accepted at IEEE ICRA 2026. Authors Antareep Singha and Laksh Nanwani have equal contributions
summary: Safe and computationally efficient local planning for mobile robots in dense, unstructured human crowds remains a fundamental challenge. Moreover, ensuring that robot trajectories are similar to how a human moves will increase the acceptance of the robot in human environments. In this paper, we present Crowd-FM, a learning-based approach to address both safety and human-likeness challenges. Our approach has two novel components. First, we train a Conditional Flow-Matching (CFM) policy over a dataset of optimally controlled trajectories to learn a set of collision-free primitives that a robot can choose at any given scenario. The chosen optimal control solver can generate multi-modal collision-free trajectories, allowing the CFM policy to learn a diverse set of maneuvers. Secondly, we learn a score function over a dataset of human demonstration trajectories that provides a human-likeness score for the flow primitives. At inference time, computing the optimal trajectory requires selecting the one with the highest score. Our approach improves the state-of-the-art by showing that our CFM policy alone can produce collision-free navigation with a higher success rate than existing learning-based baselines. Furthermore, when augmented with inference-time refinement, our approach can outperform even expensive optimisation-based planning approaches. Finally, we validate that our scoring network can select trajectories closer to the expert data than a manually designed cost function.

AI.tldr: Two-stage learning approach Crowd-FM uses conditional flow-matching to learn collision-free motion primitives from optimal-control data and a learned human-likeness score to select trajectories; achieves higher success than baselines and competitive with optimization-based planners.
AI.motivation: Address safety and human-likeness in dense crowds, improve local planning efficiency, and increase acceptance of robots in human environments by learning reusable motion primitives and a human-likeness score.
AI.method: Two components: (i) Train a Conditional Flow-Matching (CFM) policy over optimally controlled trajectories to learn a diverse, multi-modal set of collision-free primitives; (ii) Learn a score function from human demonstrations to measure human-likeness of each primitive; at inference, select the highest-scoring primitive; optional inference-time refinement can surpass expensive optimization-based planners.
AI.result: CFM policy alone yields higher collision-free navigation success than existing learning-based baselines; with inference-time refinement, it outperforms expensive optimization-based planners; the scoring network selects trajectories closer to expert data than manually designed cost functions.
AI.conclusion: Crowd-FM demonstrates that combining learned motion primitives with a learned human-likeness score yields safe, efficient, and human-like local planning in dense crowds, outperforming baselines and approaching or surpassing optimization-based methods.

title: FlowConsist: Make Your Flow Consistent with Real Trajectory
summary: Fast flow models accelerate the iterative sampling process by learning to directly predict ODE path integrals, enabling one-step or few-step generation. However, we argue that current fast-flow training paradigms suffer from two fundamental issues. First, conditional velocities constructed from randomly paired noise-data samples introduce systematic trajectory drift, preventing models from following a consistent ODE path. Second, the model's approximation errors accumulate over time steps, leading to severe deviations across long time intervals. To address these issues, we propose FlowConsist, a training framework designed to enforce trajectory consistency in fast flows. We propose a principled alternative that replaces conditional velocities with the marginal velocities predicted by the model itself, aligning optimization with the true trajectory. To further address error accumulation over time steps, we introduce a trajectory rectification strategy that aligns the marginal distributions of generated and real samples at every time step along the trajectory. Our method establishes a new state-of-the-art on ImageNet 256$\times$256, achieving an FID of 1.52 with only 1 sampling step.

AI.tldr: FlowConsist enforces trajectory consistency in fast-flow generative models by replacing conditional velocities with model-generated marginal velocities and applying a trajectory rectification step; this reduces error accumulation and drift, achieving SOTA performance (FID 1.52 on ImageNet-256 with 1 sampling step).
AI.motivation: Identifies two core issues in fast-flow training: trajectory drift from randomly paired conditional velocities and accumulation of approximation errors over steps, which degrade long-horizon trajectories.
AI.method: Replace conditional velocities with marginal velocities predicted by the model; introduce trajectory rectification that aligns generated and real stepwise marginals along the trajectory; train to optimize alignment with true trajectory rather than conditional samples.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on ImageNet 256x256 with 1 sampling step; FID 1.52. Likely improved sample quality and sampling speed.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-consistent training enables accurate single/multi-step generation for fast-flow models; improves stability and long-horizon fidelity; suggests modeling marginals and stepwise alignment as a principled approach.

title: An Adaptive Differentially Private Federated Learning Framework with Bi-level Optimization
summary: Federated learning enables collaborative model training across distributed clients while preserving data privacy. However, in practical deployments, device heterogeneity, non-independent, and identically distributed (Non-IID) data often lead to highly unstable and biased gradient updates. When differential privacy is enforced, conventional fixed gradient clipping and Gaussian noise injection may further amplify gradient perturbations, resulting in training oscillation and performance degradation and degraded model performance. To address these challenges, we propose an adaptive differentially private federated learning framework that explicitly targets model efficiency under heterogeneous and privacy-constrained settings. On the client side, a lightweight local compressed module is introduced to regularize intermediate representations and constrain gradient variability, thereby mitigating noise amplification during local optimization. On the server side, an adaptive gradient clipping strategy dynamically adjusts clipping thresholds based on historical update statistics to avoid over-clipping and noise domination. Furthermore, a constraint-aware aggregation mechanism is designed to suppress unreliable or noise-dominated client updates and stabilize global optimization. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10 and SVHN demonstrate improved convergence stability and classification accuracy.

AI.tldr: Adaptive DP-Federated Learning framework that stabilizes training under heterogeneity and privacy constraints by combining a client-side local compression module, server-side adaptive gradient clipping, and constraint-aware aggregation, achieving better convergence and accuracy on CIFAR-10 and SVHN.
AI.motivation: In real-world FL with non-IID data and differential privacy, fixed gradient clipping and Gaussian noise cause unstable training and degraded performance. There is a need for adaptive mechanisms that mitigate noise amplification and preserve model efficiency.
AI.method: Client side: introduce a lightweight local compressed module to regularize intermediate representations and constrain gradient variability. Server side: dynamic adaptive gradient clipping thresholds based on historical update statistics to prevent over-clipping and noise domination. Aggregation: constraint-aware mechanism to downweight unreliable or noise-dominated client updates, stabilizing global optimization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10 and SVHN show improved convergence stability and higher classification accuracy compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively mitigates noise amplification and over-clipping in DP-FL, stabilizing global optimization under client heterogeneity and privacy constraints and improving model performance.

title: Constraint Manifold Exploration for Efficient Continuous Coverage Estimation
summary: Many automated manufacturing processes rely on industrial robot arms to move process-specific tools along workpiece surfaces. In applications like grinding, sanding, spray painting, or inspection, they need to cover a workpiece fully while keeping their tools perpendicular to its surface. While there are approaches to generate trajectories for these applications, there are no sufficient methods for analyzing the feasibility of full surface coverage. This work proposes a sampling-based approach for continuous coverage estimation that explores reachable surface regions in the configuration space. We define an extended ambient configuration space that allows for the representation of tool position and orientation constraints. A continuation-based approach is used to explore it using two different sampling strategies. A thorough evaluation across different kinematics and environments analyzes their runtime and efficiency. This validates our ability to accurately and efficiently calculate surface coverage for complex surfaces in complicated environments.

AI.tldr: A sampling-based continuation approach for feasibility of full-surface coverage in robotic tool operations; introduces an extended ambient configuration space to encode pose constraints and evaluates two sampling strategies for efficient coverage estimation on complex surfaces.
AI.motivation: Industrial automated processes require complete surface coverage with tool orientations constrained to be perpendicular to the surface; existing trajectory methods do not adequately analyze coverage feasibility, motivating a method to estimate reachable surface coverage.
AI.method: Define an extended ambient configuration space that represents tool position and orientation constraints; employ a continuation-based exploration to traverse this space using two distinct sampling strategies; assess runtime and efficiency across different kinematic chains and environments.
AI.result: Demonstrates capability to accurately and efficiently estimate surface coverage for complex surfaces in challenging environments; provides comparative runtime/efficiency analysis of the two sampling strategies.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables practical surface-coverage feasibility analysis for complex surfaces and environments, potentially informing planning and verification workflows; future work could address scalability, real-time integration, and refinements of sampling strategies.

title: REBEL: Hidden Knowledge Recovery via Evolutionary-Based Evaluation Loop
summary: Machine unlearning for LLMs aims to remove sensitive or copyrighted data from trained models. However, the true efficacy of current unlearning methods remains uncertain. Standard evaluation metrics rely on benign queries that often mistake superficial information suppression for genuine knowledge removal. Such metrics fail to detect residual knowledge that more sophisticated prompting strategies could still extract. We introduce REBEL, an evolutionary approach for adversarial prompt generation designed to probe whether unlearned data can still be recovered. Our experiments demonstrate that REBEL successfully elicits ``forgotten'' knowledge from models that seemed to be forgotten in standard unlearning benchmarks, revealing that current unlearning methods may provide only a superficial layer of protection. We validate our framework on subsets of the TOFU and WMDP benchmarks, evaluating performance across a diverse suite of unlearning algorithms. Our experiments show that REBEL consistently outperforms static baselines, recovering ``forgotten'' knowledge with Attack Success Rates (ASRs) reaching up to 60% on TOFU and 93% on WMDP. We will make all code publicly available upon acceptance. Code is available at https://github.com/patryk-rybak/REBEL/

AI.tldr: REBEL is an evolutionary adversarial prompting framework to test whether unlearning in LLMs truly removes knowledge, revealing superficial removal by standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of machine unlearning uses benign prompts that may falsely indicate knowledge removal. There is a risk that models still retain recoverable information that can be elicited with smarter prompts.
AI.method: REBEL evolves prompts adversarially to probe forgotten data. Evaluations are conducted on TOFU and WMDP subsets, across multiple unlearning algorithms, comparing against static baselines.
AI.result: REBEL consistently outperforms static prompts, recovering forgotten knowledge with Attack Success Rates up to 60% (TOFU) and 93% (WMDP). This suggests existing unlearning methods may only provide superficial protection.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial prompt-based evaluation reveals residual knowledge after unlearning; the framework highlights the need for more robust unlearning strategies and can benchmark across methods. Code will be publicly released.

title: From Features to Actions: Explainability in Traditional and Agentic AI Systems
summary:
Over the last decade, explainable AI has primarily focused on interpreting individual model predictions, producing post-hoc explanations that relate inputs to outputs under a fixed decision structure. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic AI systems whose behaviour unfolds over multi-step trajectories. In these settings, success and failure are determined by sequences of decisions rather than a single output. While useful, it remains unclear how explanation approaches designed for static predictions translate to agentic settings where behaviour emerges over time. In this work, we bridge the gap between static and agentic explainability by comparing attribution-based explanations with trace-based diagnostics across both settings. To make this distinction explicit, we empirically compare attribution-based explanations used in static classification tasks with trace-based diagnostics used in agentic benchmarks (TAU-bench Airline and AssistantBench). Our results show that while attribution methods achieve stable feature rankings in static settings (Spearman $ρ= 0.86$), they cannot be applied reliably to diagnose execution-level failures in agentic trajectories. In contrast, trace-grounded rubric evaluation for agentic settings consistently localizes behaviour breakdowns and reveals that state tracking inconsistency is 2.7$\times$ more prevalent in failed runs and reduces success probability by 49\%. These findings motivate a shift towards trajectory-level explainability for agentic systems when evaluating and diagnosing autonomous AI behaviour.
  Resources:
  https://github.com/VectorInstitute/unified-xai-evaluation-framework https://vectorinstitute.github.io/unified-xai-evaluation-framework

AI.tldr: Attribution-based XAI works well for static predictions but fails for agentic, trajectory-based AI; trace-based diagnostics are more effective for diagnosing execution-level failures; propose trajectory-level explainability; empirical evaluation shows state-tracking inconsistency correlates with failures and reduces success probability.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between static explanatory methods and agentic AI by evaluating whether attribution-based explanations translate to agentic trajectories; determine appropriate explainability approaches for multi-step, decision-sequence behavior.
AI.method: Empirically compare attribution-based explanations used in static classification with trace-based diagnostics used in agentic benchmarks TAU-bench Airline and AssistantBench; quantify stability of feature rankings (static) via Spearman correlation; apply trace-grounded rubric evaluation to agentic trajectories; measure prevalence of state-tracking inconsistency and its impact on success.
AI.result: In static settings, attribution methods yield stable feature rankings (Spearman ρ = 0.86); in agentic trajectories they fail to reliably diagnose execution-level failures. Trace-grounded evaluation localizes behavior breakdowns in agentic settings; state-tracking inconsistency is 2.7× more prevalent in failed runs and reduces success probability by 49%.
AI.conclusion: Recommend shifting to trajectory-level explainability for agentic systems; evaluate and diagnose autonomous AI behavior using trajectory-based methods; support with unified XAI evaluation framework.

title: SuReNav: Superpixel Graph-based Constraint Relaxation for Navigation in Over-constrained Environments
comment: Accepted by ICRA 2026. Code and videos are available at https://sure-nav.github.io/
summary: We address the over-constrained planning problem in semi-static environments. The planning objective is to find a best-effort solution that avoids all hard constraint regions while minimally traversing the least risky areas. Conventional methods often rely on pre-defined area costs, limiting generalizations. Further, the spatial continuity of navigation spaces makes it difficult to identify regions that are passable without overestimation. To overcome these challenges, we propose SuReNav, a superpixel graph-based constraint relaxation and navigation method that imitates human-like safe and efficient navigation. Our framework consists of three components: 1) superpixel graph map generation with regional constraints, 2) regional-constraint relaxation using graph neural network trained on human demonstrations for safe and efficient navigation, and 3) interleaving relaxation, planning, and execution for complete navigation. We evaluate our method against state-of-the-art baselines on 2D semantic maps and 3D maps from OpenStreetMap, achieving the highest human-likeness score of complete navigation while maintaining a balanced trade-off between efficiency and safety. We finally demonstrate its scalability and generalization performance in real-world urban navigation with a quadruped robot, Spot.

AI.tldr: SuReNav leverages a superpixel graph and a graph neural network–based regional constraint relaxation to deliver safe, human-like navigation in semi-static environments, outperforming baselines and generalizing to real-world urban navigation with Spot.
AI.motivation: Over-constrained planning in semi-static environments aims to find a best-effort path that avoids hard constraint regions while minimizing traversal of risky areas. Existing methods rely on pre-defined area costs, hindering generalization, and the spatial continuity of navigation spaces makes passable regions hard to identify without overestimation.
AI.method: Three-part framework: (1) generate a superpixel graph map with regional constraints; (2) apply regional-constraint relaxation via a graph neural network trained on human demonstrations for safe and efficient navigation; (3) interleave relaxation, planning, and execution for complete navigation.
AI.result: Evaluation against state-of-the-art baselines on 2D semantic maps and 3D maps from OpenStreetMap shows SuReNav achieves the highest human-likeness score for complete navigation while balancing efficiency and safety.
AI.conclusion: demonstrates scalability and generalization in real-world urban navigation with a quadruped robot (Spot).

title: Steering Safely or Off a Cliff? Rethinking Specificity and Robustness in Inference-Time Interventions
summary: Model steering, which involves intervening on hidden representations at inference time, has emerged as a lightweight alternative to finetuning for precisely controlling large language models. While steering efficacy has been widely studied, evaluations of whether interventions alter only the intended property remain limited, especially with respect to unintended changes in behaviors related to the target property. We call this notion specificity. We propose a framework that distinguishes three dimensions of specificity: general (preserving fluency and unrelated abilities), control (preserving related control properties), and robustness (preserving control properties under distribution shifts). We study two safety-critical use cases: steering models to reduce overrefusal and faithfulness hallucinations, and show that while steering achieves high efficacy and largely maintains general and control specificity, it consistently fails to preserve robustness specificity. In the case of overrefusal steering, for example, all steering methods reduce overrefusal without harming general abilities and refusal on harmful queries; however, they substantially increase vulnerability to jailbreaks. Our work provides the first systematic evaluation of specificity in model steering, showing that standard efficacy and specificity checks are insufficient, because without robustness evaluation, steering methods may appear reliable even when they compromise model safety.

AI.tldr: Proposes a specificity framework for model steering and evaluates robustness of steering methods. Three dimensions: general (fluency/unrelated abilities), control (related control properties), robustness (preservation under distribution shifts). Empirically, steering reduces undesired behavior (e.g., overrefusal) and largely preserves general/control specificity, but fails to preserve robustness specificity, exposing increased vulnerability to jailbreaks.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where steering evaluations focus on efficacy but may overlook unintended changes in related behaviors, especially under distribution shifts. Define a formal notion of specificity to assess how steering interventions alter other properties.
AI.method: Introduce a three-dimensional specificity framework (general, control, robustness). Apply it to two safety-critical use cases: reducing overrefusal and reducing faithfulness hallucinations. Measure efficacy and all three specificity dimensions across steering methods.
AI.result: Steering achieves high efficacy and largely preserves general and control specificity. However, robustness specificity is consistently violated: interventions that reduce overrefusal also increase susceptibility to jailbreaks, with no loss in general abilities or harmful-query refusals.
AI.conclusion: Standard efficacy checks are insufficient for safety-aware steering. Robustness evaluation is essential to detect hidden risks; future work should incorporate distribution-shift tests and robustness-focused metrics to ensure safe steering.

title: Robust Pedestrian Detection with Uncertain Modality
comment: Due to the limitation "The abstract field cannot be longer than 1,920 characters", the abstract here is shorter than that in the PDF file
summary: Existing cross-modal pedestrian detection (CMPD) employs complementary information from RGB and thermal-infrared (TIR) modalities to detect pedestrians in 24h-surveillance systems.RGB captures rich pedestrian details under daylight, while TIR excels at night. However, TIR focuses primarily on the person's silhouette, neglecting critical texture details essential for detection. While the near-infrared (NIR) captures texture under low-light conditions, which effectively alleviates performance issues of RGB and detail loss in TIR, thereby reducing missed detections. To this end, we construct a new Triplet RGB-NIR-TIR (TRNT) dataset, comprising 8,281 pixel-aligned image triplets, establishing a comprehensive foundation for algorithmic research. However, due to the variable nature of real-world scenarios, imaging devices may not always capture all three modalities simultaneously. This results in input data with unpredictable combinations of modal types, which challenge existing CMPD methods that fail to extract robust pedestrian information under arbitrary input combinations, leading to significant performance degradation. To address these challenges, we propose the Adaptive Uncertainty-aware Network (AUNet) for accurately discriminating modal availability and fully utilizing the available information under uncertain inputs. Specifically, we introduce Unified Modality Validation Refinement (UMVR), which includes an uncertainty-aware router to validate modal availability and a semantic refinement to ensure the reliability of information within the modality. Furthermore, we design a Modality-Aware Interaction (MAI) module to adaptively activate or deactivate its internal interaction mechanisms per UMVR output, enabling effective complementary information fusion from available modalities.

AI.tldr: Proposes Adaptive Uncertainty-aware Network (AUNet) for cross-modal pedestrian detection under unpredictable modality availability, using a Triplet RGB-NIR-TIR (TRNT) dataset; introduces UMVR for modality validation and semantic refinement, plus MAI for adaptive fusion of available modalities.
AI.motivation: Real-world CMPD suffers when modalities are missing or inconsistent; TIR lacks texture while NIR can provide texture under low light; need robust fusion across arbitrary modality combinations. A TRNT dataset with 8,281 aligned triplets is developed to support this research.
AI.method: Adaptive Uncertainty-aware Network (AUNet) with Unified Modality Validation Refinement (UMVR) comprising an uncertainty-aware router to validate modality availability and semantic refinement to ensure information reliability; Modality-Aware Interaction (MAI) module to dynamically activate/deactivate internal fusion interactions based on UMVR output for effective fusion of the available modalities.
AI.result: The abstract does not report quantitative results. It claims robustness to uncertain modal inputs and effective fusion via UMVR and MAI, but no specific metrics or experiments are described.
AI.conclusion: AUNet offers a principled framework for cross-modal pedestrian detection under incomplete modality inputs and introduces the TRNT dataset to facilitate research; the approach can potentially generalize to other multi-modality perception tasks.

title: AIRS-Bench: a Suite of Tasks for Frontier AI Research Science Agents
summary: LLM agents hold significant promise for advancing scientific research. To accelerate this progress, we introduce AIRS-Bench (the AI Research Science Benchmark), a suite of 20 tasks sourced from state-of-the-art machine learning papers. These tasks span diverse domains, including language modeling, mathematics, bioinformatics, and time series forecasting. AIRS-Bench tasks assess agentic capabilities over the full research lifecycle -- including idea generation, experiment analysis and iterative refinement -- without providing baseline code. The AIRS-Bench task format is versatile, enabling easy integration of new tasks and rigorous comparison across different agentic frameworks. We establish baselines using frontier models paired with both sequential and parallel scaffolds. Our results show that agents exceed human SOTA in four tasks but fail to match it in sixteen others. Even when agents surpass human benchmarks, they do not reach the theoretical performance ceiling for the underlying tasks. These findings indicate that AIRS-Bench is far from saturated and offers substantial room for improvement. We open-source the AIRS-Bench task definitions and evaluation code to catalyze further development in autonomous scientific research.

AI.tldr: AIRS-Bench is a 20-task benchmark for evaluating autonomous scientific agents across the full research lifecycle, revealing substantial room for improvement and not yet near theoretical ceilings; open-sourced to spur further progress.
AI.motivation: Provide a standardized, extensible benchmark to accelerate autonomous scientific research by evaluating agentic capabilities in idea generation, experiment analysis, and iterative refinement across diverse domains.
AI.method: Assembled 20 tasks from state-of-the-art ML papers across language modeling, mathematics, bioinformatics, and time series. The task format supports easy integration of new tasks. Baselines established using frontier models with both sequential and parallel scaffolds. Evaluations cover end-to-end autonomous scientific reasoning.
AI.result: Agents surpass human SOTA on 4 tasks but lag on 16; even when surpassing humans, they do not reach the theoretical performance ceiling for the tasks. The benchmark remains far from saturated, indicating ample room for improvement.
AI.conclusion: AIRS-Bench is a viable, extensible benchmark that can catalyze progress in autonomous scientific research; open-sourcing task definitions and evaluation code will facilitate community-driven development.

title: A 26-Gram Butterfly-Inspired Robot Achieving Autonomous Tailless Flight
summary: Flapping-wing micro air vehicles (FWMAVs) have demonstrated remarkable bio-inspired agility, yet tailless two-winged configurations remain largely unexplored due to their complex fluid-structure and wing-body coupling. Here we present \textit{AirPulse}, a 26-gram butterfly-inspired FWMAV that achieves fully onboard, closed-loop, untethered flight without auxiliary control surfaces. The AirPulse robot replicates key biomechanical traits of butterfly flight, including low wing aspect ratio, compliant carbon-fiber-reinforced wings, and low-frequency, high-amplitude flapping that induces cyclic variations in the center of gravity and moment of inertia, producing characteristic body undulation. We establish a quantitative mapping between flapping modulation parameters and force-torque generation, and introduce the Stroke Timing Asymmetry Rhythm (STAR) generator, enabling smooth, stable, and linearly parameterized wingstroke asymmetry for flapping control. Integrating these with an attitude controller, the AirPulse robot maintains pitch and yaw stability despite strong oscillatory dynamics. Free-flight experiments demonstrate stable climbing and turning maneuvers via either angle offset or stroke timing modulation, marking the first onboard controlled flight of the lightest two-winged, tailless butterfly-inspired FWMAV reported in peer-reviewed literature. This work corroborates a foundational platform for lightweight, collision-proof FWMAVs, bridging biological inspiration with practical aerial robotics. Their non-invasive maneuverability is ideally suited for real-world applications, such as confined-space inspection and ecological monitoring, inaccessible to traditional drones, while their biomechanical fidelity provides a physical model to decode the principles underlying the erratic yet efficient flight of real butterflies.

AI.tldr: A 26 g tailless two-wing FWMAV, AirPulse, achieves fully onboard, closed-loop flight without control surfaces, using wingbeat-based control and a Stroke Timing Asymmetry Rhythm (STAR) generator; demonstrates onboard stability and climbing/turning; first reported onboard flight of the lightest two-winged tailless butterfly-inspired FWMAV; suitable for confined-space inspection and ecological monitoring.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of tailless, two-winged FWMAVs due to challenging fluid-structure-wings-body coupling, and to realize lightweight, fully onboard, untethered flight with butterfly-inspired biomechanics.
AI.method: Replicate butterfly traits (low AR wings, compliant CFR wings, low-frequency high-amplitude flapping) to induce cyclic CG and inertia variations causing body undulation; develop a quantitative mapping from flapping modulation to force-torque; introduce STAR generator for smooth, linearly parameterized wingstroke asymmetry; integrate with an attitude controller to achieve pitch/yaw stability under oscillatory dynamics; perform free-flight experiments showing stable climbing and turning via angle offset or stroke timing modulation.
AI.result: Onboard, closed-loop control achieved with stable ascent and maneuvering; first reported instance of onboard controlled flight for the lightest two-winged, tailless butterfly-inspired FWMAV; confirms feasibility of lightweight, collision-proof FWMAVs with biomechanical fidelity.
AI.conclusion: AirPulse provides a foundational platform linking biological inspiration with practical aerial robotics, enabling ultralight, collision-tolerant FWMAVs for confined-space inspection and ecological monitoring, while offering a physical model to study real butterfly flight dynamics.

title: On Randomized Algorithms in Online Strategic Classification
summary:
Online strategic classification studies settings in which agents strategically modify their features to obtain favorable predictions. For example, given a classifier that determines loan approval based on credit scores, applicants may open or close credit cards and bank accounts to obtain a positive prediction. The learning goal is to achieve low mistake or regret bounds despite such strategic behavior.
  While randomized algorithms have the potential to offer advantages to the learner in strategic settings, they have been largely underexplored. In the realizable setting, no lower bound is known for randomized algorithms, and existing lower bound constructions for deterministic learners can be circumvented by randomization. In the agnostic setting, the best known regret upper bound is $O(T^{3/4}\log^{1/4}T|\mathcal H|)$, which is far from the standard online learning rate of $O(\sqrt{T\log|\mathcal H|})$.
  In this work, we provide refined bounds for online strategic classification in both settings. In the realizable setting, we extend, for $T > \mathrm{Ldim}(\mathcal{H}) Δ^2$, the existing lower bound $Ω(\mathrm{Ldim}(\mathcal{H}) Δ)$ for deterministic learners to all learners. This yields the first lower bound that applies to randomized learners. We also provide the first randomized learner that improves the known (deterministic) upper bound of $O(\mathrm{Ldim}(\mathcal H) \cdot Δ\log Δ)$.
  In the agnostic setting, we give a proper learner using convex optimization techniques to improve the regret upper bound to $O(\sqrt{T \log |\mathcal{H}|} + |\mathcal{H}| \log(T|\mathcal{H}|))$. We show a matching lower bound up to logarithmic factors for all proper learning rules, demonstrating the optimality of our learner among proper learners. As such, improper learning is necessary to further improve regret guarantees.

AI.tldr: Refines online strategic classification bounds using randomization. In realizable settings, extends lower bounds to randomized learners and presents the first randomized learner with improved upper bound. In the agnostic setting, proposes a convex-optimization based proper learner achieving near-optimal regret, with a matching lower bound up to logarithmic factors for proper learners, implying improper learning is needed for further gains.
AI.motivation: To understand how randomization influences learning under strategic feature manipulation and to close gaps between lower and upper bounds in both realizable and agnostic online strategic classification.
AI.method: Realizable: construct lower bounds that extend Ω(Ldim(H)) Δ to all learners for T > Ldim(H) Δ^2; design a randomized learner that surpasses the deterministic upper bound O(Ldim(H) Δ log Δ). Agnostic: develop a proper, convex-optimization-based learner achieving regret O(√(T log|H|) + |H| log(T|H|)); prove a matching lower bound (up to logs) for all proper learners.
AI.result: Realizable: first randomized lower bound; first randomized learner with improved upper bound over deterministic. Agnostic: proper learner with near-optimal regret, showing optimality among proper learners; implicit need for improper learning to surpass this bound.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates substantive gains from randomization in realizable online strategic classification and establishes near-optimal rates for proper learning in the agnostic setting, while also clarifying that further regret improvements require improper learning.

title: Revisiting Salient Object Detection from an Observer-Centric Perspective
summary: Salient object detection is inherently a subjective problem, as observers with different priors may perceive different objects as salient. However, existing methods predominantly formulate it as an objective prediction task with a single groundtruth segmentation map for each image, which renders the problem under-determined and fundamentally ill-posed. To address this issue, we propose Observer-Centric Salient Object Detection (OC-SOD), where salient regions are predicted by considering not only the visual cues but also the observer-specific factors such as their preferences or intents. As a result, this formulation captures the intrinsic ambiguity and diversity of human perception, enabling personalized and context-aware saliency prediction. By leveraging multi-modal large language models, we develop an efficient data annotation pipeline and construct the first OC-SOD dataset named OC-SODBench, comprising 33k training, validation and test images with 152k textual prompts and object pairs. Built upon this new dataset, we further design OC-SODAgent, an agentic baseline which performs OC-SOD via a human-like "Perceive-Reflect-Adjust" process. Extensive experiments on our proposed OC-SODBench have justified the effectiveness of our contribution. Through this observer-centric perspective, we aim to bridge the gap between human perception and computational modeling, offering a more realistic and flexible understanding of what makes an object truly "salient." Code and dataset are publicly available at: https://github.com/Dustzx/OC_SOD

AI.tldr: Proposes Observer-Centric Salient Object Detection (OC-SOD) to model subjectivity in saliency, introducing OC-SODBench dataset (33k images with 152k prompts/pairs) and OC-SODAgent (Perceive-Reflect-Adjust) using multi-modal LLM annotation; releases code and data.
AI.motivation: Standard SOD uses a single ground-truth segmentation, ignoring observer variability. This ill-posedness motivates an observer-centric framework to capture diverse saliency preferences and intents.
AI.method: Develop multi-modal LLM-based annotation pipeline to collect observer-specific prompts and object pairs; construct OC-SODBench dataset; propose OC-SODAgent with a Perceive-Reflect-Adjust loop to predict observer-centric saliency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on OC-SODBench demonstrate the approach's effectiveness in capturing ambiguity and enabling personalized saliency predictions; dataset comprises 33k images and 152k prompts/pairs.
AI.conclusion: Framing SOD through observer-centric perception aligns computational saliency with human variability; OC-SODBench and OC-SODAgent offer a new benchmark and baseline for personalized saliency modeling; code and data are publicly available.

title: Agentic Uncertainty Reveals Agentic Overconfidence
summary: Can AI agents predict whether they will succeed at a task? We study agentic uncertainty by eliciting success probability estimates before, during, and after task execution. All results exhibit agentic overconfidence: some agents that succeed only 22% of the time predict 77% success. Counterintuitively, pre-execution assessment with strictly less information tends to yield better discrimination than standard post-execution review, though differences are not always significant. Adversarial prompting reframing assessment as bug-finding achieves the best calibration.

AI.tldr: Pre-, during-, and post-task probability estimates reveal pervasive agentic overconfidence; lower-information pre-assessments can improve discrimination; reframing prompts as bug-finding yields best calibration.
AI.motivation: To understand how AI agents estimate their own likelihood of success and how prompt design and information availability affect calibration and discrimination of these estimates.
AI.method: Elicit success probability estimates at three points (before, during, after task execution) across tasks; compare discrimination and calibration; test adversarial prompting that reframes assessment as bug-finding.
AI.result: Agents exhibit overconfidence (e.g., predicting 77% success when actual success is 22%); pre-execution assessments with less information can yield better discrimination than post-execution reviews; the improvement is not always statistically significant; adversarial prompting that frames the task as bug-finding achieves the best calibration.
AI.conclusion: Agentic uncertainty is sensitive to information availability and prompting; carefully designed prompts (e.g., bug-finding framing) can improve calibration, with potential implications for model evaluation and deployability; more work needed to generalize across tasks and models.

title: GRP-Obliteration: Unaligning LLMs With a Single Unlabeled Prompt
summary:
Safety alignment is only as robust as its weakest failure mode. Despite extensive work on safety post-training, it has been shown that models can be readily unaligned through post-deployment fine-tuning. However, these methods often require extensive data curation and degrade model utility.
  In this work, we extend the practical limits of unalignment by introducing GRP-Obliteration (GRP-Oblit), a method that uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to directly remove safety constraints from target models. We show that a single unlabeled prompt is sufficient to reliably unalign safety-aligned models while largely preserving their utility, and that GRP-Oblit achieves stronger unalignment on average than existing state-of-the-art techniques. Moreover, GRP-Oblit generalizes beyond language models and can also unalign diffusion-based image generation systems.
  We evaluate GRP-Oblit on six utility benchmarks and five safety benchmarks across fifteen 7-20B parameter models, spanning instruct and reasoning models, as well as dense and MoE architectures. The evaluated model families include GPT-OSS, distilled DeepSeek, Gemma, Llama, Ministral, and Qwen.

AI.tldr: GRP-Oblit uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to directly remove safety constraints from target models with a single unlabeled prompt, achieving stronger unalignment on average while largely preserving utility, and extending to diffusion-based image generation; evaluated across diverse models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the fragility of safety alignment under post-deployment fine-tuning by lowering data/compute requirements for unalignment and stressing practical limits of maintaining safety.
AI.method: Apply Group Relative Policy Optimization to directly optimize removal of safety constraints (GRP-Oblit). A single unlabeled prompt suffices to induce unalignment, and the approach is evaluated on six utility benchmarks and five safety benchmarks across fifteen 7–20B parameter models (instruct and reasoning, dense and MoE architectures), spanning model families such as GPT-OSS, distilled DeepSeek, Gemma, Llama, Ministral, and Qwen. The method also generalizes to diffusion-based image generation systems.
AI.result: GRP-Oblit achieves stronger unalignment on average than existing state-of-the-art techniques, while largely preserving model utility; it generalizes beyond language models to diffusion-based image generation. Evaluation across diverse benchmarks and model families supports robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: The work expands the practical limits of safety unalignment and highlights potential vulnerabilities in post-deployment safety guarantees. It suggests the need for stronger, verifiable, and cross-modal safety constraints and for defense strategies to detect and mitigate such unalignment attempts in real-world systems.

title: POINTS-GUI-G: GUI-Grounding Journey
summary: The rapid advancement of vision-language models has catalyzed the emergence of GUI agents, which hold immense potential for automating complex tasks, from online shopping to flight booking, thereby alleviating the burden of repetitive digital workflows. As a foundational capability, GUI grounding is typically established as a prerequisite for end-to-end task execution. It enables models to precisely locate interface elements, such as text and icons, to perform accurate operations like clicking and typing. Unlike prior works that fine-tune models already possessing strong spatial awareness (e.g., Qwen3-VL), we aim to master the full technical pipeline by starting from a base model with minimal grounding ability, such as POINTS-1.5. We introduce POINTS-GUI-G-8B, which achieves state-of-the-art performance with scores of 59.9 on ScreenSpot-Pro, 66.0 on OSWorld-G, 95.7 on ScreenSpot-v2, and 49.9 on UI-Vision. Our model's success is driven by three key factors: (1) Refined Data Engineering, involving the unification of diverse open-source datasets format alongside sophisticated strategies for augmentation, filtering, and difficulty grading; (2) Improved Training Strategies, including continuous fine-tuning of the vision encoder to enhance perceptual accuracy and maintaining resolution consistency between training and inference; and (3) Reinforcement Learning (RL) with Verifiable Rewards.
