While RL is traditionally used to bolster reasoning, we demonstrate that it significantly improves precision in the perception-intensive GUI grounding task. Furthermore, GUI grounding provides a natural advantage for RL, as rewards are easily verifiable and highly accurate.

AI.tldr: POINTS-GUI-G-8B establishes state-of-the-art GUI grounding by starting from a minimal grounding base (POINTS-1.5) and leveraging refined data engineering, enhanced training strategies, and RL with verifiable rewards across four GUI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: GUI grounding is essential for reliable end-to-end automated interaction with graphical interfaces. Existing work often fine-tunes models with pre-existing strong spatial awareness; this work aims to build the whole grounding-to-action pipeline from a low-grounding base, enabling robust element localization and subsequent interaction.
AI.method: Threefold strategy: (1) Refined Data Engineering by unifying diverse open-source GUI datasets, with advanced augmentation, filtering, and difficulty grading. (2) Improved Training Strategies, including continuous vision encoder fine-tuning to improve perceptual accuracy and ensuring resolution consistency between training and inference. (3) Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards, showing that RL can enhance perception precision and that GUI-grounding rewards are easily verifiable and advantageous in RL settings.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art scores on four benchmarks: ScreenSpot-Pro 59.9, OSWorld-G 66.0, ScreenSpot-v2 95.7, UI-Vision 49.9.
AI.conclusion: RL-guided optimization can significantly improve GUI-grounding perception. The combination of unified data, robust training, and verifiable rewards presents a practical pathway to high-precision GUI element localization and reliable downstream interactions.

title: EUGens: Efficient, Unified, and General Dense Layers
summary: Efficient neural networks are essential for scaling machine learning models to real-time applications and resource-constrained environments. Fully-connected feedforward layers (FFLs) introduce computation and parameter count bottlenecks within neural network architectures. To address this challenge, in this work, we propose a new class of dense layers that generalize standard fully-connected feedforward layers, \textbf{E}fficient, \textbf{U}nified and \textbf{Gen}eral dense layers (EUGens). EUGens leverage random features to approximate standard FFLs and go beyond them by incorporating a direct dependence on the input norms in their computations. The proposed layers unify existing efficient FFL extensions and improve efficiency by reducing inference complexity from quadratic to linear time. They also lead to \textbf{the first} unbiased algorithms approximating FFLs with arbitrary polynomial activation functions. Furthermore, EuGens reduce the parameter count and computational overhead while preserving the expressive power and adaptability of FFLs. We also present a layer-wise knowledge transfer technique that bypasses backpropagation, enabling efficient adaptation of EUGens to pre-trained models. Empirically, we observe that integrating EUGens into Transformers and MLPs yields substantial improvements in inference speed (up to \textbf{27}\%) and memory efficiency (up to \textbf{30}\%) across a range of tasks, including image classification, language model pre-training, and 3D scene reconstruction. Overall, our results highlight the potential of EUGens for the scalable deployment of large-scale neural networks in real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: A novel dense layer family (EUGens) uses random features and input-norm dependent computations to approximate standard FFLs, reducing inference from quadratic to linear time, unifying prior efficient FFLs, enabling unbiased approximation for polynomial activations, and showing speed/memory gains when integrated into Transformers/MLPs across tasks.
AI.motivation: Address computation and parameter bottlenecks of fully-connected layers to enable real-time and resource-constrained deployment of large models; extend efficiency beyond existing FFLs with an unbiased, norm-aware approach.
AI.method: Introduce EUGens: dense layers using random feature approximations of FFLs with explicit input-norm dependence; unify existing efficient FFL extensions; provide unbiased algorithms for arbitrary polynomial activations; propose layer-wise knowledge transfer bypassing backprop for adapting pre-trained models.
AI.result: Empirical results show up to 27% faster inference and up to 30% memory savings for EUGens integrated into Transformers and MLPs on image classification, language model pre-training, and 3D scene reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: EUGens offer scalable, expressive dense layers that reduce computation and parameters while maintaining performance; layer-wise transfer enables efficient adaptation; they pave the way for deploying large-scale models in real-world scenarios.

title: Perception-Control Coupled Visual Servoing for Textureless Objects Using Keypoint-Based EKF
summary: Visual servoing is fundamental to robotic applications, enabling precise positioning and control. However, applying it to textureless objects remains a challenge due to the absence of reliable visual features. Moreover, adverse visual conditions, such as occlusions, often corrupt visual feedback, leading to reduced accuracy and instability in visual servoing. In this work, we build upon learning-based keypoint detection for textureless objects and propose a method that enhances robustness by tightly integrating perception and control in a closed loop. Specifically, we employ an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) that integrates per-frame keypoint measurements to estimate 6D object pose, which drives pose-based visual servoing (PBVS) for control. The resulting camera motion, in turn, enhances the tracking of subsequent keypoints, effectively closing the perception-control loop. Additionally, unlike standard PBVS, we propose a probabilistic control law that computes both camera velocity and its associated uncertainty, enabling uncertainty-aware control for safe and reliable operation. We validate our approach on real-world robotic platforms using quantitative metrics and grasping experiments, demonstrating that our method outperforms traditional visual servoing techniques in both accuracy and practical application.

AI.tldr: A closed-loop perception-control framework for textureless-object visual servoing via EKF-based 6D pose estimation from learning-based keypoints, PBVS with probabilistic control, and uncertainty-aware camera motion, validated on real robots.
AI.motivation: Textureless objects lack reliable visual features; occlusions corrupt feedback; need robust, safe servoing in realistic conditions.
AI.method: EKF fuses per-frame keypoint observations to estimate 6D pose; PBVS uses estimated pose for camera velocity commands; a probabilistic control law outputs both the velocity and its uncertainty; closed loop improves keypoint tracking.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show improved accuracy and grasping performance vs traditional PBVS; robustness under occlusions; quantitative metrics reported.
AI.conclusion: Joint perception-control with uncertainty-aware PBVS enhances robustness for textureless-object visual servoing, enabling safer and more reliable operation in challenging conditions.

title: Swap Regret Minimization Through Response-Based Approachability
summary:
We consider the problem of minimizing different notions of swap regret in online optimization. These forms of regret are tightly connected to correlated equilibrium concepts in games, and have been more recently shown to guarantee non-manipulability against strategic adversaries. The only computationally efficient algorithm for minimizing linear swap regret over a general convex set in $\mathbb{R}^d$ was developed recently by Daskalakis, Farina, Fishelson, Pipis, and Schneider (STOC '25). However, it incurs a highly suboptimal regret bound of $Ω(d^4 \sqrt{T})$ and also relies on computationally intensive calls to the ellipsoid algorithm at each iteration.
  In this paper, we develop a significantly simpler, computationally efficient algorithm that guarantees $O(d^{3/2} \sqrt{T})$ linear swap regret for a general convex set and $O(d \sqrt{T})$ when the set is centrally symmetric. Our approach leverages the powerful response-based approachability framework of Bernstein and Shimkin (JMLR '15) -- previously overlooked in the line of work on swap regret minimization -- combined with geometric preconditioning via the John ellipsoid. Our algorithm simultaneously minimizes profile swap regret, which was recently shown to guarantee non-manipulability. Moreover, we establish a matching information-theoretic lower bound: any learner must incur in expectation $Ω(d \sqrt{T})$ linear swap regret for large enough $T$, even when the set is centrally symmetric. This also shows that the classic algorithm of Gordon, Greenwald, and Marks (ICML '08) is existentially optimal for minimizing linear swap regret, although it is computationally inefficient. Finally, we extend our approach to minimize regret with respect to the set of swap deviations with polynomial dimension, unifying and strengthening recent results in equilibrium computation and online learning.

AI.tldr: Introduces a simpler, computationally efficient algorithm for minimizing linear swap regret with near-optimal bounds: O(d^{3/2} sqrt{T}) for general convex sets and O(d sqrt{T}) for centrally symmetric sets, using a response-based approachability framework with John ellipsoid preconditioning. Establishes matching lower bound Ω(d sqrt{T}); shows Gordon–Greenwald–Marks is existentially optimal for linear swap regret (though not efficient); extends to swap deviations of polynomial dimension.
AI.motivation: Swap regret minimization is tied to correlated equilibria and non-manipulability in games. Prior efficient algorithm (STOC '25) has suboptimal Ω(d^4 sqrt{T}) regret and relies on ellipsoid calls, motivating a simpler, tighter, and computationally lighter approach.
AI.method: Combine Bernstein–Shimkin’s response-based approachability with John ellipsoid-based geometric preconditioning to minimize linear swap regret. The method also minimizes profile swap regret and extends to polynomial-dimension swap deviations.
AI.result: Algorithm achieves improved regret bounds: O(d^{3/2} sqrt{T}) for general sets and O(d sqrt{T}) for centrally symmetric sets. Proves a matching information-theoretic lower bound Ω(d sqrt{T}) and demonstrates existential optimality of Gordon–Greenwald–Marks for linear swap regret. Extends framework to swap deviations with polynomial dimension.
AI.conclusion: The work unifies online learning and equilibrium computation approaches to swap regret, delivering a simpler, computationally efficient algorithm with tight bounds and broad applicability, including extensions to more general swap deviation notions.

title: TFusionOcc: Student's t-Distribution Based Object-Centric Multi-Sensor Fusion Framework for 3D Occupancy Prediction
summary: 3D semantic occupancy prediction enables autonomous vehicles (AVs) to perceive fine-grained geometric and semantic structure of their surroundings from onboard sensors, which is essential for safe decision-making and navigation. Recent models for 3D semantic occupancy prediction have successfully addressed the challenge of describing real-world objects with varied shapes and classes. However, the intermediate representations used by existing methods for 3D semantic occupancy prediction rely heavily on 3D voxel volumes or a set of 3D Gaussians, hindering the model's ability to efficiently and effectively capture fine-grained geometric details in the 3D driving environment. This paper introduces TFusionOcc, a novel object-centric multi-sensor fusion framework for predicting 3D semantic occupancy. By leveraging multi-stage multi-sensor fusion, Student's t-distribution, and the T-Mixture model (TMM), together with more geometrically flexible primitives, such as the deformable superquadric (superquadric with inverse warp), the proposed method achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the nuScenes benchmark. In addition, extensive experiments were conducted on the nuScenes-C dataset to demonstrate the robustness of the proposed method in different camera and lidar corruption scenarios. The code will be available at: https://github.com/DanielMing123/TFusionOcc

AI.tldr: TFusionOcc: an object-centric multi-sensor fusion framework using deformable superquadrics and robust statistics (Student's t, T-Mixture) for 3D semantic occupancy; achieves SOTA on nuScenes and demonstrates robustness on nuScenes-C.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D semantic occupancy representations rely on voxels or 3D Gaussians, which limit geometric detail and efficiency in driving environments; there is a need for flexible primitives and robust multi-sensor fusion.
AI.method: Proposes an object-centric, multi-stage multi-sensor fusion framework. Objects are modeled with deformable superquadrics (superquadric with inverse warp). Employs Student's t distribution and a T-Mixture model to robustly fuse observations across sensors. Trains/evaluates on nuScenes; code released.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the nuScenes benchmark; demonstrates robustness to camera and LiDAR corruption on nuScenes-C.
AI.conclusion: TFusionOcc provides a geometrically flexible, robust 3D semantic occupancy framework with effective multi-sensor fusion, enabling fine-grained geometric details for autonomous driving and showing strong real-world robustness.

title: SURE: Safe Uncertainty-Aware Robot-Environment Interaction using Trajectory Optimization
summary: Robotic tasks involving contact interactions pose significant challenges for trajectory optimization due to discontinuous dynamics. Conventional formulations typically assume deterministic contact events, which limit robustness and adaptability in real-world settings. In this work, we propose SURE, a robust trajectory optimization framework that explicitly accounts for contact timing uncertainty. By allowing multiple trajectories to branch from possible pre-impact states and later rejoin a shared trajectory, SURE achieves both robustness and computational efficiency within a unified optimization framework. We evaluate SURE on two representative tasks with unknown impact times. In a cart-pole balancing task involving uncertain wall location, SURE achieves an average improvement of 21.6% in success rate when branch switching is enabled during control. In an egg-catching experiment using a robotic manipulator, SURE improves the success rate by 40%. These results demonstrate that SURE substantially enhances robustness compared to conventional nominal formulations.

AI.tldr: SURE: a robust trajectory optimization framework that handles contact timing uncertainty by branching pre-impact trajectories and rejoining a shared trajectory, improving robustness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Robotic tasks with contact involve discontinuous dynamics and uncertain impact times; conventional deterministic contact assumptions limit robustness in real-world settings.
AI.method: Introduce trajectory branching at possible pre-impact states with a unified optimization that allows branches to switch during control and later rejoin a shared trajectory, enabling robustness to timing uncertainty while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.result: Evaluated on two tasks with unknown impact times: cart-pole with uncertain wall location shows 21.6% average improvement in success rate with branch switching; egg-catching with a robotic manipulator shows a 40% improvement in success rate, indicating robustness gains over nominal formulations.
AI.conclusion: SURE substantially enhances robustness to contact timing uncertainty compared to conventional nominal trajectory optimization, achieving better success rates while preserving computational efficiency.

title: PurSAMERE: Reliable Adversarial Purification via Sharpness-Aware Minimization of Expected Reconstruction Error
summary: We propose a novel deterministic purification method to improve adversarial robustness by mapping a potentially adversarial sample toward a nearby sample that lies close to a mode of the data distribution, where classifiers are more reliable. We design the method to be deterministic to ensure reliable test accuracy and to prevent the degradation of effective robustness observed in stochastic purification approaches when the adversary has full knowledge of the system and its randomness. We employ a score model trained by minimizing the expected reconstruction error of noise-corrupted data, thereby learning the structural characteristics of the input data distribution. Given a potentially adversarial input, the method searches within its local neighborhood for a purified sample that minimizes the expected reconstruction error under noise corruption and then feeds this purified sample to the classifier. During purification, sharpness-aware minimization is used to guide the purified samples toward flat regions of the expected reconstruction error landscape, thereby enhancing robustness. We further show that, as the noise level decreases, minimizing the expected reconstruction error biases the purified sample toward local maximizers of the Gaussian-smoothed density; under additional local assumptions on the score model, we prove recovery of a local maximizer in the small-noise limit. Experimental results demonstrate significant gains in adversarial robustness over state-of-the-art methods under strong deterministic white-box attacks.

AI.tldr: A deterministic purification framework uses a score-based model to move adversarial inputs toward high-density, near-data regions by minimizing the expected reconstruction error under noise; guided by sharpness-aware optimization, it yields improved deterministic robustness, with theoretical recovery in the small-noise limit and strong empirical gains under white-box attacks.
AI.motivation: To achieve reliable test-time robustness against adversaries while avoiding randomness-based defenses, by leveraging the geometry of the data distribution and ensuring deterministic purification performance.
AI.method: Train a score model by minimizing the expected reconstruction error of noise-corrupted data. For a potentially adversarial input, search its local neighborhood for a purified sample that minimizes the expected reconstruction error under noise, then classify that purified sample. Employ sharpness-aware minimization to guide purified samples toward flat regions of the ERR landscape. In the small-noise limit, ERR minimization biases toward local maximizers of the Gaussian-smoothed density; under local score-model assumptions, recovery of a local maximizer is proven.
AI.result: Experimental results show significant gains in adversarial robustness over state-of-the-art methods under strong deterministic white-box attacks.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic purification leveraging score-based reconstruction error minimization and sharpness-aware optimization provides robust performance with theoretical support in the small-noise regime, though practical considerations (e.g., computational cost, model quality) should be examined.

title: MeDocVL: A Visual Language Model for Medical Document Understanding and Parsing
summary: Medical document OCR is challenging due to complex layouts, domain-specific terminology, and noisy annotations, while requiring strict field-level exact matching. Existing OCR systems and general-purpose vision-language models often fail to reliably parse such documents. We propose MeDocVL, a post-trained vision-language model for query-driven medical document parsing. Our framework combines Training-driven Label Refinement to construct high-quality supervision from noisy annotations, with a Noise-aware Hybrid Post-training strategy that integrates reinforcement learning and supervised fine-tuning to achieve robust and precise extraction. Experiments on medical invoice benchmarks show that MeDocVL consistently outperforms conventional OCR systems and strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance under noisy supervision.

AI.tldr: A post-trained vision-language model, MeDocVL, improves query-driven medical document parsing under noisy supervision by combining Training-driven Label Refinement and a Noise-aware Hybrid Post-training strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results on medical invoice benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Medical document OCR is hard due to complex layouts, domain-specific terminology, and noisy annotations; existing OCR systems and generic vision-language models struggle with field-level exact matching in clinical documents.
AI.method: MeDocVL uses Training-driven Label Refinement to derive high-quality supervision from noisy annotations, and a Noise-aware Hybrid Post-training strategy that blends reinforcement learning with supervised fine-tuning for robust, precise extraction.
AI.result: On medical invoice benchmarks, MeDocVL outperforms conventional OCR systems and strong vision-language model baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance under noisy supervision.
AI.conclusion: MeDocVL effectively mitigates label noise and layout complexity in medical document parsing, enabling reliable, precise field-level extraction.

title: Consensus-based optimization (CBO): Towards Global Optimality in Robotics
summary: Zero-order optimization has recently received significant attention for designing optimal trajectories and policies for robotic systems. However, most existing methods (e.g., MPPI, CEM, and CMA-ES) are local in nature, as they rely on gradient estimation. In this paper, we introduce consensus-based optimization (CBO) to robotics, which is guaranteed to converge to a global optimum under mild assumptions. We provide theoretical analysis and illustrative examples that give intuition into the fundamental differences between CBO and existing methods. To demonstrate the scalability of CBO for robotics problems, we consider three challenging trajectory optimization scenarios: (1) a long-horizon problem for a simple system, (2) a dynamic balance problem for a highly underactuated system, and (3) a high-dimensional problem with only a terminal cost. Our results show that CBO is able to achieve lower costs with respect to existing methods on all three challenging settings. This opens a new framework to study global trajectory optimization in robotics.

AI.tldr: Introduces consensus-based optimization (CBO) for robotic trajectory optimization, with theory and intuition on global convergence, and shows it outperforms local gradient-based methods on three challenging problems, suggesting a new global optimization framework for robotics.
AI.motivation: Local zero-order optimization methods in robotics (e.g., MPPI, CEM, CMA-ES) are prone to local minima and rely on gradient estimation; a globally convergent method is desirable for robust, scalable trajectory optimization.
AI.method: Apply consensus-based optimization (CBO) to robotics, provide theoretical analysis and intuitive contrasts with existing methods, and validate on three challenging trajectory tasks: (1) long-horizon simple system, (2) dynamic balance of a highly underactuated system, and (3) a high-dimensional problem with only a terminal cost.
AI.result: CBO achieves lower cost than existing methods across all three scenarios, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness and supporting the view of CBO as a new framework for global trajectory optimization in robotics.
AI.conclusion: CBO offers global convergence guarantees under mild assumptions and provides a new framework for global trajectory optimization in robotics; further work could extend analysis and experiments to broader robotics applications and refine theoretical bounds.

title: Statistical Learning from Attribution Sets
summary: We address the problem of training conversion prediction models in advertising domains under privacy constraints, where direct links between ad clicks and conversions are unavailable. Motivated by privacy-preserving browser APIs and the deprecation of third-party cookies, we study a setting where the learner observes a sequence of clicks and a sequence of conversions, but can only link a conversion to a set of candidate clicks (an attribution set) rather than a unique source. We formalize this as learning from attribution sets generated by an oblivious adversary equipped with a prior distribution over the candidates. Despite the lack of explicit labels, we construct an unbiased estimator of the population loss from these coarse signals via a novel approach. Leveraging this estimator, we show that Empirical Risk Minimization achieves generalization guarantees that scale with the informativeness of the prior and is also robust against estimation errors in the prior, despite complex dependencies among attribution sets. Simple empirical evaluations on standard datasets suggest our unbiased approach significantly outperforms common industry heuristics, particularly in regimes where attribution sets are large or overlapping.

AI.tldr: Unbiased loss estimation from attribution sets under privacy constraints and ERM with guarantees that scale with prior informativeness; robust to prior errors; empirical gains over heuristics when attribution sets are large/overlapping.
AI.motivation: Privacy-preserving attribution in ads where direct click-conversion links are unavailable due to browser APIs and cookie deprecation; need learning from coarse attribution signals.
AI.method: Formalize learning from attribution sets generated by an oblivious adversary with a prior over candidate clicks; construct an unbiased estimator of population loss from coarse signals; prove ERM generalization guarantees and robustness to prior estimation errors; handle dependencies among attribution sets.
AI.result: Unbiased population loss estimator; ERM generalization bounds that depend on prior informativeness; robust to prior misspecification; empirical results on standard datasets show substantial gains over industry heuristics, especially for large or overlapping attribution sets.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of training conversion models under privacy constraints using attribution-set supervision; provides unbiased estimator, theoretical guarantees, and practical performance improvements; highlights potential for privacy-preserving advertising analytics.

title: A neuromorphic model of the insect visual system for natural image processing
summary: Insect vision supports complex behaviors including associative learning, navigation, and object detection, and has long motivated computational models for understanding biological visual processing. However, many contemporary models prioritize task performance while neglecting biologically grounded processing pathways. Here, we introduce a bio-inspired vision model that captures principles of the insect visual system to transform dense visual input into sparse, discriminative codes. The model is trained using a fully self-supervised contrastive objective, enabling representation learning without labeled data and supporting reuse across tasks without reliance on domain-specific classifiers. We evaluated the resulting representations on flower recognition tasks and natural image benchmarks. The model consistently produced reliable sparse codes that distinguish visually similar inputs. To support different modelling and deployment uses, we have implemented the model as both an artificial neural network and a spiking neural network. In a simulated localization setting, our approach outperformed a simple image downsampling comparison baseline, highlighting the functional benefit of incorporating neuromorphic visual processing pathways. Collectively, these results advance insect computational modelling by providing a generalizable bio-inspired vision model capable of sparse computation across diverse tasks.

AI.tldr: A bio-inspired, sparse, self-supervised vision model based on insect processing; yields sparse, discriminative representations, works in both ANN and SNN, and improves localization over a downsampling baseline, with cross-task generality.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where contemporary vision models prioritize performance over biologically grounded processing; leverage principles of the insect visual system to produce sparse codes and neuromorphic pathways, enabling self-supervised learning and reuse across tasks without domain-specific classifiers.
AI.method: Develop a vision pipeline that transforms dense inputs into sparse codes emulating insect vision. Train it with a fully self-supervised contrastive objective. Implement the model as both an artificial neural network (ANN) and a spiking neural network (SNN). Evaluate on flower recognition and natural image benchmarks, plus a simulated localization task comparing against a simple downsampling baseline.
AI.result: The model consistently produces reliable sparse codes that discriminate visually similar inputs. In localization simulations, it outperforms the downsampling baseline, demonstrating the value of incorporating neuromorphic visual processing. The approach shows generalizability across tasks without relying on domain-specific classifiers and is applicable to both ANN and SNN implementations.
AI.conclusion: The work advances insect-inspired computational modeling by providing a generalizable bio-inspired vision model that supports sparse computation across diverse tasks and modalities, contributing to neuromorphic vision research and cross-task reuse.

title: Strategizing at Speed: A Learned Model Predictive Game for Multi-Agent Drone Racing
summary: Autonomous drone racing pushes the boundaries of high-speed motion planning and multi-agent strategic decision-making. Success in this domain requires drones not only to navigate at their limits but also to anticipate and counteract competitors' actions. In this paper, we study a fundamental question that arises in this domain: how deeply should an agent strategize before taking an action? To this end, we compare two planning paradigms: the Model Predictive Game (MPG), which finds interaction-aware strategies at the expense of longer computation times, and contouring Model Predictive Control (MPC), which computes strategies rapidly but does not reason about interactions. We perform extensive experiments to study this trade-off, revealing that MPG outperforms MPC at moderate velocities but loses its advantage at higher speeds due to latency. To address this shortcoming, we propose a Learned Model Predictive Game (LMPG) approach that amortizes model predictive gameplay to reduce latency. In both simulation and hardware experiments, we benchmark our approach against MPG and MPC in head-to-head races, finding that LMPG outperforms both baselines.

AI.tldr: Learned Model Predictive Game (LMPG) amortizes interaction-aware planning to reduce latency, outperforming MPG and MPC in drone-racing experiments.
AI.motivation: Investigate how deep planning for multi-agent interaction trades off with computation latency in high-speed autonomous drone racing.
AI.method: Compare MPG and contouring MPC; propose LMPG that learns to approximate or reuse MPG-level decisions to lower runtime; evaluate in simulation and on hardware with head-to-head races against MPG and MPC.
AI.result: MPG wins over MPC at moderate speeds; at high speeds latency erodes MPG's advantage; LMPG mitigates latency and outperforms both MPG and MPC in head-to-head races in both simulation and hardware.
AI.conclusion: Latency-aware planning is crucial; learning-based amortization of model predictive gameplay can achieve superior performance by balancing planning depth and computation time.

title: SOCKET: SOft Collison Kernel EsTimator for Sparse Attention
summary: Exploiting sparsity during long-context inference is central to scaling large language models, as attention dominates the cost of autoregressive decoding. Sparse attention reduces this cost by restricting computation to a subset of tokens, but its effectiveness depends critically on efficient scoring and selection of relevant tokens at inference time. We revisit Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) as a sparsification primitive and introduce SOCKET, a SOft Collision Kernel EsTimator that replaces hard bucket matches with probabilistic, similarity-aware aggregation. Our key insight is that hard LSH produces discrete collision signals and is therefore poorly suited for ranking. In contrast, soft LSH aggregates graded collision evidence across hash tables, preserving the stability of relative ordering among the true top-$k$ tokens. This transformation elevates LSH from a candidate-generation heuristic to a principled and mathematically grounded scoring kernel for sparse attention. Leveraging this property, SOCKET enables efficient token selection without ad-hoc voting mechanism, and matches or surpasses established sparse attention baselines across multiple long-context benchmarks using diverse set of models. With a custom CUDA kernel for scoring keys and a Flash Decode Triton backend for sparse attention, SOCKET achieves up to 1.5$\times$ higher throughput than FlashAttention, making it an effective tool for long-context inference. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/amarka8/SOCKET.

AI.tldr: SOCKET replaces hard LSH bucketing with a soft, probabilistic, similarity-aware scoring kernel for sparse attention, yielding principled token ranking and up to 1.5x throughput over FlashAttention on long contexts.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference in LLMs is dominated by attention; efficient scoring/selection of relevant tokens is crucial. Classical hard LSH yields discrete, brittle collision signals and poor ranking stability, motivating a probabilistic reformulation.
AI.method: Introduce SOCKET: Soft Collision Kernel Estimator that aggregates graded collision evidence across multiple hash tables (soft LSH) instead of relying on hard bucket hits. Do not use ad-hoc voting; employ a custom CUDA kernel for scoring keys and a Triton backend (Flash Decode) for parallel sparse attention.
AI.result: SOCKET matches or surpasses established sparse attention baselines on multiple long-context benchmarks across diverse model families, and delivers up to 1.5x higher throughput than FlashAttention.
AI.conclusion: Soft LSH provides a principled scoring kernel for sparse attention, improving token ranking stability and efficiency for long-context decoding. Code is open-sourced at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Point Virtual Transformer
summary: LiDAR-based 3D object detectors often struggle to detect far-field objects due to the sparsity of point clouds at long ranges, which limits the availability of reliable geometric cues. To address this, prior approaches augment LiDAR data with depth-completed virtual points derived from RGB images; however, directly incorporating all virtual points leads to increased computational cost and introduces challenges in effectively fusing real and virtual information. We present Point Virtual Transformer (PointViT), a transformer-based 3D object detection framework that jointly reasons over raw LiDAR points and selectively sampled virtual points. The framework examines multiple fusion strategies, ranging from early point-level fusion to BEV-based gated fusion, and analyses their trade-offs in terms of accuracy and efficiency. The fused point cloud is voxelized and encoded using sparse convolutions to form a BEV representation, from which a compact set of high-confidence object queries is initialised and refined through a transformer-based context aggregation module. Experiments on the KITTI benchmark report 91.16% 3D AP, 95.94% BEV AP, and 99.36% AP on the KITTI 2D detection benchmark for the Car class.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based 3D detector (PointViT) fuses raw LiDAR points with selectively sampled virtual depth-completed points to improve far-field detection, using a BEV-based sparse conv and transformer refinement; multiple fusion strategies evaluated.
AI.motivation: Far-field LiDAR sparsity limits reliable geometric cues; while depth-completed virtual points from RGB can help, indiscriminate use increases cost and complicates fusion.
AI.method: Joint reasoning over raw LiDAR points and selectively sampled virtual points; explore fusion strategies from early point-level fusion to BEV-based gated fusion; voxelize fused points and encode with sparse convolutions to form a BEV representation; initialize a compact set of high-confidence object queries and refine via a transformer-based context aggregation module.
AI.result: On KITTI, Car class: 3D AP 91.16%, BEV AP 95.94%, 2D AP 99.36%.
AI.conclusion: PointViT effectively fuses real and virtual points within a BEV-based transformer pipeline, achieving strong 3D/BEV/2D performance on KITTI; analysis of fusion strategies highlights trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency.

title: DreamDojo: A Generalist Robot World Model from Large-Scale Human Videos
summary: Being able to simulate the outcomes of actions in varied environments will revolutionize the development of generalist agents at scale. However, modeling these world dynamics, especially for dexterous robotics tasks, poses significant challenges due to limited data coverage and scarce action labels. As an endeavor towards this end, we introduce DreamDojo, a foundation world model that learns diverse interactions and dexterous controls from 44k hours of egocentric human videos. Our data mixture represents the largest video dataset to date for world model pretraining, spanning a wide range of daily scenarios with diverse objects and skills. To address the scarcity of action labels, we introduce continuous latent actions as unified proxy actions, enhancing interaction knowledge transfer from unlabeled videos. After post-training on small-scale target robot data, DreamDojo demonstrates a strong understanding of physics and precise action controllability. We also devise a distillation pipeline that accelerates DreamDojo to a real-time speed of 10.81 FPS and further improves context consistency. Our work enables several important applications based on generative world models, including live teleoperation, policy evaluation, and model-based planning. Systematic evaluation on multiple challenging out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks verifies the significance of our method for simulating open-world, contact-rich tasks, paving the way for general-purpose robot world models.

AI.tldr: A large-scale egocentric video–based world model (DreamDojo) learns diverse interactions and dexterous control via continuous latent actions, with post-training on robot data and a distillation pipeline for real-time use, enabling teleoperation and planning on open-world tasks.
AI.motivation: To enable generalist, model-based robots by learning rich world dynamics from unlabeled, diverse human videos, addressing data scarcity and lack of action labels.
AI.method: Train on 44k hours of egocentric human videos to learn diverse interactions; introduce continuous latent actions as unified proxy actions to bridge unlabeled data; post-train on small-scale target robot data; distill the model to real-time speed (10.81 FPS) and improve context consistency.
AI.result: The model exhibits strong physics understanding and precise action controllability; achieves real-time performance; improves context consistency; supports live teleoperation, policy evaluation, and model-based planning; validated on multiple challenging out-of-distribution benchmarks for open-world, contact-rich tasks.
AI.conclusion: DreamDojo advances foundation world models for robotics by combining large-scale unlabeled video data with latent-action representations and an efficient distillation pipeline, paving the way for general-purpose robot world models and broad applications in open-world manipulation.

title: Learning Human Visual Attention on 3D Surfaces through Geometry-Queried Semantic Priors
summary: Human visual attention on three-dimensional objects emerges from the interplay between bottom-up geometric processing and top-down semantic recognition. Existing 3D saliency methods rely on hand-crafted geometric features or learning-based approaches that lack semantic awareness, failing to explain why humans fixate on semantically meaningful but geometrically unremarkable regions. We introduce SemGeo-AttentionNet, a dual-stream architecture that explicitly formalizes this dichotomy through asymmetric cross-modal fusion, leveraging diffusion-based semantic priors from geometry-conditioned multi-view rendering and point cloud transformers for geometric processing. Cross-attention ensures geometric features query semantic content, enabling bottom-up distinctiveness to guide top-down retrieval. We extend our framework to temporal scanpath generation through reinforcement learning, introducing the first formulation respecting 3D mesh topology with inhibition-of-return dynamics. Evaluation on SAL3D, NUS3D and 3DVA datasets demonstrates substantial improvements, validating how cognitively motivated architectures effectively model human visual attention on three-dimensional surfaces.

AI.tldr: SemGeo-AttentionNet: a dual-stream 3D saliency model with asymmetric cross-modal fusion that combines diffusion-based semantic priors and geometry processing, extended with RL-based temporal scanpaths; shows clear improvements on SAL3D, NUS3D, and 3DVA.
AI.motivation: Current 3D saliency methods lack semantic awareness and cannot explain fixations on semantically meaningful but geometrically unremarkable regions. There is a need to integrate bottom-up geometric processing with top-down semantic recognition to better model human attention on 3D surfaces.
AI.method: Proposes SemGeo-AttentionNet, a dual-stream architecture with asymmetric cross-modal fusion: a geometry stream using point-cloud transformers and a semantic stream leveraging diffusion-based priors from geometry-conditioned multi-view rendering. Cross-attention makes geometric features query semantic content. Extends to temporal scanpath generation via reinforcement learning with a formulation that respects 3D mesh topology and inhibition-of-return dynamics.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial improvements on SAL3D, NUS3D, and 3DVA datasets, validating that cognitively motivated architectures better model human attention on 3D surfaces.
AI.conclusion: A cognitively inspired, cross-modal architecture combining bottom-up geometry with top-down semantics improves 3D saliency modeling and enables realistic scanpath generation, highlighting the value of semantic priors and asymmetrical fusion in 3D attention research.

title: The Condensate Theorem: Transformers are O(n), Not $O(n^2)$
summary: We present the Condensate Theorem: attention sparsity is a learned topological property, not an architectural constraint. Through empirical analysis of trained language models, we find that attention mass concentrates on a distinct topological manifold -- and this manifold can be identified dynamically without checking every position. We prove a general result: for any query, projecting attention onto the Condensate Manifold (Anchor + Window + Dynamic Top-k) achieves 100% output equivalence with full $O(n^2)$ attention. This is not an approximation -- it is lossless parity. We validate this across GPT-2, Pythia, Qwen2, TinyLlama, and Mistral, demonstrating bit-exact token matching on 1,500+ generated tokens. By mapping this topology to hardware, our Topological Attention kernel achieves a 159x measured speedup at 131K tokens (3.94ms vs 628ms) and a projected >1,200x speedup at 1M tokens, reducing inference costs by >99.9% compared to Flash Attention. We conclude that the quadratic bottleneck is an artifact of naive implementation, not intelligence.

AI.tldr: Attention sparsity is a learned topological property; a Condensate Manifold enables lossless projection that matches full attention, with significant reported speedups; validated across multiple models with bit-exact token matching.
AI.motivation: Address the quadratic complexity of attention by showing that sparsity is intrinsic and can be exploited via a topological representation, rather than relying on architectural constraints.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of trained language models to identify a Condensate Manifold (Anchor + Window + Dynamic Top-k); prove a general result that projecting attention onto this manifold yields 100% output equivalence with full O(n^2) attention; implement a Topological Attention kernel mapping topology to hardware; validate with bit-exact token matching across several models.
AI.result: Demonstrates bit-exact token matching for 1,500+ tokens; reports 159x speedup at 131K tokens and projected >1,200x at 1M tokens; claims >99.9% inference cost reduction versus Flash Attention.
AI.conclusion: Quadratic attention bottleneck is an artifact of naive implementation; the approach implies exact parity with full attention and suggests a path to substantial practical speedups without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Alleviating Sparse Rewards by Modeling Step-Wise and Long-Term Sampling Effects in Flow-Based GRPO
summary: Deploying GRPO on Flow Matching models has proven effective for text-to-image generation. However, existing paradigms typically propagate an outcome-based reward to all preceding denoising steps without distinguishing the local effect of each step. Moreover, current group-wise ranking mainly compares trajectories at matched timesteps and ignores within-trajectory dependencies, where certain early denoising actions can affect later states via delayed, implicit interactions. We propose TurningPoint-GRPO (TP-GRPO), a GRPO framework that alleviates step-wise reward sparsity and explicitly models long-term effects within the denoising trajectory. TP-GRPO makes two key innovations: (i) it replaces outcome-based rewards with step-level incremental rewards, providing a dense, step-aware learning signal that better isolates each denoising action's "pure" effect, and (ii) it identifies turning points-steps that flip the local reward trend and make subsequent reward evolution consistent with the overall trajectory trend-and assigns these actions an aggregated long-term reward to capture their delayed impact. Turning points are detected solely via sign changes in incremental rewards, making TP-GRPO efficient and hyperparameter-free. Extensive experiments also demonstrate that TP-GRPO exploits reward signals more effectively and consistently improves generation. Demo code is available at https://github.com/YunzeTong/TurningPoint-GRPO.

AI.tldr: TurningPoint-GRPO (TP-GRPO) enhances GRPO for flow-matching diffusion models by replacing outcome rewards with step-level incremental rewards and by aggregating long-term effects at turning points, improving step-wise credit assignment and generation quality.
AI.motivation: In diffusion-guided optimization, rewards are sparse and propagated only to earlier steps, obscuring each denoising action's true impact and ignoring within-trajectory dependencies and delayed interactions that influence later states.
AI.method: Introduce step-level incremental rewards instead of outcome-based rewards and detect turning points via sign changes in incremental rewards. Assign aggregated long-term rewards to turning-point steps to capture delayed effects, enabling dense, hyperparameter-free learning signals without relying on per-timestep trajectory matching.
AI.result: Empirical results show TP-GRPO more effectively utilizes reward signals and consistently improves text-to-image generation quality; implementation is available (demo code).
AI.conclusion: TP-GRPO alleviates reward sparsity and models long-term denoising effects by using dense step-wise rewards and turning-point credit assignment, yielding more reliable improvements in generation while remaining efficient and hyperparameter-free.

title: How (Not) to Hybridize Neural and Mechanistic Models for Epidemiological Forecasting
summary: Epidemiological forecasting from surveillance data is a hard problem and hybridizing mechanistic compartmental models with neural models is a natural direction. The mechanistic structure helps keep trajectories epidemiologically plausible, while neural components can capture non-stationary, data-adaptive effects. In practice, however, many seemingly straightforward couplings fail under partial observability and continually shifting transmission dynamics driven by behavior, waning immunity, seasonality, and interventions. We catalog these failure modes and show that robust performance requires making non-stationarity explicit: we extract multi-scale structure from the observed infection series and use it as an interpretable control signal for a controlled neural ODE coupled to an epidemiological model. Concretely, we decompose infections into trend, seasonal, and residual components and use these signals to drive continuous-time latent dynamics while jointly forecasting and inferring time-varying transmission, recovery, and immunity-loss rates. Across seasonal and non-seasonal settings, including early outbreaks and multi-wave regimes, our approach reduces long-horizon RMSE by 15-35%, improves peak timing error by 1-3 weeks, and lowers peak magnitude bias by up to 30% relative to strong time-series, neural ODE, and hybrid baselines, without relying on auxiliary covariates.

AI.tldr: Hybrid mechanistic–neural ODE approach for epidemiological forecasting that explicitly models non-stationarity via multi-scale decomposition (trend, seasonal, residual) as control signals, yielding robust long-horizon accuracy without auxiliary covariates.
AI.motivation: Epidemiological forecasts suffer from partial observability and time-varying transmission due to behavior, waning immunity, seasonality, and interventions. Standard couplings often fail under these non-stationarities; explicit non-stationarity modeling is needed.
AI.method: Decompose infections into trend, seasonal, and residual components. Use these signals to drive a controlled neural ODE linked to an epidemiological model, jointly forecasting and inferring time-varying transmission, recovery, and immunity-loss rates.
AI.result: Across both seasonal and non-seasonal settings (including early outbreaks and multi-wave regimes) the approach reduces long-horizon RMSE by 15–35%, improves peak-time accuracy by 1–3 weeks, and reduces peak-magnitude bias by up to 30% relative to strong baselines (time-series, neural ODE, and hybrid models) without auxiliary covariates.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, interpretable non-stationarity modeling via multi-scale structure and a controlled neural ODE enhances robustness and accuracy of epidemiological forecasts across diverse regimes, without relying on extra covariates.

title: POPL-KF: A Pose-Only Geometric Representation-Based Kalman Filter for Point-Line-Based Visual-Inertial Odometry
summary:
Mainstream Visual-inertial odometry 
(VIO) systems rely on point features for motion estimation and localization. However, their performance degrades in challenging scenarios. Moreover, the localization accuracy of multi-state constraint Kalman filter (MSCKF)-based VIO systems suffers from linearization errors associated with feature 3D coordinates and delayed measurement updates. To improve the performance of VIO in challenging scenes, we first propose a pose-only geometric representation for line features. Building on this, we develop POPL-KF, a Kalman filter-based VIO system that employs a pose-only geometric representation for both point and line features. POPL-KF mitigates linearization errors by explicitly eliminating both point and line feature coordinates from the measurement equations, while enabling immediate update of visual measurements. We also design a unified base-frames selection algorithm for both point and line features to ensure optimal constraints on camera poses within the pose-only measurement model. To further improve line feature quality, a line feature filter based on image grid segmentation and bidirectional optical flow consistency is proposed. Our system is evaluated on public datasets and real-world experiments, demonstrating that POPL-KF outperforms the state-of-the-art (SOTA) filter-based methods (OpenVINS, PO-KF) and optimization-based methods (PL-VINS, EPLF-VINS), while maintaining real-time performance.

AI.tldr: POPL-KF introduces pose-only representations for both point and line features in visual-inertial odometry, removing feature coordinates from measurement equations to mitigate linearization errors and enable immediate, real-time updates.
AI.motivation: MSCKF-based and point-feature–reliant VIO suffer from linearization errors associated with 3D feature coordinates and delays in measurement updates; challenging scenes degrade localization and pose estimation performance.
AI.method: Develop a pose-only geometric representation for line features and extend it to points, forming POPL-KF. Use a unified pose-only measurement model with base-frame selection for optimal pose constraints, and implement a line feature filter via image-grid segmentation and bidirectional optical-flow consistency. The system updates measurements immediately without recovering feature coordinates.
AI.result: Evaluations on public datasets and real-world tests show POPL-KF outperforms state-of-the-art filter-based methods (OpenVINS, PO-KF) and optimization-based methods (PL-VINS, EPLF-VINS), while maintaining real-time performance.
AI.conclusion: A pose-only, line-inclusive VIO framework reduces linearization errors and enables immediate measurement updates, yielding improved accuracy and robustness in challenging environments without sacrificing real-time operation.

title: Online Adaptive Reinforcement Learning with Echo State Networks for Non-Stationary Dynamics
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) policies trained in simulation often suffer from severe performance degradation when deployed in real-world environments due to non-stationary dynamics. While Domain Randomization (DR) and meta-RL have been proposed to address this issue, they typically rely on extensive pretraining, privileged information, or high computational cost, limiting their applicability to real-time and edge systems. In this paper, we propose a lightweight online adaptation framework for RL based on Reservoir Computing. Specifically, we integrate an Echo State Networks (ESNs) as an adaptation module that encodes recent observation histories into a latent context representation, and update its readout weights online using Recursive Least Squares (RLS). This design enables rapid adaptation without backpropagation, pretraining, or access to privileged information. We evaluate the proposed method on CartPole and HalfCheetah tasks with severe and abrupt environment changes, including periodic external disturbances and extreme friction variations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly outperforms DR and representative adaptive baselines under out-of-distribution dynamics, achieving stable adaptation within a few control steps. Notably, the method successfully handles intra-episode environment changes without resetting the policy. Due to its computational efficiency and stability, the proposed framework provides a practical solution for online adaptation in non-stationary environments and is well suited for real-world robotic control and edge deployment.

AI.tldr: Lightweight online RL adaptation using Echo State Networks with online RLS for rapid, no-backprop adaptation in non-stationary environments.
AI.motivation: RL policies trained in simulation degrade under non-stationary real-world dynamics; need real-time adaptation without heavy pretraining, privileged information, or backpropagation.
AI.method: Use an Echo State Network as an adaptation module to encode recent observations into a latent context; update ESN readout weights online via Recursive Least Squares (RLS); no backpropagation, no pretraining, no privileged information access.
AI.result: Evaluated on CartPole and HalfCheetah with severe, abrupt changes (disturbances, friction variations); outperforms Domain Randomization and adaptive baselines, achieving stable adaptation within a few steps and handling intra-episode changes without resetting the policy; suitable for real-time, edge deployment due to computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Reservoir computing–based online adaptation provides a practical, stable, and efficient solution for non-stationary RL, enabling real-time adaptation and edge deployment without heavy training.

title: Bridging the Indoor-Outdoor Gap: Vision-Centric Instruction-Guided Embodied Navigation for the Last Meters
summary: Embodied navigation holds significant promise for real-world applications such as last-mile delivery. However, most existing approaches are confined to either indoor or outdoor environments and rely heavily on strong assumptions, such as access to precise coordinate systems. While current outdoor methods can guide agents to the vicinity of a target using coarse-grained localization, they fail to enable fine-grained entry through specific building entrances, critically limiting their utility in practical deployment scenarios that require seamless outdoor-to-indoor transitions. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel task: out-to-in prior-free instruction-driven embodied navigation. This formulation explicitly eliminates reliance on accurate external priors, requiring agents to navigate solely based on egocentric visual observations guided by instructions. To tackle this task, we propose a vision-centric embodied navigation framework that leverages image-based prompts to drive decision-making. Additionally, we present the first open-source dataset for this task, featuring a pipeline that integrates trajectory-conditioned video synthesis into the data generation process. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across key metrics including success rate and path efficiency.

AI.tldr: Introduces a new out-to-in prior-free instruction-driven embodied navigation task, a vision-centric method using image prompts, and an open-source dataset with trajectory-conditioned video synthesis; shows improvements over baselines on success rate and path efficiency.
AI.motivation: Bridges the gap between outdoor coarse localization and indoor fine-grained entry, removing reliance on external priors to enable seamless outdoor-to-indoor navigation in real-world settings.
AI.method: Vision-centric navigation framework that uses egocentric visual observations guided by natural language/visual prompts; operates without precise coordinate priors; dataset pipeline integrates trajectory-conditioned video synthesis to generate training data for the task.
AI.result: Empirical results show the proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on key metrics such as success rate and path efficiency across experiments.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first open-source dataset for out-to-in prior-free instruction-driven embodied navigation and a pipeline for data generation; establishes a strong baseline and paves the way for robust outdoor-to-indoor embodied navigation without external priors.

title: Don't Break the Boundary: Continual Unlearning for OOD Detection Based on Free Energy Repulsion
summary: Deploying trustworthy AI in open-world environments faces a dual challenge: the necessity for robust Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection to ensure system safety, and the demand for flexible machine unlearning to satisfy privacy compliance and model rectification. However, this objective encounters a fundamental geometric contradiction: current OOD detectors rely on a static and compact data manifold, whereas traditional classification-oriented unlearning methods disrupt this delicate structure, leading to a catastrophic loss of the model's capability to discriminate anomalies while erasing target classes. To resolve this dilemma, we first define the problem of boundary-preserving class unlearning and propose a pivotal conceptual shift: in the context of OOD detection, effective unlearning is mathematically equivalent to transforming the target class into OOD samples. Based on this, we propose the TFER (Total Free Energy Repulsion) framework. Inspired by the free energy principle, TFER constructs a novel Push-Pull game mechanism: it anchors retained classes within a low-energy ID manifold through a pull mechanism, while actively expelling forgotten classes to high-energy OOD regions using a free energy repulsion force. This approach is implemented via parameter-efficient fine-tuning, circumventing the prohibitive cost of full retraining. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TFER achieves precise unlearning while maximally preserving the model's discriminative performance on remaining classes and external OOD data. More importantly, our study reveals that the unique Push-Pull equilibrium of TFER endows the model with inherent structural stability, allowing it to effectively resist catastrophic forgetting without complex additional constraints, thereby demonstrating exceptional potential in continual unlearning tasks.

AI.tldr: TFER offers a boundary-preserving, parameter-efficient unlearning framework for OOD detection by reframing unlearning as converting a target class into OOD samples. It uses a free-energy based push-pull mechanism to separate retained (ID) and forgotten (OOD) regions, yielding robust continual unlearning with preserved discriminative performance.
AI.motivation: Open-world AI demands robust OOD detection and compliant, privacy-preserving unlearning. Traditional classifiers rely on a fixed data manifold, but standard unlearning disrupts this, causing degradation in anomaly discrimination. The work reframes unlearning as moving the target class to the OOD region to maintain a stable manifold for OOD detection.
AI.method: TFER integrates a boundary-preserving unlearning scheme with a Push-Pull game inspired by the free energy principle. It pulls retained classes toward a low-energy ID manifold and repels forgotten classes toward high-energy OOD regions, realized via parameter-efficient fine-tuning to avoid full retraining.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows precise unlearning with minimal impact on accuracy for remaining classes and external OOD data. The Push-Pull equilibrium confers structural stability, reducing catastrophic forgetting and enabling effective continual unlearning.
AI.conclusion: TFER provides an effective, efficient framework for boundary-preserving unlearning aligned with OOD detection, offering strong performance and stability for continual unlearning tasks.

title: ChatUMM: Robust Context Tracking for Conversational Interleaved Generation
summary: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) have achieved remarkable progress yet remain constrained by a single-turn interaction paradigm, effectively functioning as solvers for independent requests rather than assistants in continuous dialogue. To bridge this gap, we present ChatUMM. As a conversational unified model, it excels at robust context tracking to sustain interleaved multimodal generation. ChatUMM derives its capabilities from two key innovations: an interleaved multi-turn training strategy that models serialized text-image streams as a continuous conversational flow, and a systematic conversational data synthesis pipeline. This pipeline transforms a diverse set of standard single-turn datasets into fluid dialogues through three progressive stages: constructing basic stateful dialogues, enforcing long-range dependency resolution via ``distractor'' turns with history-dependent query rewriting, and synthesizing naturally interleaved multimodal responses. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that ChatUMM achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source unified models on visual understanding and instruction-guided editing benchmarks, while maintaining competitive fidelity in text-to-image generation. Notably, ChatUMM exhibits superior robustness in complex multi-turn scenarios, ensuring fluid, context-aware dialogues.

AI.tldr: ChatUMM is a conversational unified multimodal model trained with interleaved multi-turn strategies and a synthetic dialogue pipeline, enabling robust context tracking and fluid interleaved text-image generation, achieving state-of-the-art results among open-source UMMs on vision and editing benchmarks while maintaining reasonable text-to-image fidelity.
AI.motivation: Unified multimodal models are strong but predominantly single-turn solvers; there is a need for continuous dialogue capability with robust context tracking and interleaved multimodal generation.
AI.method: Interleaved multi-turn training that treats serialized text-image streams as a continuous conversation. A data synthesis pipeline converts single-turn datasets into dialogues via three stages: (1) create basic stateful dialogues, (2) introduce distractor turns with history-dependent query rewriting to enforce long-range dependencies, (3) synthesize naturally interleaved multimodal responses.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source unified models on visual understanding and instruction-guided editing benchmarks; competitive fidelity in text-to-image generation; superior robustness in complex multi-turn dialogues.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that interleaved multi-turn training and deliberate synthetic dialogue data yield robust, context-aware conversational multimodal models capable of sustained interleaved generation, bridging the gap between single-turn UMMs and interactive assistants.

title: DriveWorld-VLA: Unified Latent-Space World Modeling with Vision-Language-Action for Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end (E2E) autonomous driving has recently attracted increasing interest in unifying Vision-Language-Action (VLA) with World Models to enhance decision-making and forward-looking imagination. However, existing methods fail to effectively unify future scene evolution and action planning within a single architecture due to inadequate sharing of latent states, limiting the impact of visual imagination on action decisions. To address this limitation, we propose DriveWorld-VLA, a novel framework that unifies world modeling and planning within a latent space by tightly integrating VLA and world models at the representation level, which enables the VLA planner to benefit directly from holistic scene-evolution modeling and reducing reliance on dense annotated supervision. Additionally, DriveWorld-VLA incorporates the latent states of the world model as core decision-making states for the VLA planner, facilitating the planner to assess how candidate actions impact future scene evolution. By conducting world modeling entirely in the latent space, DriveWorld-VLA supports controllable, action-conditioned imagination at the feature level, avoiding expensive pixel-level rollouts. Extensive open-loop and closed-loop evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of DriveWorld-VLA, which achieves state-of-the-art performance with 91.3 PDMS on NAVSIMv1, 86.8 EPDMS on NAVSIMv2, and 0.16 3-second average collision rate on nuScenes. Code and models will be released in https://github.com/liulin815/DriveWorld-VLA.git.

AI.tldr: DriveWorld-VLA unifies Vision-Language-Action and world models in a latent space to enable action-conditioned imagination and coherent planning, achieving state-of-the-art results on NAVSIMv1/v2 and nuScenes without pixel-level rollouts.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches fail to tightly couple future scene evolution with action planning due to insufficient sharing of latent representations, limiting the impact of imagined scenes on decisions and requiring dense supervision.
AI.method: Integrate VLA and world models at the latent representation level; use world-model latent states as the core decision-making state for the VLA planner; perform world modeling entirely in latent space to support controllable, action-conditioned imagination at the feature level, avoiding pixel-space rollouts.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on NAVSIMv1 (91.3 PDMS), NAVSIMv2 (86.8 EPDMS), and nuScenes (0.16 3-second average collision rate); validated via extensive open-loop and closed-loop evaluations; code and models to be released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: A unified latent-space framework (DriveWorld-VLA) effectively couples world modeling and planning for E2E autonomous driving, leveraging holistic scene evolution for improved decision-making while reducing supervision needs; supports future extensions and broader applicability.

title: Adversarial Learning in Games with Bandit Feedback: Logarithmic Pure-Strategy Maximin Regret
summary:
Learning to play zero-sum games is a fundamental problem in game theory and machine learning. While significant progress has been made in minimizing external regret in the self-play settings or with full-information feedback, real-world applications often force learners to play against unknown, arbitrary opponents and restrict learners to bandit feedback where only the payoff of the realized action is observable. In such challenging settings, it is well-known that $Ω(\sqrt{T})$ external regret is unavoidable (where T is the number of rounds). To overcome this barrier, we investigate adversarial learning in zero-sum games under bandit feedback, aiming to minimize the deficit against the maximin pure strategy -- a metric we term Pure-Strategy Maximin Regret.
  We analyze this problem under two bandit feedback models: uninformed (only the realized reward is revealed) and informed (both the reward and the opponent's action are revealed). For uninformed bandit learning of normal-form games, we show that the Tsallis-INF algorithm achieves $O(c \log T)$ instance-dependent regret with a game-dependent parameter $c$. Crucially, we prove an information-theoretic lower bound showing that the dependence on c is necessary. To overcome this hardness, we turn to the informed setting and introduce Maximin-UCB, which obtains another regret bound of the form $O(c' \log T)$ for a different game-dependent parameter $c'$ that could potentially be much smaller than $c$. Finally, we generalize both results to bilinear games over an arbitrary, large action set, proposing Tsallis-FTRL-SPM and Maximin-LinUCB for the uninformed and informed setting respectively and establishing similar game-dependent logarithmic regret bounds.

AI.tldr: Proposes and analyzes bandit-form zero-sum game algorithms to minimize Pure-Strategy Maximin Regret, achieving instance-dependent logarithmic regret in uninformed and informed settings, with lower bounds and extensions to bilinear games.
AI.motivation: Overcome the sqrt(T) external-regret barrier in adversarial bandit zero-sum games by focusing on regret against a maximin pure strategy; compare uninformed vs informed bandit feedback.
AI.method: Two-bandit models studied: uninformed (only payoff) and informed (payoff + opponent action). In uninformed, Tsallis-INF yields O(c log T) regret with lower bound showing dependence on c is necessary. Informed setting, Maximin-UCB achieves O(c' log T). Extension to bilinear games with Tsallis-FTRL-SPM (uninformed) and Maximin-LinUCB (informed) yielding similar logarithmic regret with game-dependent constants.
AI.result: Logarithmic-in-T regret bounds for both feedback models, with game-dependent constants c and c'. Information-theoretic lower bound proving necessity of the c-dependence in the uninformed setting. Generalization to large action sets (bilinear games) with analogous bounds.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that in adversarial zero-sum games under bandit feedback, logarithmic regret against a maximin pure strategy is achievable by tailoring algorithms to the information revealed; the performance hinges on game-specific constants and the feedback model; provides a coherent framework for scalable, game-theoretic bandit learning.

title: What Is Wrong with Synthetic Data for Scene Text Recognition? A Strong Synthetic Engine with Diverse Simulations and Self-Evolution
summary: Large-scale and categorical-balanced text data is essential for training effective Scene Text Recognition (STR) models, which is hard to achieve when collecting real data. Synthetic data offers a cost-effective and perfectly labeled alternative. However, its performance often lags behind, revealing a significant domain gap between real and current synthetic data. In this work, we systematically analyze mainstream rendering-based synthetic datasets and identify their key limitations: insufficient diversity in corpus, font, and layout, which restricts their realism in complex scenarios. To address these issues, we introduce UnionST, a strong data engine synthesizes text covering a union of challenging samples and better aligns with the complexity observed in the wild. We then construct UnionST-S, a large-scale synthetic dataset with improved simulations in challenging scenarios. Furthermore, we develop a self-evolution learning (SEL) framework for effective real data annotation. Experiments show that models trained on UnionST-S achieve significant improvements over existing synthetic datasets. They even surpass real-data performance in certain scenarios. Moreover, when using SEL, the trained models achieve competitive performance by only seeing 9% of real data labels.

AI.tldr: Proposes UnionST: a unified, diverse synthetic text engine to-close the gap between real and synthetic data for Scene Text Recognition (STR); introduces UnionST-S, a large-scale, improved-simulation synthetic dataset; adds Self-Evolution Learning (SEL) to label real data efficiently; shows strong gains, including surpassing some real-data baselines, with only 9% of real labels.
AI.motivation: Address the persistent domain gap between real and synthetic data in STR by enhancing diversity (corpus, fonts, layouts) and simulating challenging scenarios; enable efficient use of limited real labels through self-supervised/active-like strategies.
AI.method: 1) Systematic analysis of mainstream rendering-based synthetic datasets to identify limitations in diversity and realism. 2) Develop UnionST, a data engine synthesizing a union of challenging samples to better match wild-world complexity. 3) Construct UnionST-S, a large-scale synthetic dataset with improved simulations. 4) Propose Self-Evolution Learning (SEL) framework to annotate real data more effectively (likely weakly/semi-supervised).
AI.result: UnionST-S yields substantial improvements over existing synthetic datasets. In some scenarios, models trained on UnionST-S surpass real-data performance. When coupled with SEL, models achieve competitive performance with only 9% of real data labels.
AI.conclusion: Align synthetic data more closely with real-world complexity (UnionST-S) and reduce labeling costs via SEL. This approach narrows the synthetic-real domain gap and enhances STR performance with fewer real labels.

title: LIBERO-X: Robustness Litmus for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Reliable benchmarking is critical for advancing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, as it reveals their generalization, robustness, and alignment of perception with language-driven manipulation tasks. However, existing benchmarks often provide limited or misleading assessments due to insufficient evaluation protocols that inadequately capture real-world distribution shifts. This work systematically rethinks VLA benchmarking from both evaluation and data perspectives, introducing LIBERO-X, a benchmark featuring: 1) A hierarchical evaluation protocol with progressive difficulty levels targeting three core capabilities: spatial generalization, object recognition, and task instruction understanding. This design enables fine-grained analysis of performance degradation under increasing environmental and task complexity; 2) A high-diversity training dataset collected via human teleoperation, where each scene supports multiple fine-grained manipulation objectives to bridge the train-evaluation distribution gap. Experiments with representative VLA models reveal significant performance drops under cumulative perturbations, exposing persistent limitations in scene comprehension and instruction grounding. By integrating hierarchical evaluation with diverse training data, LIBERO-X offers a more reliable foundation for assessing and advancing VLA development.

AI.tldr: LIBERO-X introduces a hierarchical, difficulty-aware benchmark for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, combining a three-tier evaluation on spatial generalization, object recognition, and instruction understanding with a diverse, teleoperation-based training dataset; it reveals robustness and grounding weaknesses under perturbations.
AI.motivation: Current VLA benchmarks inadequately capture real-world distribution shifts, leading to misleading assessments of generalization, robustness, and language-grounded manipulation.
AI.method: Propose LIBERO-X: (1) a hierarchical evaluation protocol with progressively harder levels targeting spatial generalization, object recognition, and task instruction understanding; (2) a high-diversity training dataset collected via human teleoperation, supporting multiple fine-grained manipulation objectives per scene to bridge train-evaluation gaps.
AI.result: Experiments on representative VLA models show significant performance drops under cumulative perturbations, exposing persistent limitations in scene comprehension and instruction grounding; the hierarchical-evaluation plus diverse training data provide a more reliable benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Integrating hierarchical evaluation with diverse training data yields a more reliable foundation for assessing and advancing VLA development, enabling fine-grained analysis of robustness and generalization across complex manipulation tasks.

title: Enhance and Reuse: A Dual-Mechanism Approach to Boost Deep Forest for Label Distribution Learning
summary: Label distribution learning (LDL) requires the learner to predict the degree of correlation between each sample and each label. To achieve this, a crucial task during learning is to leverage the correlation among labels. Deep Forest (DF) is a deep learning framework based on tree ensembles, whose training phase does not rely on backpropagation. DF performs in-model feature transform using the prediction of each layer and achieves competitive performance on many tasks. However, its exploration in the field of LDL is still in its infancy. The few existing methods that apply DF to the field of LDL do not have effective ways to utilize the correlation among labels. Therefore, we propose a method named Enhanced and Reused Feature Deep Forest (ERDF). It mainly contains two mechanisms: feature enhancement exploiting label correlation and measure-aware feature reuse. The first one is to utilize the correlation among labels to enhance the original features, enabling the samples to acquire more comprehensive information for the task of LDL. The second one performs a reuse operation on the features of samples that perform worse than the previous layer on the validation set, in order to ensure the stability of the training process.
