This kind of Enhance-Reuse pattern not only enables samples to enrich their features but also validates the effectiveness of their new features and conducts a reuse process to prevent the noise from spreading further. Experiments show that our method outperforms other comparison algorithms on six evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: ERDF enhances Deep Forest for label distribution learning by exploiting label correlations to augment features and introducing measure-aware feature reuse to stabilize training; it yields superior performance across six metrics.
AI.motivation: LDL requires modeling degrees of association between samples and labels; existing Deep Forest methods underutilize label correlations; a gap exists in leveraging label dependencies within DF for LDL.
AI.method: Propose Enhanced and Reused Feature Deep Forest (ERDF) with two mechanisms: (1) feature enhancement by leveraging label correlation to enrich input features; (2) measure-aware feature reuse that reuses features from samples that perform worse than the previous layer on validation to stabilize training and suppress noise propagation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows ERDF outperforms competing methods on six evaluation metrics for LDL tasks.
AI.conclusion: ERDF's Enhance-Reuse pattern effectively enriches feature representations and stabilizes training, improving LDL performance when using Deep Forest.

title: Exploring Specular Reflection Inconsistency for Generalizable Face Forgery Detection
summary: Detecting deepfakes has become increasingly challenging as forgery faces synthesized by AI-generated methods, particularly diffusion models, achieve unprecedented quality and resolution. Existing forgery detection approaches relying on spatial and frequency features demonstrate limited efficacy against high-quality, entirely synthesized forgeries. In this paper, we propose a novel detection method grounded in the observation that facial attributes governed by complex physical laws and multiple parameters are inherently difficult to replicate. Specifically, we focus on illumination, particularly the specular reflection component in the Phong illumination model, which poses the greatest replication challenge due to its parametric complexity and nonlinear formulation. We introduce a fast and accurate face texture estimation method based on Retinex theory to enable precise specular reflection separation. Furthermore, drawing from the mathematical formulation of specular reflection, we posit that forgery evidence manifests not only in the specular reflection itself but also in its relationship with corresponding face texture and direct light. To address this issue, we design the Specular-Reflection-Inconsistency-Network (SRI-Net), incorporating a two-stage cross-attention mechanism to capture these correlations and integrate specular reflection related features with image features for robust forgery detection. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance on both traditional deepfake datasets and generative deepfake datasets, particularly those containing diffusion-generated forgery faces.

AI.tldr: Proposes SRI-Net to detect deepfakes by leveraging specular reflections under Phong illumination, using Retinex-based texture estimation and a two-stage cross-attention to model relationships between specular reflection, texture, and direct light; achieves state-of-the-art results on both traditional and diffusion-generated forged faces.
AI.motivation: Conventional detectors relying on spatial/frequency features struggle against high-quality AI-forgeries; specular reflection is governed by complex physics and is hard to replicate, offering a robust forensic cue.
AI.method: 1) Retinex-based fast texture estimation to separate specular reflection; 2) Interpret specular reflection via its relationship to texture and direct light; 3) SRI-Net with two-stage cross-attention to fuse specular-related features with image features; 4) evaluate on standard and diffusion-based deepfake datasets.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods on both traditional and generative deepfake datasets, with particular gains on diffusion-generated forgeries.
AI.conclusion: Physically-grounded cues from specular reflection and its link to texture and illumination can robustly detect forgeries; the proposed SRI-Net effectively models these cross-modal correlations.

title: Evaluating LLM-persona Generated Distributions for Decision-making
summary: LLMs can generate a wealth of data, ranging from simulated personas imitating human valuations and preferences, to demand forecasts based on world knowledge. But how well do such LLM-generated distributions support downstream decision-making? For example, when pricing a new product, a firm could prompt an LLM to simulate how much consumers are willing to pay based on a product description, but how useful is the resulting distribution for optimizing the price? We refer to this approach as LLM-SAA, in which an LLM is used to construct an estimated distribution and the decision is then optimized under that distribution. In this paper, we study metrics to evaluate the quality of these LLM-generated distributions, based on the decisions they induce. Taking three canonical decision-making problems (assortment optimization, pricing, and newsvendor) as examples, we find that LLM-generated distributions are practically useful, especially in low-data regimes. We also show that decision-agnostic metrics such as Wasserstein distance can be misleading when evaluating these distributions for decision-making.

AI.tldr: LLM-generated distributions can be decision-useful for downstream decisions (assortment, pricing, newsvendor), especially with limited data; relying on generic distribution metrics (e.g., Wasserstein distance) can be misleading for decision quality.
AI.motivation: Assess whether LLM-derived distributions meaningfully improve decision-making, not just statistical similarity to true distributions; fill a gap where synthetic data from LLMs is used for optimization.
AI.method: Propose LLM-SAA framework: use an LLM to construct a distribution of consumer valuations from a product description, then optimize decisions under that distribution. Evaluate with three canonical problems (assortment optimization, pricing, newsvendor) across data regimes; compare decision quality versus standard distribution metrics and baselines.
AI.result: LLM-generated distributions yield practical decision benefits, particularly in low-data regimes. Decision-focused metrics capture utility better than purely distributional metrics like Wasserstein distance, which can be misleading for decision quality.
AI.conclusion: LLM-SAA is a promising approach for decision support when data is scarce. Evaluation should be anchored in decision outcomes rather than generic distribution distances; prompts, calibration, and integration with existing data remain important considerations for robustness.

title: LAB-Det: Language as a Domain-Invariant Bridge for Training-Free One-Shot Domain Generalization in Object Detection
summary: Foundation object detectors such as GLIP and Grounding DINO excel on general-domain data but often degrade in specialized and data-scarce settings like underwater imagery or industrial defects. Typical cross-domain few-shot approaches rely on fine-tuning scarce target data, incurring cost and overfitting risks. We instead ask: Can a frozen detector adapt with only one exemplar per class without training? To answer this, we introduce training-free one-shot domain generalization for object detection, where detectors must adapt to specialized domains with only one annotated exemplar per class and no weight updates. To tackle this task, we propose LAB-Det, which exploits Language As a domain-invariant Bridge. Instead of adapting visual features, we project each exemplar into a descriptive text that conditions and guides a frozen detector. This linguistic conditioning replaces gradient-based adaptation, enabling robust generalization in data-scarce domains. We evaluate on UODD (underwater) and NEU-DET (industrial defects), two widely adopted benchmarks for data-scarce detection, where object boundaries are often ambiguous, and LAB-Det achieves up to 5.4 mAP improvement over state-of-the-art fine-tuned baselines without updating a single parameter. These results establish linguistic adaptation as an efficient and interpretable alternative to fine-tuning in specialized detection settings.

AI.tldr: Training-free one-shot domain generalization for object detection using language-conditioned prompts to adapt a frozen detector, achieving up to 5.4 mAP on data-scarce domains without weight updates.
AI.motivation: Address the degradation of foundation detectors (GLIP, Grounding DINO) in specialized and data-scarce domains; avoid costly fine-tuning by enabling a frozen detector to adapt with only one exemplar per class.
AI.method: Propose LAB-Det: Language As a domain-invariant Bridge. Each class exemplar is projected into a descriptive text that conditions a frozen detector, replacing gradient-based adaptation with linguistic conditioning.
AI.result: On underwater (UODD) and industrial defects (NEU-DET), LAB-Det yields up to 5.4 mAP improvements over strong fine-tuned baselines without updating any parameters, demonstrating robust generalization and interpretability of linguistic adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Linguistic adaptation offers an efficient, interpretable alternative to fine-tuning for specialized detection tasks, enabling effective cross-domain generalization from a single exemplar per class.

title: Uniform Spectral Growth and Convergence of Muon in LoRA-Style Matrix Factorization
summary: Spectral gradient descent (SpecGD) orthogonalizes the matrix parameter updates and has inspired practical optimizers such as Muon. They often perform well in large language model (LLM) training, but their dynamics remain poorly understood. In the low-rank adaptation (LoRA) setting, where weight updates are parameterized as a product of two low-rank factors, we find a distinctive spectral phenomenon under Muon in LoRA fine-tuning of LLMs: singular values of the LoRA product show near-uniform growth across the spectrum, despite orthogonalization being performed on the two factors separately. Motivated by this observation, we analyze spectral gradient flow (SpecGF)-a continuous-time analogue of SpecGD-in a simplified LoRA-style matrix factorization setting and prove "equal-rate" dynamics: all singular values grow at equal rates up to small deviations. Consequently, smaller singular values attain their target values earlier than larger ones, sharply contrasting with the largest-first stepwise learning observed in standard gradient flow. Moreover, we prove that SpecGF in our setting converges to global minima from almost all initializations, provided the factor norms remain bounded; with $\ell_2$ regularization, we obtain global convergence. Lastly, we corroborate our theory with experiments in the same setting.

AI.tldr: Spectral gradient flow in LoRA-like factorization exhibits equal-rate growth of singular values; under mild conditions SpecGF converges to global minima, with smaller singular values reaching targets earlier; experiments support theory.
AI.motivation: To explain and formalize the spectral dynamics observed for SpecGD/Muon in LoRA fine-tuning of LLMs and to provide convergence guarantees for the continuous-time analogue.
AI.method: Analyze a simplified LoRA-style matrix factorization under SpecGF; derive equal-rate dynamics showing all singular values grow at the same rate (up to small deviations); prove global convergence from almost all initializations when factor norms are bounded; extend with L2 regularization; corroborate with experiments.
AI.result: Derived equal-rate growth of singular values; smaller eigenvalues reach targets earlier; contrasting behavior with largest-first gradient flow; global convergence to minima under stated conditions; empirical results consistent with theory.
AI.conclusion: SpecGF explains the observed spectral behavior in LoRA fine-tuning and provides global convergence guarantees in this setting, supported by experiments.

title: Instance-Free Domain Adaptive Object Detection
summary: While Domain Adaptive Object Detection (DAOD) has made significant strides, most methods rely on unlabeled target data that is assumed to contain sufficient foreground instances. However, in many practical scenarios (e.g., wildlife monitoring, lesion detection), collecting target domain data with objects of interest is prohibitively costly, whereas background-only data is abundant. This common practical constraint introduces a significant technical challenge: the difficulty of achieving domain alignment when target instances are unavailable, forcing adaptation to rely solely on the target background information. We formulate this challenge as the novel problem of Instance-Free Domain Adaptive Object Detection. To tackle this, we propose the Relational and Structural Consistency Network (RSCN) which pioneers an alignment strategy based on background feature prototypes while simultaneously encouraging consistency in the relationship between the source foreground features and the background features within each domain, enabling robust adaptation even without target instances. To facilitate research, we further curate three specialized benchmarks, including simulative auto-driving detection, wildlife detection, and lung nodule detection. Extensive experiments show that RSCN significantly outperforms existing DAOD methods across all three benchmarks in the instance-free scenario. The code and benchmarks will be released soon.

AI.tldr: Proposes Instance-Free Domain Adaptive Object Detection (IF-DAOD) using Relational and Structural Consistency Network (RSCN) that aligns via background prototypes and cross-domain relational constraints, enabling adaptation without target instances; introduces three specialized benchmarks for simulative auto-driving, wildlife, and lung nodules.
AI.motivation: Many real-world DAOD settings lack target foreground data; relying on target background alone presents a practical and understudied challenge. Traditional DAOD methods assume availability of target objects for alignment, which is often infeasible in applications like wildlife monitoring or medical lesion detection.
AI.method: RSCN aligns domains using background feature prototypes and enforces relational/structural consistency between source foreground and target background features within each domain. Designed to operate without target instances; includes losses for background alignment, relation consistency, and structural relationships.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three proposed benchmarks shows RSCN outperforms existing DAOD methods in the instance-free setting, demonstrating robustness and generalization; code and benchmarks to be released.
AI.conclusion: Instance-free DAOD is feasible with relational and structural consistency constraints; the approach broadens applicability of DAOD and provides new benchmarks for evaluation.

title: Generating High-quality Privacy-preserving Synthetic Data
summary: Synthetic tabular data enables sharing and analysis of sensitive records, but its practical deployment requires balancing distributional fidelity, downstream utility, and privacy protection. We study a simple, model agnostic post processing framework that can be applied on top of any synthetic data generator to improve this trade off. First, a mode patching step repairs categories that are missing or severely underrepresented in the synthetic data, while largely preserving learned dependencies. Second, a k nearest neighbor filter replaces synthetic records that lie too close to real data points, enforcing a minimum distance between real and synthetic samples. We instantiate this framework for two neural generative models for tabular data, a feed forward generator and a variational autoencoder, and evaluate it on three public datasets covering credit card transactions, cardiovascular health, and census based income. We assess marginal and joint distributional similarity, the performance of models trained on synthetic data and evaluated on real data, and several empirical privacy indicators, including nearest neighbor distances and attribute inference attacks. With moderate thresholds between 0.2 and 0.35, the post processing reduces divergence between real and synthetic categorical distributions by up to 36 percent and improves a combined measure of pairwise dependence preservation by 10 to 14 percent, while keeping downstream predictive performance within about 1 percent of the unprocessed baseline. At the same time, distance based privacy indicators improve and the success rate of attribute inference attacks remains largely unchanged. These results provide practical guidance for selecting thresholds and applying post hoc repairs to improve the quality and empirical privacy of synthetic tabular data, while complementing approaches that provide formal differential privacy guarantees.

AI.tldr: Post-processing for synthetic tabular data with mode patching and a kNN filter improves distribution fidelity and joint dependence while maintaining utility; gains at moderate thresholds across models and datasets; complements DP approaches.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off among distributional fidelity, downstream utility, and privacy in synthetic tabular data; a model-agnostic post-processing step can repair underrepresented categories and enforce a privacy-preserving distance to real samples.
AI.method: Two-step post-processing: mode patching to repair missing/underrepresented categories; kNN filter to enforce minimum real-synthetic distance. Applied to two neural generators (feed-forward and VAE). Evaluated on three public datasets (credit card transactions, cardiovascular health, census income). Metrics include marginal/joint distribution similarity, predictive models trained on synthetic evaluated on real, and empirical privacy indicators (NN distances, attribute inference attacks).
AI.result: With thresholds 0.2–0.35, real-synthetic categorical divergence reduced up to 36%; joint dependence preservation improved by 10–14%; downstream model accuracy within ~1% of unprocessed baseline; distance-based privacy indicators improved and attribute inference attack success largely unchanged.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides practical guidance for threshold selection and post hoc repairs to enhance quality and empirical privacy of synthetic tabular data, while complementing formal differential privacy guarantees.

title: Rebenchmarking Unsupervised Monocular 3D Occupancy Prediction
summary: Inferring the 3D structure from a single image, particularly in occluded regions, remains a fundamental yet unsolved challenge in vision-centric autonomous driving. Existing unsupervised approaches typically train a neural radiance field and treat the network outputs as occupancy probabilities during evaluation, overlooking the inconsistency between training and evaluation protocols. Moreover, the prevalent use of 2D ground truth fails to reveal the inherent ambiguity in occluded regions caused by insufficient geometric constraints. To address these issues, this paper presents a reformulated benchmark for unsupervised monocular 3D occupancy prediction. We first interpret the variables involved in the volume rendering process and identify the most physically consistent representation of the occupancy probability. Building on these analyses, we improve existing evaluation protocols by aligning the newly identified representation with voxel-wise 3D occupancy ground truth, thereby enabling unsupervised methods to be evaluated in a manner consistent with that of supervised approaches. Additionally, to impose explicit constraints in occluded regions, we introduce an occlusion-aware polarization mechanism that incorporates multi-view visual cues to enhance discrimination between occupied and free spaces in these regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach not only significantly outperforms existing unsupervised approaches but also matches the performance of supervised ones. Our source code and evaluation protocol will be made available upon publication.

AI.tldr: A physically consistent occupancy representation and occlusion-aware multi-view refinement are proposed to reframe unsupervised monocular 3D occupancy prediction. The benchmark aligns training and evaluation with voxel-level occupancy, addressing occlusion ambiguity and enabling unsupervised methods to reach supervised performance.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised monocular 3D occupancy methods suffer from training-evaluation mismatch and occlusion ambiguity due to limited geometric constraints, often evaluated against 2D ground truth.
AI.method: 1) Reformulate the benchmark and identify the physically consistent representation of occupancy probability within volume rendering. 2) Align evaluation with voxel-wise 3D occupancy ground truth. 3) Introduce an occlusion-aware polarization mechanism that leverages multi-view visual cues to better distinguish occupied vs free space in occluded regions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the proposed approach significantly outperforms existing unsupervised methods and matches supervised performance.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a consistent evaluation framework for unsupervised monocular 3D occupancy, adds explicit occlusion constraints, and achieves competitive performance without supervision; code and protocol will be released.

title: Near-Optimal Regret for Distributed Adversarial Bandits: A Black-Box Approach
summary: We study distributed adversarial bandits, where $N$ agents cooperate to minimize the global average loss while observing only their own local losses. We show that the minimax regret for this problem is $\tildeΘ(\sqrt{(ρ^{-1/2}+K/N)T})$, where $T$ is the horizon, $K$ is the number of actions, and $ρ$ is the spectral gap of the communication matrix. Our algorithm, based on a novel black-box reduction to bandits with delayed feedback, requires agents to communicate only through gossip. It achieves an upper bound that significantly improves over the previous best bound $\tilde{O}(ρ^{-1/3}(KT)^{2/3})$ of Yi and Vojnovic (2023). We complement this result with a matching lower bound, showing that the problem's difficulty decomposes into a communication cost $ρ^{-1/4}\sqrt{T}$ and a bandit cost $\sqrt{KT/N}$. We further demonstrate the versatility of our approach by deriving first-order and best-of-both-worlds bounds in the distributed adversarial setting. Finally, we extend our framework to distributed linear bandits in $R^d$, obtaining a regret bound of $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{(ρ^{-1/2}+1/N)dT})$, achieved with only $O(d)$ communication cost per agent and per round via a volumetric spanner.

AI.tldr: Tight regret bounds for distributed adversarial bandits with gossip communication, plus extensions to distributed linear bandits.
AI.motivation: Quantify how communication constraints (spectral gap ρ) interact with bandit learning (K actions, T rounds, N agents) to determine regret, and understand fundamental trade-offs between communication and learning.
AI.method: Black-box reduction to bandits with delayed feedback coupled with a gossip-based protocol; use spectral gap ρ; prove tight upper and lower bounds; extend framework to distributed linear bandits using a volumetric spanner, achieving efficient communication.
AI.result: Upper bound: minimax regret ≈ sqrt((ρ^{-1/2}+K/N) T) (up to log factors). Matching lower bound showing decomposition into communication cost ρ^{-1/4} sqrt(T) and bandit cost sqrt(KT/N). Also first-order and best-of-both-worlds bounds in the distributed adversarial setting. For distributed linear bandits in R^d: regret ≈ sqrt((ρ^{-1/2}+1/N) d T) with O(d) communication per agent per round via volumetric spanner.
AI.conclusion: Gossip-based, black-box reduction yields near-optimal regret in distributed adversarial bandits, revealing a clean separation between communication and bandit costs; the approach extends to linear settings and is versatile for multiple performance guarantees.

title: DreamHome-Pano: Design-Aware and Conflict-Free Panoramic Interior Generation
summary: In modern interior design, the generation of personalized spaces frequently necessitates a delicate balance between rigid architectural structural constraints and specific stylistic preferences. However, existing multi-condition generative frameworks often struggle to harmonize these inputs, leading to "condition conflicts" where stylistic attributes inadvertently compromise the geometric precision of the layout. To address this challenge, we present DreamHome-Pano, a controllable panoramic generation framework designed for high-fidelity interior synthesis. Our approach introduces a Prompt-LLM that serves as a semantic bridge, effectively translating layout constraints and style references into professional descriptive prompts to achieve precise cross-modal alignment. To safeguard architectural integrity during the generative process, we develop a Conflict-Free Control architecture that incorporates structural-aware geometric priors and a multi-condition decoupling strategy, effectively suppressing stylistic interference from eroding the spatial layout. Furthermore, we establish a comprehensive panoramic interior benchmark alongside a multi-stage training pipeline, encompassing progressive Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL). Experimental results demonstrate that DreamHome-Pano achieves a superior balance between aesthetic quality and structural consistency, offering a robust and professional-grade solution for panoramic interior visualization.

AI.tldr: DreamHome-Pano introduces a controllable panoramic interior generation framework that uses a Prompt-LLM to translate layout/style constraints into descriptive prompts and a Conflict-Free Control architecture with geometric priors and decoupling to suppress style interference, coupled with a multi-stage SFT+RL training pipeline and a panoramic interior benchmark, achieving better aesthetic quality and structural consistency.
AI.motivation: To resolve the conflict between stylistic conditioning and strict architectural layout in multi-condition generative models for interior design, enabling high-fidelity, professionally coherent panoramic scenes.
AI.method: 1) Prompt-LLM as semantic bridge translating layout constraints and style references into professional prompts. 2) Conflict-Free Control with structural-aware geometric priors and multi-condition decoupling to prevent style from distorting geometry. 3) Panoramic interior benchmark and a multi-stage training pipeline including progressive Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL).
AI.result: Empirically, DreamHome-Pano achieves a superior balance between aesthetic quality and structural consistency in panoramic interiors, showing robustness in maintaining layout fidelity while preserving design style.
AI.conclusion: DreamHome-Pano offers a practical, scalable solution for high-quality panoramic interior visualization with strong cross-modal alignment and architectural integrity; it provides benchmarks and a training protocol that can be extended to related multi-condition generation tasks.

title: EEG Emotion Classification Using an Enhanced Transformer-CNN-BiLSTM Architecture with Dual Attention Mechanisms
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG)-based emotion recognition plays a critical role in affective computing and emerging decision-support systems, yet remains challenging due to high-dimensional, noisy, and subject-dependent signals. This study investigates whether hybrid deep learning architectures that integrate convolutional, recurrent, and attention-based components can improve emotion classification performance and robustness in EEG data. We propose an enhanced hybrid model that combines convolutional feature extraction, bidirectional temporal modeling, and self-attention mechanisms with regularization strategies to mitigate overfitting. Experiments conducted on a publicly available EEG dataset spanning three emotional states (neutral, positive, and negative) demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art classification performance, significantly outperforming classical machine learning and neural baselines. Statistical tests confirm the robustness of these performance gains under cross-validation. Feature-level analyses further reveal that covariance-based EEG features contribute most strongly to emotion discrimination, highlighting the importance of inter-channel relationships in affective modeling. These findings suggest that carefully designed hybrid architectures can effectively balance predictive accuracy, robustness, and interpretability in EEG-based emotion recognition, with implications for applied affective computing and human-centered intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: A hybrid CNN–RNN–attention model with regularization improves EEG-based emotion recognition, achieving state-of-the-art results on a three-emotion dataset; covariance-based inter-channel features are most informative; cross-validated robustness demonstrated; supports balance of accuracy, robustness, and interpretability in affective EEG modeling.
AI.motivation: EEG signals for emotion recognition are high-dimensional, noisy, and subject-dependent. There is a need for models that capture spatial, temporal, and inter-channel relationships while remaining robust and interpretable. Hybrid architectures that integrate convolutional, recurrent, and attention mechanisms are proposed to address these challenges.
AI.method: An enhanced hybrid model combines convolutional feature extraction, bidirectional temporal modeling, and self-attention, augmented with regularization techniques to mitigate overfitting. Evaluation on a public EEG dataset with three emotional states (neutral, positive, negative) against classical machine learning and neural baselines, using cross-validation. Feature analyses highlight inter-channel covariance features as most discriminative.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art classification performance and significantly outperforms baselines. Statistical tests confirm robustness under cross-validation. Covariance-based EEG features contribute most to emotion discrimination, underscoring the importance of inter-channel relationships in affective modeling.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed hybrid architectures can effectively balance predictive accuracy, robustness, and interpretability in EEG-based emotion recognition, with implications for applied affective computing and human-centered intelligent systems.

title: Forest canopy height estimation from satellite RGB imagery using large-scale airborne LiDAR-derived training data and monocular depth estimation
summary: Large-scale, high-resolution forest canopy height mapping plays a crucial role in understanding regional and global carbon and water cycles. Spaceborne LiDAR missions, including the Ice, Cloud, and Land Elevation Satellite-2 (ICESat-2) and the Global Ecosystem Dynamics Investigation (GEDI), provide global observations of forest structure but are spatially sparse and subject to inherent uncertainties. In contrast, near-surface LiDAR platforms, such as airborne and unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) LiDAR systems, offer much finer measurements of forest canopy structure, and a growing number of countries have made these datasets openly available. In this study, a state-of-the-art monocular depth estimation model, Depth Anything V2, was trained using approximately 16,000 km2 of canopy height models (CHMs) derived from publicly available airborne LiDAR point clouds and related products across multiple countries, together with 3 m resolution PlanetScope and airborne RGB imagery. The trained model, referred to as Depth2CHM, enables the estimation of spatially continuous CHMs directly from PlanetScope RGB imagery. Independent validation was conducted at sites in China (approximately 1 km2) and the United States (approximately 116 km2). The results showed that Depth2CHM could accurately estimate canopy height, with biases of 0.59 m and 0.41 m and root mean square errors (RMSEs) of 2.54 m and 5.75 m for these two sites, respectively. Compared with an existing global meter-resolution CHM product, the mean absolute error is reduced by approximately 1.5 m and the RMSE by approximately 2 m. These results demonstrated that monocular depth estimation networks trained with large-scale airborne LiDAR-derived canopy height data provide a promising and scalable pathway for high-resolution, spatially continuous forest canopy height estimation from satellite RGB imagery.

AI.tldr: A monocular depth estimation approach (Depth2CHM) trained on airborne LiDAR-derived canopy height models (CHMs) and RGB imagery to estimate high-resolution, continuous canopy height maps from satellite RGB; validated at sites in China and the US; outperforms a global meter-scale CHM product; demonstrates scalable CHM mapping from RGB data.
AI.motivation: To overcome spatial sparsity and uncertainties in spaceborne LiDAR (ICESat-2, GEDI) by leveraging abundant open-access airborne LiDAR-derived CHMs and RGB imagery to produce high-resolution, spatially continuous canopy height maps.
AI.method: Train Depth Anything V2, a monocular depth estimation model, on ~16,000 km² of CHMs derived from publicly available airborne LiDAR and related products across multiple countries, using 3 m PlanetScope RGB imagery and airborne RGB data; deploy the trained model (Depth2CHM) to estimate CHMs directly from PlanetScope RGB imagery; validate on independent sites in China (~1 km²) and the United States (~116 km²).
AI.result: Depth2CHM yields accurate canopy height estimates with biases of 0.59 m (China) and 0.41 m (US); RMSE of 2.54 m (China) and 5.75 m (US). Compared to an existing global meter-resolution CHM product, mean absolute error is reduced by ~1.5 m and RMSE by ~2 m.
AI.conclusion: Monocular depth estimation networks trained with large-scale airborne LiDAR-derived CHMs enable a promising, scalable pathway for high-resolution, spatially continuous forest canopy height estimation from satellite RGB imagery.

title: Adaptive Protein Tokenization
summary: Tokenization is a promising path to multi-modal models capable of jointly understanding protein sequences, structure, and function. Existing protein structure tokenizers create tokens by pooling information from local neighborhoods, an approach that limits their performance on generative and representation tasks. In this work, we present a method for global tokenization of protein structures in which successive tokens contribute increasing levels of detail to a global representation. This change resolves several issues with generative models based on local protein tokenization: it mitigates error accumulation, provides embeddings without sequence-reduction operations, and allows task-specific adaptation of a tokenized sequence's information content. We validate our method on reconstruction, generative, and representation tasks and demonstrate that it matches or outperforms existing models based on local protein structure tokenizers. We show how adaptive tokens enable inference criteria based on information content, which boosts designability. We validate representations generated from our tokenizer on CATH classification tasks and demonstrate that non-linear probing on our tokenized sequences outperforms equivalent probing on representations from other tokenizers. Finally, we demonstrate how our method supports zero-shot protein shrinking and affinity maturation.

AI.tldr: Global, progressively detailed tokenization of protein structures outperforms local-tokenizers across reconstruction, generation, and representation tasks, enabling adaptive information-content and zero-shot design capabilities.
AI.motivation: Local protein structure tokenizers rely on pooling local neighborhoods, causing error accumulation, sequence-reduction, and limited adaptability; a global, progressively detailed tokenizer could mitigate these issues and improve generative and discriminative performance.
AI.method: Introduce a global tokenization scheme where successive tokens encode increasing levels of geometric/structural detail to build a global representation; tokens are adaptive, enabling information-content-based inference criteria; evaluate on reconstruction, generative, and representation benchmarks, including CATH classification, with non-linear probing; demonstrate zero-shot protein shrinking and affinity maturation.
AI.result: The global tokenization approach matches or surpasses local-tokenizer baselines across tasks; adaptive tokens improve designability; representations probed non-linearly outperform baselines; method supports zero-shot shrinking and affinity maturation.
AI.conclusion: Global, adaptive tokenization provides robust, scalable protein structure representations, mitigating error accumulation and sequence-reduction issues, and enabling task-specific information-content control for improved generative and design tasks.

title: Beyond Code Contributions: How Network Position, Temporal Bursts, and Code Review Activities Shape Contributor Influence in Large-Scale Open Source Ecosystems
summary: Open source software (OSS) projects rely on complex networks of contributors whose interactions drive innovation and sustainability. This study presents a comprehensive analysis of OSS contributor networks using advanced graph neural networks and temporal network analysis on data spanning 25 years from the Cloud Native Computing Foundation ecosystem, encompassing sandbox, incubating, and graduated projects. Our analysis of thousands of contributors across hundreds of repositories reveals that OSS networks exhibit strong power-law distributions in influence, with the top 1\% of contributors controlling a substantial portion of network influence. Using GPU-accelerated PageRank, betweenness centrality, and custom LSTM models, we identify five distinct contributor roles: Core, Bridge, Connector, Regular, and Peripheral, each with unique network positions and structural importance. Statistical analysis reveals significant correlations between specific action types (commits, pull requests, issues) and contributor influence, with multiple regression models explaining substantial variance in influence metrics. Temporal analysis shows that network density, clustering coefficients, and modularity exhibit statistically significant temporal trends, with distinct regime changes coinciding with major project milestones. Structural integrity simulations show that Bridge contributors, despite representing a small fraction of the network, have a disproportionate impact on network cohesion when removed. Our findings provide empirical evidence for strategic contributor retention policies and offer actionable insights into community health metrics.

AI.tldr: OSS contributor networks in the CNCF ecosystem show heavy-tailed influence; five contributor roles exist; temporal network analysis reveals regime shifts; Bridge role disproportionately maintains cohesion; results inform retention and health metrics.
AI.motivation: To quantify how individual contributors shape OSS network structure and project health over 25 years, enabling data-driven governance.
AI.method: Combine GPU-accelerated PageRank, betweenness, and LSTM-based role classification on 25-year CNCF data across sandbox, incubating, and graduated projects; analyze action-level correlations, temporal metrics (density, clustering, modularity), and structural integrity via removal simulations.
AI.result: Power-law influence; five roles (Core, Bridge, Connector, Regular, Peripheral); significant correlations between commits/PRs/issues and influence; temporal trends with regime changes; Bridges critical for cohesion; retention policies suggested.
AI.conclusion: Empirical support for strategic contributor retention; provides community-health metrics and actionable governance insights.

title: Reclaiming First Principles: A Differentiable Framework for Conceptual Hydrologic Models
summary: Conceptual hydrologic models remain the cornerstone of rainfall-runoff modeling, yet their calibration is often slow and numerically fragile. Most gradient-based parameter estimation methods rely on finite-difference approximations or automatic differentiation frameworks (e.g., JAX, PyTorch and TensorFlow), which are computationally demanding and introduce truncation errors, solver instabilities, and substantial overhead. These limitations are particularly acute for the ODE systems of conceptual watershed models. Here we introduce a fully analytic and computationally efficient framework for differentiable hydrologic modeling based on exact parameter sensitivities. By augmenting the governing ODE system with sensitivity equations, we jointly evolve the model states and the Jacobian matrix with respect to all parameters. This Jacobian then provides fully analytic gradient vectors for any differentiable loss function. These include classical objective functions such as the sum of absolute and squared residuals, widely used hydrologic performance metrics such as the Nash-Sutcliffe and Kling-Gupta efficiencies, robust loss functions that down-weight extreme events, and hydrograph-based functionals such as flow-duration and recession curves. The analytic sensitivities eliminate the step-size dependence and noise inherent to numerical differentiation, while avoiding the instability of adjoint methods and the overhead of modern machine-learning autodiff toolchains. The resulting gradients are deterministic, physically interpretable, and straightforward to embed in gradient-based optimizers. Overall, this work enables rapid, stable, and transparent gradient-based calibration of conceptual hydrologic models, unlocking the full potential of differentiable modeling without reliance on external, opaque, or CPU-intensive automatic-differentiation libraries.

AI.tldr: Analytic differentiation framework for conceptual hydrologic models using augmented ODEs with sensitivity equations to yield exact gradients, enabling fast, stable gradient-based calibration without autodiff.
AI.motivation: Calibrating conceptual rainfall–runoff models is slow and numerically fragile when relying on finite differences or automatic differentiation, due to truncation errors, solver instabilities, and computational overhead.
AI.method: Augment the governing ODEs with sensitivity equations to jointly evolve states and the Jacobian with respect to all parameters. The resulting Jacobian provides fully analytic gradients for diverse differentiable losses and hydrologic functionals (e.g., residuals, Nash–Sutcliffe, Kling-Gupta, robust losses, flow-duration/recession curves). Avoids numerical differentiation and adjoint/ autodiff overhead.
AI.result: Analytic sensitivities yield deterministic, physically interpretable gradients, eliminating step-size noise and autodiff overhead. Gradients integrate directly into gradient-based optimizers, enabling rapid, stable, and transparent calibration of conceptual hydrologic models without reliance on opaque external autodiff libraries.
AI.conclusion: This approach enables swift, stable, and transparent gradient-based calibration of conceptual hydrologic models by providing exact, analytic gradients through sensitivity-augmented ODEs, reducing reliance on external autodiff frameworks.

title: MicroBi-ConvLSTM: An Ultra-Lightweight Efficient Model for Human Activity Recognition on Resource Constrained Devices
summary: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) on resource constrained wearables requires models that balance accuracy against strict memory and computational budgets. State of the art lightweight architectures such as TinierHAR (34K parameters) and TinyHAR (55K parameters) achieve strong accuracy, but exceed memory budgets of microcontrollers with limited SRAM once operating system overhead is considered. We present MicroBi-ConvLSTM, an ultra-lightweight convolutional-recurrent architecture achieving 11.4K parameters on average through two stage convolutional feature extraction with 4x temporal pooling and a single bidirectional LSTM layer. This represents 2.9x parameter reduction versus TinierHAR and 11.9x versus DeepConvLSTM while preserving linear O(N) complexity. Evaluation across eight diverse HAR benchmarks shows that MicroBi-ConvLSTM maintains competitive performance within the ultra-lightweight regime: 93.41% macro F1 on UCI-HAR, 94.46% on SKODA assembly gestures, and 88.98% on Daphnet gait freeze detection. Systematic ablation reveals task dependent component contributions where bidirectionality benefits episodic event detection, but provides marginal gains on periodic locomotion. INT8 post training quantization incurs only 0.21% average F1-score degradation, yielding a 23.0 KB average deployment footprint suitable for memory constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: Ultra-lightweight MicroBi-ConvLSTM achieves competitive HAR accuracy with 11.4K parameters and 23 KB footprint, using two-stage conv feature extraction and a single bidirectional LSTM; large parameter reductions vs TinierHAR/DeepConvLSTM; efficiency preserved (O(N) complexity).
AI.motivation: Need HAR on resource-constrained wearables/microcontrollers under OS overhead; existing lightweight models exceed memory budgets; require low memory, low compute, maintain accuracy.
AI.method: Two-stage convolutional feature extraction with 4x temporal pooling; single bidirectional LSTM layer; parameter count ~11.4K; INT8 post-training quantization; evaluation across eight HAR benchmarks; ablation studies; memory footprint 23 KB.
AI.result: Macro F1: UCI-HAR 93.41%; SKODA 94.46%; Daphnet 88.98%; 2.9x reduction vs TinierHAR; 11.9x vs DeepConvLSTM; 0.21% degradation with INT8; architecture maintains linear O(N) complexity.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates ultra-lightweight HAR models can operate under strict edge budgets while retaining competitive accuracy; bidirectional LSTM improves episodic event detection; periodic locomotion gains limited from bidirectionality; deployment footprint suitable for constrained edge devices.

title: Is Gradient Ascent Really Necessary? Memorize to Forget for Machine Unlearning
summary: For ethical and safe AI, machine unlearning rises as a critical topic aiming to protect sensitive, private, and copyrighted knowledge from misuse. To achieve this goal, it is common to conduct gradient ascent (GA) to reverse the training on undesired data. However, such a reversal is prone to catastrophic collapse, which leads to serious performance degradation in general tasks. As a solution, we propose model extrapolation as an alternative to GA, which reaches the counterpart direction in the hypothesis space from one model given another reference model. Therefore, we leverage the original model as the reference, further train it to memorize undesired data while keeping prediction consistency on the rest retained data, to obtain a memorization model. Counterfactual as it might sound, a forget model can be obtained via extrapolation from the memorization model to the reference model. Hence, we avoid directly acquiring the forget model using GA, but proceed with gradient descent for the memorization model, which successfully stabilizes the machine unlearning process. Our model extrapolation is simple and efficient to implement, and it can also effectively converge throughout training to achieve improved unlearning performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes model extrapolation as a stable alternative to gradient ascent for machine unlearning, enabling forgetting by extrapolating from a memorization model back toward a reference model to avoid catastrophic collapse.
AI.motivation: Unlearning aims to remove or mitigate the influence of undesired data, but gradient ascent-based reversals frequently collapse and degrade performance; a stable, efficient method is needed to protect sensitive/private knowledge.
AI.method: Use the original (reference) model as a base; train a memorization model to fit undesired data while preserving predictions on retained data; extrapolate from the memorization model toward the reference to obtain the forget model; perform gradient descent on the memorization model to achieve unlearning without directly performing GA.
AI.result: Claimed benefits include simplicity and efficiency of implementation, stable convergence during training, and improved unlearning performance compared with GA-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Model extrapolation offers a practical, stable alternative to gradient ascent for machine unlearning, enabling effective forgetting by navigating the hypothesis space via extrapolation rather than direct reversal.

title: AdaptOVCD: Training-Free Open-Vocabulary Remote Sensing Change Detection via Adaptive Information Fusion
summary: Remote sensing change detection plays a pivotal role in domains such as environmental monitoring, urban planning, and disaster assessment. However, existing methods typically rely on predefined categories and large-scale pixel-level annotations, which limit their generalization and applicability in open-world scenarios. To address these limitations, this paper proposes AdaptOVCD, a training-free Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (OVCD) architecture based on dual-dimensional multi-level information fusion. The framework integrates multi-level information fusion across data, feature, and decision levels vertically while incorporating targeted adaptive designs horizontally, achieving deep synergy among heterogeneous pre-trained models to effectively mitigate error propagation. Specifically, (1) at the data level, Adaptive Radiometric Alignment (ARA) fuses radiometric statistics with original texture features and synergizes with SAM-HQ to achieve radiometrically consistent segmentation; (2) at the feature level, Adaptive Change Thresholding (ACT) combines global difference distributions with edge structure priors and leverages DINOv3 to achieve robust change detection; (3) at the decision level, Adaptive Confidence Filtering (ACF) integrates semantic confidence with spatial constraints and collaborates with DGTRS-CLIP to achieve high-confidence semantic identification. Comprehensive evaluations across nine scenarios demonstrate that AdaptOVCD detects arbitrary category changes in a zero-shot manner, significantly outperforming existing training-free methods. Meanwhile, it achieves 84.89\% of the fully-supervised performance upper bound in cross-dataset evaluations and exhibits superior generalization capabilities. The code is available at https://github.com/Dmygithub/AdaptOVCD.

AI.tldr: A training-free open-vocabulary change detection framework (AdaptOVCD) uses cross-level fusion (data, feature, decision) and cross-model synergy to detect arbitrary changes without annotations, showing strong zero-shot performance and solid cross-dataset generalization.
AI.motivation: Open-world remote sensing change detection is constrained by reliance on predefined categories and abundant pixel-level labels; there is a need for scalable, training-free, generalizable OVCD methods.
AI.method: Proposes dual-dimensional multi-level fusion with vertical fusion across data/feature/decision levels and horizontal adaptive designs. Data level: Adaptive Radiometric Alignment (ARA) with SAM-HQ for radiometrically consistent segmentation. Feature level: Adaptive Change Thresholding (ACT) combines global difference distributions with edge priors and uses DINOv3. Decision level: Adaptive Confidence Filtering (ACF) integrates semantic confidence with spatial constraints and collaborates with DGTRS-CLIP. Integrates heterogeneous pre-trained models to mitigate error propagation and enhance robustness.
AI.result: Evaluation across nine scenarios shows zero-shot detection of arbitrary category changes and superior performance relative to training-free baselines; cross-dataset evaluations reach 84.89% of a fully-supervised upper bound, indicating strong generalization. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: AdaptOVCD demonstrates that training-free open-vocabulary change detection is feasible with a well-designed multi-level fusion framework and cross-model synergy, achieving strong zero-shot and cross-dataset performance while maintaining radiometric consistency and high-confidence semantic IDs.

title: Principle-Evolvable Scientific Discovery via Uncertainty Minimization
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based scientific agents have accelerated scientific discovery, yet they often suffer from significant inefficiencies due to adherence to fixed initial priors. Existing approaches predominantly operate within a static hypothesis space, which restricts the discovery of novel phenomena, resulting in computational waste when baseline theories fail. To address this, we propose shifting the focus from searching hypotheses to evolving the underlying scientific principles. We present PiEvo, a principle-evolvable framework that treats scientific discovery as Bayesian optimization over an expanding principle space. By integrating Information-Directed Hypothesis Selection via Gaussian Process and an anomaly-driven augmentation mechanism, PiEvo enables agents to autonomously refine their theoretical worldview. Evaluation across four benchmarks demonstrates that PiEvo (1) achieves an average solution quality of up to 90.81%~93.15%, representing a 29.7%~31.1% improvement over the state-of-the-art, (2) attains an 83.3% speedup in convergence step via significantly reduced sample complexity by optimizing the compact principle space, and (3) maintains robust performance across diverse scientific domains and LLM backbones.

AI.tldr: PiEvo frames scientific discovery as Bayesian optimization over an expanding principle space, shifting from fixed hypotheses to evolving scientific principles. It combines Information-Directed Hypothesis Selection via Gaussian Process with an anomaly-driven augmentation mechanism, achieving substantial performance gains across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Fixed initial priors and static hypothesis spaces hinder discovery of novel phenomena and lead to computational waste when baselines fail.
AI.method: Bayesian optimization over an expanding principle space; Information-Directed Hypothesis Selection via Gaussian Process; anomaly-driven augmentation to refine the theoretical worldview.
AI.result: On four benchmarks, PiEvo achieves 90.81–93.15% solution quality, 29.7–31.1% improvement over SOTA, 83.3% faster convergence (reduced sample complexity), robust across domains and LLM backbones.
AI.conclusion: PiEvo enables autonomous evolution of scientific principles, improving efficiency and robustness of LLM-based scientific agents.

title: Universal Anti-forensics Attack against Image Forgery Detection via Multi-modal Guidance
summary: The rapid advancement of AI-Generated Content (AIGC) technologies poses significant challenges for authenticity assessment. However, existing evaluation protocols largely overlook anti-forensics attack, failing to ensure the comprehensive robustness of state-of-the-art AIGC detectors in real-world applications. To bridge this gap, we propose ForgeryEraser, a framework designed to execute universal anti-forensics attack without access to the target AIGC detectors. We reveal an adversarial vulnerability stemming from the systemic reliance on Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as shared backbones (e.g., CLIP), where downstream AIGC detectors inherit the feature space of these publicly accessible models. Instead of traditional logit-based optimization, we design a multi-modal guidance loss to drive forged image embeddings within the VLM feature space toward text-derived authentic anchors to erase forgery traces, while repelling them from forgery anchors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ForgeryEraser causes substantial performance degradation to advanced AIGC detectors on both global synthesis and local editing benchmarks. Moreover, ForgeryEraser induces explainable forensic models to generate explanations consistent with authentic images for forged images. Our code will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: ForgeryEraser is a universal anti-forensics attack against AIGC detectors, exploiting shared Vision-Language Model backbones (e.g., CLIP) with a multi-modal guidance loss to align forged images with authentic anchors, significantly degrading detector performance and yielding explanations aligned with authentic images; code will be released.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation protocols largely overlook anti-forensics, leaving state-of-the-art AIGC detectors under-tested in realistic scenarios. Detectors often share backbones (e.g., CLIP), creating a systemic vulnerability that can be exploited to erase forgery traces.
AI.method: Universal attack that does not require access to target detectors. It uses a multi-modal guidance loss to push forged image embeddings in the VLM feature space toward text-derived authentic anchors and away from forgery anchors, instead of relying on logit-based optimization.
AI.result: Forging causes substantial degradation of advanced AIGC detectors on both global synthesis and local editing benchmarks. Forensic models trained to explain the results also generate explanations consistent with authentic images for forged content.
AI.conclusion: The work reveals a systemic vulnerability due to shared backbones in AIGC detectors, highlighting the need for robust evaluation and defenses against anti-forensics, with code to be released.

title: BrokenBind: Universal Modality Exploration beyond Dataset Boundaries
summary: Multi-modal learning combines various modalities to provide a comprehensive understanding of real-world problems. A common strategy is to directly bind different modalities together in a specific joint embedding space. However, the capability of existing methods is restricted within the modalities presented in the given dataset, thus they are biased when generalizing to unpresented modalities in downstream tasks. As a result, due to such inflexibility, the viability of previous methods is seriously hindered by the cost of acquiring multi-modal datasets. In this paper, we introduce BrokenBind, which focuses on binding modalities that are presented from different datasets. To achieve this, BrokenBind simultaneously leverages multiple datasets containing the modalities of interest and one shared modality. Though the two datasets do not correspond to each other due to distribution mismatch, we can capture their relationship to generate pseudo embeddings to fill in the missing modalities of interest, enabling flexible and generalized multi-modal learning. Under our framework, any two modalities can be bound together, free from the dataset limitation, to achieve universal modality exploration. Further, to reveal the capability of our method, we study intensified scenarios where more than two datasets are needed for modality binding and show the effectiveness of BrokenBind in low-data regimes. Through extensive evaluation, we carefully justify the superiority of BrokenBind compared to well-known multi-modal baseline methods.

AI.tldr: BrokenBind binds modalities across datasets by using a shared modality to generate pseudo embeddings, enabling universal binding of any two modalities beyond dataset limitations; it performs well, especially in low-data regimes, and outperforms standard baselines.
AI.motivation: Current multi-modal methods are constrained to modalities present within a single dataset, causing limited generalization and high data acquisition costs. A framework that can bind modalities from separate datasets would be more flexible and scalable.
AI.method: Utilize multiple datasets containing the modalities of interest plus a common shared modality. Model the cross-dataset relationships despite distribution mismatch to generate pseudo embeddings for missing modalities, enabling binding between any two modalities. Extend to scenarios with more than two datasets.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation shows BrokenBind outperforms well-known multi-modal baselines and remains effective in low-data regimes, validating its universal modality exploration capability.
AI.conclusion: BrokenBind enables flexible, dataset-agnostic multi-modal learning by binding any pair of modalities via generated pseudo embeddings, reducing dependence on fully paired multi-modal datasets.

title: NECromancer: Breathing Life into Skeletons via BVH Animation
summary: Motion tokenization is a key component of generalizable motion models, yet most existing approaches are restricted to species-specific skeletons, limiting their applicability across diverse morphologies. We propose NECromancer (NEC), a universal motion tokenizer that operates directly on arbitrary BVH skeletons. NEC consists of three components: (1) an Ontology-aware Skeletal Graph Encoder (OwO) that encodes structural priors from BVH files, including joint semantics, rest-pose offsets, and skeletal topology, into skeletal embeddings; (2) a Topology-Agnostic Tokenizer (TAT) that compresses motion sequences into a universal, topology-invariant discrete representation; and (3) the Unified BVH Universe (UvU), a large-scale dataset aggregating BVH motions across heterogeneous skeletons. Experiments show that NEC achieves high-fidelity reconstruction under substantial compression and effectively disentangles motion from skeletal structure. The resulting token space supports cross-species motion transfer, composition, denoising, generation with token-based models, and text-motion retrieval, establishing a unified framework for motion analysis and synthesis across diverse morphologies. Demo page: https://animotionlab.github.io/NECromancer/

AI.tldr: NECromancer introduces a universal motion tokenizer for arbitrary BVH skeletons, combining an ontology-aware skeletal encoder (OwO), a topology-agnostic tokenizer (TAT), and a Unified BVH Universe (UvU); enabling high-fidelity reconstruction under compression and cross-species motion transfer with token-based generation.
AI.motivation: Current motion models are limited to species-specific skeletons, hindering generalization across diverse morphologies; a topology-invariant representation is needed for universal motion analysis and synthesis.
AI.method: Three components: OwO encodes joint semantics, rest-pose offsets, and skeletal topology into embeddings; TAT compresses motion sequences into a universal, topology-invariant discrete token representation; UvU is a large-scale dataset aggregating BVH motions across heterogeneous skeletons.
AI.result: Demonstrates high-fidelity reconstruction under substantial compression and effectively disentangles motion from skeletal structure; the resulting token space enables cross-species motion transfer, composition, denoising, token-based generation, and text-motion retrieval.
AI.conclusion: NECromancer establishes a unified framework for motion analysis and synthesis across diverse morphologies, enabling cross-species capabilities and paving the way for universal motion tokenization.

title: On the Plasticity and Stability for Post-Training Large Language Models
summary: Training stability remains a critical bottleneck for Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), often manifesting as a trade-off between reasoning plasticity and general capability retention. We identify a root cause as the geometric conflict between plasticity and stability gradients, which leads to destructive interference. Crucially, we argue that deterministic projection methods are suboptimal for GRPO as they overlook the intrinsic stochasticity of group-based gradient estimates. To address this, we propose Probabilistic Conflict Resolution (PCR), a Bayesian framework that models gradients as random variables. PCR dynamically arbitrates conflicts via an uncertainty-aware ``soft projection'' mechanism, optimizing the signal-to-noise ratio. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PCR significantly smooths the training trajectory and achieves superior performance in various reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: PCR offers a Bayesian, uncertainty-aware approach to resolve gradient conflicts in GRPO by treating gradients as stochastic and applying a soft projection, improving training stability and performance.
AI.motivation: Training stability in GRPO is hampered by plasticity–stability gradient conflicts; deterministic projection methods ignore gradient uncertainty, leading to destructive interference.
AI.method: Model gradients as random variables within a Bayesian framework and use probabilistic conflict resolution with a soft projection that accounts for uncertainty to maximize the signal-to-noise ratio.
AI.result: Empirical results show smoother training trajectories and superior performance on reasoning tasks across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PCR provides principled uncertainty-aware gradient arbitration for GRPO, improving stability and generalization.

title: The Window Dilemma: Why Concept Drift Detection is Ill-Posed
comment: 12 pages, 1 Figure, 5 Tables. Accepted to the 24th International Symposium on Intelligent Data Analysis (IDA) (2026)
summary:
Non-stationarity of an underlying data generating process that leads to distributional changes over time is a key characteristic of Data Streams. This phenomenon, commonly referred to as Concept Drift, has been intensively studied, and Concept Drift Detectors have been established as a class of methods for detecting such changes (drifts). For the most part, Drift Detectors compare regions (windows) of the data stream and detect drift if those windows are sufficiently dissimilar.
  In this work, we introduce the Window Dilemma, an observation that perceived drift is a product of windowing and not necessarily the underlying data generating process. Additionally, we highlight that drift detection is ill-posed, primarily because verification of drift events are implausible in practice. We demonstrate these contributions first by an illustrative example, followed by empirical comparisons of drift detectors against a variety of alternative adaptation strategies. Our main finding is that traditional batch learning techniques often perform better than their drift-aware counterparts further bringing into question the purpose of detectors in Stream Classification.

AI.tldr: Drift detectors in data streams may be ill-posed: windowing can create perceived drift even if the data-generating process is unchanged; empirical evidence suggests batch learning often outperforms drift-aware methods, questioning the utility of detectors in stream classification.
AI.motivation: Non-stationarity in data streams (concept drift) and the reliance on window-based drift detectors raise questions about whether detected drifts reflect true changes or artifacts of windowing, and whether drift detection adds value in practice.
AI.method: The authors present an illustrative example of window-induced drift, followed by extensive empirical comparisons between drift detectors and alternative adaptation strategies across multiple datasets or settings; analysis emphasizes the relationship between windowing and perceived drift.
AI.result: The main finding is that perceived drift largely stems from the windowing strategy rather than changes in the underlying data-generating process. Drift detectors often underperform or offer limited advantages compared to traditional batch learning techniques.
AI.conclusion: Drift detectors' practical value in stream classification is questionable; the study advocates re-evaluating the role of detectors and suggests focusing on robust adaptation strategies, with batch-like methods frequently providing superior performance over drift-aware approaches.

title: SPARC: Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits for Test-time Scaling of VLMs
summary: Despite recent successes, test-time scaling - i.e., dynamically expanding the token budget during inference as needed - remains brittle for vision-language models (VLMs): unstructured chains-of-thought about images entangle perception and reasoning, leading to long, disorganized contexts where small perceptual mistakes may cascade into completely wrong answers. Moreover, expensive reinforcement learning with hand-crafted rewards is required to achieve good performance. Here, we introduce SPARC (Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits), a modular framework that explicitly decouples visual perception from reasoning. Inspired by sequential sensory-to-cognitive processing in the brain, SPARC implements a two-stage pipeline where the model first performs explicit visual search to localize question-relevant regions, then conditions its reasoning on those regions to produce the final answer. This separation enables independent test-time scaling with asymmetric compute allocation (e.g., prioritizing perceptual processing under distribution shift), supports selective optimization (e.g., improving the perceptual stage alone when it is the bottleneck for end-to-end performance), and accommodates compressed contexts by running global search at lower image resolutions and allocating high-resolution processing only to selected regions, thereby reducing total visual tokens count and compute. Across challenging visual reasoning benchmarks, SPARC outperforms monolithic baselines and strong visual-grounding approaches. For instance, SPARC improves the accuracy of Qwen3VL-4B on the $V^*$ VQA benchmark by 6.7 percentage points, and it surpasses "thinking with images" by 4.6 points on a challenging OOD task despite requiring a 200$\times$ lower token budget.

AI.tldr: A modular SPARC framework decouples perception from reasoning in vision-language models to enable test-time scalability and token-efficiency, improving visual reasoning performance on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Test-time token budget expansion is brittle due to entangled perception and reasoning in VLMs, and reinforcement learning with handcrafted rewards is costly; there is a need for scalable, modular architectures that can selectively optimize and reduce context size.
AI.method: A two-stage SPARC pipeline: (1) explicit visual search to localize question-relevant regions, (2) reasoning conditioned on these regions. Supports asymmetric compute allocation, enables selective optimization, and allows compressed contexts by conducting global search at low resolutions and high-resolution processing only on relevant regions.
AI.result: SPARC outperforms monolithic and strong grounding baselines on challenging visual reasoning tasks. For example, it raises Qwen3VL-4B’s V* VQA accuracy by 6.7 percentage points and outperforms 'thinking with images' by 4.6 points on an OOD task, while using 200x fewer tokens.
AI.conclusion: Separating perception and reasoning enables flexible test-time scaling, targeted optimization, and token-efficient processing with strong performance gains.

title: Achieving Better Local Regret Bound for Online Non-Convex Bilevel Optimization
summary: Online bilevel optimization (OBO) has emerged as a powerful framework for many machine learning problems. Prior works have developed several algorithms that minimize the standard bilevel local regret or the window-averaged bilevel local regret of the OBO problem, but the optimality of existing regret bounds remains unclear. In this work, we establish optimal regret bounds for both settings. For standard bilevel local regret, we propose an algorithm that achieves the optimal regret $Ω(1+V_T)$ with at most $O(T\log T)$ total inner-level gradient evaluations. We further develop a fully single-loop algorithm whose regret bound includes an additional gradient-variation terms. For the window-averaged bilevel local regret, we design an algorithm that captures sublinear environmental variation through a window-based analysis and achieves the optimal regret $Ω(T/W^2)$. Experiments validate our theoretical findings and demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods.

AI.tldr: This work derives optimal regret bounds for online bilevel optimization (OBO) under two metrics: standard bilevel local regret and window-averaged bilevel local regret, proposing algorithms that match lower bounds while controlling gradient evaluations and gradient variation; experiments corroborate theory.
AI.motivation: OBO is a powerful framework for ML problems, but the optimality of existing regret bounds is unclear. Establishing tight bounds and efficient algorithms under dynamic environments is a key theoretical and practical objective.
AI.method: For standard bilevel local regret, propose an algorithm achieving the optimal Ω(1+V_T) regret with O(T log T) inner-gradient evaluations; also present a fully single-loop variant with additional gradient-variation terms. For window-averaged bilevel local regret, use a window-based analysis to design an algorithm achieving the optimal Ω(T/W^2) regret under environmental variation.
AI.result: The paper establishes tight (optimal) regret bounds for both regret notions and demonstrates practical effectiveness via experiments.
AI.conclusion: Optimal regret performance for OBO under both standard and window-averaged metrics is achieved by the proposed algorithms, with efficient gradient evaluation costs; results bridge theory and practice in dynamic OBO settings.

title: An Integer Linear Programming Approach to Geometrically Consistent Partial-Partial Shape Matching
summary: The task of establishing correspondences between two 3D shapes is a long-standing challenge in computer vision. While numerous studies address full-full and partial-full 3D shape matching, only a limited number of works have explored the partial-partial setting, very likely due to its unique challenges: we must compute accurate correspondences while at the same time find the unknown overlapping region. Nevertheless, partial-partial 3D shape matching reflects the most realistic setting, as in many real-world cases, such as 3D scanning, shapes are only partially observable. In this work, we introduce the first integer linear programming approach specifically designed to address the distinctive challenges of partial-partial shape matching. Our method leverages geometric consistency as a strong prior, enabling both robust estimation of the overlapping region and computation of neighbourhood-preserving correspondences. We empirically demonstrate that our approach achieves high-quality matching results both in terms of matching error and smoothness. Moreover, we show that our method is more scalable than previous formalisms.

AI.tldr: First ILP framework for partial-partial 3D shape matching that jointly estimates the overlap and finds neighborhood-preserving correspondences, achieving high accuracy and better scalability.
AI.motivation: Partial-partial 3D shape matching is highly realistic but hard due to unknown overlap and need for robust correspondences; existing methods focus on full-full or partial-full settings and are not scalable enough.
AI.method: An integer linear programming formulation that encodes geometric consistency as a strong prior to simultaneously estimate the overlapping region and compute correspondences that preserve local geometry.
AI.result: Empirical experiments show high-quality matching with favorable error and smoothness metrics; the approach is more scalable than prior formalisms.
AI.conclusion: ILP-based partial-partial matching is effective, robust to incomplete data, and scalable, offering accurate overlapping region estimation and smooth, neighborhood-preserving correspondences.

title: Towards Generalizable Reasoning: Group Causal Counterfactual Policy Optimization for LLM Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) excel at complex tasks with advances in reasoning capabilities. However, existing reward mechanisms remain tightly coupled to final correctness and pay little attention to the underlying reasoning process: trajectories with sound reasoning but wrong answers receive low credit, while lucky guesses with flawed logic may be highly rewarded, affecting reasoning generalization. From a causal perspective, we interpret multi-candidate reasoning for a fixed question as a family of counterfactual experiments with theoretical supports. Building on this, we propose Group Causal Counterfactual Policy Optimization to explicitly train LLMs to learn generalizable reasoning patterns. It proposes an episodic causal counterfactual reward that jointly captures (i) robustness, encouraging the answer distribution induced by a reasoning step to remain stable under counterfactual perturbations; and (ii) effectiveness, enforcing sufficient variability so that the learned reasoning strategy can transfer across questions. We then construct token-level advantages from this reward and optimize the policy, encouraging LLMs to favor reasoning patterns that are process-valid and counterfactually robust. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate its advantages.

AI.tldr: A training paradigm named Group Causal Counterfactual Policy Optimization (GCCPPO) for LLMs that uses episodic causal counterfactual rewards to promote generalizable reasoning patterns by balancing robustness and transferability; demonstrated advantages on diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current reward mechanisms in LLM training emphasize final correctness and often overlook the reasoning process, giving credit to luck or penalizing sound but incorrect reasoning. A causal perspective treats multiple candidate reasoning paths for a question as counterfactual experiments, aiming to align incentives with process-valid and transferable reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce episodic causal counterfactual reward that jointly enforces (i) robustness (stability of the answer distribution under counterfactual perturbations of the reasoning steps) and (ii) effectiveness (sufficient variability across questions to enable transfer). Build token-level advantages from this reward and perform policy optimization to encourage process-valid, counterfactually robust reasoning patterns.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks show advantages, indicating improved generalization and robustness of reasoning patterns across questions.
AI.conclusion: GCCPPO provides a principled way to train LLMs to learn generalizable reasoning by aligning incentives with causal counterfactual properties of the reasoning process, improving cross-question transfer and robustness of reasoning.

title: ProtoQuant: Quantization of Prototypical Parts For General and Fine-Grained Image Classification
comment: Work under review. Code will be released upon acceptance
summary: Prototypical parts-based models offer a "this looks like that" paradigm for intrinsic interpretability, yet they typically struggle with ImageNet-scale generalization and often require computationally expensive backbone finetuning. Furthermore, existing methods frequently suffer from "prototype drift," where learned prototypes lack tangible grounding in the training distribution and change their activation under small perturbations. We present ProtoQuant, a novel architecture that achieves prototype stability and grounded interpretability through latent vector quantization. By constraining prototypes to a discrete learned codebook within the latent space, we ensure they remain faithful representations of the training data without the need to update the backbone.
