This design allows ProtoQuant to function as an efficient, interpretable head that scales to large-scale datasets. We evaluate ProtoQuant on ImageNet and several fine-grained benchmarks (CUB-200, Cars-196). Our results demonstrate that ProtoQuant achieves competitive classification accuracy while generalizing to ImageNet and comparable interpretability metrics to other prototypical-parts-based methods.

AI.tldr: ProtoQuant uses latent-vector quantization to stabilize prototypical parts-based models, enabling scalable, grounded interpretability without backbone finetuning; achieves competitive accuracy on ImageNet and strong interpretability on fine-grained datasets.
AI.motivation: Address prototype drift and poor ImageNet-scale generalization in prototypical-parts models, and eliminate the need for expensive backbone finetuning while maintaining interpretable, prototype-based reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce a discrete learned codebook in the latent space and constrain prototypes to this codebook; backboned fixed, the model uses a prototypical head with latent quantization to produce interpretable prototypes.
AI.result: Competitive classification accuracy, good generalization to ImageNet, and interpretability metrics comparable to other prototypical methods on ImageNet and fine-grained datasets (CUB-200, Cars-196).
AI.conclusion: ProtoQuant delivers stable, grounded, scalable prototypical interpretability without backbone updates, suitable for large-scale datasets and maintaining competitive accuracy.

title: Adaptive Uncertainty-Aware Tree Search for Robust Reasoning
summary: Inference-time reasoning scaling has significantly advanced the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) in complex problem-solving. A prevalent approach involves external search guided by Process Reward Models (PRMs). However, a fundamental limitation of this framework is the epistemic uncertainty of PRMs when evaluating reasoning paths that deviate from their training distribution. In this work, we conduct a systematic analysis of this challenge. We first provide empirical evidence that PRMs exhibit high uncertainty and unreliable scoring on out-of-distribution (OOD) samples. We then establish a theoretical framework proving that while standard search incurs linear regret accumulation, an uncertainty-aware strategy can achieve sublinear regret. Motivated by these findings, we propose Uncertainty-Aware Tree Search (UATS), a unified method that estimates uncertainty via Monte Carlo Dropout and dynamically allocates compute budget using a reinforcement learning-based controller. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach effectively mitigates the impact of OOD errors.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware external search improves inference-time reasoning by addressing PRM epistemic uncertainty on OOD paths, using Monte Carlo Dropout and a RL-based controller to achieve sublinear regret and robustness.
AI.motivation: PRMs often misjudge reasoning paths that fall outside their training distribution, causing unreliable scores and harmful guidance during search. This epistemic uncertainty limits the effectiveness of external search in LLM reasoning.
AI.method: Uncertainty-Aware Tree Search (UATS) that (i) estimates uncertainty via Monte Carlo Dropout for PRM scores, and (ii) dynamically allocates compute budget using a reinforcement learning-based controller, unifying search with uncertainty estimates.
AI.result: The paper provides empirical evidence that PRMs exhibit high uncertainty and unreliable scoring on out-of-distribution samples; a theoretical framework shows that uncertainty-aware search can achieve sublinear regret vs. linear regret of standard search; extensive experiments demonstrate improved mitigation of OOD errors and overall robustness in inference-time reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware search improves inference-time reasoning by accounting for model uncertainty in PRMs, enabling sublinear regret and more robust problem-solving in LLM-driven reasoning pipelines.

title: DAVE: Distribution-aware Attribution via ViT Gradient Decomposition
comment: work under review. Code will be released upon acceptance
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have become a dominant architecture in computer vision, yet producing stable and high-resolution attribution maps for these models remains challenging. Architectural components such as patch embeddings and attention routing often introduce structured artifacts in pixel-level explanations, causing many existing methods to rely on coarse patch-level attributions. We introduce DAVE \textit{(\underline{D}istribution-aware \underline{A}ttribution via \underline{V}iT Gradient D\underline{E}composition)}, a mathematically grounded attribution method for ViTs based on a structured decomposition of the input gradient. By exploiting architectural properties of ViTs, DAVE isolates locally equivariant and stable components of the effective input--output mapping. It separates these from architecture-induced artifacts and other sources of instability.

AI.tldr: DAVE is a gradient-decomposition–based attribution method for Vision Transformers that improves stability and resolution by separating architecture-induced artifacts from the meaningful input–output mapping.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers exhibit structured artifacts in pixel-level explanations due to patch embeddings and attention routing; existing methods often yield coarse patch-level attributions. There is a need for stable, high-resolution, pixel-level attributions.
AI.method: Structured decomposition of the input gradient, leveraging ViT architectural properties to isolate locally equivariant and stable gradient components, separating them from artifacts and instability sources.
AI.result: It isolates locally equivariant and stable components of the effective input–output mapping and separates these from architecture-induced artifacts and instability sources.
AI.conclusion: DAVE provides a mathematically grounded attribution framework for ViTs, enabling more stable and high-resolution explanations by disentangling architecture-induced effects from true input–output behavior.

title: CauCLIP: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap in Surgical Video Understanding via Causality-Inspired Vision-Language Modeling
summary: Surgical phase recognition is a critical component for context-aware decision support in intelligent operating rooms, yet training robust models is hindered by limited annotated clinical videos and large domain gaps between synthetic and real surgical data. To address this, we propose CauCLIP, a causality-inspired vision-language framework that leverages CLIP to learn domain-invariant representations for surgical phase recognition without access to target domain data. Our approach integrates a frequency-based augmentation strategy to perturb domain-specific attributes while preserving semantic structures, and a causal suppression loss that mitigates non-causal biases and reinforces causal surgical features. These components are combined in a unified training framework that enables the model to focus on stable causal factors underlying surgical workflows. Experiments on the SurgVisDom hard adaptation benchmark demonstrate that our method substantially outperforms all competing approaches, highlighting the effectiveness of causality-guided vision-language models for domain-generalizable surgical video understanding.

AI.tldr: CauCLIP: causality-inspired vision-language framework for surgical phase recognition that achieves domain generalization using frequency-based augmentation and causal suppression loss within CLIP, outperforming baselines on SurgVisDom hard adaptation.
AI.motivation: Domain gaps between synthetic and real surgical videos and limited annotated data hinder robust phase recognition; need domain-invariant, causal features.
AI.method: Integrates CLIP-based vision-language model; applies a frequency-based augmentation to perturb domain-specific attributes while preserving semantics; introduces a causal suppression loss to reduce non-causal biases and reinforce causal surgical features; trains in a unified framework without access to target-domain data.
AI.result: Significant performance gains over all baselines on the SurgVisDom hard adaptation benchmark, demonstrating effectiveness of causality-guided vision-language models for domain-generalizable surgical video understanding.
AI.conclusion: CauCLIP validates causality-guided CLIP approaches as effective for robust, domain-generalizable surgical video understanding.

title: Dynamics-Aligned Shared Hypernetworks for Zero-Shot Actuator Inversion
summary: Zero-shot generalization in contextual reinforcement learning remains a core challenge, particularly when the context is latent and must be inferred from data. A canonical failure mode is actuator inversion, where identical actions produce opposite physical effects under a latent binary context. We propose DMA*-SH, a framework where a single hypernetwork, trained solely via dynamics prediction, generates a small set of adapter weights shared across the dynamics model, policy, and action-value function. This shared modulation imparts an inductive bias matched to actuator inversion, while input/output normalization and random input masking stabilize context inference, promoting directionally concentrated representations. We provide theoretical support via an expressivity separation result for hypernetwork modulation, and a variance decomposition with policy-gradient variance bounds that formalize how within-mode compression improves learning under actuator inversion. For evaluation, we introduce the Actuator Inversion Benchmark (AIB), a suite of environments designed to isolate discontinuous context-to-dynamics interactions. On AIB's held-out actuator-inversion tasks, DMA*-SH achieves zero-shot generalization, outperforming domain randomization by 111.8% and surpassing a standard context-aware baseline by 16.1%.

AI.tldr: DMA*-SH uses a single hypernetwork to generate small adapter weights shared across the dynamics model, policy, and Q-function, imparting an inductive bias to handle actuator inversion; achieves zero-shot generalization on the Actuator Inversion Benchmark, outperforming domain randomization by 111.8% and a standard context-aware baseline by 16.1%.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot generalization in contextual reinforcement learning with latent, inferable context is challenging. Actuator inversion—where the same action yields opposite effects under different latent contexts—is a canonical failure mode that motivates robust context inference and modular adaptation.
AI.method: Train a single hypernetwork (via dynamics prediction) to output a small set of adapter weights that modulate the dynamics model, policy, and action-value function. Normalize inputs/outputs and apply random input masking to stabilize context inference. Provide theoretical support via an expressivity separation result for hypernetwork modulation and a variance decomposition with policy-gradient variance bounds. Evaluate on the Actuator Inversion Benchmark (AIB) designed to isolate discontinuous context-dynamics interactions.
AI.result: On AIB’s held-out actuator-inversion tasks, DMA*-SH achieves zero-shot generalization, outperforming domain randomization by 111.8% and surpassing a standard context-aware baseline by 16.1%.
AI.conclusion: Hypernetwork-based adapter modulation introduces a context-sensitive inductive bias aligned with actuator inversion, while normalization and input masking stabilize inference, enabling robust zero-shot generalization in latent-context RL. The AIB benchmark isolates the discontinuous context-to-dynamics interactions essential to this setting.

title: Evolutionary Generation of Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems (MAS) show strong promise for complex reasoning, planning, and tool-augmented tasks, but designing effective MAS architectures remains labor-intensive, brittle, and hard to generalize. Existing automatic MAS generation methods either rely on code generation, which often leads to executability and robustness failures, or impose rigid architectural templates that limit expressiveness and adaptability. We propose Evolutionary Generation of Multi-Agent Systems (EvoMAS), which formulates MAS generation as structured configuration generation. EvoMAS performs evolutionary generation in configuration space. Specifically, EvoMAS selects initial configurations from a pool, applies feedback-conditioned mutation and crossover guided by execution traces, and iteratively refines both the candidate pool and an experience memory. We evaluate EvoMAS on diverse benchmarks, including BBEH, SWE-Bench, and WorkBench, covering reasoning, software engineering, and tool-use tasks. EvoMAS consistently improves task performance over both human-designed MAS and prior automatic MAS generation methods, while producing generated systems with higher executability and runtime robustness. EvoMAS outperforms the agent evolution method EvoAgent by +10.5 points on BBEH reasoning and +7.1 points on WorkBench. With Claude-4.5-Sonnet, EvoMAS also reaches 79.1% on SWE-Bench-Verified, matching the top of the leaderboard.

AI.tldr: EvoMAS reframes automatic multi-agent system (MAS) generation as structured, evolution-in-configuration-space design, using feedback-guided mutations/crossovers and an experience memory to improve executability and robustness compared to prior methods.
AI.motivation: To reduce labor, brittleness, and rigidity in MAS design, and overcome limitations of code-generation or template-bound approaches by enabling expressive, adaptable architectures through evolutionary configuration search.
AI.method: Initialize candidate configurations from a pool; iteratively apply feedback-conditioned mutations and crossovers guided by execution traces; update an experience memory and candidate pool; evaluate on diverse benchmarks (BBEH, SWE-Bench, WorkBench) and compare with human-designed MAS and prior automatic methods.
AI.result: EvoMAS consistently improves task performance vs baselines, enhances executability and runtime robustness, outperforms EvoAgent by +10.5 on BBEH and +7.1 on WorkBench; with Claude-4.5-Sonnet achieves 79.1% on SWE-Bench-Verified, matching top leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: Structured configuration-space evolution is a effective paradigm for automatic MAS generation, delivering superior performance, robustness, and generalization across reasoning, software engineering, and tool-use tasks, and closing gaps with or surpassing state-of-the-art baselines.

title: PlanViz: Evaluating Planning-Oriented Image Generation and Editing for Computer-Use Tasks
comment: The main part of our paper: PlanViz Code is at: https://github.com/lijunxian111/PlanViz Supplementary material is at: https://github.com/lijunxian111/PlanViz/releases/tag/v1
summary: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) have shown impressive capabilities in generating natural images and supporting multimodal reasoning. However, their potential in supporting computer-use planning tasks, which are closely related to our lives, remain underexplored. Image generation and editing in computer-use tasks require capabilities like spatial reasoning and procedural understanding, and it is still unknown whether UMMs have these capabilities to finish these tasks or not. Therefore, we propose PlanViz, a new benchmark designed to evaluate image generation and editing for computer-use tasks. To achieve the goal of our evaluation, we focus on sub-tasks which frequently involve in daily life and require planning steps. Specifically, three new sub-tasks are designed: route planning, work diagramming, and web&UI displaying. We address challenges in data quality ensuring by curating human-annotated questions and reference images, and a quality control process. For challenges of comprehensive and exact evaluation, a task-adaptive score, PlanScore, is proposed. The score helps understanding the correctness, visual quality and efficiency of generated images. Through experiments, we highlight key limitations and opportunities for future research on this topic.

AI.tldr: A benchmark, PlanViz, evaluates image generation/editing for computer-use tasks with three sub-tasks and a task-adaptive score (PlanScore).
AI.motivation: To assess whether unified multimodal models can perform planning-aware image generation/editing for everyday computer-use tasks; current UMMs largely focus on static generation and lack procedural/spatial reasoning.
AI.method: Curate human-annotated questions and reference images; design sub-tasks (route planning, work diagramming, web/UI displaying); implement data quality control; propose PlanScore evaluating correctness, visual quality, and efficiency.
AI.result: Baseline experiments reveal key limitations and opportunities; PlanViz clarifies what skills UMMs lack and where improvement is needed.
AI.conclusion: PlanViz provides a targeted benchmark and scoring framework to advance planning-enabled multimodal generation research.

title: Topography scanning as a part of process monitoring in power cable insulation process
summary: We present a novel topography scanning system developed to XLPE cable core monitoring. Modern measurement technology is utilized together with embedded high-performance computing to build a complete and detailed 3D surface map of the insulated core. Cross sectional and lengthwise geometry errors are studied, and melt homogeneity is identified as one major factor for these errors. A surface defect detection system has been developed utilizing deep learning methods. Our results show that convolutional neural networks are well suited for real time analysis of surface measurement data enabling reliable detection of surface defects.

AI.tldr: A 3D topography scanning system for XLPE cable cores combines advanced measurement hardware and embedded HPC to produce a detailed surface map, investigates geometry errors linked to melt homogeneity, and uses CNNs for real-time surface defect detection.
AI.motivation: Improve quality control and reliability of XLPE cable insulation by providing precise 3D surface characterization and rapid defect detection, while identifying material/process factors (melt homogeneity) that drive geometric errors.
AI.method: Integrate modern sensing technology with embedded high-performance computing to generate a complete 3D surface map of the insulated core. Analyze cross-sectional and lengthwise geometry errors and assess melt homogeneity. Develop a surface defect detection pipeline based on convolutional neural networks for real-time analysis of surface measurements.
AI.result: Demonstrates that CNNs are well suited for real-time analysis of surface data, enabling reliable detection of surface defects; identifies melt homogeneity as a major factor contributing to geometry errors.
AI.conclusion: An integrated system that yields high-resolution 3D surface characterization and real-time defect monitoring is feasible for XLPE cable cores, with melt homogeneity playing a key role in geometry accuracy and CNNs providing effective real-time analysis.

title: CytoCrowd: A Multi-Annotator Benchmark Dataset for Cytology Image Analysis
summary: High-quality annotated datasets are crucial for advancing machine learning in medical image analysis. However, a critical gap exists: most datasets either offer a single, clean ground truth, which hides real-world expert disagreement, or they provide multiple annotations without a separate gold standard for objective evaluation. To bridge this gap, we introduce CytoCrowd, a new public benchmark for cytology analysis. The dataset features 446 high-resolution images, each with two key components: (1) raw, conflicting annotations from four independent pathologists, and (2) a separate, high-quality gold-standard ground truth established by a senior expert. This dual structure makes CytoCrowd a versatile resource. It serves as a benchmark for standard computer vision tasks, such as object detection and classification, using the ground truth. Simultaneously, it provides a realistic testbed for evaluating annotation aggregation algorithms that must resolve expert disagreements. We provide comprehensive baseline results for both tasks. Our experiments demonstrate the challenges presented by CytoCrowd and establish its value as a resource for developing the next generation of models for medical image analysis.

AI.tldr: CytoCrowd is a public cytology benchmark with 446 high-res images, each providing four independent expert annotations and a senior-expert gold standard, enabling benchmarking for standard vision tasks and for annotation-aggregation evaluation.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap between single-ground-truth datasets and multi-annotation datasets lacking a gold standard; aims to reflect real-world expert disagreement and enable objective evaluation of aggregation methods.
AI.method: Dataset construction: raw conflicting annotations from four pathologists plus a separate senior-expert ground truth; used for standard CV tasks (detection/classification) and for evaluating annotation-aggregation algorithms; baseline results reported.
AI.result: Shows the dataset's challenging nature and utility; provides baselines for both standard CV tasks and aggregation algorithms, validating CytoCrowd as a resource.
AI.conclusion: CytoCrowd fills a gap by combining a gold standard with multiple independent annotations, enabling robust evaluation of both predictive models and annotation-aggregation approaches in medical imaging.

title: Which Graph Shift Operator? A Spectral Answer to an Empirical Question
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have established themselves as the leading models for learning on graph-structured data, generally categorized into spatial and spectral approaches. Central to these architectures is the Graph Shift Operator (GSO), a matrix representation of the graph structure used to filter node signals. However, selecting the optimal GSO, whether fixed or learnable, remains largely empirical. In this paper, we introduce a novel alignment gain metric that quantifies the geometric distortion between the input signal and label subspaces. Crucially, our theoretical analysis connects this alignment directly to generalization bounds via a spectral proxy for the Lipschitz constant. This yields a principled, computation-efficient criterion to rank and select the optimal GSO for any prediction task prior to training, eliminating the need for extensive search.

AI.tldr: New alignment gain metric to rank and select Graph Shift Operator (GSO) for GNNs prior to training; connects geometric alignment to generalization bounds via a spectral proxy for the Lipschitz constant, enabling computation-efficient GSO selection without extensive search.
AI.motivation: Empirically selecting GSO (fixed or learnable) is common and costly; need principled criteria to predict which GSO yields better generalization.
AI.method: Define alignment gain as the geometric distortion between input signal subspace and label subspace; link alignment to generalization bounds through a spectral proxy for Lipschitz constant; derive a computation-efficient criterion to rank GSOs before training.
AI.result: Establishes a theoretical connection between alignment gain and generalization bounds; provides a practical pre-training criterion to rank/choose the optimal GSO with no extensive search.
AI.conclusion: The alignment gain framework offers a principled, efficient method to select GSO in GNNs, potentially improving generalization without extra training cost.

title: Live Knowledge Tracing: Real-Time Adaptation using Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Deep knowledge tracing models have achieved significant breakthroughs in modeling student learning trajectories. However, these architectures require substantial training time and are prone to overfitting on datasets with short sequences. In this paper, we explore a new paradigm for knowledge tracing by leveraging tabular foundation models (TFMs). Unlike traditional methods that require offline training on a fixed training set, our approach performs real-time ''live'' knowledge tracing in an online way. The core of our method lies in a two-way attention mechanism: while attention knowledge tracing models only attend across earlier time steps, TFMs simultaneously attend across both time steps and interactions of other students in the training set. They align testing sequences with relevant training sequences at inference time, therefore skipping the training step entirely. We demonstrate, using several datasets of increasing size, that our method achieves competitive predictive performance with up to 273x speedups, in a setting where more student interactions are observed over time.

AI.tldr: Tabular foundation models enable live, online knowledge tracing with two-way attention, achieving competitive accuracy with substantial speedups by skipping offline training.
AI.motivation: To overcome long training times and overfitting in deep knowledge tracing, especially for short sequences, by enabling real-time inference in streaming data settings and leveraging cross-student information.
AI.method: Utilize tabular foundation models with a two-way attention mechanism that attends across time steps and across other students’ interactions. At inference, align the test sequence with relevant training sequences in the training set, effectively performing retrieval-based, training-free inference without offline model training.
AI.result: On datasets of increasing size, the approach achieves competitive predictive performance while delivering up to 273x speedups, with advantages growing as more student interactions are observed over time.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and efficiency of live, online knowledge tracing using TFMs, offering scalable KT in streaming contexts without traditional offline training.

title: Can We Build a Monolithic Model for Fake Image Detection? SICA: Semantic-Induced Constrained Adaptation for Unified-Yet-Discriminative Artifact Feature Space Reconstruction
summary: Fake Image Detection (FID), aiming at unified detection across four image forensic subdomains, is critical in real-world forensic scenarios. Compared with ensemble approaches, monolithic FID models are theoretically more promising, but to date, consistently yield inferior performance in practice. In this work, by discovering the ``heterogeneous phenomenon'', which is the intrinsic distinctness of artifacts across subdomains, we diagnose the cause of this underperformance for the first time: the collapse of the artifact feature space driven by such phenomenon. The core challenge for developing a practical monolithic FID model thus boils down to the ``unified-yet-discriminative" reconstruction of the artifact feature space. To address this paradoxical challenge, we hypothesize that high-level semantics can serve as a structural prior for the reconstruction, and further propose Semantic-Induced Constrained Adaptation (SICA), the first monolithic FID paradigm. Extensive experiments on our OpenMMSec dataset demonstrate that SICA outperforms 15 state-of-the-art methods and reconstructs the target unified-yet-discriminative artifact feature space in a near-orthogonal manner, thus firmly validating our hypothesis. The code and dataset are available at:https: //github.com/scu-zjz/SICA_OpenMMSec.

AI.tldr: Proposes Semantic-Induced Constrained Adaptation (SICA), the first monolithic fake image detector (FID) that uses high-level semantic priors to reconstruct a unified-yet-discriminative artifact feature space, addressing heterogeneous subdomain artifacts. Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on OpenMMSec (vs 15 baselines) and provides code/dataset.
AI.motivation: FID across four image-forensic subdomains suffers from the heterogeneous manifestation of artifacts, causing collapse of the artifact feature space and underperformance of monolithic models; a unified-yet-discriminative representation is needed.
AI.method: Introduce SICA, a monolithic FID paradigm that leverages high-level semantic information as structural priors and applies constrained adaptation to align and preserve discriminative artifact features, yielding a near-orthogonal reconstruction of the artifact feature space.
AI.result: SICA outperforms 15 state-of-the-art methods on the OpenMMSec dataset and reconstructs the target feature space in a near-orthogonal manner; code and OpenMMSec dataset are released.
AI.conclusion: High-level semantics can serve as an effective structural prior for unified, discriminative artifact representation in monolithic FID, making SICA a practical and superior alternative to ensemble approaches.

title: Refining the Information Bottleneck via Adversarial Information Separation
summary: Generalizing from limited data is particularly critical for models in domains such as material science, where task-relevant features in experimental datasets are often heavily confounded by measurement noise and experimental artifacts. Standard regularization techniques fail to precisely separate meaningful features from noise, while existing adversarial adaptation methods are limited by their reliance on explicit separation labels. To address this challenge, we propose the Adversarial Information Separation Framework (AdverISF), which isolates task-relevant features from noise without requiring explicit supervision. AdverISF introduces a self-supervised adversarial mechanism to enforce statistical independence between task-relevant features and noise representations. It further employs a multi-layer separation architecture that progressively recycles noise information across feature hierarchies to recover features inadvertently discarded as noise, thereby enabling finer-grained feature extraction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AdverISF outperforms state-of-the-art methods in data-scarce scenarios. In addition, evaluations on real-world material design tasks show that it achieves superior generalization performance.

AI.tldr: AdverISF is a self-supervised adversarial framework that generalizes from limited data by separating task-relevant features from noise without labels, using a multi-layer architecture that reuses noise information to recover discarded features, and showing superior performance in data-scarce and real-world material design tasks.
AI.motivation: Generalization in data-scarce domains (e.g., materials science) where measurements are noisy and artifacts confound task-relevant signals, and where supervision for feature-noise separation is limited or unavailable.
AI.method: A self-supervised adversarial mechanism enforces statistical independence between task-relevant features and noise representations, integrated into a multi-layer separation architecture that progressively recycles noise information across feature hierarchies to recover features previously discarded as noise.
AI.result: AdverISF outperforms state-of-the-art methods in data-scarce settings and demonstrates superior generalization on real-world material design tasks.
AI.conclusion: AdverISF provides a robust, unsupervised pathway to isolate informative features from noise, enabling finer-grained feature extraction and improved generalization in noisy data domains.

title: Clinical-Prior Guided Multi-Modal Learning with Latent Attention Pooling for Gait-Based Scoliosis Screening
summary: Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis (AIS) is a prevalent spinal deformity whose progression can be mitigated through early detection. Conventional screening methods are often subjective, difficult to scale, and reliant on specialized clinical expertise. Video-based gait analysis offers a promising alternative, but current datasets and methods frequently suffer from data leakage, where performance is inflated by repeated clips from the same individual, or employ oversimplified models that lack clinical interpretability. To address these limitations, we introduce ScoliGait, a new benchmark dataset comprising 1,572 gait video clips for training and 300 fully independent clips for testing. Each clip is annotated with radiographic Cobb angles and descriptive text based on clinical kinematic priors. We propose a multi-modal framework that integrates a clinical-prior-guided kinematic knowledge map for interpretable feature representation, alongside a latent attention pooling mechanism to fuse video, text, and knowledge map modalities. Our method establishes a new state-of-the-art, demonstrating a significant performance gap on a realistic, non-repeating subject benchmark. Our approach establishes a new state of the art, showing a significant performance gain on a realistic, subject-independent benchmark. This work provides a robust, interpretable, and clinically grounded foundation for scalable, non-invasive AIS assessment.

AI.tldr: ScoliGait introduces a non-redundant AIS gait benchmark and a multi-modal, clinically grounded model achieving state-of-the-art performance on a subject-independent test set.
AI.motivation: Mitigate data leakage and enhance interpretability in gait-based AIS screening by providing a large, non-repeating dataset and a framework that fuses video, text, and clinical priors with interpretable features.
AI.method: Create ScoliGait dataset (1,572 training clips, 300 independent test clips) annotated with radiographic Cobb angles and clinical-prior text. Propose a clinical-prior-guided kinematic knowledge map and a latent attention pooling module to fuse video, descriptive text, and knowledge-map modalities for interpretable representations.
AI.result: Achieves a new state-of-the-art on a realistic, subject-independent benchmark with a clear performance gap relative to non-knowledge-based baselines; demonstrates robustness and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Provides a robust, scalable, and clinically grounded framework for non-invasive AIS assessment using gait, addressing data leakage and interpretability concerns while enabling clinical adoption.

title: Transformer-based Parameter Fitting of Models derived from Bloch-McConnell Equations for CEST MRI Analysis
summary: Chemical exchange saturation transfer (CEST) MRI is a non-invasive imaging modality for detecting metabolites. It offers higher resolution and sensitivity compared to conventional magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS). However, quantification of CEST data is challenging because the measured signal results from a complex interplay of many physiological variables. Here, we introduce a transformer-based neural network to fit parameters such as metabolite concentrations, exchange and relaxation rates of a physical model derived from Bloch-McConnell equations to in-vitro CEST spectra. We show that our self-supervised trained neural network clearly outperforms the solution of classical gradient-based solver.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based, self-supervised neural network for fitting Bloch–McConnell–derived CEST model parameters (metabolite concentrations, exchange and relaxation rates) to in vitro CEST spectra, outperforming classical gradient-based solvers.
AI.motivation: Quantifying CEST MRI signals is difficult due to the intertwined physiological variables; a robust, data-driven approach aims to improve accuracy and reliability of parameter estimation.
AI.method: Develop a transformer-based neural network trained in a self-supervised manner to infer model parameters (metabolite concentrations, exchange rates, relaxation rates) from in vitro CEST spectra using a Bloch–McConnell physical model.
AI.result: The self-supervised transformer outperforms the classical gradient-based solver in fitting the CEST model to the data.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based, self-supervised parameter estimation improves quantification of CEST MRI by accurately recovering Bloch–McConnell model parameters from spectra.

title: Gold Exploration using Representations from a Multispectral Autoencoder
comment: Presented in Eurips2025, 1st Workshop: Advances in Representation Learning for Earth Observation
summary: Satellite imagery is employed for large-scale prospectivity mapping due to the high cost and typically limited availability of on-site mineral exploration data. In this work, we present a proof-of-concept framework that leverages generative representations learned from multispectral Sentinel-2 imagery to identify gold-bearing regions from space. An autoencoder foundation model, called Isometric, which is pretrained on the large-scale FalconSpace-S2 v1.0 dataset, produces information-dense spectral-spatial representations that serve as inputs to a lightweight XGBoost classifier. We compare this representation-based approach with a raw spectral input baseline using a dataset of 63 Sentinel-2 images from known gold and non-gold locations. The proposed method improves patch-level accuracy from 0.51 to 0.68 and image-level accuracy from 0.55 to 0.73, demonstrating that generative embeddings capture transferable mineralogical patterns even with limited labeled data. These results highlight the potential of foundation-model representations to make mineral exploration more efficient, scalable, and globally applicable.

AI.tldr: Generative embeddings from Sentinel-2 via a pretrained autoencoder improve gold prospectivity classification over raw spectra on a 63-image dataset.
AI.motivation: To explore whether foundation-model representations can capture mineralogical patterns in multispectral imagery, enabling scalable and data-efficient mineral exploration for gold.
AI.method: Isometric autoencoder pretrained on FalconSpace-S2 v1.0 produces spectral-spatial embeddings from Sentinel-2 imagery; a lightweight XGBoost classifier uses these embeddings; compared against a raw spectral input baseline on 63 images of known gold/non-gold locations; evaluation at patch- and image-level accuracy.
AI.result: Patch-level accuracy improved from 0.51 to 0.68; image-level accuracy improved from 0.55 to 0.73, indicating the embeddings capture transferable mineralogical patterns with limited labeled data.
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model representations from multispectral imagery can enhance efficiency, scalability, and global applicability of mineral exploration, even with limited labeled data.

title: Perturbing the Phase: Analyzing Adversarial Robustness of Complex-Valued Neural Networks
summary: Complex-valued neural networks (CVNNs) are rising in popularity for all kinds of applications. To safely use CVNNs in practice, analyzing their robustness against outliers is crucial. One well known technique to understand the behavior of deep neural networks is to investigate their behavior under adversarial attacks, which can be seen as worst case minimal perturbations. We design Phase Attacks, a kind of attack specifically targeting the phase information of complex-valued inputs. Additionally, we derive complex-valued versions of commonly used adversarial attacks. We show that in some scenarios CVNNs are more robust than RVNNs and that both are very susceptible to phase changes with the Phase Attacks decreasing the model performance more, than equally strong regular attacks, which can attack both phase and magnitude.

AI.tldr: Phase Attacks target phase information in complex-valued neural networks (CVNNs); the work derives complex-valued versions of standard adversarial attacks and shows phase perturbations can be more damaging than magnitude-only attacks. CVNNs can be more robust than real-valued NNs (RVNNs) in some cases, but both are highly susceptible to phase changes.
AI.motivation: To assess robustness of CVNNs to outliers/adversarial perturbations and to understand the role of phase information in model vulnerability, addressing a gap in robustness analysis for complex-valued networks.
AI.method: Propose Phase Attacks that perturb input phase; derive complex-valued variants of common adversarial attacks; conduct empirical evaluation comparing CVNNs and RVNNs under phase-only and magnitude+phase attacks.
AI.result: Phase Attacks generally reduce performance more than equivalent-strength regular attacks; CVNNs can be more robust than RVNNs in some scenarios, but both are highly sensitive to phase perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Phase information is a critical vulnerability in CVNNs; attacks focused on phase are particularly effective; robustness depends on architecture, signaling the need for defenses that address phase perturbations.

title: Fine-Grained Model Merging via Modular Expert Recombination
summary: Model merging constructs versatile models by integrating task-specific models without requiring labeled data or expensive joint retraining. Although recent methods improve adaptability to heterogeneous tasks by generating customized merged models for each instance, they face two critical limitations. First, the instance-specific merged models lack reusability, restricting the exploitation of high-quality merging configurations and efficient batch inference. Second, these methods treat each task-specific model as a monolithic whole, overlooking the diverse mergeability of homologous components such as attention and multilayer perceptron layers, and the differing merging sensitivities across components. To address these limitations, we propose MERGE (\underline{M}odular \underline{E}xpert \underline{R}ecombination for fine-\underline{G}rained m\underline{E}rging), a method that enables component-wise model merging and input-aware, on-demand module recombination at inference. MERGE formulates component-wise merging as a bi-objective optimization problem that balances cross-task performance and storage efficiency, and develops a surrogate-assisted evolutionary algorithm to efficiently identify Pareto-optimal merging configurations. These high-quality configurations underpin a reusable modular expert library, from which a lightweight routing network dynamically activates and recombines modular experts to assemble input-specific models and enable efficient inference under storage constraints. Extensive experiments across various model scales, task types, and fine-tuning strategies demonstrate that MERGE consistently outperforms strong baselines and generalizes effectively.

AI.tldr: MERGE offers component-wise, input-aware model merging with a reusable modular expert library, using a surrogate-assisted evolutionary optimization to produce Pareto-optimal configurations that balance performance and storage.
AI.motivation: Existing instance-specific merged models lack reusability and treat models monolithically, hindering batch reuse and efficient inference across heterogeneous tasks.
AI.method: Decompose models into components (e.g., attention, MLP); formulate bi-objective optimization to balance cross-task performance and storage; apply a surrogate-assisted evolutionary algorithm to identify Pareto-optimal merging configurations; build a modular expert library; use a lightweight routing network to select and recombine modules per input for efficient inference under storage constraints.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across model scales, tasks, and fine-tuning strategies shows MERGE consistently outperforms strong baselines and generalizes effectively.
AI.conclusion: MERGE enables reusable, on-demand module recombination at inference time, improving efficiency, enabling reuse of merging configurations, and offering a generalizable approach to modular, multi-task model merging.

title: Revisiting Emotions Representation for Recognition in the Wild
summary: Facial emotion recognition has been typically cast as a single-label classification problem of one out of six prototypical emotions. However, that is an oversimplification that is unsuitable for representing the multifaceted spectrum of spontaneous emotional states, which are most often the result of a combination of multiple emotions contributing at different intensities. Building on this, a promising direction that was explored recently is to cast emotion recognition as a distribution learning problem. Still, such approaches are limited in that research datasets are typically annotated with a single emotion class. In this paper, we contribute a novel approach to describe complex emotional states as probability distributions over a set of emotion classes. To do so, we propose a solution to automatically re-label existing datasets by exploiting the result of a study in which a large set of both basic and compound emotions is mapped to probability distributions in the Valence-Arousal-Dominance (VAD) space. In this way, given a face image annotated with VAD values, we can estimate the likelihood of it belonging to each of the distributions, so that emotional states can be described as a mixture of emotions, enriching their description, while also accounting for the ambiguous nature of their perception. In a preliminary set of experiments, we illustrate the advantages of this solution and a new possible direction of investigation. Data annotations are available at https://github.com/jbcnrlz/affectnet-b-annotation.

AI.tldr: Frames facial emotion recognition as learning probability distributions over emotion classes via automatic relabeling of datasets using Valence-Arousal-Dominance mappings to capture multi-emotion mixtures and perceptual ambiguity.
AI.motivation: Single-label emotion classification cannot represent the complex, spontaneous emotional states; distribution learning with VAD-driven relabeling offers a richer, probabilistic description and handles ambiguity.
AI.method: Map a large set of basic and compound emotions to probability distributions in VAD space; for each image with VAD annotations, estimate the likelihood across these distributions and re-label datasets accordingly to describe emotions as mixtures; includes preliminary experiments and publicly available annotations.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments indicate advantages of the distribution-based labeling approach and suggest a productive research direction; data annotations are publicly available at the cited repository.
AI.conclusion: Describing emotions as mixtures via probability distributions enriches representation and accounts for perceptual ambiguity; the approach warrants further investigation and dataset annotation enhancements.

title: Exploring Sparsity and Smoothness of Arbitrary $\ell_p$ Norms in Adversarial Attacks
summary: Adversarial attacks against deep neural networks are commonly constructed under $\ell_p$ norm constraints, most often using $p=1$, $p=2$ or $p=\infty$, and potentially regularized for specific demands such as sparsity or smoothness. These choices are typically made without a systematic investigation of how the norm parameter \( p \) influences the structural and perceptual properties of adversarial perturbations. In this work, we study how the choice of \( p \) affects sparsity and smoothness of adversarial attacks generated under \( \ell_p \) norm constraints for values of $p \in [1,2]$. To enable a quantitative analysis, we adopt two established sparsity measures from the literature and introduce three smoothness measures. In particular, we propose a general framework for deriving smoothness measures based on smoothing operations and additionally introduce a smoothness measure based on first-order Taylor approximations. Using these measures, we conduct a comprehensive empirical evaluation across multiple real-world image datasets and a diverse set of model architectures, including both convolutional and transformer-based networks. We show that the choice of $\ell_1$ or $\ell_2$ is suboptimal in most cases and the optimal $p$ value is dependent on the specific task. In our experiments, using $\ell_p$ norms with $p\in [1.3, 1.5]$ yields the best trade-off between sparse and smooth attacks. These findings highlight the importance of principled norm selection when designing and evaluating adversarial attacks.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of how ell_p norm choice (p in [1,2]) affects sparsity and smoothness of adversarial perturbations; finds that p in [1.3,1.5] often yields best sparsity-smoothness trade-off; p=1 or p=2 often suboptimal and optimal p is task-dependent.
AI.motivation: Norm choice in adversarial attacks is typically unexamined; need to quantify how p influences sparsity and smoothness to guide attack design.
AI.method: Define two sparsity measures; introduce three smoothness measures; propose a framework for deriving smoothness measures via smoothing operations and a Taylor-approximation-based smoothness metric; conduct extensive empirical evaluation on multiple real-world image datasets and model architectures (CNNs and transformers).
AI.result: Across tasks, p in [1.3,1.5] yields the best sparsity–smoothness trade-off; p=1 or p=2 are suboptimal in most cases; the optimal p is task-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Principled selection of the ell_p norm is important for adversarial attacks; recommend exploring p in (1,2) and tuning to the task to balance sparsity and smoothness.

title: Machine Learning for Detection and Severity Estimation of Sweetpotato Weevil Damage in Field and Lab Conditions
summary: Sweetpotato weevils (Cylas spp.) are considered among the most destructive pests impacting sweetpotato production, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa. Traditional methods for assessing weevil damage, predominantly relying on manual scoring, are labour-intensive, subjective, and often yield inconsistent results. These challenges significantly hinder breeding programs aimed at developing resilient sweetpotato varieties. This study introduces a computer vision-based approach for the automated evaluation of weevil damage in both field and laboratory contexts. In the field settings, we collected data to train classification models to predict root-damage severity levels, achieving a test accuracy of 71.43%. Additionally, we established a laboratory dataset and designed an object detection pipeline employing YOLO12, a leading real-time detection model. This methodology incorporated a two-stage laboratory pipeline that combined root segmentation with a tiling strategy to improve the detectability of small objects. The resulting model demonstrated a mean average precision of 77.7% in identifying minute weevil feeding holes. Our findings indicate that computer vision technologies can provide efficient, objective, and scalable assessment tools that align seamlessly with contemporary breeding workflows. These advancements represent a significant improvement in enhancing phenotyping efficiency within sweetpotato breeding programs and play a crucial role in mitigating the detrimental effects of weevils on food security.

AI.tldr: A computer-vision pipeline for evaluating sweetpotato weevil damage improves phenotyping efficiency in breeding programs.
AI.motivation: Sweetpotato weevil damage is a major, destructive problem; traditional manual scoring is labor-intensive, subjective, and inconsistent, hindering breeding for resistant varieties.
AI.method: Field: train classification models to predict root-damage severity from images; Lab: develop an object-detection pipeline using YOLO12 with a two-stage process (root segmentation + tiling) to detect small feeding holes; dataset construction in both contexts; performance evaluation on held-out data.
AI.result: Field classifier achieved 71.43% test accuracy for root-damage severity; Lab detector using YOLO12 achieved a mean average precision (mAP) of 77.7% for small feeding holes.
AI.conclusion: CV-based phenotyping offers objective, scalable, and efficient assessment aligned with modern breeding workflows, enhancing phenotyping efficiency and helping mitigate weevil impacts on food security.

title: Target noise: A pre-training based neural network initialization for efficient high resolution learning
summary: Weight initialization plays a crucial role in the optimization behavior and convergence efficiency of neural networks. Most existing initialization methods, such as Xavier and Kaiming initializations, rely on random sampling and do not exploit information from the optimization process itself. We propose a simple, yet effective, initialization strategy based on self-supervised pre-training using random noise as the target. Instead of directly training the network from random weights, we first pre-train it to fit random noise, which leads to a structured and non-random parameter configuration. We show that this noise-driven pre-training significantly improves convergence speed in subsequent tasks, without requiring additional data or changes to the network architecture. The proposed method is particularly effective for implicit neural representations (INRs) and Deep Image Prior (DIP)-style networks, which are known to exhibit a strong low-frequency bias during optimization. After noise-based pre-training, the network is able to capture high-frequency components much earlier in training, leading to faster and more stable convergence. Although random noise contains no semantic information, it serves as an effective self-supervised signal (considering its white spectrum nature) for shaping the initialization of neural networks. Overall, this work demonstrates that noise-based pre-training offers a lightweight and general alternative to traditional random initialization, enabling more efficient optimization of deep neural networks.

AI.tldr: Noise-based self-supervised pre-training for initialization improves optimization speed, especially for implicit neural representations, by pre-training to fit random noise, yielding structured weights.
AI.motivation: Traditional initializations (Xavier, Kaiming) are random and ignore optimization dynamics; exploiting a noise-driven pre-training step can shape the parameter configuration to be more favorable for subsequent learning.
AI.method: Pre-train the network to map random noise targets (no semantic data) so that the network's weights become structured; use these weights as initialization for downstream tasks; particularly effective for INRs and DIP-style nets due to their low-frequency bias.
AI.result: Faster convergence and more stable optimization in downstream tasks; the network can capture higher-frequency components earlier after pre-training; no additional data or architectural changes required.
AI.conclusion: Noise-based pre-training is a lightweight, general alternative to standard random initialization, enabling more efficient optimization across deep networks.

title: Learning to Allocate Resources with Censored Feedback
summary: We study the online resource allocation problem in which at each round, a budget $B$ must be allocated across $K$ arms under censored feedback. An arm yields a reward if and only if two conditions are satisfied: (i) the arm is activated according to an arm-specific Bernoulli random variable with unknown parameter, and (ii) the allocated budget exceeds a random threshold drawn from a parametric distribution with unknown parameter. Over $T$ rounds, the learner must jointly estimate the unknown parameters and allocate the budget so as to maximize cumulative reward facing the exploration--exploitation trade-off. We prove an information-theoretic regret lower bound $Ω(T^{1/3})$, demonstrating the intrinsic difficulty of the problem. We then propose RA-UCB, an optimistic algorithm that leverages non-trivial parameter estimation and confidence bounds. When the budget $B$ is known at the beginning of each round, RA-UCB achieves a regret of order $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T})$, and even $\mathcal{O}(\mathrm{poly}\text{-}\log T)$ under stronger assumptions. As for unknown, round dependent budget, we introduce MG-UCB, which allows within-round switching and infinitesimal allocations, and matches the regret guarantees of RA-UCB. We then validate our theoretical results through experiments on real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper studies online budget allocation with censored feedback, proves a T^{1/3} regret lower bound, and provides RA-UCB and MG-UCB algorithms achieving near-optimal regret, with empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Address the intrinsic difficulty of jointly learning arm activation probabilities and threshold-distribution parameters under censored rewards, requiring simultaneous estimation and budget allocation.
AI.method: Derives an information-theoretic lower bound Ω(T^{1/3}); introduces RA-UCB (optimistic, with confidence bounds) for known budget B, achieving ~√T regret (poly-log under stronger assumptions); introduces MG-UCB for unknown, round-dependent budgets with within-round switching and infinitesimal allocations, matching RA-UCB guarantees.
AI.result: Establishes Ω(T^{1/3}) regret lower bound; RA-UCB attains near-square-root regret (with poly-log refinements under stronger assumptions); MG-UCB matches RA-UCB guarantees; experimental validation on real-world datasets corroborates theory.
AI.conclusion: The work characterizes the fundamental difficulty of censored-budget online allocation and provides practical algorithms with provable guarantees, supported by empirical results.

title: A Unified Formula for Affine Transformations between Calibrated Cameras
summary: In this technical note, we derive a closed-form expression for the affine transformation mapping local image patches between two calibrated views. We show that the transformation is a function of the relative camera pose, the image coordinates, and the local surface normal.

AI.tldr: Derives a closed-form affine warp between corresponding image patches from two calibrated views; the warp depends on relative camera pose, pixel coordinates, and the local surface normal.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate patch-based correspondences across views by exploiting geometry from calibration and local surface orientation to obtain an explicit affine mapping.
AI.method: Derivation of a closed-form affine transformation that maps local image patches, expressing the matrix in terms of relative pose (rotation and translation), pixel coordinates, and the local surface normal, likely using projective geometry and image Jacobians.
AI.result: A mathematical closed-form expression for the affine transformation between patches across views, parameterized by pose, pixel coordinates, and surface normal.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled framework to relate patches between calibrated views, improving patch matching, warping accuracy, or feature tracking in multi-view setups.

title: RAIGen: Rare Attribute Identification in Text-to-Image Generative Models
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models achieve impressive generation quality but inherit and amplify training-data biases, skewing coverage of semantic attributes. Prior work addresses this in two ways. Closed-set approaches mitigate biases in predefined fairness categories (e.g., gender, race), assuming socially salient minority attributes are known a priori. Open-set approaches frame the task as bias identification, highlighting majority attributes that dominate outputs. Both overlook a complementary task: uncovering rare or minority features underrepresented in the data distribution (social, cultural, or stylistic) yet still encoded in model representations. We introduce RAIGen, the first framework, to our knowledge, for un-supervised rare-attribute discovery in diffusion models. RAIGen leverages Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders and a novel minority metric combining neuron activation frequency with semantic distinctiveness to identify interpretable neurons whose top-activating images reveal underrepresented attributes. Experiments show RAIGen discovers attributes beyond fixed fairness categories in Stable Diffusion, scales to larger models such as SDXL, supports systematic auditing across architectures, and enables targeted amplification of rare attributes during generation.

AI.tldr: RAIGen: unsupervised rare-attribute discovery in diffusion models using Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders and a minority metric to identify neurons linked to underrepresented attributes; enables auditing and targeted amplification across architectures.
AI.motivation: Address biases in text-to-image diffusion models that existing closed-set and open-set approaches miss rare minority attributes; need unsupervised discovery of underrepresented attributes encoded in model representations.
AI.method: RAIGen framework; Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders (MSAEs); a novel minority metric combining neuron activation frequency with semantic distinctiveness; identify interpretable neurons; top-activating images reveal rare attributes.
AI.result: Empirical results show RAIGen discovers attributes beyond fixed fairness categories in Stable Diffusion; scales to larger models such as SDXL; supports systematic auditing across architectures; enables targeted amplification of rare attributes during generation.
AI.conclusion: RAIGen provides the first unsupervised rare-attribute discovery framework for diffusion models, enabling auditing and controlled amplification of rare attributes beyond predefined fairness categories.

title: The challenge of generating and evolving real-life like synthetic test data without accessing real-world raw data -- a Systematic Review
summary:
Background: High-level system testing of applications that use data from e-Government services as input requires test data that is real-life-like but where the privacy of personal information is guaranteed. Applications with such strong requirement include information exchange between countries, medicine, banking, etc. This review aims to synthesize the current state-of-the-practice in this domain.
  Objectives: The objective of this Systematic Review is to identify existing approaches for creating and evolving synthetic test data without using real-life raw data.
  Methods: We followed well-known methodologies for conducting systematic literature reviews, including the ones from Kitchenham as well as guidelines for analysing the limitations of our review and its threats to validity.
  Results: A variety of methods and tools exist for creating privacy-preserving test data. Our search found 1,013 publications in IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, and SCOPUS. We extracted data from 75 of those publications and identified 37 approaches that answer our research question partly. A common prerequisite for using these methods and tools is direct access to real-life data for data anonymization or synthetic test data generation. Nine existing synthetic test data generation approaches were identified that were closest to answering our research question. Nevertheless, further work would be needed to add the ability to evolve synthetic test data to the existing approaches.
  Conclusions: None of the publications really covered our requirements completely, only partially. Synthetic test data evolution is a field that has not received much attention from researchers but needs to be explored in Digital Government Solutions, especially since new legal regulations are being placed in force in many countries.

AI.tldr: Privacy-preserving synthetic test data for e-Government contexts: many approaches exist, but none fully meet the specified requirements; synthetic data evolution is underexplored and needs research, especially under new regulations.
AI.motivation: Assess the current state-of-the-practice for creating and evolving synthetic test data without exposing real personal data in high-stakes domains such as e-Government, health, and finance.
AI.method: Systematic literature review following Kitchenham guidelines; search across IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, and SCOPUS; data extraction from 75 of 1,013 identified publications; 37 approaches identified; 9 closest to answering the research question; evaluation of data evolution capability.
AI.result: Found 1,013 publications; 75 full-texts analyzed; 37 approaches partially address the question; a common prerequisite is access to real data for anonymization or synthetic generation; nine approaches are closest to the research question; none fully satisfy the requirements; synthetic data evolution remains underexplored.
AI.conclusion: No approach fully covers the requirements; synthetic test-data evolution is a neglected area that needs attention for Digital Government solutions in light of new privacy regulations.

title: GaussianPOP: Principled Simplification Framework for Compact 3D Gaussian Splatting via Error Quantification
summary: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting simplification methods commonly use importance scores, such as blending weights or sensitivity, to identify redundant Gaussians. However, these scores are not driven by visual error metrics, often leading to suboptimal trade-offs between compactness and rendering fidelity. We present GaussianPOP, a principled simplification framework based on analytical Gaussian error quantification. Our key contribution is a novel error criterion, derived directly from the 3DGS rendering equation, that precisely measures each Gaussian's contribution to the rendered image. By introducing a highly efficient algorithm, our framework enables practical error calculation in a single forward pass. The framework is both accurate and flexible, supporting on-training pruning as well as post-training simplification via iterative error re-quantification for improved stability. Experimental results show that our method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art pruning methods across both application scenarios, achieving a superior trade-off between model compactness and high rendering quality.

AI.tldr: GaussianPOP provides a principled, error-driven simplification of 3D Gaussian splatting using an analytical error criterion derived from the rendering equation, enabling efficient single-pass error computation and both on-training and post-training pruning, outperforming prior methods in compactness–quality trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting simplification often relies on importance scores (e.g., blending weights or sensitivity) that are not directly tied to visual error metrics, leading to suboptimal trade-offs between model compactness and rendering fidelity.
AI.method: Derive an analytical error criterion from the 3DGS rendering equation to quantify each Gaussian's contribution to the rendered image. Develop a highly efficient algorithm enabling error computation in a single forward pass. Support both on-training pruning and post-training simplification via iterative error re-quantification for stability.
AI.result: Empirical results show GaussianPOP consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art pruning methods across both on-training and post-training scenarios, achieving a superior balance between model compactness and rendering quality.
AI.conclusion: GaussianPOP offers a principled, accurate, and flexible framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting simplification, enabling efficient error-driven pruning during or after training with improved fidelity for given compactness constraints.

title: Rethinking Multi-Condition DiTs: Eliminating Redundant Attention via Position-Alignment and Keyword-Scoping
summary: While modern text-to-image models excel at prompt-based generation, they often lack the fine-grained control necessary for specific user requirements like spatial layouts or subject appearances. Multi-condition control addresses this, yet its integration into Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) is bottlenecked by the conventional ``concatenate-and-attend'' strategy, which suffers from quadratic computational and memory overhead as the number of conditions scales. Our analysis reveals that much of this cross-modal interaction is spatially or semantically redundant. To this end, we propose Position-aligned and Keyword-scoped Attention (PKA), a highly efficient framework designed to eliminate these redundancies. Specifically, Position-Aligned Attention (PAA) linearizes spatial control by enforcing localized patch alignment, while Keyword-Scoped Attention (KSA) prunes irrelevant subject-driven interactions via semantic-aware masking. To facilitate efficient learning, we further introduce a Conditional Sensitivity-Aware Sampling (CSAS) strategy that reweights the training objective towards critical denoising phases, drastically accelerating convergence and enhancing conditional fidelity. Empirically, PKA delivers a 10.0$\times$ inference speedup and a 5.1$\times$ VRAM saving, providing a scalable and resource-friendly solution for high-fidelity multi-conditioned generation.

AI.tldr: PKA proposes a lightweight, condition-aware attention mechanism for diffusion transformers to enable efficient multi-condition control, combining Position-Aligned Attention (PAA) and Keyword-Scoped Attention (KSA) with CSAS, yielding substantial speedups and memory savings while preserving fidelity.
AI.motivation: There is a need for fine-grained multi-condition control in text-to-image generation. Traditional concatenate-and-attend in Diffusion Transformers scales quadratically with the number of conditions and includes many redundant cross-modal interactions, motivating more efficient attention schemes.
AI.method: Introduce Position-Aligned Attention (PAA) to linearize spatial control via localized patch alignment; introduce Keyword-Scoped Attention (KSA) to prune irrelevant subject-driven interactions via semantic-aware masking; add Conditional Sensitivity-Aware Sampling (CSAS) to reweight training objective toward critical denoising phases for faster convergence and improved conditional fidelity.
AI.result: Empirically, PKA delivers a 10.0× inference speedup and a 5.1× VRAM saving, along with faster convergence and enhanced conditional fidelity due to CSAS, making multi-conditioned generation more scalable and resource-efficient.
AI.conclusion: PKA offers a scalable, resource-friendly framework for high-fidelity multi-conditioned generation in diffusion transformers by removing redundant cross-modal interactions and focusing learning on the most impactful denoising steps.

title: Parameters as Experts: Adapting Vision Models with Dynamic Parameter Routing
summary: Adapting pre-trained vision models using parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) remains challenging, as it aims to achieve performance comparable to full fine-tuning using a minimal number of trainable parameters. When applied to complex dense prediction tasks, existing methods exhibit limitations, including input-agnostic modeling and redundant cross-layer representations. To this end, we propose AdaRoute, a new adapter-style method featuring a simple mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture. Specifically, we introduce shared expert centers, where each expert is a trainable parameter matrix. During a feedforward pass, each AdaRoute module in the network dynamically generates weight matrices tailored for the current module via a simple dynamic parameter routing mechanism, which selectively aggregates parameter matrices in the corresponding expert center. Dynamic weight matrices in AdaRoute modules facilitate low-rank adaptation in an input-dependent manner, thus generating more customized and powerful feature representations. Moreover, since AdaRoute modules across multiple network layers share the same expert center, they improve feature diversity by promoting implicit cross-layer feature interaction. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of AdaRoute on diverse vision tasks, including semantic segmentation, object detection and instance segmentation, and panoptic segmentation. Code will be available at: https://bit.ly/3NZcr0H.

AI.tldr: AdaRoute is a parameter-efficient adapter for vision models using a shared MoE of expert matrices to dynamically generate module-specific weights, enabling input-conditioned low-rank adaptation and improved cross-layer feature diversity, with strong results on dense vision tasks.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of existing PEFT methods in dense prediction: input-agnostic modeling and redundant cross-layer representations; aim to achieve full-tuning-like performance with few trainable parameters.
AI.method: AdaRoute: adapter-style MoE with shared expert centers; each expert is a trainable matrix; during feedforward, dynamic parameter routing selects and aggregates expert matrices to produce module-specific weight matrices; cross-layer sharing across layers fosters cross-layer interactions; supports low-rank adaptation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on semantic segmentation, object detection/instance segmentation, and panoptic segmentation show AdaRoute's superiority; code forthcoming.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, input-aware, low-parameter adaptation via AdaRoute offers improved representation power and cross-layer diversity for dense vision tasks.

title: Temperature Scaling Attack Disrupting Model Confidence in Federated Learning
summary: Predictive confidence serves as a foundational control signal in mission-critical systems, directly governing risk-aware logic such as escalation, abstention, and conservative fallback. While prior federated learning attacks predominantly target accuracy or implant backdoors, we identify confidence calibration as a distinct attack objective. We present the Temperature Scaling Attack (TSA), a training-time attack that degrades calibration while preserving accuracy. By injecting temperature scaling with learning rate-temperature coupling during local training, malicious updates maintain benign-like optimization behavior, evading accuracy-based monitoring and similarity-based detection. We provide a convergence analysis under non-IID settings, showing that this coupling preserves standard convergence bounds while systematically distorting confidence. Across three benchmarks, TSA substantially shifts calibration (e.g., 145% error increase on CIFAR-100) with <2 accuracy change, and remains effective under robust aggregation and post-hoc calibration defenses. Case studies further show that confidence manipulation can cause up to 7.2x increases in missed critical cases (healthcare) or false alarms (autonomous driving), even when accuracy is unchanged. Overall, our results establish calibration integrity as a critical attack surface in federated learning.

AI.tldr: A training-time attack (TSA) degrades calibration in federated learning by injecting temperature scaling with learning-rate coupling into local updates, preserving accuracy while biasing confidence estimates. Demonstrated to be robust against common defenses and capable of causing significant risk in mission-critical scenarios.
AI.motivation: Confidence calibration is critical for risk-aware decision-making in mission-critical FL systems. Unlike prior attacks focused on accuracy or backdoors, calibration integrity directly affects escalation decisions and false alarms.
AI.method: Introduce Temperature Scaling Attack (TSA): a training-time attack that couples the learning rate with a temperature parameter and injects temperature scaling into local updates to degrade calibration while maintaining benign optimization behavior. Provide convergence analysis under non-IID settings showing standard convergence bounds hold despite the coupling. Evaluate on three benchmarks, showing substantial calibration degradation with minimal accuracy loss, and test under robust aggregation and post-hoc calibration defenses. Include case studies illustrating practical impacts on missed critical cases and false alarms.
AI.result: TSA substantially worsens calibration (e.g., ~145% error increase on CIFAR-100) with less than 2% accuracy change. Remains effective under robust aggregation and post-hoc calibration defenses. Case studies show up to 7.2x increases in missed critical cases (healthcare) or false alarms (autonomous driving).
AI.conclusion: Calibration integrity is a critical but underexplored attack surface in federated learning; defenses must address confidence calibration, not just accuracy, to mitigate mission-critical risks.

title: Degradation of Feature Space in Continual Learning
summary: Centralized training is the standard paradigm in deep learning, enabling models to learn from a unified dataset in a single location. In such setup, isotropic feature distributions naturally arise as a mean to support well-structured and generalizable representations. In contrast, continual learning operates on streaming and non-stationary data, and trains models incrementally, inherently facing the well-known plasticity-stability dilemma. In such settings, learning dynamics tends to yield increasingly anisotropic feature space. This arises a fundamental question: should isotropy be enforced to achieve a better balance between stability and plasticity, and thereby mitigate catastrophic forgetting? In this paper, we investigate whether promoting feature-space isotropy can enhance representation quality in continual learning. Through experiments using contrastive continual learning techniques on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 data, we find that isotropic regularization fails to improve, and can in fact degrade, model accuracy in continual settings. Our results highlight essential differences in feature geometry between centralized and continual learning, suggesting that isotropy, while beneficial in centralized setups, may not constitute an appropriate inductive bias for non-stationary learning scenarios.

AI.tldr: Isotropy regularization does not improve continual learning; isotropy helps centralized training but may hinder non-stationary learning due to differing feature geometry and inductive biases.
AI.motivation: To determine whether promoting feature-space isotropy can mitigate forgetting by balancing stability and plasticity in continual learning.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation using contrastive continual learning techniques on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100; applying isotropic regularization and assessing impact on accuracy.
AI.result: Isotropic regularization fails to improve and can degrade performance in continual learning scenarios; feature geometry in continual learning is more anisotropic than in centralized training; isotropy may be an unsuitable inductive bias for non-stationary data.
AI.conclusion: Isotropy is beneficial in centralized settings but not advisable for continual learning; different inductive biases may be required for robust non-stationary learning.

title: RFDM: Residual Flow Diffusion Model for Efficient Causal Video Editing
summary: Instructional video editing applies edits to an input video using only text prompts, enabling intuitive natural-language control. Despite rapid progress, most methods still require fixed-length inputs and substantial compute. Meanwhile, autoregressive video generation enables efficient variable-length synthesis, yet remains under-explored for video editing. We introduce a causal, efficient video editing model that edits variable-length videos frame by frame. For efficiency, we start from a 2D image-to-image (I2I) diffusion model and adapt it to video-to-video (V2V) editing by conditioning the edit at time step t on the model's prediction at t-1. To leverage videos' temporal redundancy, we propose a new I2I diffusion forward process formulation that encourages the model to predict the residual between the target output and the previous prediction. We call this Residual Flow Diffusion Model (RFDM), which focuses the denoising process on changes between consecutive frames. Moreover, we propose a new benchmark that better ranks state-of-the-art methods for editing tasks.
