Trained on paired video data for global/local style transfer and object removal, RFDM surpasses I2I-based methods and competes with fully spatiotemporal (3D) V2V models, while matching the compute of image models and scaling independently of input video length. More content can be found in: https://smsd75.github.io/RFDM_page/

AI.tldr: A novel Residual Flow Diffusion Model (RFDM) enables efficient, variable-length video editing by advancing a 2D image-to-image diffusion model to video, conditioning each frame's edit on the previous frame prediction and predicting residual changes to leverage temporal redundancy; introduces a new benchmark and achieves competitive results with lower compute.
AI.motivation: Current video editing methods struggle with fixed input lengths and high computational cost, while autoregressive video generation for editing is under-explored. A frame-by-frame, efficient V2V editing approach that scales with video length and preserves temporal coherence is needed.
AI.method: Adapt a 2D I2I diffusion model to V2V editing by conditioning the edit at time t on the model’s prediction at t-1. Introduce a new forward process that encourages the model to predict the residual between the target output and the previous prediction, focusing denoising on changes between consecutive frames. Train on paired video data for global/local style transfer and object removal. Propose a new benchmark for better ranking state-of-the-art editing methods.
AI.result: RFDM surpasses I2I-based methods and competes with full spatiotemporal (3D) V2V models, while matching the compute of image models. It scales with input video length and remains efficient, achieving competitive quality even for longer or variable-length videos.
AI.conclusion: RFDM demonstrates that residual-based, frame-by-frame diffusion can deliver efficient and scalable video editing with natural-language prompts, narrowing the gap between image-based editing models and full video models while offering fixed compute akin to image models and flexibility across input lengths.

title: DiTS: Multimodal Diffusion Transformers Are Time Series Forecasters
summary: While generative modeling on time series facilitates more capable and flexible probabilistic forecasting, existing generative time series models do not address the multi-dimensional properties of time series data well. The prevalent architecture of Diffusion Transformers (DiT), which relies on simplistic conditioning controls and a single-stream Transformer backbone, tends to underutilize cross-variate dependencies in covariate-aware forecasting. Inspired by Multimodal Diffusion Transformers that integrate textual guidance into video generation, we propose Diffusion Transformers for Time Series (DiTS), a general-purpose architecture that frames endogenous and exogenous variates as distinct modalities. To better capture both inter-variate and intra-variate dependencies, we design a dual-stream Transformer block tailored for time-series data, comprising a Time Attention module for autoregressive modeling along the temporal dimension and a Variate Attention module for cross-variate modeling. Unlike the common approach for images, which flattens 2D token grids into 1D sequences, our design leverages the low-rank property inherent in multivariate dependencies, thereby reducing computational costs. Experiments show that DiTS achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks, regardless of the presence of future exogenous variate observations, demonstrating unique generative forecasting strengths over traditional deterministic deep forecasting models.

AI.tldr: DiTS introduces a diffusion-based, dual-stream Transformer for time series that treats endogenous and exogenous variates as distinct modalities, using Time Attention for temporal autoregression and Variate Attention for cross-variate modeling while leveraging low-rank multivariate dependencies to reduce compute; it achieves state-of-the-art generative forecasting on benchmarks with or without future exogenous observations.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based time-series models (e.g., DiT) underutilize cross-variate dependencies and single-stream backbones, limiting predictive flexibility for covariate-aware forecasting. There is a need to explicitly model multi-variate relationships and autoregressive dynamics in a scalable, modality-aware framework inspired by multimodal diffusion approaches.
AI.method: Propose DiTS, a general-purpose architecture that treats endogenous and exogenous variates as separate modalities in a dual-stream Transformer. Introduce a Time Attention module for autoregressive temporal modeling and a Variate Attention module for cross-variate modeling. Avoid flattening 2D token grids; exploit low-rank structure in multivariate dependencies to reduce computation. Train with diffusion-based generative objective for forecasting with or without future exogenous observations.
AI.result: DiTS achieves state-of-the-art performance on time-series forecasting benchmarks across settings with and without future exogenous observations, demonstrating strong generative capabilities beyond traditional deterministic deep forecasting models.
AI.conclusion: A modality-aware, dual-stream Transformer with time- and variate-attention enables efficient, accurate generative forecasting for multi-variate time series by capturing both inter- and intra-variate dependencies while leveraging low-rank structure to reduce computation.

title: NanoFLUX: Distillation-Driven Compression of Large Text-to-Image Generation Models for Mobile Devices
summary: While large-scale text-to-image diffusion models continue to improve in visual quality, their increasing scale has widened the gap between state-of-the-art models and on-device solutions. To address this gap, we introduce NanoFLUX, a 2.4B text-to-image flow-matching model distilled from 17B FLUX.1-Schnell using a progressive compression pipeline designed to preserve generation quality. Our contributions include: (1) A model compression strategy driven by pruning redundant components in the diffusion transformer, reducing its size from 12B to 2B; (2) A ResNet-based token downsampling mechanism that reduces latency by allowing intermediate blocks to operate on lower-resolution tokens while preserving high-resolution processing elsewhere; (3) A novel text encoder distillation approach that leverages visual signals from early layers of the denoiser during sampling. Empirically, NanoFLUX generates 512 x 512 images in approximately 2.5 seconds on mobile devices, demonstrating the feasibility of high-quality on-device text-to-image generation.

AI.tldr: NanoFLUX compresses a 17B diffusion model into a 2B on-device text-to-image model via pruning, token downsampling, and text encoder distillation from denoiser signals, achieving 512×512 images in ~2.5s on mobile.
AI.motivation: Close the performance gap between state-of-the-art large diffusion models and practical on-device generation, while preserving image quality under aggressive model compression.
AI.method: 1) Prune redundant components in the diffusion transformer, shrinking parameters from 12B to 2B. 2) Introduce a ResNet-based token downsampling mechanism so intermediate blocks operate on lower-resolution tokens while maintaining high-resolution processing elsewhere. 3) Distill the text encoder by leveraging visual signals from early denoiser layers during sampling.
AI.result: NanoFLUX is a 2.4B T2I flow-matching model capable of generating 512×512 images in approximately 2.5 seconds on mobile devices, indicating feasible high-quality on-device T2I generation.
AI.conclusion: A successful demonstration of aggressive compression with targeted architectural innovations enabling practical on-device diffusion-based T2I generation, balancing latency, size, and visual quality.

title: The hidden risks of temporal resampling in clinical reinforcement learning
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures. Currently under submission to npj Digital Medicine
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (ORL) has shown potential for improving decision-making in healthcare. However, contemporary research typically aggregates patient data into fixed time intervals, simplifying their mapping to standard ORL frameworks. The impact of these temporal manipulations on model safety and efficacy remains poorly understood. In this work, using both a gridworld navigation task and the UVA/Padova clinical diabetes simulator, we demonstrate that temporal resampling significantly degrades the performance of offline reinforcement learning algorithms during live deployment. We propose three mechanisms that drive this failure: (i) the generation of counterfactual trajectories, (ii) the distortion of temporal expectations, and (iii) the compounding of generalisation errors. Crucially, we find that standard off-policy evaluation metrics can fail to detect these drops in performance. Our findings reveal a fundamental risk in current healthcare ORL pipelines and emphasise the need for methods that explicitly handle the irregular timing of clinical decision-making.

AI.tldr: Temporal resampling harms offline RL in healthcare, revealing three failure pathways and limitations of standard off-policy evaluation, underscoring need for irregular-timing-aware methods.
AI.motivation: offline RL in healthcare often relies on fixed-interval data; the impact of temporal manipulations on safety and efficacy is poorly understood, posing deployment risks.
AI.method: Experiments on a gridworld navigation task and the UVA/Padova diabetes simulator to study offline RL under temporal resampling; identify three driving mechanisms (counterfactual trajectories, distortion of temporal expectations, and compounded generalisation errors); evaluate the effectiveness of off-policy evaluation metrics.
AI.result: Temporal resampling significantly degrades live performance of offline RL; the three identified mechanisms drive failures; standard off-policy evaluation metrics can fail to detect performance drops.
AI.conclusion: Highlights fundamental risk in current healthcare ORL pipelines and calls for methods that explicitly handle irregular timing in clinical decision-making.

title: Prompt Reinjection: Alleviating Prompt Forgetting in Multimodal Diffusion Transformers
summary: Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MMDiTs) for text-to-image generation maintain separate text and image branches, with bidirectional information flow between text tokens and visual latents throughout denoising. In this setting, we observe a prompt forgetting phenomenon: the semantics of the prompt representation in the text branch is progressively forgotten as depth increases. We further verify this effect on three representative MMDiTs--SD3, SD3.5, and FLUX.1 by probing linguistic attributes of the representations over the layers in the text branch. Motivated by these findings, we introduce a training-free approach, prompt reinjection, which reinjects prompt representations from early layers into later layers to alleviate this forgetting. Experiments on GenEval, DPG, and T2I-CompBench++ show consistent gains in instruction-following capability, along with improvements on metrics capturing preference, aesthetics, and overall text--image generation quality.

AI.tldr: Prompt forgetting occurs in MMDiTs: the text branch's prompt semantics fade with depth during denoising. A training-free prompt reinjection reintroduces early-layer prompt representations into later layers, mitigating forgetting and boosting instruction-following and generation quality across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To preserve and leverage prompt semantics in the text stream of bidirectional text-vision diffusion models, where cross-modal interactions can dilute the original prompt as denoising progresses.
AI.method: A training-free technique that reinjects prompt representations from early text-branch layers into later layers during the diffusion denoising process, without additional training or fine-tuning.
AI.result: Consistent gains in instruction-following and improvements on metrics for preference, aesthetics, and overall text–image generation quality across GenEval, DPG, and T2I-CompBench++, demonstrated on SD3, SD3.5, and FLUX.
AI.conclusion: Prompt reinjection effectively mitigates prompt forgetting in MMDiTs, enabling better alignment between user prompts and generated images without retraining, and is broadly applicable across multiple multimodal diffusion architectures.

title: SaDiT: Efficient Protein Backbone Design via Latent Structural Tokenization and Diffusion Transformers
summary: Generative models for de novo protein backbone design have achieved remarkable success in creating novel protein structures. However, these diffusion-based approaches remain computationally intensive and slower than desired for large-scale structural exploration. While recent efforts like Proteina have introduced flow-matching to improve sampling efficiency, the potential of tokenization for structural compression and acceleration remains largely unexplored in the protein domain. In this work, we present SaDiT, a novel framework that accelerates protein backbone generation by integrating SaProt Tokenization with a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture. SaDiT leverages a discrete latent space to represent protein geometry, significantly reducing the complexity of the generation process while maintaining theoretical SE(3) equivalence. To further enhance efficiency, we introduce an IPA Token Cache mechanism that optimizes the Invariant Point Attention (IPA) layers by reusing computed token states during iterative sampling. Experimental results demonstrate that SaDiT outperforms state-of-the-art models, including RFDiffusion and Proteina, in both computational speed and structural viability. We evaluate our model across unconditional backbone generation and fold-class conditional generation tasks, where SaDiT shows superior ability to capture complex topological features with high designability.

AI.tldr: SaDiT introduces a discrete latent tokenization for protein backbone diffusion with a Diffusion Transformer and an IPA token cache, achieving faster sampling and improved structural viability over RFDiffusion and Proteina for unconditional and fold-class conditional design.
AI.motivation: Address the high computational cost of diffusion-based de novo protein backbone design and explore whether tokenization can compress structural information to accelerate generation while preserving SE(3) equivariance.
AI.method: Combine SaProt Tokenization with a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) in a discrete latent space to model protein geometry. Introduce IPA Token Cache to reuse computed token states in Invariant Point Attention layers during iterative sampling, enhancing efficiency. Evaluate on unconditional backbone generation and fold-class conditional tasks.
AI.result: SaDiT outperforms state-of-the-art models (RFDiffusion, Proteina) in speed and structural viability, better capturing complex topologies and achieving high designability across both unconditional and fold-class conditional generations.
AI.conclusion: Discrete latent tokenization with diffusion Transformers and caching substantially boosts efficiency and quality for protein backbone design, enabling faster large-scale structural exploration while maintaining important geometric properties; results encourage further exploration of token-based representations in protein design.

title: PANC: Prior-Aware Normalized Cut for Object Segmentation
summary:
Fully unsupervised segmentation pipelines naively seek the most salient object, should this be present. As a result, most of the methods reported in the literature deliver non-deterministic partitions that are sensitive to initialization, seed order, and threshold heuristics.
  We propose PANC, a weakly supervised spectral segmentation framework that uses a minimal set of annotated visual tokens to produce stable, controllable, and reproducible object masks. From the TokenCut approach, we augment the token-token affinity graph with a handful of priors coupled to anchor nodes. By manipulating the graph topology, we bias the spectral eigenspace toward partitions that are consistent with the annotations. Our approach preserves the global grouping enforced by dense self-supervised visual features, trading annotated tokens for significant gains in reproducibility, user control, and segmentation quality.
  Using 5 to 30 annotations per dataset, our training-free method achieves state-of-the-art performance among weakly and unsupervised approaches on standard benchmarks (e.g., DUTS-TE, ECSSD, MS COCO). Contrarily, it excels in domains where dense labels are costly or intra-class differences are subtle. We report strong and reliable results on homogeneous, fine-grained, and texture-limited domains, achieving 96.8% (+14.43% over SotA), 78.0% (+0.2%), and 78.8% (+0.37%) average mean intersection-over-union (mIoU) on CrackForest (CFD), CUB-200-2011, and HAM10000 datasets, respectively. For multi-object benchmarks, the framework showcases explicit, user-controllable semantic segmentation.

AI.tldr: PANC is a training-free, weakly supervised spectral segmentation framework that injects a few annotated tokens into a TokenCut graph to bias partitions, achieving reproducible, controllable segmentation with minimal supervision and state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised segmentation often yields non-deterministic, initialization-sensitive partitions and lacks user control. A method that provides reproducible, controllable segmentation with minimal annotations is desirable, especially in domains with limited labeling.
AI.method: Augment the token-token affinity graph from TokenCut with priors anchored to annotated tokens; bias the spectral eigenspace via graph topology changes; rely on dense self-supervised features for global grouping; training-free; uses 5–30 annotations; capable of multi-object segmentation with explicit user control.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among weakly supervised and unsupervised methods on benchmarks such as DUTS-TE, ECSSD, and MS COCO; excels in domains with costly dense labels or subtle intra-class differences; reported mIoU on CFD 96.8% (+14.43% over SotA), CUB-200-2011 78.0% (+0.2%), HAM10000 78.8% (+0.37%); demonstrates explicit, user-controllable segmentation for multi-object cases.
AI.conclusion: PANC provides robust, reproducible, and controllable segmentation with minimal supervision by leveraging annotated tokens to steer spectral partitions while preserving global grouping from dense features; broad applicability to homogeneous, fine-grained, and texture-limited domains and multi-object scenarios.

title: F-GRPO: Don't Let Your Policy Learn the Obvious and Forget the Rare
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is commonly based on group sampling to estimate advantages and stabilize policy updates. In practice, large group sizes are not feasible due to computational limits, which biases learning toward trajectories that are already likely. Smaller groups often miss rare-correct trajectories while still containing mixed rewards, concentrating probability on common solutions. We derive the probability that updates miss rare-correct modes as a function of group size, showing non-monotonic behavior, and characterize how updates redistribute mass within the correct set, revealing that unsampled-correct mass can shrink even as total correct mass grows. Motivated by this analysis, we propose a difficulty-aware advantage scaling coefficient, inspired by Focal loss, that down-weights updates on high-success prompts. The lightweight modification can be directly integrated into any group-relative RLVR algorithm such as GRPO, DAPO, and CISPO. On Qwen2.5-7B across in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, our method improves pass@256 from 64.1 $\rightarrow$ 70.3 (GRPO), 69.3 $\rightarrow$ 72.5 (DAPO), and 73.2 $\rightarrow$ 76.8 (CISPO), while preserving or improving pass@1, without increasing group size or computational cost.

AI.tldr: RLVR suffers sampling bias from group sizes, which can miss rare-correct trajectories. The authors quantify this miss probability, show non-monotonic effects on updates, and introduce a focal-like difficulty-aware advantage scaling to down-weight easy/high-success updates, improving pass@256 across three group-relative RLVR methods without extra cost.
AI.motivation: Group sampling in RLVR biases updates toward common trajectories and can miss rare-correct ones, especially with small group sizes. Understanding how group size affects update distribution and proposing a bias-robust remedy is needed to improve robustness and performance across domains.
AI.method: 1) Derive the probability of missing rare-correct modes as a function of group size and analyze how updates redistribute probability mass within the correct set. 2) Propose a difficulty-aware advantage scaling coefficient (inspired by Focal loss) that down-weights updates on high-success prompts. 3) Integrate this lightweight modification into group-relative RLVR algorithms (GRPO, DAPO, CISPO).
AI.result: Empirically, on Qwen2.5-7B across in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, pass@256 improves from 64.1 to 70.3 (GRPO), 69.3 to 72.5 (DAPO), and 73.2 to 76.8 (CISPO); pass@1 is preserved or improved; no increase in group size or computational cost.
AI.conclusion: A simple, resource-light adjustment—focal-like down-weighting of easy/high-success updates—mitigates sampling bias in RLVR, yielding substantial gains in higher-quality solution discovery without added cost, and is directly compatible with existing group-relative RLVR frameworks.

title: Adaptive-CaRe: Adaptive Causal Regularization for Robust Outcome Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of outcomes is crucial for clinical decision-making and personalized patient care. Supervised machine learning algorithms, which are commonly used for outcome prediction in the medical domain, optimize for predictive accuracy, which can result in models latching onto spurious correlations instead of robust predictors. Causal structure learning methods on the other hand have the potential to provide robust predictors for the target, but can be too conservative because of algorithmic and data assumptions, resulting in loss of diagnostic precision. Therefore, we propose a novel model-agnostic regularization strategy, Adaptive-CaRe, for generalized outcome prediction in the medical domain. Adaptive-CaRe strikes a balance between both predictive value and causal robustness by incorporating a penalty that is proportional to the difference between the estimated statistical contribution and estimated causal contribution of the input features for model predictions. Our experiments on synthetic data establish the efficacy of the proposed Adaptive-CaRe regularizer in finding robust predictors for the target while maintaining competitive predictive accuracy. With experiments on a standard causal benchmark, we provide a blueprint for navigating the trade-off between predictive accuracy and causal robustness by tweaking the regularization strength, $λ$. Validation using real-world dataset confirms that the results translate to practical, real-domain settings. Therefore, Adaptive-CaRe provides a simple yet effective solution to the long-standing trade-off between predictive accuracy and causal robustness in the medical domain. Future work would involve studying alternate causal structure learning frameworks and complex classification models to provide deeper insights at a larger scale.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic regularizer (Adaptive-CaRe) balances predictive accuracy and causal robustness for medical outcomes by penalizing the mismatch between statistical and causal feature contributions; validated on synthetic data, a causal benchmark, and real data.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off between high predictive accuracy (risk of exploiting spurious correlations) and causal robustness (potential loss in diagnostic precision) in medical outcome prediction.
AI.method: Introduce Adaptive-CaRe, a regularizer that is model-agnostic and proportional to the difference between estimated statistical contribution and estimated causal contribution of input features to predictions; controlled by regularization strength λ.
AI.result: Synthetic data show robustness with competitive accuracy; a standard causal benchmark demonstrates controllable trade-off via λ; real-world data validate practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive-CaRe offers a simple, effective solution to the predictive accuracy–causal robustness trade-off in medical settings; future work includes exploring other causal structure learning frameworks and more complex models at larger scales.

title: Seeing Beyond Redundancy: Task Complexity's Role in Vision Token Specialization in VLLMs
summary: Vision capabilities in vision large language models (VLLMs) have consistently lagged behind their linguistic capabilities. In particular, numerous benchmark studies have demonstrated that VLLMs struggle when fine-grained visual information or spatial reasoning is required. However, we do not yet understand exactly why VLLMs struggle so much with these tasks relative to others. Some works have focused on visual redundancy as an explanation, where high-level visual information is uniformly spread across numerous tokens and specific, fine-grained visual information is discarded. In this work, we investigate this premise in greater detail, seeking to better understand exactly how various types of visual information are processed by the model and what types of visual information are discarded. To do so, we introduce a simple synthetic benchmark dataset that is specifically constructed to probe various visual features, along with a set of metrics for measuring visual redundancy, allowing us to better understand the nuances of their relationship. Then, we explore fine-tuning VLLMs on a number of complex visual tasks to better understand how redundancy and compression change based upon the complexity of the data that a model is trained on. We find that there is a connection between task complexity and visual compression, implying that having a sufficient ratio of high complexity visual data is crucial for altering the way that VLLMs distribute their visual representation and consequently improving their performance on complex visual tasks. We hope that this work will provide valuable insights for training the next generation of VLLMs.

AI.tldr: VLLMs lag on fine-grained visual/spatial tasks; the paper investigates visual redundancy as a cause, builds a synthetic benchmark with redundancy metrics, and studies how data complexity during fine-tuning affects visual representations and performance. A key finding is that higher visual data complexity is needed to shift representations and improve performance on complex tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand why VLLMs struggle with fine-grained/relational visual reasoning and how redundancy and compression in visual representations relate to training data, with the goal of guiding training of future VLLMs.
AI.method: Introduce a synthetic benchmark to probe different visual features and define metrics for visual redundancy; conduct fine-tuning of VLLMs on increasingly complex visual tasks to observe changes in redundancy/compression as data complexity varies.
AI.result: There is a link between task complexity and visual compression: higher complexity visual data is crucial for altering how VLLMs distribute visual representations and for improving performance on complex tasks.
AI.conclusion: Insights should inform training data design and curricula for next-generation VLLMs, emphasizing sufficient high-complexity visual data to enable better fine-grained visual understanding.

title: Pairwise is Not Enough: Hypergraph Neural Networks for Multi-Agent Pathfinding
summary: Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) is a representative multi-agent coordination problem, where multiple agents are required to navigate to their respective goals without collisions. Solving MAPF optimally is known to be NP-hard, leading to the adoption of learning-based approaches to alleviate the online computational burden. Prevailing approaches, such as Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), are typically constrained to pairwise message passing between agents. However, this limitation leads to suboptimal behaviours and critical issues, such as attention dilution, particularly in dense environments where group (i.e. beyond just two agents) coordination is most critical. Despite the importance of such higher-order interactions, existing approaches have not been able to fully explore them. To address this representational bottleneck, we introduce HMAGAT (Hypergraph Multi-Agent Attention Network), a novel architecture that leverages attentional mechanisms over directed hypergraphs to explicitly capture group dynamics. Empirically, HMAGAT establishes a new state-of-the-art among learning-based MAPF solvers: e.g., despite having just 1M parameters and being trained on 100$\times$ less data, it outperforms the current SoTA 85M parameter model. Through detailed analysis of HMAGAT's attention values, we demonstrate how hypergraph representations mitigate the attention dilution inherent in GNNs and capture complex interactions where pairwise methods fail. Our results illustrate that appropriate inductive biases are often more critical than the training data size or sheer parameter count for multi-agent problems.

AI.tldr: Introduces HMAGAT, a hypergraph-based attention network for MAPF that models higher-order group interactions; achieves state-of-the-art with far fewer parameters and data by alleviating attention dilution in pairwise GNNs.
AI.motivation: MAPF is NP-hard and existing pairwise GNN approaches fail to capture higher-order group dynamics, causing attention dilution and suboptimal coordination in dense environments.
AI.method: Proposes HMAGAT (Hypergraph Multi-Agent Attention Network) that operates on directed hypergraphs to explicitly model group interactions among agents, using attention mechanisms over hyperedges for message passing.
AI.result: HMAGAT achieves state-of-the-art performance among learning-based MAPF solvers, outperforming an 85M-parameter model with only 1M parameters and 100x less data; attention-value analyses show reduced dilution and improved capture of complex interactions; inductive bias via hypergraphs is more impactful than data size or parameter count.
AI.conclusion: Hypergraph-based attention provides a powerful inductive bias for multi-agent coordination, enabling effective modeling of higher-order interactions and superior performance with limited data and smaller models; warrants further exploration of hypergraph architectures in MAPF and related multi-agent tasks.

title: Reliable Mislabel Detection for Video Capsule Endoscopy Data
summary: The classification performance of deep neural networks relies strongly on access to large, accurately annotated datasets. In medical imaging, however, obtaining such datasets is particularly challenging since annotations must be provided by specialized physicians, which severely limits the pool of annotators. Furthermore, class boundaries can often be ambiguous or difficult to define which further complicates machine learning-based classification. In this paper, we want to address this problem and introduce a framework for mislabel detection in medical datasets. This is validated on the two largest, publicly available datasets for Video Capsule Endoscopy, an important imaging procedure for examining the gastrointestinal tract based on a video stream of lowresolution images. In addition, potentially mislabeled samples identified by our pipeline were reviewed and re-annotated by three experienced gastroenterologists. Our results show that the proposed framework successfully detects incorrectly labeled data and results in an improved anomaly detection performance after cleaning the datasets compared to current baselines.

AI.tldr: A framework for mislabel detection in medical imaging, validated on Video Capsule Endoscopy datasets; suspected mislabeled samples were reviewed by gastroenterologists, leading to improved anomaly detection after cleaning compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Deep neural networks for medical imaging require large, accurately annotated datasets. Obtaining annotations is costly and accurate boundaries can be ambiguous, causing mislabeled data that degrade model performance. A mislabel-detection framework can improve data quality and downstream tasks.
AI.method: Proposes a mislabel detection framework and validates it on the two largest public Video Capsule Endoscopy datasets. The pipeline flags potentially mislabeled samples; these samples are reviewed and re-annotated by three experienced gastroenterologists. Performance is evaluated by anomaly detection accuracy before and after data cleaning, compared to baseline methods.
AI.result: The framework successfully detects mislabeled data; expert review confirms incorrect labels and enables re-annotation. After cleaning the datasets, anomaly detection performance improves relative to current baselines.
AI.conclusion: A mislabel-detection framework can effectively identify mislabeled samples in medical imaging datasets, and data cleaning based on this pipeline enhances downstream anomaly detection performance, supporting higher-quality medical AI systems.

title: Optimal Abstractions for Verifying Properties of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs)
summary: We present a novel approach for verifying properties of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), a class of neural networks characterized by nonlinear, univariate activation functions typically implemented as piecewise polynomial splines or Gaussian processes. Our method creates mathematical ``abstractions'' by replacing each KAN unit with a piecewise affine (PWA) function, providing both local and global error estimates between the original network and its approximation. These abstractions enable property verification by encoding the problem as a Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP), determining whether outputs satisfy specified properties when inputs belong to a given set. A critical challenge lies in balancing the number of pieces in the PWA approximation: too many pieces add binary variables that make verification computationally intractable, while too few pieces create excessive error margins that yield uninformative bounds. Our key contribution is a systematic framework that exploits KAN structure to find optimal abstractions. By combining dynamic programming at the unit level with a knapsack optimization across the network, we minimize the total number of pieces while guaranteeing specified error bounds. This approach determines the optimal approximation strategy for each unit while maintaining overall accuracy requirements. Empirical evaluation across multiple KAN benchmarks demonstrates that the upfront analysis costs of our method are justified by superior verification results.

AI.tldr: Framework to verify properties of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) by abstracting units as piecewise-affine (PWA) functions, bounding errors, and encoding verification as MILP. It uses dynamic programming and knapsack optimization to minimize the total number of PWA pieces under error constraints; empirical results show favorable verification performance.
AI.motivation: Enable provable verification of KANs with nonlinear univariate activations. Reduce MILP complexity and tighten bounds by optimally trading off approximation granularity (piece count) against error tolerance.
AI.method: Replace each KAN unit with a PWA surrogate; derive local/global error bounds between original and surrogate; encode the property verification problem as a MILP using these bounds; solve a joint optimization over units to minimize total pieces subject to error constraints via dynamic programming (unit-level) and a knapsack (network-wide) formulation.
AI.result: Upfront abstraction analysis costs yield superior verification results with tighter bounds and reduced verification effort across multiple KAN benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Presents an optimal abstraction framework that leverages KAN structure to allocate piecewise-affine approximations per unit while guaranteeing global accuracy, enabling scalable formal verification for KANs.

title: CineScene: Implicit 3D as Effective Scene Representation for Cinematic Video Generation
summary: Cinematic video production requires control over scene-subject composition and camera movement, but live-action shooting remains costly due to the need for constructing physical sets. To address this, we introduce the task of cinematic video generation with decoupled scene context: given multiple images of a static environment, the goal is to synthesize high-quality videos featuring dynamic subject while preserving the underlying scene consistency and following a user-specified camera trajectory. We present CineScene, a framework that leverages implicit 3D-aware scene representation for cinematic video generation. Our key innovation is a novel context conditioning mechanism that injects 3D-aware features in an implicit way: By encoding scene images into visual representations through VGGT, CineScene injects spatial priors into a pretrained text-to-video generation model by additional context concatenation, enabling camera-controlled video synthesis with consistent scenes and dynamic subjects. To further enhance the model's robustness, we introduce a simple yet effective random-shuffling strategy for the input scene images during training. To address the lack of training data, we construct a scene-decoupled dataset with Unreal Engine 5, containing paired videos of scenes with and without dynamic subjects, panoramic images representing the underlying static scene, along with their camera trajectories. Experiments show that CineScene achieves state-of-the-art performance in scene-consistent cinematic video generation, handling large camera movements and demonstrating generalization across diverse environments.

AI.tldr: CineScene enables cinematic video generation from decoupled scene context by using implicit 3D-aware scene representations and VGGT-based 3D-aware feature conditioning to a pretrained text-to-video model, along with a dataset and a training strategy for robustness, achieving state-of-the-art scene-consistent results with large camera movements.
AI.motivation: Live-action production is costly due to constructing physical sets and coordinating camera trajectories. The paper aims to synthesize high-quality, scene-consistent videos of a dynamic subject from static scene images, with user-specified camera motion, and to address data scarcity with a synthetic Unreal Engine 5 dataset.
AI.method: Introduce an implicit 3D-aware scene representation and a context conditioning mechanism that injects 3D-aware features into a pretrained text-to-video model. Scene images are encoded into visual representations via VGGT and concatenated as additional context to guide video synthesis. A random-shuffling strategy for input scene images during training improves robustness. A scene-decoupled dataset is created in Unreal Engine 5, featuring paired scenes with/without dynamic subjects, panoramic scene images, and corresponding camera trajectories.
AI.result: CineScene achieves state-of-the-art performance in scene-consistent cinematic video generation, handling large camera movements and generalizing across diverse environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed decoupled-scene framework successfully enables camera-controlled, scene-consistent video synthesis of dynamic subjects using 3D-aware context injected into a pretrained model, supported by a synthetic Unreal Engine 5 dataset and a simple training strategy.

title: Pruning at Initialisation through the lens of Graphon Limit: Convergence, Expressivity, and Generalisation
summary: Pruning at Initialisation methods discover sparse, trainable subnetworks before training, but their theoretical mechanisms remain elusive. Existing analyses are often limited to finite-width statistics, lacking a rigorous characterisation of the global sparsity patterns that emerge as networks grow large. In this work, we connect discrete pruning heuristics to graph limit theory via graphons, establishing the graphon limit of PaI masks. We introduce a Factorised Saliency Model that encompasses popular pruning criteria and prove that, under regularity conditions, the discrete masks generated by these algorithms converge to deterministic bipartite graphons. This limit framework establishes a novel topological taxonomy for sparse networks: while unstructured methods (e.g., Random, Magnitude) converge to homogeneous graphons representing uniform connectivity, data-driven methods (e.g., SNIP, GraSP) converge to heterogeneous graphons that encode implicit feature selection. Leveraging this continuous characterisation, we derive two fundamental theoretical results: (i) a Universal Approximation Theorem for sparse networks that depends only on the intrinsic dimension of active coordinate subspaces; and (ii) a Graphon-NTK generalisation bound demonstrating how the limit graphon modulates the kernel geometry to align with informative features. Our results transform the study of sparse neural networks from combinatorial graph problems into a rigorous framework of continuous operators, offering a new mechanism for analysing expressivity and generalisation in sparse neural networks.

AI.tldr: Pruning at initialization can be characterized in the graphon limit; discrete masks converge to deterministic bipartite graphons, yielding a topological taxonomy and two main results (universal approximation and Graphon-NTK bounds) for sparse networks.
AI.motivation: To rigorously understand global sparsity patterns emerging from pruning methods as networks grow large and to connect pruning heuristics with graph limit theory.
AI.method: Propose a Factorised Saliency Model, prove convergence of discrete pruning masks to deterministic bipartite graphons under regularity, classify unstructured vs data-driven pruning into homogeneous vs heterogeneous graphons, and derive a Universal Approximation Theorem and a Graphon-NTK generalisation bound.
AI.result: Discrete pruning masks converge to deterministic bipartite graphons in the limit; unstructured methods lead to homogeneous graphons, data-driven methods to heterogeneous graphons; two fundamental results: universal approximation depending on active subspace dimension, and a bound showing Graphon-NTK geometry aligns with informative features.
AI.conclusion: Maps sparse neural networks into a continuous operator framework via graphons, enabling rigorous analysis of expressivity and generalisation for sparse architectures.

title: MedMO: Grounding and Understanding Multimodal Large Language Model for Medical Images
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have rapidly advanced, yet their adoption in medicine remains limited by gaps in domain coverage, modality alignment, and grounded reasoning. In this work, we introduce MedMO, a medical foundation model built upon a generalized MLLM architecture and trained exclusively on large-scale, domain-specific data. MedMO follows a multi-stage training recipe: (i) cross-modal pretraining to align heterogeneous visual encoders with a medical language backbone; (ii) instruction tuning on multi-task supervision that spans captioning, VQA, report generation, retrieval, and grounded disease localization with bounding boxes; and (iii) reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards that combine factuality checks with a box-level GIoU reward to strengthen spatial grounding and step-by-step reasoning in complex clinical scenarios. MedMO consistently outperforms strong open-source medical MLLMs across multiple modalities and tasks. On VQA benchmarks, MedMO achieves an average accuracy improvement of +13.7% over the baseline and performs within 1.9% of the SOTA Fleming-VL. For text-based QA, it attains +6.9% over the baseline and +14.5% over Fleming-VL. In medical report generation, MedMO delivers significant gains in both semantic and clinical accuracy. Moreover, it exhibits strong grounding capability, achieving an IoU improvement of +40.4 over the baseline and +37.0% over Fleming-VL, underscoring its robust spatial reasoning and localization performance. Evaluations across radiology, ophthalmology, and pathology-microscopy confirm MedMO's broad cross-modality generalization. We release two versions of MedMO: 4B and 8B. Project is available at https://genmilab.github.io/MedMO-Page

AI.tldr: MedMO is a domain-specific medical foundation model that extends generalized MLLMs with cross-modal pretraining, multi-task instruction tuning, and RL-based grounding, achieving strong cross-modality performance across radiology, ophthalmology, and pathology tasks, in 4B and 8B sizes.
AI.motivation: Addresses gaps in domain coverage, modality alignment, and grounded reasoning in medical MLLMs by training exclusively on medical data and incorporating explicit grounding and factuality constraints.
AI.method: Three-stage: (i) cross-modal pretraining to align medical visual encoders with a medical language backbone; (ii) multi-task instruction tuning across captioning, VQA, report generation, retrieval, and grounded disease localization with bounding boxes; (iii) reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards combining factuality checks and a box-level GIoU term to enhance spatial grounding and stepwise clinical reasoning.
AI.result: MedMO outperforms strong open-source medical MLLMs across tasks. VQA: +13.7% accuracy vs baseline; within 1.9% of Fleming-VL SOTA. Text QA: +6.9% vs baseline; +14.5% vs Fleming-VL. Report generation shows semantic and clinical gains. Grounding: IoU up by +40.4 vs baseline and +37.0% vs Fleming-VL. Demonstrates broad generalization across radiology, ophthalmology, pathology-microscopy. Released in 4B and 8B variants; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: MedMO demonstrates strong cross-modality generalization and robust grounding/stepwise reasoning in medical AI, achieving competitive to SOTA performance and offering scalable model sizes for practical deployment.

title: AEGIS: Adversarial Target-Guided Retention-Data-Free Robust Concept Erasure from Diffusion Models
summary: Concept erasure helps stop diffusion models (DMs) from generating harmful content; but current methods face robustness retention trade off. Robustness means the model fine-tuned by concept erasure methods resists reactivation of erased concepts, even under semantically related prompts. Retention means unrelated concepts are preserved so the model's overall utility stays intact. Both are critical for concept erasure in practice, yet addressing them simultaneously is challenging, as existing works typically improve one factor while sacrificing the other. Prior work typically strengthens one while degrading the other, e.g., mapping a single erased prompt to a fixed safe target leaves class level remnants exploitable by prompt attacks, whereas retention-oriented schemes underperform against adaptive adversaries. This paper introduces Adversarial Erasure with Gradient Informed Synergy (AEGIS), a retention-data-free framework that advances both robustness and retention.

AI.tldr: AEGIS is a retention-data-free adversarial erasure framework that jointly improves robustness against reactivation of erased concepts and retention of unrelated content in diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Current concept erasure methods trade robustness for retention; they either map erased prompts to fixed safe targets (robustness loss) or preserve utility but are vulnerable to adaptive prompts. A robust, retention-preserving erasure is needed.
AI.method: Proposes Adversarial Erasure with Gradient Informed Synergy (AEGIS), a retention-data-free framework that uses adversarial prompts and gradient-informed optimization to jointly maximize erasure robustness and retention without requiring retention data.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that AEGIS achieves improved robustness against reactivation and better retention of unrelated concepts compared to prior methods.
AI.conclusion: AEGIS provides a practical, retention-data-free solution to the robustness-retention trade-off in concept erasure for diffusion models.

title: Memory-Conditioned Flow-Matching for Stable Autoregressive PDE Rollouts
summary: Autoregressive generative PDE solvers can be accurate one step ahead yet drift over long rollouts, especially in coarse-to-fine regimes where each step must regenerate unresolved fine scales. This is the regime of diffusion and flow-matching generators: although their internal dynamics are Markovian, rollout stability is governed by per-step \emph{conditional law} errors. Using the Mori--Zwanzig projection formalism, we show that eliminating unresolved variables yields an exact resolved evolution with a Markov term, a memory term, and an orthogonal forcing, exposing a structural limitation of memoryless closures. Motivated by this, we introduce memory-conditioned diffusion/flow-matching with a compact online state injected into denoising via latent features. Via disintegration, memory induces a structured conditional tail prior for unresolved scales and reduces the transport needed to populate missing frequencies. We prove Wasserstein stability of the resulting conditional kernel. We then derive discrete Grönwall rollout bounds that separate memory approximation from conditional generation error. Experiments on compressible flows with shocks and multiscale mixing show improved accuracy and markedly more stable long-horizon rollouts, with better fine-scale spectral and statistical fidelity.

AI.tldr: Memory-conditioned diffusion/flow-matching is proposed to curb long-horizon drift in autoregressive PDE solvers by incorporating Mori–Zwanzig memory terms. The approach derives a decomposed dynamics (Markov, memory, orthogonal forcing), injects online latent memory into denoising, and establishes Wasserstein stability plus discrete Grönwall bounds, with empirically improved stability and fidelity on compressible, multiscale flows.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive PDE solvers are accurate one step ahead but drift over long rollouts due to unresolved fine scales in coarse-to-fine regimes. Memoryless closures fail to capture structured correlations across scales, particularly in diffusion/flow-matching generators where per-step conditional errors dominate rollout stability.
AI.method: Apply Mori–Zwanzig projection to obtain an exact resolved system with a Markov term, a memory term, and an orthogonal forcing. Introduce memory-conditioned diffusion/flow-matching by injecting a compact online state into the denoising network via learned latent features. Use disintegration to model memory as a structured conditional tail prior for unresolved scales and reduce transport needed to populate missing frequencies. Prove Wasserstein stability of the conditional kernel. Derive discrete Grönwall rollout bounds separating memory approximation error from conditional generation error.
AI.result: Memory-conditioned scheme yields more stable long-horizon rollouts and improved fine-scale fidelity. The memory term acts as an explicit conduit for unresolved dynamics, and the derived stability results (Wasserstein) and Grönwall-type bounds provide a theoretical foundation for improved robustness. Empirical tests on compressible flows with shocks and multiscale mixing show enhanced accuracy and spectral/statistical fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating memory via Mori–Zwanzig-inspired conditioning mitigates roll-out drift and yields provable stability guarantees for conditional diffusion/flow-matching. The framework generalizes to multiscale physics problems where unresolved scales drive instability, with potential for broader adoption in diffusion-based solvers and other autoregressive PDE models. Further work should quantify memory complexity, training dynamics, and generalization to different regimes.

title: CORP: Closed-Form One-shot Representation-Preserving Structured Pruning for Vision Transformers
summary: Vision Transformers achieve strong accuracy but incur high compute and memory cost. Structured pruning can reduce inference cost, but most methods rely on retraining or multi-stage optimization. These requirements limit post-training deployment. We propose \textbf{CORP}, a closed-form one-shot structured pruning framework for Vision Transformers. CORP removes entire MLP hidden dimensions and attention substructures without labels, gradients, or fine-tuning. It operates under strict post-training constraints using only a small unlabeled calibration set. CORP formulates structured pruning as a representation recovery problem. It models removed activations and attention logits as affine functions of retained components and derives closed-form ridge regression solutions that fold compensation into model weights. This minimizes expected representation error under the calibration distribution. Experiments on ImageNet with DeiT models show strong redundancy in MLP and attention representations. Without compensation, one-shot structured pruning causes severe accuracy degradation. With CORP, models preserve accuracy under aggressive sparsity. On DeiT-Huge, CORP retains 82.8\% Top-1 accuracy after pruning 50\% of both MLP and attention structures. CORP completes pruning in under 20 minutes on a single GPU and delivers substantial real-world efficiency gains.

AI.tldr: A concise one-shot structured pruning method for Vision Transformers (CORP) that prunes MLP hidden dimensions and attention substructures without labels or fine-tuning via closed-form ridge regression, enabling fast post-training deployment while preserving accuracy under aggressive sparsity.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference cost of Vision Transformers without retraining; address the gap in post-training deployment where many pruning methods require labels, gradients, or multi-stage optimization.
AI.method: Model removed activations and attention logits as affine functions of retained components; derive closed-form ridge regression solutions that fold compensation into weights; use a small unlabeled calibration set; perform one-shot pruning of MLP and attention blocks.
AI.result: Empirical results on ImageNet with DeiT show redundant representations; with CORP, accuracy preserved under aggressive sparsity; on DeiT-Huge pruning 50% of MLP and attention yields 82.8% Top-1; pruning completed in under 20 minutes on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: CORP demonstrates that one-shot structured pruning without labels, gradients, or fine-tuning is feasible for Vision Transformers, delivering meaningful efficiency gains with minimal accuracy loss.

title: On the Identifiability of Steering Vectors in Large Language Models
summary: Activation steering methods, such as persona vectors, are widely used to control large language model behavior and increasingly interpreted as revealing meaningful internal representations. This interpretation implicitly assumes steering directions are identifiable and uniquely recoverable from input-output behavior. We formalize steering as an intervention on internal representations and prove that, under realistic modeling and data conditions, steering vectors are fundamentally non-identifiable due to large equivalence classes of behaviorally indistinguishable interventions. Empirically, we validate this across multiple models and semantic traits, showing orthogonal perturbations achieve near-equivalent efficacy with negligible effect sizes. However, identifiability is recoverable under structural assumptions including statistical independence, sparsity constraints, multi-environment validation or cross-layer consistency. These findings reveal fundamental interpretability limits and clarify structural assumptions required for reliable safety-critical control.

AI.tldr: Steering vectors are fundamentally non-identifiable under realistic conditions; many distinct interventions produce indistinguishable behavior.
AI.motivation: Assess interpretability and safety implications of activation steering used to control LLMs.
AI.method: Formalize steering as interventions on internal representations; prove non-identifiability via equivalence classes; empirical validation across models/traits; explore when identifiability can be recovered.
AI.result: Existence of large equivalence classes of interventions with near-identical effects; orthogonal perturbations yield similar efficacy; identifiability recovers under independence, sparsity, multi-environment validation, cross-layer consistency.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable control via steering is limited unless structural assumptions hold; in safety-critical settings, require such assumptions to justify causal inference about hidden representations.

title: NanoQuant: Efficient Sub-1-Bit Quantization of Large Language Models
comment: 26 pages. Hyochan Chong and Dongkyu Kim contributed equally to this work
summary: Weight-only quantization has become a standard approach for efficiently serving large language models (LLMs). However, existing methods fail to efficiently compress models to binary (1-bit) levels, as they either require large amounts of data and compute or incur additional storage. In this work, we propose NanoQuant, the first post-training quantization (PTQ) method to compress LLMs to both binary and sub-1-bit levels. NanoQuant formulates quantization as a low-rank binary factorization problem, and compresses full-precision weights to low-rank binary matrices and scales. Specifically, it utilizes an efficient alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) method to precisely initialize latent binary matrices and scales, and then tune the initialized parameters through a block and model reconstruction process. Consequently, NanoQuant establishes a new Pareto frontier in low-memory post-training quantization, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy even at sub-1-bit compression rates. NanoQuant makes large-scale deployment feasible on consumer hardware. For example, it compresses Llama2-70B by 25.8$\times$ in just 13 hours on a single H100, enabling a 70B model to operate on a consumer 8 GB GPU.

AI.tldr: NanoQuant enables binary and sub-1-bit post-training quantization for LLMs via low-rank binary factorization with ADMM-based initialization and block/model reconstruction, achieving a new Pareto frontier for memory-efficient deployment.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory and compute barriers for deploying large language models by enabling 1-bit or sub-1-bit quantization without prohibitive data or compute overhead; current weight-only quantization struggles to reach binary levels.
AI.method: Formulate quantization as low-rank binary factorization of full-precision weights into binary matrices and scales. Use an efficient ADMM-based initialization to set latent binary matrices and scale factors, followed by block- and model-reconstruction-based fine-tuning to restore performance.
AI.result: Establishes a new Pareto frontier for low-memory PTQ; achieves state-of-the-art accuracy at sub-1-bit compression; demonstrates 25.8× compression of Llama2-70B in 13 hours on a single H100, enabling a 70B model to run on an 8 GB GPU.
AI.conclusion: NanoQuant makes large-scale deployment feasible on consumer hardware by delivering efficient binary/sub-1-bit PTQ for LLMs.

title: OmniVideo-R1: Reinforcing Audio-visual Reasoning with Query Intention and Modality Attention
summary: While humans perceive the world through diverse modalities that operate synergistically to support a holistic understanding of their surroundings, existing omnivideo models still face substantial challenges on audio-visual understanding tasks. In this paper, we propose OmniVideo-R1, a novel reinforced framework that improves mixed-modality reasoning. OmniVideo-R1 empowers models to "think with omnimodal cues" by two key strategies: (1) query-intensive grounding based on self-supervised learning paradigms; and (2) modality-attentive fusion built upon contrastive learning paradigms. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that OmniVideo-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines, highlighting its effectiveness and robust generalization capabilities.

AI.tldr: OmniVideo-R1 introduces a reinforced framework for omnivideo models that boosts mixed-modality reasoning through query-intensive grounding (self-supervised) and modality-attentive fusion (contrastive), yielding strong cross-benchmark gains.
AI.motivation: Audio-visual understanding in omnivideo models remains challenging. The paper targets improved mixed-modality reasoning by leveraging omnimodal cues via self-supervised grounding and contrastive fusion.
AI.method: Proposes two strategies: (1) query-intensive grounding based on self-supervised learning; (2) modality-attentive fusion built upon contrastive learning, integrated within a reinforced framework.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple benchmarks show OmniVideo-R1 consistently outperforming strong baselines, with robust generalization.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables models to effectively reason with omnimodal cues, enhancing audio-visual understanding and generalization across tasks.

title: Diffeomorphism-Equivariant Neural Networks
summary: Incorporating group symmetries via equivariance into neural networks has emerged as a robust approach for overcoming the efficiency and data demands of modern deep learning. While most existing approaches, such as group convolutions and averaging-based methods, focus on compact, finite, or low-dimensional groups with linear actions, this work explores how equivariance can be extended to infinite-dimensional groups. We propose a strategy designed to induce diffeomorphism equivariance in pre-trained neural networks via energy-based canonicalisation. Formulating equivariance as an optimisation problem allows us to access the rich toolbox of already established differentiable image registration methods. Empirical results on segmentation and classification tasks confirm that our approach achieves approximate equivariance and generalises to unseen transformations without relying on extensive data augmentation or retraining.

AI.tldr: Extends equivariance to infinite-dimensional groups by energy-based canonicalisation to induce diffeomorphism equivariance in pre-trained networks, enabling adaptability to continuous transformations without heavy retraining.
AI.motivation: Group-equivariant networks excel for finite/low-dimensional groups but struggle with continuous/infinite transformations like diffeomorphisms. There is a need to handle continuous symmetry in a data-efficient way, leveraging pre-trained models.
AI.method: Formulate equivariance as an optimization problem. Use energy-based (differentiable) image registration methods to canonicalise inputs or representations, achieving diffeomorphism equivariance. Applied to pre-trained networks to induce equivariance without retraining or extensive augmentation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on segmentation and classification tasks demonstrates approximate diffeomorphism equivariance and generalisation to unseen transformations, reducing reliance on extensive augmentation and retraining.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a viable route to continuous-group equivariance in deep networks via optimization-based canonicalisation, with potential benefits in data efficiency and transferability; further work needed on theoretical guarantees and computational efficiency.

title: AEGPO: Adaptive Entropy-Guided Policy Optimization for Diffusion Models
summary:
Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) shows promise for aligning diffusion and flow models, yet policy optimization methods such as GRPO suffer from inefficient and static sampling strategies. These methods treat all prompts and denoising steps uniformly, ignoring substantial variations in sample learning value as well as the dynamic nature of critical exploration moments.
  To address this issue, we conduct a detailed analysis of the internal attention dynamics during GRPO training and uncover a key insight: attention entropy can serve as a powerful dual-signal proxy. First, across different samples, the relative change in attention entropy (ΔEntropy), which reflects the divergence between the current policy and the base policy, acts as a robust indicator of sample learning value. Second, during the denoising process, the peaks of absolute attention entropy (Entropy(t)), which quantify attention dispersion, effectively identify critical timesteps where high-value exploration occurs.
  Building on this observation, we propose Adaptive Entropy-Guided Policy Optimization (AEGPO), a novel dual-signal, dual-level adaptive optimization strategy. At the global level, AEGPO uses ΔEntropy to dynamically allocate rollout budgets, prioritizing prompts with higher learning value. At the local level, it exploits the peaks of Entropy(t) to guide exploration selectively at critical high-dispersion timesteps rather than uniformly across all denoising steps.
  By focusing computation on the most informative samples and the most critical moments, AEGPO enables more efficient and effective policy optimization. Experiments on text-to-image generation tasks demonstrate that AEGPO significantly accelerates convergence and achieves superior alignment performance compared to standard GRPO variants.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Entropy-Guided Policy Optimization (AEGPO) uses attention-entropy signals to adapt rollout budgets and denoising-step exploration in RLHF GRPO, yielding faster convergence and better alignment in text-to-image diffusion.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies of uniform sampling in GRPO by leveraging variations in sample learning value and critical exploration moments via attention entropy.
AI.method: Analyze attention entropy dynamics during GRPO; define ΔEntropy as the relative change in attention entropy to indicate learning value; identify Entropy(t) peaks during denoising as critical moments; implement AEGPO with global ΔEntropy-guided rollout budgeting and local Entropy(t)-guided selective exploration at high-dispersion timesteps.
AI.result: AEGPO accelerates convergence and improves alignment compared with standard GRPO variants in text-to-image generation experiments.
AI.conclusion: Dual-signal, dual-level adaptive optimization focusing on informative prompts and high-dispersion timesteps enhances the efficiency and effectiveness of RLHF with diffusion/flow models.

title: Explaining Grokking in Transformers through the Lens of Inductive Bias
summary: We investigate grokking in transformers through the lens of inductive bias: dispositions arising from architecture or optimization that let the network prefer one solution over another. We first show that architectural choices such as the position of Layer Normalization (LN) strongly modulates grokking speed. This modulation is explained by isolating how LN on specific pathways shapes shortcut-learning and attention entropy. Subsequently, we study how different optimization settings modulate grokking, inducing distinct interpretations of previously proposed controls such as readout scale. Particularly, we find that using readout scale as a control for lazy training can be confounded by learning rate and weight decay in our setting. Accordingly, we show that features evolve continuously throughout training, suggesting grokking in transformers can be more nuanced than a lazy-to-rich transition of the learning regime. Finally, we show how generalization predictably emerges with feature compressibility in grokking, across different modulators of inductive bias. Our code is released at https://tinyurl.com/y52u3cad.

AI.tldr: Grokking in transformers is shaped by inductive biases from architecture and optimization; LayerNorm placement modulates grokking speed via shortcut-learning and attention entropy; readout-scale as a lazy-training control is confounded by learning rate and weight decay; features evolve continuously; generalization tracks feature compressibility; code released.
AI.motivation: To understand why grokking occurs in transformers by isolating architectural and optimization-induced biases and to clarify interpretations of previous controls like readout scale.
AI.method: Empirical analysis: vary LayerNorm placement to assess its effect on grokking speed; analyze how LN pathways influence shortcut-learning and attention entropy; test optimization settings including learning rate, weight decay, and readout scale; track feature evolution during training; assess generalization via feature compressibility; validate across modulators of inductive bias; release code.
AI.result: LayerNorm location significantly changes grokking speed; LN pathways shape shortcut-learning and attention entropy, explaining the modulation; readout scale's role as a lazy-training control is confounded by lr and weight decay; features evolve continuously, challenging binary transitions; generalization correlates with feature compressibility and is predictable across inductive-bias modulators.
AI.conclusion: Grokking in transformers is governed by inductive biases from architecture and optimization, with continuous feature evolution and a compressibility-based route to generalization; the phenomenon is nuanced rather than a simple lazy-to-rich transition, and code is available for replication.

title: Zero-shot Generalizable Graph Anomaly Detection with Mixture of Riemannian Experts
summary: Graph Anomaly Detection (GAD) aims to identify irregular patterns in graph data, and recent works have explored zero-shot generalist GAD to enable generalization to unseen graph datasets. However, existing zero-shot GAD methods largely ignore intrinsic geometric differences across diverse anomaly patterns, substantially limiting their cross-domain generalization. In this work, we reveal that anomaly detectability is highly dependent on the underlying geometric properties and that embedding graphs from different domains into a single static curvature space can distort the structural signatures of anomalies. To address the challenge that a single curvature space cannot capture geometry-dependent graph anomaly patterns, we propose GAD-MoRE, a novel framework for zero-shot Generalizable Graph Anomaly Detection with a Mixture of Riemannian Experts architecture. Specifically, to ensure that each anomaly pattern is modeled in the Riemannian space where it is most detectable, GAD-MoRE employs a set of specialized Riemannian expert networks, each operating in a distinct curvature space. To align raw node features with curvature-specific anomaly characteristics, we introduce an anomaly-aware multi-curvature feature alignment module that projects inputs into parallel Riemannian spaces, enabling the capture of diverse geometric characteristics. Finally, to facilitate better generalization beyond seen patterns, we design a memory-based dynamic router that adaptively assigns each input to the most compatible expert based on historical reconstruction performance on similar anomalies. Extensive experiments in the zero-shot setting demonstrate that GAD-MoRE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art generalist GAD baselines, and even surpasses strong competitors that are few-shot fine-tuned with labeled data from the target domain.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot generalizable graph anomaly detection using GAD-MoRE: a mixture of Riemannian experts across multiple curvature spaces with anomaly-aware feature alignment and a memory-based dynamic router to assign inputs to the most compatible expert, achieving strong cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Anomaly detectability in graphs is geometry-dependent; a single static curvature space distorts anomalies when transferring across domains, limiting cross-domain generalization for zero-shot GAD.
AI.method: GAD-MoRE introduces a mixture of Riemannian experts, each operating in a distinct curvature space, coupled with an anomaly-aware multi-curvature feature alignment module that maps inputs into parallel Riemannian spaces. A memory-based dynamic router assigns inputs to the best-suited expert based on historical reconstruction performance on similar anomalies.
AI.result: In zero-shot experiments, GAD-MoRE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art generalist GAD baselines and even surpasses strong few-shot fine-tuned competitors on target domains.
AI.conclusion: Modeling geometry-dependent anomaly patterns with a mixture of curvature-specific experts and adaptive routing improves zero-shot generalization in graph anomaly detection across diverse domains.

title: Supercharging Simulation-Based Inference for Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design
summary: Bayesian optimal experimental design (BOED) seeks to maximize the expected information gain (EIG) of experiments. This requires a likelihood estimate, which in many settings is intractable. Simulation-based inference (SBI) provides powerful tools for this regime. However, existing work explicitly connecting SBI and BOED is restricted to a single contrastive EIG bound. We show that the EIG admits multiple formulations which can directly leverage modern SBI density estimators, encompassing neural posterior, likelihood, and ratio estimation. Building on this perspective, we define a novel EIG estimator using neural likelihood estimation. Further, we identify optimization as a key bottleneck of gradient based EIG maximization and show that a simple multi-start parallel gradient ascent procedure can substantially improve reliability and performance. With these innovations, our SBI-based BOED methods are able to match or outperform by up to $22\%$ existing state-of-the-art approaches across standard BOED benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Unifies multiple EIG formulations within SBI-based BOED, introduces a neural likelihood estimator-based EIG, and shows multi-start parallel gradient ascent yields gains up to 22% on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: EIG is the objective in BOED but often intractable; prior work linking SBI and BOED relied on a single EIG bound, limiting estimator choices. Exploring multiple SBI-based EIG formulations can broaden tools and improve efficiency.
AI.method: Demonstrates that EIG can be formulated via neural posterior, likelihood, and ratio estimators from SBI; defines a novel EIG estimator using neural likelihood estimation; identifies optimization as a bottleneck and applies multi-start parallel gradient ascent to enhance reliability.
AI.result: SBI-based BOED methods match or surpass state-of-the-art approaches by up to 22% on standard BOED benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Broadens the EIG estimation toolkit for BOED by leveraging diverse SBI estimators and improving optimization, leading to stronger performance in active data collection.

title: Disentanglement by means of action-induced representations
summary: Learning interpretable representations with variational autoencoders (VAEs) is a major goal of representation learning. The main challenge lies in obtaining disentangled representations, where each latent dimension corresponds to a distinct generative factor. This difficulty is fundamentally tied to the inability to perform nonlinear independent component analysis. Here, we introduce the framework of action-induced representations (AIRs) which models representations of physical systems given experiments (or actions) that can be performed on them. We show that, in this framework, we can provably disentangle degrees of freedom w.r.t. their action dependence. We further introduce a variational AIR architecture (VAIR) that can extract AIRs and therefore achieve provable disentanglement where standard VAEs fail. Beyond state representation, VAIR also captures the action dependence of the underlying generative factors, directly linking experiments to the degrees of freedom they influence.

AI.tldr: Action-induced representations (AIRs) provide an intervention-based framework to achieve disentangled latent factors by exploiting controllable actions; VAIR extends VAEs to extract AIRs with provable disentanglement tied to actions, and to reveal how latent factors depend on actions, outperforming standard VAEs in disentanglement.
AI.motivation: Disentangling latent factors in VAEs is challenging due to nonlinear ICA limitations; leveraging interventions/experiments allows identifying and separating factors by their action dependencies.
AI.method: Define AIR framework; prove that latent degrees of freedom can be disentangled with respect to their action dependence; introduce a variational AIR architecture (VAIR) that learns AIRs from data; analyze action-dependence of generative factors and link experiments to influenced degrees of freedom.
AI.result: The approach provides theoretical guarantees of disentanglement under action interventions; VAIR can extract AIRs and capture how factors depend on actions, achieving disentanglement where standard VAEs fail.
AI.conclusion: AIRs enable provable, intervention-guided disentanglement and directly relate experiments to latent factors; VAIR extends VAEs to learn AIRs, offering more interpretable and experiment-linked representations.

title: From Kepler to Newton: Inductive Biases Guide Learned World Models in Transformers
summary: Can general-purpose AI architectures go beyond prediction to discover the physical laws governing the universe? True intelligence relies on "world models" -- causal abstractions that allow an agent to not only predict future states but understand the underlying governing dynamics. While previous "AI Physicist" approaches have successfully recovered such laws, they typically rely on strong, domain-specific priors that effectively "bake in" the physics. Conversely, Vafa et al. recently showed that generic Transformers fail to acquire these world models, achieving high predictive accuracy without capturing the underlying physical laws. We bridge this gap by systematically introducing three minimal inductive biases. We show that ensuring spatial smoothness (by formulating prediction as continuous regression) and stability (by training with noisy contexts to mitigate error accumulation) enables generic Transformers to surpass prior failures and learn a coherent Keplerian world model, successfully fitting ellipses to planetary trajectories. However, true physical insight requires a third bias: temporal locality. By restricting the attention window to the immediate past -- imposing the simple assumption that future states depend only on the local state rather than a complex history -- we force the model to abandon curve-fitting and discover Newtonian force representations. Our results demonstrate that simple architectural choices determine whether an AI becomes a curve-fitter or a physicist, marking a critical step toward automated scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: Minimal inductive biases enable generic Transformers to learn physical laws instead of mere curve-fitting; with spatial smoothness and stability they capture Keplerian dynamics, and with temporal locality they recover Newtonian forces, showing simple architectural choices steer AI toward scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where general-purpose AI may predict yet fail to infer governing dynamics.
