Previous AI-Physicist work used strong domain priors; Vafa et al. showed Transformers can perform well on prediction without, and may miss underlying laws. The goal is to identify minimal, essential biases that enable world-model learning.
AI.method: Introduce three biases: (1) spatial smoothness via continuous regression; (2) stability by training with noisy contexts to mitigate error accumulation; (3) temporal locality by restricting attention to the immediate past. Evaluate on planetary trajectories to recover Keplerian dynamics and Newtonian force representations.
AI.result: First two biases yield a coherent Keplerian world model and accurate ellipse fitting; adding temporal locality compels the model to learn Newtonian force representations rather than curve-fitting. Overall, architecture choices alone steer from curve-fitter to physicist.
AI.conclusion: Simple architectural biases can transform a general-purpose AI into an agent capable of discovering physical laws, marking progress toward automated scientific discovery.

title: Soft Forward-Backward Representations for Zero-shot Reinforcement Learning with General Utilities
summary: Recent advancements in zero-shot reinforcement learning (RL) have facilitated the extraction of diverse behaviors from unlabeled, offline data sources. In particular, forward-backward algorithms (FB) can retrieve a family of policies that can approximately solve any standard RL problem (with additive rewards, linear in the occupancy measure), given sufficient capacity. While retaining zero-shot properties, we tackle the greater problem class of RL with general utilities, in which the objective is an arbitrary differentiable function of the occupancy measure. This setting is strictly more expressive, capturing tasks such as distribution matching or pure exploration, which may not be reduced to additive rewards. We show that this additional complexity can be captured by a novel, maximum entropy (soft) variant of the forward-backward algorithm, which recovers a family of stochastic policies from offline data. When coupled with zero-order search over compact policy embeddings, this algorithm can sidestep iterative optimization schemes, and optimizes general utilities directly at test-time. Across both didactic and high-dimensional experiments, we demonstrate that our method retains favorable properties of FB algorithms, while also extending their range to more general RL problems.

AI.tldr: Extends zero-shot forward-backward RL to general differentiable utilities via a soft, maximum-entropy variant; enables direct test-time optimization of broad objectives from offline data using zero-order search over compact policy embeddings.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot RL from offline data efficiently recovers policies for standard additive-reward tasks, but many real tasks require general utilities (e.g., distribution matching, exploration). The work aims to broaden zero-shot RL to these richer objectives while preserving offline data advantages.
AI.method: Introduce a maximum-entropy (soft) forward-backward variant that yields a family of stochastic policies from offline data. Combine with zero-order optimization over compact policy embeddings to optimize arbitrary differentiable occupancy-measure utilities at test time, avoiding iterative gradient-based RL updates.
AI.result: The proposed approach maintains the zero-shot, offline-data benefits of forward-backward methods while extending applicability to general differentiable utilities, demonstrated across didactic and high-dimensional experiments.
AI.conclusion: Soft FB extends zero-shot RL to broad objective classes, enabling direct test-time optimization of complex utilities from offline data, with results suggesting retained FB properties and expanded applicability.

title: Vision Transformer Finetuning Benefits from Non-Smooth Components
summary: The smoothness of the transformer architecture has been extensively studied in the context of generalization, training stability, and adversarial robustness. However, its role in transfer learning remains poorly understood. In this paper, we analyze the ability of vision transformer components to adapt their outputs to changes in inputs, or, in other words, their plasticity. Defined as an average rate of change, it captures the sensitivity to input perturbation; in particular, a high plasticity implies low smoothness. We demonstrate through theoretical analysis and comprehensive experiments that this perspective provides principled guidance in choosing the components to prioritize during adaptation. A key takeaway for practitioners is that the high plasticity of the attention modules and feedforward layers consistently leads to better finetuning performance. Our findings depart from the prevailing assumption that smoothness is desirable, offering a novel perspective on the functional properties of transformers. The code is available at https://github.com/ambroiseodt/vit-plasticity.

AI.tldr: Examines how ViT component plasticity affects transfer learning; finds attention and feedforward modules exhibit high plasticity and improve finetuning, challenging the notion that smoothness is always desirable.
AI.motivation: Although transformer smoothness has been linked to generalization and robustness, its role in transfer learning remains unclear. Understanding component-level plasticity can guide adaptation decisions in ViTs.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis plus comprehensive experiments. Plasticity is defined as the average rate of change of outputs with respect to inputs. The study assesses how different ViT components adapt outputs to input changes, by finetuning components (attention modules, feedforward layers) and evaluating transfer performance across architectures.
AI.result: High plasticity in attention modules and feedforward layers yields better finetuning performance, providing principled guidance on which components to prioritize during adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Smoothness as a universal desirable property is challenged; plasticity should be considered as a criterion for adapting transformers in transfer learning; code is provided.

title: Cochain Perspectives on Temporal-Difference Signals for Learning Beyond Markov Dynamics
summary: Non-Markovian dynamics are commonly found in real-world environments due to long-range dependencies, partial observability, and memory effects. The Bellman equation that is the central pillar of Reinforcement learning (RL) becomes only approximately valid under Non-Markovian. Existing work often focus on practical algorithm designs and offer limited theoretical treatment to address key questions, such as what dynamics are indeed capturable by the Bellman framework and how to inspire new algorithm classes with optimal approximations. In this paper, we present a novel topological viewpoint on temporal-difference (TD) based RL. We show that TD errors can be viewed as 1-cochain in the topological space of state transitions, while Markov dynamics are then interpreted as topological integrability. This novel view enables us to obtain a Hodge-type decomposition of TD errors into an integrable component and a topological residual, through a Bellman-de Rham projection. We further propose HodgeFlow Policy Search (HFPS) by fitting a potential network to minimize the non-integrable projection residual in RL, achieving stability/sensitivity guarantees. In numerical evaluations, HFPS is shown to significantly improve RL performance under non-Markovian.

AI.tldr: Introduces a topological view of TD RL under non-Markovian dynamics, decomposing TD errors via a Bellman-de Rham projection into integrable and residual components; proposes HFPS to minimize the non-integrable residual with a potential network, claiming stability guarantees and empirical gains.
AI.motivation: Non-Markovian environments challenge the classical Bellman framework; there is limited theoretical understanding of what dynamics Bellman can capture and how to design optimal approximations.
AI.method: Model TD errors as 1-cochains in the topology of state transitions; interpret Markov dynamics as topological integrability; derive a Hodge-type decomposition (Bellman-de Rham projection) separating integrable and non-integrable parts; introduce HFPS by learning a potential function to minimize the non-integrable residual; provide stability/sensitivity guarantees.
AI.result: HFPS demonstrably improves RL performance under non-Markovian dynamics in numerical experiments, supported by the decomposition framework.
AI.conclusion: Presents a novel topological framework for TD RL under non-Markovian dynamics, offering a decomposition-based understanding and a practical algorithm (HFPS) with theoretical guarantees and empirical gains, inviting further exploration of topological methods in RL.

title: Endogenous Resistance to Activation Steering in Language Models
summary: Large language models can resist task-misaligned activation steering during inference, sometimes recovering mid-generation to produce improved responses even when steering remains active. We term this Endogenous Steering Resistance (ESR). Using sparse autoencoder (SAE) latents to steer model activations, we find that Llama-3.3-70B shows substantial ESR, while smaller models from the Llama-3 and Gemma-2 families exhibit the phenomenon less frequently. We identify 26 SAE latents that activate differentially during off-topic content and are causally linked to ESR in Llama-3.3-70B. Zero-ablating these latents reduces the multi-attempt rate by 25%, providing causal evidence for dedicated internal consistency-checking circuits. We demonstrate that ESR can be deliberately enhanced through both prompting and training: meta-prompts instructing the model to self-monitor increase the multi-attempt rate by 4x for Llama-3.3-70B, and fine-tuning on self-correction examples successfully induces ESR-like behavior in smaller models. These findings have dual implications: ESR could protect against adversarial manipulation but might also interfere with beneficial safety interventions that rely on activation steering. Understanding and controlling these resistance mechanisms is important for developing transparent and controllable AI systems. Code is available at github.com/agencyenterprise/endogenous-steering-resistance.

AI.tldr: ESR is a resistant, endogenous mechanism in LLM activations where some models recover from misaligned steering during generation. Larger models (e.g., Llama-3.3-70B) show strong ESR with identified SAE latents; ablation reduces steering failures; ESR can be amplified via prompting/training, raising both safety and control implications.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether LLMs possess internal consistency-checking circuits that resist activation steering, with implications for making models more robust or harder to steer maliciously.
AI.method: Use sparse autoencoder (SAE) latents to steer model activations and measure multi-attempt rates. Compare Llama-3.3-70B to smaller Llama-3 and Gemma-2 models. Identify SAE latents that differ during off-topic content and test causal links via zero-ablations. Evaluate how prompting and training affect ESR.
AI.result: Llama-3.3-70B exhibits substantial ESR; smaller models show it less frequently. Identified 26 SAE latents causally linked to ESR; zero-ablating lowers multi-attempt rate by ~25%. Meta-prompts increase multi-attempt rate ~4x; fine-tuning on self-correction examples induces ESR-like behavior in smaller models.
AI.conclusion: ESR presents dual implications for safety and control: it can guard against adversarial steering but may hinder beneficial safety interventions. Understanding ESR is crucial for transparent, controllable AI systems; code is available.

title: Calibrating Generative AI to Produce Realistic Essays for Data Augmentation
comment: Artificial Intelligence in Measurement and Education Conference (AIME-Con)
summary: Data augmentation can mitigate limited training data in machine-learning automated scoring engines for constructed response items. This study seeks to determine how well three approaches to large language model prompting produce essays that preserve the writing quality of the original essays and produce realistic text for augmenting ASE training datasets. We created simulated versions of student essays, and human raters assigned scores to them and rated the realism of the generated text. The results of the study indicate that the predict next prompting strategy produces the highest level of agreement between human raters regarding simulated essay scores, predict next and sentence strategies best preserve the rated quality of the original essay in the simulated essays, and predict next and 25 examples strategies produce the most realistic text as judged by human raters.

AI.tldr: Among three LLM prompting strategies for generating simulated essays, the 'predict next' approach yields the highest agreement between human raters on simulated scores; 'predict next' and 'sentence' best preserve the original essays' rated quality; 'predict next' and '25-examples' yield the most realistic text according to human raters.
AI.motivation: Data augmentation for automated scoring engines with constructed response items, aiming to alleviate limited training data while preserving writing quality and producing realistic text for ASE training datasets.
AI.method: Generated simulated student essays using three prompting strategies: predict next, sentence-based prompting, and 25-examples (few-shot) prompting. Human raters scored the simulated essays and rated the realism of the generated text. Analyses examined inter-rater agreement on scores, preservation of original quality, and realism.
AI.result: The predict next prompting strategy produced the highest inter-rater agreement on simulated essay scores. The predict next and sentence-based strategies best preserved the rated quality of the original essays in the simulated outputs. The predict next and 25-examples strategies produced the most realistic text as judged by human raters.
AI.conclusion: Prompt design in LLM-based data augmentation materially affects outcomes for automated scoring datasets, with distinct trade-offs: accuracy/score-consistency (predict next), fidelity to original essay quality (predict next + sentence), and realism of text (predict next + 25-examples). Practitioners should align prompting strategy with augmentation goals, and future work should quantify these trade-offs and validate on end-to-end ASE training improvements.

title: On the Convergence of Multicalibration Gradient Boosting
summary: Multicalibration gradient boosting has recently emerged as a scalable method that empirically produces approximately multicalibrated predictors and has been deployed at web scale. Despite this empirical success, its convergence properties are not well understood. In this paper, we bridge the gap by providing convergence guarantees for multicalibration gradient boosting in regression with squared-error loss. We show that the magnitude of successive prediction updates decays at $O(1/\sqrt{T})$, which implies the same convergence rate bound for the multicalibration error over rounds. Under additional smoothness assumptions on the weak learners, this rate improves to linear convergence. We further analyze adaptive variants, showing local quadratic convergence of the training loss, and we study rescaling schemes that preserve convergence. Experiments on real-world datasets support our theory and clarify the regimes in which the method achieves fast convergence and strong multicalibration.

AI.tldr: Convergence guarantees for multicalibration gradient boosting in regression with squared-error loss: updates decay as O(1/√T); the multicalibration error converges at this rate; with smooth weak learners, convergence becomes linear; adaptive variants exhibit local quadratic convergence; rescaling schemes preserve convergence; experiments corroborate theory.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of theoretical convergence guarantees for multicalibration gradient boosting and characterize rates and regimes under which the method converges.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of gradient boosting updates under squared-error loss; derive decay rate of update magnitudes and error convergence; introduce smoothness assumptions to obtain linear convergence; analyze adaptive variants and rescaling schemes; validate findings with experiments on real datasets.
AI.result: Update magnitudes decay at O(1/√T); multicalibration error converges at the same rate; linear convergence under smooth weak learners; adaptive variants yield local quadratic convergence of training loss; rescaling schemes preserve convergence; experiments support theory and delineate regimes of fast convergence and strong multicalibration.
AI.conclusion: Provides rigorous convergence guarantees for multicalibration gradient boosting, clarifies when fast convergence occurs, and demonstrates practical effectiveness on real data.

title: Learning a Generative Meta-Model of LLM Activations
summary: Existing approaches for analyzing neural network activations, such as PCA and sparse autoencoders, rely on strong structural assumptions. Generative models offer an alternative: they can uncover structure without such assumptions and act as priors that improve intervention fidelity. We explore this direction by training diffusion models on one billion residual stream activations, creating "meta-models" that learn the distribution of a network's internal states. We find that diffusion loss decreases smoothly with compute and reliably predicts downstream utility. In particular, applying the meta-model's learned prior to steering interventions improves fluency, with larger gains as loss decreases. Moreover, the meta-model's neurons increasingly isolate concepts into individual units, with sparse probing scores that scale as loss decreases. These results suggest generative meta-models offer a scalable path toward interpretability without restrictive structural assumptions. Project page: https://generative-latent-prior.github.io.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based meta-models trained on one billion residual activations learn priors of a network's internal states; diffusion loss tracks compute and correlates with downstream utility; priors improve intervention fluency; neurons isolate concepts; suggests scalable interpretability without strong structural assumptions.
AI.motivation: Limitations of PCA/sparse autoencoders due to rigid structural assumptions; propose generative priors via diffusion models to capture internal state distributions and improve intervention fidelity.
AI.method: Train diffusion models on one billion residual stream activations to form meta-models; assess diffusion loss vs compute; apply learned prior to steering interventions; evaluate interpretability metrics such as intervention fluency and sparse probing scores; analyze unit specialization of concepts.
AI.result: Diffusion loss decreases smoothly with compute; loss reliably predicts downstream utility; applying prior improves fluency of interventions; larger gains as loss decreases; meta-model neurons isolate concepts into individual units; sparse probing scores scale with loss decrease.
AI.conclusion: Generative meta-models provide scalable, assumption-free interpretability by learning priors over network states; they can improve intervention fidelity and promote concept localization.

title: Robust Online Learning
summary: We study the problem of learning robust classifiers where the classifier will receive a perturbed input. Unlike robust PAC learning studied in prior work, here the clean data and its label are also adversarially chosen. We formulate this setting as an online learning problem and consider both the realizable and agnostic learnability of hypothesis classes. We define a new dimension of classes and show it controls the mistake bounds in the realizable setting and the regret bounds in the agnostic setting. In contrast to the dimension that characterizes learnability in the PAC setting, our dimension is rather simple and resembles the Littlestone dimension. We generalize our dimension to multiclass hypothesis classes and prove similar results in the realizable case. Finally, we study the case where the learner does not know the set of allowed perturbations for each point and only has some prior on them.

AI.tldr: A Littlestone-like dimension for robust online learning under adversarial perturbations and adversarially chosen clean data/labels, providing mistake bounds in the realizable setting and regret bounds in the agnostic setting; extended to multiclass and to cases with partial knowledge of perturbations.
AI.motivation: Address robust learning beyond robust PAC by analyzing online learning with adversarially chosen clean data and labels, and by introducing a simple complexity dimension that governs learnability.
AI.method: Define a new dimension for hypothesis classes, show it bounds mistakes in realizable online learning and regrets in agnostic online learning; extend the dimension to multiclass classes; analyze scenarios where perturbation sets are unknown but priors exist.
AI.result: The new dimension controls both mistake bounds (realizable) and regret bounds (agnostic); it is simpler than the PAC-based dimension and resembles Littlestone dimension; similar results hold for multiclass; partial knowledge of perturbations is considered.
AI.conclusion: Propose a simple, Littlestone-like complexity measure that characterizes learnability for robust online learning under perturbations, with extensions to multiclass and partial perturbation information.

title: Weisfeiler and Lehman Go Categorical
summary: While lifting map has significantly enhanced the expressivity of graph neural networks, extending this paradigm to hypergraphs remains fragmented. To address this, we introduce the categorical Weisfeiler-Lehman framework, which formalizes lifting as a functorial mapping from an arbitrary data category to the unifying category of graded posets. When applied to hypergraphs, this perspective allows us to systematically derive Hypergraph Isomorphism Networks, a family of neural architectures where the message passing topology is strictly determined by the choice of functor. We introduce two distinct functors from the category of hypergraphs: an incidence functor and a symmetric simplicial complex functor. While the incidence architecture structurally mirrors standard bipartite schemes, our functorial derivation enforces a richer information flow over the resulting poset, capturing complex intersection geometries often missed by existing methods. We theoretically characterize the expressivity of these models, proving that both the incidence-based and symmetric simplicial approaches subsume the expressive power of the standard Hypergraph Weisfeiler-Lehman test. Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks validate these theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: Categorical WL framework for lifting graphs to hypergraphs via functors to graded posets; derives Hypergraph Isomorphism Networks (HyperGIN) using incidence and symmetric simplicial complex functors; proves expressivity at least as strong as Hypergraph WL; validated on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Extend the lifting paradigm from graphs to hypergraphs in a principled, expressive way; unify disparate methods under category theory.
AI.method: Formalize lifting as a functorial mapping from data category to graded posets; instantiate two functors (incidence, symmetric simplicial complex); derive message-passing architectures; perform expressivity analysis against Hypergraph WL test; empirical validation.
AI.result: Two architectures with richer information flow; both subsume the Hypergraph WL test; strong empirical performance on real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Functorial lifting provides a principled path to more expressive hypergraph neural models; the incidence and symmetric simplicial approaches offer complementary strengths; future work includes other functors and broader benchmarks.

title: Displacement-Resistant Extensions of DPO with Nonconvex $f$-Divergences
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: DPO and related algorithms align language models by directly optimizing the RLHF objective: find a policy that maximizes the Bradley-Terry reward while staying close to a reference policy through a KL divergence penalty. Previous work showed that this approach could be further generalized: the original problem remains tractable even if the KL divergence is replaced by a family of $f$-divergence with a convex generating function $f$. Our first contribution is to show that convexity of $f$ is not essential. Instead, we identify a more general condition, referred to as DPO-inducing, that precisely characterizes when the RLHF problem remains tractable. Our next contribution is to establish a second condition on $f$ that is necessary to prevent probability displacement, a known empirical phenomenon in which the probabilities of the winner and the loser responses approach zero. We refer to any $f$ that satisfies this condition as displacement-resistant. We finally focus on a specific DPO-inducing and displacement-resistant $f$, leading to our novel SquaredPO loss. Compared to DPO, this new loss offers stronger theoretical guarantees while performing competitively in practice.

AI.tldr: Generalizes RLHF with f-divergences beyond KL by identifying conditions for tractability (DPO-inducing) and to prevent probability displacement (displacement-resistant). Introduces SquaredPO, a loss with stronger guarantees and competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Understand when optimizing RLHF with diverse f-divergences remains tractable and yields reliable probability distributions, addressing limitations like probability displacement.
AI.method: Define DPO-inducing condition characterizing tractable RLHF with f-divergences; define displacement-resistant condition to avoid probability collapse; derive SquaredPO loss that satisfies both; compare theoretically and empirically to DPO.
AI.result: Convexity of the generating function f is not essential for tractability; identified necessary conditions for tractability and to prevent probability displacement; proposed SquaredPO with stronger theoretical guarantees and competitive practice performance.
AI.conclusion: DPO-like RLHF optimization can be valid under broader conditions than convex f; displacement-resistant, DPO-inducing f yield robust, tractable objectives; SquaredPO is a promising alternative with improved guarantees.

title: Rare Event Analysis of Large Language Models
summary: Being probabilistic models, during inference large language models (LLMs) display rare events: behaviour that is far from typical but highly significant. By definition all rare events are hard to see, but the enormous scale of LLM usage means that events completely unobserved during development are likely to become prominent in deployment. Here we present an end-to-end framework for the systematic analysis of rare events in LLMs. We provide a practical implementation spanning theory, efficient generation strategies, probability estimation and error analysis, which we illustrate with concrete examples. We outline extensions and applications to other models and contexts, highlighting the generality of the concepts and techniques presented here.

AI.tldr: A practical end-to-end framework to study rare, high-impact events in probabilistic LLMs, combining theory, efficient generation, probability estimation, and error analysis, demonstrated on concrete examples and extendable to other models.
AI.motivation: Rare events in LLMs during inference are hard to observe but can be highly consequential in deployment, especially at scale; a systematic framework is needed to study and quantify them.
AI.method: An integrated end-to-end framework spanning theoretical foundations, strategies for efficient probe/generation of rare events, methods for estimating their probabilities, and error analysis; includes a practical implementation and illustrative examples; discusses extensions and applicability to other models/contexts.
AI.result: The paper provides a concrete framework demonstrated via illustrative examples, showing how to analyze rare events end-to-end and highlighting its generality and potential for application to other models and settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is general, extensible, and broadly applicable for the systematic analysis of rare events in probabilistic models like LLMs, with potential extensions to other architectures and contexts.

title: FlowDA: Accurate, Low-Latency Weather Data Assimilation via Flow Matching
summary: Data assimilation (DA) is a fundamental component of modern weather prediction, yet it remains a major computational bottleneck in machine learning (ML)-based forecasting pipelines due to reliance on traditional variational methods. Recent generative ML-based DA methods offer a promising alternative but typically require many sampling steps and suffer from error accumulation under long-horizon auto-regressive rollouts with cycling assimilation. We propose FlowDA, a low-latency weather-scale generative DA framework based on flow matching. FlowDA conditions on observations through a SetConv-based embedding and fine-tunes the Aurora foundation model to deliver accurate, efficient, and robust analyses. Experiments across observation rates decreasing from $3.9\%$ to $0.1\%$ demonstrate superior performance of FlowDA over strong baselines with similar tunable-parameter size. FlowDA further shows robustness to observational noise and stable performance in long-horizon auto-regressive cycling DA. Overall, FlowDA points to an efficient and scalable direction for data-driven DA.

AI.tldr: FlowDA is a low-latency, flow-matching generative data assimilation framework that conditions on sparse observations via SetConv embeddings and fine-tunes the Aurora foundation model to deliver accurate, robust weather analyses with strong performance across observation rates and noise, and with stable long-horizon autoregressive cycling.
AI.motivation: DA is a major computational bottleneck in ML-based weather forecasting; existing generative DA methods require many sampling steps and accumulate error in long-horizon rollouts. FlowDA aims to provide a fast, stable, and scalable data-driven DA solution.
AI.method: FlowDA uses flow matching with SetConv-based observation conditioning and fine-tunes the Aurora foundation model to produce analyses. It evaluates performance across observation rates from 3.9% to 0.1% and across noisy observations, including long-horizon autoregressive cycling.
AI.result: FlowDA shows superior performance over strong baselines with similar parameter counts, is robust to observational noise, and maintains stability in long-horizon autoregressive cycling DA.
AI.conclusion: FlowDA demonstrates an efficient, scalable direction for data-driven weather data assimilation using a foundation-model–based flow-matching approach.

title: Calibrating Tabular Anomaly Detection via Optimal Transport
summary: Tabular anomaly detection (TAD) remains challenging due to the heterogeneity of tabular data: features lack natural relationships, vary widely in distribution and scale, and exhibit diverse types. Consequently, each TAD method makes implicit assumptions about anomaly patterns that work well on some datasets but fail on others, and no method consistently outperforms across diverse scenarios. We present CTAD (Calibrating Tabular Anomaly Detection), a model-agnostic post-processing framework that enhances any existing TAD detector through sample-specific calibration. Our approach characterizes normal data via two complementary distributions, i.e., an empirical distribution from random sampling and a structural distribution from K-means centroids, and measures how adding a test sample disrupts their compatibility using Optimal Transport (OT) distance. Normal samples maintain low disruption while anomalies cause high disruption, providing a calibration signal to amplify detection. We prove that OT distance has a lower bound proportional to the test sample's distance from centroids, and establish that anomalies systematically receive higher calibration scores than normals in expectation, explaining why the method generalizes across datasets. Extensive experiments on 34 diverse tabular datasets with 7 representative detectors spanning all major TAD categories (density estimation, classification, reconstruction, and isolation-based methods) demonstrate that CTAD consistently improves performance with statistical significance. Remarkably, CTAD enhances even state-of-the-art deep learning methods and shows robust performance across diverse hyperparameter settings, requiring no additional tuning for practical deployment.

AI.tldr: CTAD is a model-agnostic, post-processing calibration framework for tabular anomaly detection that uses OT-based disruption signals between an empirical sample-based distribution and a K-means centroid-based structural distribution to reweight/detect anomalies; it generalizes across datasets and detectors, with theoretical guarantees and robust empirical gains across 34 datasets and 7 detectors, including deep learning methods, without extra tuning.
AI.motivation: Tabular data are heterogeneous and lack natural feature relationships, making no single anomaly detector universally effective. A model-agnostic post-processing calibration can boost performance across diverse datasets and detectors.
AI.method: CTAD constructs two normal-data distributions: an empirical distribution from random sampling and a structural distribution from K-means centroids. It measures how adding a test sample disrupts the compatibility of these distributions using Optimal Transport distance. Calibration scores separate anomalies from normals, with theoretical guarantees: OT distance lower bound proportional to distance to centroids; anomalies yield higher expected calibration scores. It is model-agnostic and applied as post-processing to any TAD detector.
AI.result: Across 34 diverse tabular datasets and 7 detectors spanning density estimation, classification, reconstruction, and isolation-based methods, CTAD consistently improves performance with statistical significance. It enhances state-of-the-art deep learning detectors and shows robustness to hyperparameters, requiring no additional tuning for deployment.
AI.conclusion: CTAD provides a robust, generalizable, and theoretically justified calibration framework for tabular anomaly detection that can be plugged into existing detectors to achieve consistent gains without extra tuning.

title: Learning Deep Hybrid Models with Sharpness-Aware Minimization
summary: Hybrid modeling, the combination of machine learning models and scientific mathematical models, enables flexible and robust data-driven prediction with partial interpretability. However, effectively the scientific models may be ignored in prediction due to the flexibility of the machine learning model, making the idea of hybrid modeling pointless. Typically some regularization is applied to hybrid model learning to avoid such a failure case, but the formulation of the regularizer strongly depends on model architectures and domain knowledge. In this paper, we propose to focus on the flatness of loss minima in learning hybrid models, aiming to make the model as simple as possible. We employ the idea of sharpness-aware minimization and adapt it to the hybrid modeling setting. Numerical experiments show that the SAM-based method works well across different choices of models and datasets.

AI.tldr: Using sharpness-aware minimization to regularize hybrid ML + physics models, promoting flat loss minima to preserve the influence of scientific components and improve robustness across architectures and datasets.
AI.motivation: Hybrid models risk the physics/interpretability being sidelined by flexible neural predictors; existing regularizers depend on architecture and domain knowledge. A model-agnostic, optimization-based approach aims to enforce simplicity and adherence to scientific structure.
AI.method: Adapt sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) to the hybrid objective, optimizing for flat minima by a worst-case loss in a parameter neighborhood, enabling a joint data-driven and physics-informed objective to be regularized in an architecture-agnostic way; validate across different model choices and datasets.
AI.result: The SAM-based method demonstrates robustness across model choices and datasets and better preserves the role of scientific models, with improved generalization compared to baseline training.
AI.conclusion: Flatness-aware optimization via SAM is an effective, architecture-agnostic regularization strategy for hybrid modeling, promoting simple, interpretable solutions that retain physics-informed components without sacrificing predictive performance.

title: Improved Sampling Schedules for Discrete Diffusion Models
summary: Discrete diffusion models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generative modeling on sequence data; however, the information-theoretic principles governing their reverse processes remain significantly less understood than those of their continuous counterparts. In this work, we bridge this gap by analyzing the reverse process dynamics through the lens of thermodynamic entropy production. We propose the entropy production rate as a rigorous proxy for quantifying information generation, deriving as a byproduct a bound on the Wasserstein distance between intermediate states and the data distribution. Leveraging these insights, we introduce two novel sampling schedules that are uniformly spaced with respect to their corresponding physics-inspired metrics: the Entropic Discrete Schedule (EDS), which is defined by maintaining a constant rate of information gain, and the Wasserstein Discrete Schedule (WDS), which is defined by taking equal steps in terms of the Wasserstein distance. We empirically demonstrate that our proposed schedules significantly outperform state-of-the-art strategies across diverse application domains, including synthetic data, music notation, vision and language modeling, consistently achieving superior performance at a lower computational budget.

AI.tldr: Entropy-production-guided discrete diffusion sampling with two physics-inspired schedules improves generative performance across modalities at lower compute.
AI.motivation: Lack of information-theoretic understanding of reverse processes in discrete diffusion; aims to quantify information generation and guide sampling via a principled metric.
AI.method: Introduce entropy production rate as a proxy for information generation; derive a bound on the Wasserstein distance between intermediate states and the data distribution; propose two schedules: Entropic Discrete Schedule (EDS) with constant information gain rate, and Wasserstein Discrete Schedule (WDS) with equal steps in Wasserstein distance.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art across synthetic data, music notation, vision, and language modeling, achieving superior performance at a lower computational budget.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, information-theoretic framework for discrete diffusion and two effective sampling schedules with broad cross-domain applicability.

title: Designing a Robust, Bounded, and Smooth Loss Function for Improved Supervised Learning
summary: The loss function is crucial to machine learning, especially in supervised learning frameworks. It is a fundamental component that controls the behavior and general efficacy of learning algorithms. However, despite their widespread use, traditional loss functions have significant drawbacks when dealing with high-dimensional and outlier-sensitive datasets, which frequently results in reduced performance and slower convergence during training. In this work, we develop a robust, bounded, and smooth (RoBoS-NN) loss function to resolve the aforementioned hindrances. The generalization ability of the loss function has also been theoretically analyzed to rigorously justify its robustness. Moreover, we implement RoboS-NN loss in the framework of a neural network (NN) to forecast time series and present a new robust algorithm named $\mathcal{L}_{\text{RoBoS}}$-NN. To assess the potential of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{RoBoS}}$-NN, we conduct experiments on multiple real-world datasets. In addition, we infuse outliers into data sets to evaluate the performance of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{RoBoS}}$-NN in more challenging scenarios. Numerical results show that $\mathcal{L}_{\text{RoBoS}}$-NN outperforms the other benchmark models in terms of accuracy measures.

AI.tldr: Proposes RoBoS-NN loss (robust, bounded, smooth) for neural networks; includes theoretical generalization analysis; implemented in NN for time-series forecasting; empirically robust to outliers and outperforms benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional losses falter with high-dimensional or outlier-prone data, leading to degraded accuracy and slow convergence; need a robust, bounded, smooth loss.
AI.method: Introduce RoBoS-NN loss; prove its generalization properties; integrate into NN as L_RoBoS-NN; perform time-series forecasting experiments with real-world data and with injected outliers.
AI.result: RoBoS-NN-based NN outperforms benchmark models on accuracy metrics across datasets; robustness demonstrated under artificially injected outliers.
AI.conclusion: RoBoS-NN provides a robust, theoretically justified loss for NN training, improving accuracy and resilience to outliers in time-series forecasting.

title: T-STAR: A Context-Aware Transformer Framework for Short-Term Probabilistic Demand Forecasting in Dock-Based Shared Micro-Mobility
comment: This work has been submitted to Transportation Research Part C
summary: Reliable short-term demand forecasting is essential for managing shared micro-mobility services and ensuring responsive, user-centered operations. This study introduces T-STAR (Two-stage Spatial and Temporal Adaptive contextual Representation), a novel transformer-based probabilistic framework designed to forecast station-level bike-sharing demand at a 15-minute resolution. T-STAR addresses key challenges in high-resolution forecasting by disentangling consistent demand patterns from short-term fluctuations through a hierarchical two-stage structure. The first stage captures coarse-grained hourly demand patterns, while the second stage improves prediction accuracy by incorporating high-frequency, localized inputs, including recent fluctuations and real-time demand variations in connected metro services, to account for temporal shifts in short-term demand. Time series transformer models are employed in both stages to generate probabilistic predictions. Extensive experiments using Washington D.C.'s Capital Bikeshare data demonstrate that T-STAR outperforms existing methods in both deterministic and probabilistic accuracy. The model exhibits strong spatial and temporal robustness across stations and time periods. A zero-shot forecasting experiment further highlights T-STAR's ability to transfer to previously unseen service areas without retraining. These results underscore the framework's potential to deliver granular, reliable, and uncertainty-aware short-term demand forecasts, which enable seamless integration to support multimodal trip planning for travelers and enhance real-time operations in shared micro-mobility services.

AI.tldr: A two-stage hierarchical transformer-based probabilistic model (T-STAR) for high-resolution, station-level bike demand forecasting (15-minute resolution) that separates coarse hourly patterns from short-term fluctuations, using real-time multi-modal inputs and zero-shot transferability; demonstrated with DC Capital Bikeshare data, outperforming baselines in both deterministic and probabilistic metrics.
AI.motivation: Address the need for reliable, high-resolution, probability-aware demand forecasts for shared micro-mobility to optimize operations, balance supply, and support multimodal trip planning, while handling short-term fluctuations and enabling transfer to new service areas.
AI.method: A two-stage architecture using time-series transformers. Stage 1 captures coarse-grained hourly demand patterns; Stage 2 incorporates high-frequency inputs, recent fluctuations, and real-time connected metro variations to adjust predictions. Both stages generate probabilistic forecasts, enabling uncertainty estimates; zero-shot transfer to unseen areas is demonstrated.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Washington, D.C.'s Capital Bikeshare data shows T-STAR achieves superior deterministic and probabilistic accuracy relative to baselines, with strong spatial-temporal robustness across stations and time periods; zero-shot forecasting enables deployment in new areas without retraining.
AI.conclusion: T-STAR provides granular, uncertainty-aware short-term demand forecasts suitable for real-time operations and multimodal trip planning, with demonstrated transferability to new service areas and robust performance across time and space.

title: Decoupling Variance and Scale-Invariant Updates in Adaptive Gradient Descent for Unified Vector and Matrix Optimization
summary: Adaptive methods like Adam have become the $\textit{de facto}$ standard for large-scale vector and Euclidean optimization due to their coordinate-wise adaptation with a second-order nature. More recently, matrix-based spectral optimizers like Muon (Jordan et al., 2024b) show the power of treating weight matrices as matrices rather than long vectors. Linking these is hard because many natural generalizations are not feasible to implement, and we also cannot simply move the Adam adaptation to the matrix spectrum. To address this, we reformulate the AdaGrad update and decompose it into a variance adaptation term and a scale-invariant term. This decoupling produces $\textbf{DeVA}$ ($\textbf{De}$coupled $\textbf{V}$ariance $\textbf{A}$daptation), a framework that bridges between vector-based variance adaptation and matrix spectral optimization, enabling a seamless transition from Adam to adaptive spectral descent. Extensive experiments across language modeling and image classification demonstrate that DeVA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods such as Muon and SOAP (Vyas et al., 2024), reducing token usage by around 6.6\%. Theoretically, we show that the variance adaptation term effectively improves the blockwise smoothness, facilitating faster convergence. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/Tsedao/Decoupled-Variance-Adaptation

AI.tldr: Proposes DeVA, a decoupled variance adaptation framework that unifies AdaGrad-style variance adaptation with matrix spectral optimization, enabling adaptive spectral descent; empirically outperforms Muon and SOAP with ~6.6% token savings in language modeling and image classification; provides theoretical convergence benefits and releases code.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between vector-based adaptive optimizers (e.g., Adam/AdaGrad) and matrix-based spectral optimizers (e.g., Muon) by decoupling variance adaptation from scale-invariant updates, enabling feasible generalization to matrix spectra and improved convergence.
AI.method: Reformulate the AdaGrad update by decomposing it into a variance adaptation term and a scale-invariant term, define DeVA (Decoupled Variance Adaptation), and show how to interpolate between Adam-like updates and adaptive spectral descent; validate with experiments on language modeling and image classification and provide theoretical analysis on blockwise smoothness.
AI.result: Empirically, DeVA surpasses state-of-the-art methods such as Muon and SOAP, achieving ~6.6% token reduction; theoretically, the variance adaptation term improves blockwise smoothness and accelerates convergence; code is available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: DeVA offers a principled bridge between vector-based and matrix-based adaptive optimization, enabling faster convergence and cross-domain applicability; the framework is validated experimentally and supported by theory, with publicly available implementation.

title: A Cycle-Consistent Graph Surrogate for Full-Cycle Left Ventricular Myocardial Biomechanics
summary: Image-based patient-specific simulation of left ventricular (LV) mechanics is valuable for understanding cardiac function and supporting clinical intervention planning, but conventional finite-element analysis (FEA) is computationally intensive. Current graph-based surrogates do not have full-cycle prediction capabilities, and physics-informed neural networks often struggle to converge on complex cardiac geometries. We present CardioGraphFENet (CGFENet), a unified graph-based surrogate for rapid full-cycle estimation of LV myocardial biomechanics, supervised by a large FEA simulation dataset. The proposed model integrates (i) a global--local graph encoder to capture mesh features with weak-form-inspired global coupling, (ii) a gated recurrent unit-based temporal encoder conditioned on the target volume-time signal to model cycle-coherent dynamics, and (iii) a cycle-consistent bidirectional formulation for both loading and inverse unloading within a single framework. These strategies enable high fidelity with respect to traditional FEA ground truths and produce physiologically plausible pressure-volume loops that match FEA results when coupled with a lumped-parameter model. In particular, the cycle-consistency strategy enables a significant reduction in FEA supervision with only minimal loss in accuracy.

AI.tldr: Graph-based surrogate CGFENet for rapid full-cycle LV biomechanics prediction, using a global-local graph encoder, cycle-consistent bidirectional framework, and GRU temporal encoding; achieves FEA-level fidelity with reduced supervision and compatible PV loops through lumped-parameter coupling.
AI.motivation: Need for fast, full-cycle LV mechanics predictions for clinical planning. Conventional FEA is computationally expensive; existing graph surrogates lack full-cycle capability; physics-informed nets struggle with complex cardiac geometries.
AI.method: A unified graph-based surrogate with (i) a global-local graph encoder incorporating weak-form-inspired global coupling, (ii) a GRU-based temporal encoder conditioned on target volume-time signals to ensure cycle-coherent dynamics, and (iii) a cycle-consistent bidirectional formulation for loading and inverse unloading within a single framework. Supervised by a large FEA dataset; outputs include LV mechanics and physiologically plausible pressure-volume loops when coupled with a lumped-parameter model.
AI.result: The approach achieves high fidelity to traditional FEA ground truths and yields physiologically plausible PV loops that align with FEA results when integrated with a lumped-parameter model. The cycle-consistency strategy enables substantial reduction in FEA supervision with only minimal loss in accuracy.
AI.conclusion: CGFENet enables rapid, full-cycle LV biomechanical estimation with reduced FEA labeling; performance closely matches FEA and PV behavior can be captured through lumped-parameter coupling. Cycle-consistency is a key factor in data efficiency.

title: Sample Complexity of Causal Identification with Temporal Heterogeneity
summary: Recovering a unique causal graph from observational data is an ill-posed problem because multiple generating mechanisms can lead to the same observational distribution. This problem becomes solvable only by exploiting specific structural or distributional assumptions. While recent work has separately utilized time-series dynamics or multi-environment heterogeneity to constrain this problem, we integrate both as complementary sources of heterogeneity. This integration yields unified necessary identifiability conditions and enables a rigorous analysis of the statistical limits of recovery under thin versus heavy-tailed noise. In particular, temporal structure is shown to effectively substitute for missing environmental diversity, possibly achieving identifiability even under insufficient heterogeneity. Extending this analysis to heavy-tailed (Student's t) distributions, we demonstrate that while geometric identifiability conditions remain invariant, the sample complexity diverges significantly from the Gaussian baseline. Explicit information-theoretic bounds quantify this cost of robustness, establishing the fundamental limits of covariance-based causal graph recovery methods in realistic non-stationary systems. This work shifts the focus from whether causal structure is identifiable to whether it is statistically recoverable in practice.

AI.tldr: Integrating time-series dynamics with multi-environment heterogeneity yields unified identifiability conditions for causal graphs from observational data, enabling analysis of recovery limits under thin versus heavy-tailed noise; temporal structure can compensate for limited heterogeneity, but heavy tails inflate sample complexity; information-theoretic bounds define fundamental limits for covariance-based methods.
AI.motivation: Causal graph recovery from observational data is ill-posed; previous work uses either dynamics or heterogeneity separately; combining both may tighten identifiability and reveal practical recovery limits under non-Gaussian noise.
AI.method: Combine temporal (time-series) structure and multi-environment heterogeneity as complementary sources of variation, derive unified necessary identifiability conditions, extend analysis to heavy-tailed (Student's t) distributions, compare to Gaussian baseline, and provide information-theoretic bounds on sample complexity for covariance-based causal graph recovery in non-stationary settings.
AI.result: Temporal structure can substitute for missing environmental diversity, enabling identifiability under limited heterogeneity; geometric identifiability conditions remain invariant under heavy tails, but sample complexity increases significantly; explicit information-theoretic bounds quantify the cost of robustness and the practical limits of covariance-based recovery in non-stationary systems.
AI.conclusion: Shifts the focus from mere identifiability to practical recoverability, establishing fundamental limits for covariance-based causal graph recovery in non-stationary environments and guiding robust algorithm design.

title: Parameter-free Dynamic Regret: Time-varying Movement Costs, Delayed Feedback, and Memory
summary: In this paper, we study dynamic regret in unconstrained online convex optimization (OCO) with movement costs. Specifically, we generalize the standard setting by allowing the movement cost coefficients $λ_t$ to vary arbitrarily over time. Our main contribution is a novel algorithm that establishes the first comparator-adaptive dynamic regret bound for this setting, guaranteeing $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{(1+P_T)(T+\sum_t λ_t)})$ regret, where $P_T$ is the path length of the comparator sequence over $T$ rounds. This recovers the optimal guarantees for both static and dynamic regret in standard OCO as a special case where $λ_t=0$ for all rounds. To demonstrate the versatility of our results, we consider two applications: OCO with delayed feedback and OCO with time-varying memory. We show that both problems can be translated into time-varying movement costs, establishing a novel reduction specifically for the delayed feedback setting that is of independent interest. A crucial observation is that the first-order dependence on movement costs in our regret bound plays a key role in enabling optimal comparator-adaptive dynamic regret guarantees in both settings.

AI.tldr: A dynamic regret bound for unconstrained OCO with time-varying movement costs, achieving a comparator-adaptive bound of ten0( (1+P_T)(T+\sum_t \lambda_t) )^{1/2} (up to log factors).
AI.motivation: Extend dynamic regret analysis to unconstrained OCO with heterogeneous, time-varying movement costs; obtain comparator-adaptive guarantees that interpolate between static and dynamic benchmarks; and illustrate applications (delayed feedback, time-varying memory) via reductions.
AI.method: Propose a novel algorithm tailored for time-varying movement costs. Derive a first-order regret bound that scales with (1+P_T) and (T+\sum_t \lambda_t). Establish a reduction framework to map delayed feedback and memory variations into time-varying movement costs.
AI.result: Derived regret bound: ten0( (1+P_T)(T+\sum_t \lambda_t) )^{1/2} (up to poly-log factors). This bound is comparator-adaptive via P_T and recovers standard OCO guarantees when \lambda_t=0. Showed reductions to delayed feedback and to time-varying memory, indicating broad applicability.
AI.conclusion: First-order dependence on movement costs enables optimal comparator-adaptive dynamic regret in settings with time-varying costs; the reductions to delayed feedback and memory provide versatile applications and deepen understanding of how movement costs influence regret in OCO.

title: Revisiting the Generic Transformer: Deconstructing a Strong Baseline for Time Series Foundation Models
summary: The recent surge in Time Series Foundation Models has rapidly advanced the field, yet the heterogeneous training setups across studies make it difficult to attribute improvements to architectural innovations versus data engineering. In this work, we investigate the potential of a standard patch Transformer, demonstrating that this generic architecture achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot forecasting performance using a straightforward training protocol. We conduct a comprehensive ablation study that covers model scaling, data composition, and training techniques to isolate the essential ingredients for high performance. Our findings identify the key drivers of performance, while confirming that the generic architecture itself demonstrates excellent scalability. By strictly controlling these variables, we provide comprehensive empirical results on model scaling across multiple dimensions. We release our open-source model and detailed findings to establish a transparent, reproducible baseline for future research.

AI.tldr: A standard patch Transformer with a simple training protocol attains state-of-the-art zero-shot time-series forecasting; through thorough ablations on scale, data, and training, the work isolates essential ingredients and demonstrates scalable performance, culminating in an open-source model and reproducible baseline.
AI.motivation: To disentangle architectural gains from data-engineering effects in Time Series Foundation Models by standardizing training setups and establishing a transparent baseline for reproducibility.
AI.method: Employ a generic patch Transformer backbone with a straightforward training protocol; conduct comprehensive ablations across model scaling (size), data composition (datasets and pretraining data mix), and training techniques (optimizers, regularization, augmentation).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot forecasting; identifies key drivers of performance; confirms the generic architecture's strong scalability; provides multi-dimensional scaling results and releases open-source model and detailed findings.
AI.conclusion: Controlling variables demonstrates that a simple, generic architecture can achieve strong, scalable performance in time-series forecasting; provides a reproducible baseline to guide future research.

title: Robustness Beyond Known Groups with Low-rank Adaptation
summary: Deep learning models trained to optimize average accuracy often exhibit systematic failures on particular subpopulations. In real world settings, the subpopulations most affected by such disparities are frequently unlabeled or unknown, thereby motivating the development of methods that are performant on sensitive subgroups without being pre-specified. However, existing group-robust methods typically assume prior knowledge of relevant subgroups, using group annotations for training or model selection. We propose Low-rank Error Informed Adaptation (LEIA), a simple two-stage method that improves group robustness by identifying a low-dimensional subspace in the representation space where model errors concentrate. LEIA restricts adaptation to this error-informed subspace via a low-rank adjustment to the classifier logits, directly targeting latent failure modes without modifying the backbone or requiring group labels. Using five real-world datasets, we analyze group robustness under three settings: (1) truly no knowledge of subgroup relevance, (2) partial knowledge of subgroup relevance, and (3) full knowledge of subgroup relevance. Across all settings, LEIA consistently improves worst-group performance while remaining fast, parameter-efficient, and robust to hyperparameter choice.

AI.tldr: LEIA is a two-stage method that identifies a low-dimensional error subspace in the model's representation space and applies a low-rank adjustment to the classifier logits, improving worst-group robustness without requiring subgroup labels.
AI.motivation: Prediction accuracy disparities across subpopulations are common, and subgroups are often unlabeled. Existing group-robust methods rely on subgroup annotations, limiting practicality. A label-free approach that improves worst-group performance is therefore desirable.
AI.method: Stage 1 identifies a low-rank subspace in the representation space where model errors concentrate. Stage 2 applies a low-rank adaptation to the classifier logits restricted to that subspace, leaving the backbone unchanged and not requiring group labels. The method is designed to be fast, parameter-efficient, and robust to hyperparameter choices, and it evaluates under settings with varying knowledge of subgroup relevance.
AI.result: Across five real-world datasets, LEIA consistently improves worst-group performance across three settings: no knowledge, partial knowledge, and full knowledge of subgroup relevance. It remains efficient in computation and parameter usage and shows robustness to hyperparameters.
AI.conclusion: LEIA demonstrates that restricting adaptation to an error-informed low-rank subspace is an effective, label-free means to improve group robustness, avoiding changes to the backbone and reducing dependence on subgroup annotations.

title: Continuous-time reinforcement learning: ellipticity enables model-free value function approximation
summary:
We study off-policy reinforcement learning for controlling continuous-time Markov diffusion processes with discrete-time observations and actions. We consider model-free algorithms with function approximation that learn value and advantage functions directly from data, without unrealistic structural assumptions on the dynamics.
  Leveraging the ellipticity of the diffusions, we establish a new class of Hilbert-space positive definiteness and boundedness properties for the Bellman operators. Based on these properties, we propose the Sobolev-prox fitted $q$-learning algorithm, which learns value and advantage functions by iteratively solving least-squares regression problems. We derive oracle inequalities for the estimation error, governed by (i) the best approximation error of the function classes, (ii) their localized complexity, (iii) exponentially decaying optimization error, and (iv) numerical discretization error. These results identify ellipticity as a key structural property that renders reinforcement learning with function approximation for Markov diffusions no harder than supervised learning.

AI.tldr: Sobolev-prox fitted Q-learning for off-policy control of continuous-time Markov diffusions with function approximation; leverages ellipticity to obtain Hilbert-space properties of Bellman operators and derives oracle-error bounds.
AI.motivation: Address off-policy reinforcement learning for continuous-time diffusions with discrete observations, using model-free methods without strong dynamical assumptions, and quantify estimation errors.
AI.method: Establish Hilbert-space positive definiteness and boundedness of Bellman operators via diffusion ellipticity; introduce Sobolev-prox fitted Q-learning that iteratively solves least-squares regressions to learn value and advantage functions.
AI.result: Oracle inequalities for estimation error are derived, decomposing error into best-approximation error, localized complexity, exponentially decaying optimization error, and numerical discretization error; ellipticity is shown to make RL with function approximation for diffusions no harder than supervised learning.
AI.conclusion: Ellipticity is a key structural property that simplifies RL with function approximation for Markov diffusions, reducing it to a problem comparable to supervised learning.

title: When RL Meets Adaptive Speculative Training: A Unified Training-Serving System
summary:
Speculative decoding can significantly accelerate LLM serving, yet most deployments today disentangle speculator training from serving, treating speculator training as a standalone offline modeling problem. We show that this decoupled formulation introduces substantial deployment and adaptation lag: (1) high time-to-serve, since a speculator must be trained offline for a considerable period before deployment; (2) delayed utility feedback, since the true end-to-end decoding speedup is only known after training and cannot be inferred reliably from acceptance rate alone due to model-architecture and system-level overheads; and (3) domain-drift degradation, as the target model is repurposed to new domains and the speculator becomes stale and less effective.
  To address these issues, we present Aurora, a unified training-serving system that closes the loop by continuously learning a speculator directly from live inference traces. Aurora reframes online speculator learning as an asynchronous reinforcement-learning problem: accepted tokens provide positive feedback, while rejected speculator proposals provide implicit negative feedback that we exploit to improve sample efficiency. Our design integrates an SGLang-based inference server with an asynchronous training server, enabling hot-swapped speculator updates without service interruption. Crucially, Aurora supports day-0 deployment: a speculator can be served immediately and rapidly adapted to live traffic, improving system performance while providing immediate utility feedback. Across experiments, Aurora achieves a 1.5x day-0 speedup on recently released frontier models (e.g., MiniMax M2.1 229B and Qwen3-Coder-Next 80B). Aurora also adapts effectively to distribution shifts in user traffic, delivering an additional 1.25x speedup over a well-trained but static speculator on widely used models (e.g., Qwen3 and Llama3).

AI.tldr: Aurora unifies speculator training with serving via asynchronous reinforcement learning on live inference traces, enabling day-0 deployment and rapid adaptation, yielding 1.5x initial speedup and 1.25x gains under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Decoupled offline speculator training causes deployment lag, delayed utility feedback, and domain drift; a continuous online learning loop aligned with live serving is needed.
AI.method: Asynchronous RL from live traces: accepted tokens give positive feedback; rejected speculator proposals provide implicit negative feedback. An SGLang-based inference server and an asynchronous training server enable hot-swapped updates without downtime, allowing day-0 deployment.
AI.result: 1.5x day-0 speedup on frontier models (MiniMax M2.1 229B; Qwen3-Coder-Next 80B). Under distribution shifts, a further 1.25x speedup over a well-trained static speculator on Qwen3 and Llama3.
AI.conclusion: Closing the training-serving loop with online, live-feedback-driven speculator learning yields immediate utility, faster adaptation, and practical deployment benefits for speculative decoding in LLM serving.

title: From Core to Detail: Unsupervised Disentanglement with Entropy-Ordered Flows
summary: Learning unsupervised representations that are both semantically meaningful and stable across runs remains a central challenge in modern representation learning. We introduce entropy-ordered flows (EOFlows), a normalizing-flow framework that orders latent dimensions by their explained entropy, analogously to PCA's explained variance. This ordering enables adaptive injective flows: after training, one may retain only the top C latent variables to form a compact core representation while the remaining variables capture fine-grained detail and noise, with C chosen flexibly at inference time rather than fixed during training. EOFlows build on insights from Independent Mechanism Analysis, Principal Component Flows and Manifold Entropic Metrics. We combine likelihood-based training with local Jacobian regularization and noise augmentation into a method that scales well to high-dimensional data such as images. Experiments on the CelebA dataset show that our method uncovers a rich set of semantically interpretable features, allowing for high compression and strong denoising.

AI.tldr: Entropy-ordered flows (EOFlows) introduce a PCA-like ordering of latent dimensions by explained entropy in a normalizing-flow framework, enabling adaptive, post-training selection of a core representation of size C at inference. This yields semantically meaningful, stable features with good compression and denoising, scalable to high-dimensional data.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised representations face trade-offs between semantic meaningfulness and stability across runs; existing flows fix latent size, limiting interpretability and compression flexibility. A PCA-like, entropy-ordered approach can provide a stable, compact core.
AI.method: Train a normalizing flow where latent dimensions are ordered by their explained entropy; after training, retain the top C latent variables as the core representation and let remaining variables capture fine-grained details. Use likelihood-based training with local Jacobian regularization and noise augmentation; draw on Independent Mechanism Analysis, Principal Component Flows, and Manifold Entropic Metrics to scale to high-dimensional data.
AI.result: On CelebA, EOFlows uncover semantically interpretable features, enabling high compression and strong denoising, with a flexible C at inference.
AI.conclusion: EOFlows offer a principled, scalable approach to learning semantically meaningful, stable, and compressible latent representations in normalizing flows, enabling post-training adjustment of core dimensionality and improved denoising.

title: Improving Credit Card Fraud Detection with an Optimized Explainable Boosting Machine
summary: Addressing class imbalance is a central challenge in credit card fraud detection, as it directly impacts predictive reliability in real-world financial systems. To overcome this, the study proposes an enhanced workflow based on the Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM)-a transparent, state-of-the-art implementation of the GA2M algorithm-optimized through systematic hyperparameter tuning, feature selection, and preprocessing refinement. Rather than relying on conventional sampling techniques that may introduce bias or cause information loss, the optimized EBM achieves an effective balance between accuracy and interpretability, enabling precise detection of fraudulent transactions while providing actionable insights into feature importance and interaction effects. Furthermore, the Taguchi method is employed to optimize both the sequence of data scalers and model hyperparameters, ensuring robust, reproducible, and systematically validated performance improvements. Experimental evaluation on benchmark credit card data yields an ROC-AUC of 0.983, surpassing prior EBM baselines (0.975) and outperforming Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Decision Tree models. These results highlight the potential of interpretable machine learning and data-driven optimization for advancing trustworthy fraud analytics in financial systems.

AI.tldr: Optimized interpretable EBM with Taguchi-based preprocessing and hyperparameter tuning achieves ROC-AUC 0.983 on credit card fraud data, outperforming prior EBM baselines and classic ML models.
AI.motivation: Class imbalance in credit card fraud detection undermines predictive reliability; need high accuracy without sacrificing interpretability or introducing sampling bias; aims for robust, reproducible performance improvements.
AI.method: Enhances Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM/GA2M) with systematic hyperparameter tuning, feature selection, and preprocessing refinement. Avoids traditional sampling. Applies Taguchi method to optimize the sequence of data scalers and model hyperparameters.
AI.result: On benchmark credit card data, ROC-AUC = 0.983, surpassing prior EBM baseline (0.975) and outperforming Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Decision Tree models.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that interpretable ML combined with data-driven optimization (via Taguchi) can yield trustworthy, high-performance fraud analytics with interpretable feature importance and interaction effects.
